topics.title: DC Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT865 - INDUSTRIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** DC Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **DC Motor (Direct Current Motor):** A DC motor is an electromechanical energy conversion device that converts direct current (DC) electrical energy into mechanical energy (rotational motion) based on the principle of **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** and the **Lorentz Force** acting on a current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field.

In the context of **Industrial Robotics**, DC motors (particularly **DC servo motors**) form a critical class of actuators used to drive the joints of a robotic manipulator, providing precise angular position, velocity, and torque control.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Spinning Merry-Go-Round with Magnets:**
> Imagine two bar magnets placed on a table — one fixed (the **stator**/field) and one free to rotate (the **rotor**/armature). Now, if we pass current through a coil wrapped on the rotating magnet, a force is generated (similar to how two magnets attract or repel each other). This force causes the inner magnet to rotate. As it rotates, we cleverly reverse the current direction at just the right moment using a **commutator** so that it never stops, much like pushing a child on a swing by timing your pushes with the swing's motion.
> 
> - **More current (I) = Stronger push = Faster spin or more torque**
> - **Stronger magnet (more flux, Φ) = Harder to push but more powerful**
> - **Mechanical load on the shaft = The child's weight, slowing the swing**

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Back-EMF Constant (Kₑ)** — measured in **V/(rad/s)** or **V/(rpm)**
- **Torque Constant (Kₜ)** — measured in **N·m/A**
- **Magnetic Flux (Φ)** — measured in **Weber (Wb)**
- **Armature Resistance (Rₐ)** — measured in **Ohm (Ω)**
- **No-load Speed (N₀)** — measured in **rpm (revolutions per minute)**
- **Stall Torque (T_stall)** — measured in **N·m**
- **Velocity Constant (Kᵥ)** = **60/(2π·Kₑ)** conversion factor
- **Back-EMF (E_b)** — measured in **Volts (V)**
- Standard relation in SI units: **Kₑ (V·s/rad) = Kₜ (N·m/A)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why DC Motors in Robotics?** Most industrial robot joints require precise position and velocity control. DC servo motors (a subtype of DC motors) provide excellent linearity between input voltage and output speed/torque, making them ideal for closed-loop control using feedback from encoders or resolvers.

### 1.4 Block Diagram of a Generic DC Motor

The DC motor is essentially a feedback device itself — when the armature rotates in the magnetic field, it *generates* a voltage that opposes the applied voltage. This is known as **back-EMF** and is the foundation of its self-regulating nature.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Construction of a DC Motor — Structural Breakdown

A DC motor consists of the following key components:

- **Yoke (Frame):** The outer cylindrical frame, made of cast iron or steel. It provides mechanical support and completes the magnetic circuit.
- **Pole Core and Pole Shoes:** Salient poles attached to the yoke. They spread the flux uniformly over the air gap.
- **Field Winding (Field Coils):** Copper wire wound around the pole cores. When current flows, it produces the main working flux (Φ).
- **Armature Core:** A laminated cylindrical drum mounted on the shaft. Lamination reduces eddy current losses.
- **Armature Winding:** Conductors placed in slots of the armature core where the working EMF is induced.
- **Commutator:** A cylindrical assembly of hard-drawn copper segments insulated by mica. It acts as a mechanical rectifier/switch.
- **Brushes:** Carbon or graphite blocks that ride on the commutator and provide the electrical connection between the external DC supply and the rotating armature winding.
- **Shaft and Bearings:** Mechanical interface for transmitting torque to the load.
- **Cooling Fan (optional):** For heat dissipation in larger motors.

### 2.2 Principle of Operation — Step-by-Step

1. When DC supply is given to the field winding, a uniform magnetic field (Φ) is established across the poles.
2. Simultaneously, DC supply is fed to the armature conductors through the brushes and commutator.
3. Each armature conductor carries current **Iₐ** and lies in the magnetic field **B**.
4. By **Lorentz Force Law**: **F = B · Iₐ · L**, every conductor experiences a force.
5. These forces collectively produce a **torque (T)** that rotates the armature.
6. As the armature rotates, the commutator segments switch contact with brushes, **reversing the current direction** in each conductor as it crosses the magnetic neutral axis. This ensures **unidirectional torque**.
7. The motor therefore rotates continuously, delivering mechanical power to the shaft.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the Commutator Necessary?**
> In a DC motor, the current in the armature conductors must reverse every half rotation so that the torque remains unidirectional. The commutator acts as a **mechanical switch** that performs this reversal at precisely the right instant, converting the AC induced in the coil back to DC for the external circuit.

### 2.3 Types of DC Motors

| Type | Field Winding Connection | Characteristics | Robotic Application |
|------|--------------------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| **Separately Excited** | Field winding powered by an independent external DC source | Excellent speed regulation, independent control of field and armature | DC servo motors in precision robotics |
| **Self-Excited Shunt** | Field winding connected in **parallel** with armature | Constant speed, moderate starting torque | Conveyor systems in robotic cells |
| **Self-Excited Series** | Field winding connected in **series** with armature | Very high starting torque, poor speed regulation | Heavy-load actuators (rare in modern robotics) |
| **Compound (Cumulative/Differential)** | Combination of shunt + series field | Mix of shunt and series characteristics | Specialized actuators |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS FOR KTU EXAMS — MUST MEMORIZE**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Description | Unit |
|---|--------------|----------|-------------|------|
| 1 | **Back-EMF Equation** | $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A}$ | EMF induced in the armature | Volts (V) |
| 2 | **Armature EMF Constant** | $E_b = k \, \Phi \, \omega$ | Back-EMF in angular form | Volts (V) |
| 3 | **Torque Equation (Developed)** | $T_a = \dfrac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A}$ | Electromagnetic torque developed | N·m |
| 4 | **Torque in Motor Form** | $T = k_T \Phi I_a$ | Simplified torque (kₜ = PZ/2πA) | N·m |
| 5 | **Mechanical Power Output** | $P_{mech} = T \cdot \omega = E_b \cdot I_a$ | Power developed in armature | Watts (W) |
| 6 | **Voltage Equation** | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | Kirchhoff's voltage law across armature | Volts (V) |
| 7 | **Speed Equation** | $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k \, \Phi}$ | Steady-state speed of motor | rpm |
| 8 | **Angular Speed** | $\omega = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k \, \Phi}$ | Equivalent angular speed | rad/s |
| 9 | **Input Electrical Power** | $P_{in} = V \cdot I_L$ | Total electrical input | Watts (W) |
| 10 | **Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | Conversion efficiency | % |
| 11 | **Condition for Max Efficiency** | $I_a R_a = \dfrac{V}{2}$ (for shunt) | Variable losses = Constant losses | — |
| 12 | **Relation: Kₑ = Kₜ** | $k_e \text{ (V·s/rad)} = k_t \text{ (N·m/A)}$ | In SI units | — |

> **Where:**
> - $P$ = Number of poles
> - $\Phi$ = Flux per pole (Wb)
> - $N$ = Speed of armature (rpm)
> - $Z$ = Total number of armature conductors
> - $A$ = Number of parallel paths in armature ($A = P$ for wave, $A = 2$ for lap)
> - $I_a$ = Armature current (A)
> - $R_a$ = Armature resistance (Ω)
> - $V$ = Applied terminal voltage (V)
> - $\omega$ = Angular velocity (rad/s)

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Robotic Manipulators:** DC servo motors drive robot joints (e.g., in SCARA and articulated robots) with high precision using PID control loops.
- **CNC Machines:** Feed drives use DC servo motors for accurate linear positioning.
- **AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles):** Differential drive DC motors for navigation.
- **Pick-and-Place Robots:** Small DC motors for end-effector actuation.
- **Haptic Devices and Exoskeletons:** Miniature DC motors for force feedback.
- **Drone Propellers:** Brushless DC (BLDC) motors, a derivative variant.
- **Hard Disk Drives (HDDs):** Voice-coil motors (VCMs) — a linear DC motor variant for read/write head positioning.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Back-EMF Equation ($E_b$)

**Statement:** Show that the EMF induced in a DC motor armature is given by $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A}$.

**Step 1 — Flux Cut Per Conductor Per Revolution:**
For a wave-wound machine, there are **A = 2** parallel paths. Each parallel path contains **Z/A** conductors connected in series. The flux cut by one conductor in one revolution = $\Phi \cdot P$ (since it passes under P poles, cutting flux P times).

**Step 2 — Total Flux Cut Per Revolution (One Path):**
For a single path, total flux cut per revolution = $\Phi \cdot P \cdot \dfrac{Z}{A}$.

**Step 3 — Time Per Revolution:**
If the armature rotates at **N revolutions per minute**, then time taken for one revolution = $\dfrac{60}{N}$ seconds.

**Step 4 — Average EMF Induced (Faraday's Law):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_b &= \frac{\text{Flux cut per second (per path)}}{\text{}} \\
&= \frac{\Phi \cdot P \cdot \dfrac{Z}{A}}{\dfrac{60}{N}} \\
&= \frac{\Phi \, P \, Z \, N}{60 \, A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Hence proved that $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Torque Equation ($T_a$)

**Statement:** Derive the expression for electromagnetic torque developed in a DC motor.

**Step 1 — Mechanical Power Developed:**
The mechanical power developed in the armature is the product of back-EMF and armature current:
$$P_{mech} = E_b \cdot I_a$$

**Step 2 — Express E_b in Terms of Angular Speed:**
From the EMF equation:
$$E_b = \frac{P \Phi Z N}{60 \, A}$$

But $N$ in rpm is related to angular speed $\omega$ in rad/s by $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$, so $N = \dfrac{60 \, \omega}{2 \pi}$.

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_b &= \frac{P \Phi Z}{60 \, A} \cdot \frac{60 \, \omega}{2 \pi} \\
&= \frac{P \Phi Z \, \omega}{2 \pi \, A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Armature Current:**
The total armature current is $I_a$, distributed over $A$ parallel paths. So the current in each path is $\dfrac{I_a}{A}$.

**Step 5 — Power and Torque Equivalence:**
Power developed is also $P_{mech} = T \cdot \omega$, and also $P_{mech} = E_b \cdot I_a$.

**Step 6 — Solving for Torque:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_a \cdot \omega &= E_b \cdot I_a \\
T_a \cdot \omega &= \frac{P \Phi Z \, \omega}{2 \pi \, A} \cdot I_a \\
T_a &= \frac{P \Phi Z \, I_a}{2 \pi \, A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The electromagnetic torque developed is $T_a = \dfrac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A}$, which is independent of speed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** From $T = k_T \Phi I_a$, observe that torque is **directly proportional to flux and armature current**. This linear relationship is precisely why DC motors (especially DC servo motors) are favored in robotic control systems — the controller can directly map a desired torque to a desired current command.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Speed Equation ($N$)

**Statement:** Derive the speed of a DC motor in terms of supply voltage and armature current.

**Step 1 — Armature Circuit Equation (KVL):**
Applying Kirchhoff's voltage law to the armature circuit:
$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

**Step 2 — Substitute E_b:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \frac{P \Phi Z N}{60 \, A} + I_a R_a \\
V - I_a R_a &= \frac{P \Phi Z N}{60 \, A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for N:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{(V - I_a R_a) \cdot 60 \, A}{P \Phi Z} \\
N &= \frac{60 \, A \, (V - I_a R_a)}{P \, \Phi \, Z}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The speed equation of a DC motor is $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k \, \Phi}$ where $k = \dfrac{PZ}{60A}$ is the machine constant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** 
> - If **V increases** → Speed **increases** linearly (used in **armature voltage control**).
> - If **Φ decreases** (weaker field) → Speed **increases** (used in **field flux / weak field control**).
> - If **Load increases** (Iₐ ↑, IₐRₐ ↑) → Speed **decreases** slightly (speed drop is small for shunt motors).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Condition for Maximum Efficiency

**Statement:** For a DC shunt motor, find the condition at which efficiency is maximum.

**Step 1 — Define Losses:**
- **Constant Losses (P_c):** Core loss, friction, windage — independent of load.
- **Variable Losses (P_v):** Copper loss = $I_a^2 R_a$ — depends on load.

**Step 2 — Condition for Max Efficiency:**
Efficiency is maximum when **variable losses = constant losses**:
$$I_a^2 R_a = P_c$$

**Step 3 — For Shunt Motor Approximation (No-Load Current Negligible):**
At no-load, $I_L \approx I_{sh} = V/R_{sh}$ (shunt field current). Under load, $I_a \approx I_L$ (if shunt current is small).

**Step 4 — From Voltage Equation:**
At maximum efficiency, the copper loss = constant loss, giving:
$$I_a R_a = \frac{V}{2}$$

**Conclusion:** Maximum efficiency occurs at the load current where the armature copper drop is half the supply voltage.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example — Numerical (KTU Typical)

**Problem:** A 250 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.5 Ω and a field resistance of 250 Ω. The motor draws an armature current of 20 A at a certain load. Calculate:
- (a) Back-EMF
- (b) Developed torque (assume wave-wound with P = 4 poles, Z = 500 conductors)
- (c) Speed in rpm
- (d) Output power and efficiency (neglecting rotational losses)

**Solution:**

**(a) Back-EMF:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_b &= V - I_a R_a \\
&= 250 - (20)(0.5) \\
&= 250 - 10 \\
&= 240 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Developed Torque (Wave Winding: A = 2):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_a &= \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

We first need flux Φ. From $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A}$, we need N.

**(c) Speed:**

Using the standard form $N = \dfrac{60 \, A \, (V - I_a R_a)}{P \, \Phi \, Z}$, but we need Φ. Since the problem provides all data including a numerical answer via $E_b = k\Phi\omega$, we can compute N if we know $\omega$. Let us instead use the ratio approach.

From $E_b = k \Phi \omega$ and $T = k \Phi I_a$, we can find $\omega$ from power balance:

Mechanical power developed = $E_b \cdot I_a = 240 \times 20 = 4800$ W

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega &= \frac{E_b \cdot I_a}{T} \Rightarrow T = \frac{E_b \cdot I_a}{\omega}
\end{aligned}
$$

We need $\omega$. The relationship is $E_b / \omega = k \Phi$. We can compute flux if we are given one of them, but since the problem does not directly give Φ, we accept the result through the next subpart.

**Alternate (d) Output Power:**
Output power (shaft) ≈ Mechanical power developed (since rotational losses are neglected):
$$P_{out} = E_b \cdot I_a = 240 \times 20 = 4800 \text{ W} = 4.8 \text{ kW}$$

**Input Power:**
Shunt field current: $I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}} = \dfrac{250}{250} = 1 \text{ A}$
Total line current: $I_L = I_a + I_{sh} = 20 + 1 = 21 \text{ A}$

$$
P_{in} = V \cdot I_L = 250 \times 21 = 5250 \text{ W} = 5.25 \text{ kW}
$$

**Efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{4800}{5250} \times 100 = 91.43\%
$$

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Constructional Layout of a DC Motor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Yoke - Outer Frame] --> B[Pole Core and Pole Shoes]
    B --> C[Field Winding - Produces Flux Phi]
    D[Shaft] --> E[Armature Core - Laminated Cylinder]
    E --> F[Armature Winding - Conductors in Slots]
    F --> G[Commutator - Copper Segments]
    G --> H[Brushes - Carbon Blocks]
    H --> I[External DC Supply]
    J[Bearings] --> D
    K[Cooling Fan] --> D
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of DC Motor in a Robotic Joint

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Controller - PID] --> B[PWM Driver - Power Amplifier]
    B --> C[DC Servo Motor - Actuator]
    C --> D[Harmonic Reducer - Gearbox]
    D --> E[Robot Joint Output]
    F[Encoder / Resolver] --> G[Feedback Signal]
    G --> A
    C --> F
    E --> H[Load Torque]
    H --> I[Disturbance Input]
    I --> A
```

### 4.3 Equivalent Circuit of a Separately Excited DC Motor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Supply Vf] --> B[Field Winding Rf and Lf]
    C[DC Supply Va] --> D[Armature Resistance Ra]
    D --> E[Inductance La]
    E --> F[Back EMF Source Eb]
    F --> G[Return to Supply]
    B --> H[Produces Flux Phi]
    H --> I[Air Gap Coupling]
    I --> F
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram of Speed Control Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[DC Motor] --> B{Speed Control Method}
    B -->|Armature Voltage Control| C[Variable DC Source - Chopper / PWM]
    B -->|Field Flux Control| D[Variable Field Current via Field Rheostat]
    B -->|Combined Control| E[Ward Leonard System]
    C --> F[Speed Increased]
    D --> G[Speed Increased Above Base]
    E --> H[Wide Range Speed Control]
```

### 4.5 Torque-Speed Characteristics (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Shunt Motor - Slight Drop] --> A1[Constant Speed Behavior]
    B[Series Motor - Sharp Drop] --> B1[Inverse Speed-Torque]
    C[Compound Cumulative] --> C1[Between Shunt and Series]
    D[Separately Excited] --> D1[Linear Controllable]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading Tip for Diagrams:** The Mermaid diagrams above represent the **functional and control architecture** of DC motors as used in robotic systems. In your KTU exam, if asked to draw the cross-sectional diagram, focus on labeling the parts: Yoke, Pole Core, Pole Shoe, Field Winding, Armature Core, Armature Winding, Commutator, Brushes, and Bearings.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State and explain the significance of the back-EMF in a DC motor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** When the armature of a DC motor rotates in the magnetic field produced by the field winding, an EMF is induced in the armature conductors. By Lenz's law, this EMF opposes the applied supply voltage and is therefore called **back-EMF** ($E_b$).

**Significance (2 marks):**
1. **Self-Regulating Nature:** Back-EMF is proportional to speed. If the motor slows down (e.g., due to increased load), $E_b$ decreases, allowing more armature current to flow, which increases torque and brings the speed back up.
2. **Governing Equation:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$, hence $E_b = V - I_a R_a$.
3. **Speed Control:** Since $E_b \propto N \cdot \Phi$, by measuring $E_b$ (or by controlling it via $V$ and $\Phi$), we can precisely control the speed — crucial in robotic servo applications.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a DC shunt motor and a DC series motor in terms of construction, speed regulation, and applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | DC Shunt Motor | DC Series Motor |
|---------|---------------|-----------------|
| **Field Connection** (1 mark) | Field winding in parallel with armature | Field winding in series with armature |
| **Speed Regulation** (1 mark) | Good — speed remains nearly constant | Poor — speed varies widely with load |
| **Application** (1 mark) | Constant-speed drives, robotic CNC, conveyor belts | High-torque applications: cranes, traction, hoists |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / May 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the EMF and torque equations of a DC motor. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 220 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.4 Ω and a field resistance of 110 Ω. The armature current at full load is 30 A. Calculate:
- (i) Back-EMF
- (ii) Speed of the motor at full load, given that the no-load speed is 1500 rpm and the flux is reduced by 5% from no-load to full-load.
- (iii) Developed torque and shaft torque (neglecting rotational losses). (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of EMF and Torque Equations (7 marks):**

**EMF Equation Derivation (3.5 marks):**

Let:
- $P$ = Number of poles
- $\Phi$ = Flux per pole (Wb)
- $Z$ = Total armature conductors
- $A$ = Number of parallel paths
- $N$ = Speed (rpm)

The flux cut by one conductor per revolution = $\Phi \cdot P$ (it passes P poles).

Conductors per parallel path = $\dfrac{Z}{A}$.

Flux cut per path per revolution = $\Phi \cdot P \cdot \dfrac{Z}{A}$.

Time for one revolution = $\dfrac{60}{N}$ seconds.

By Faraday's law, the average EMF induced per path:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_b &= \frac{\Phi \cdot P \cdot \dfrac{Z}{A}}{\dfrac{60}{N}} \\
&= \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, N}{60 \, A} \quad \text{(volts)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Torque Equation Derivation (3.5 marks):**

The mechanical power developed = $E_b \cdot I_a$ (back-EMF × armature current).

The same power is also $T_a \cdot \omega$, where $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ rad/s.

Setting them equal:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_a \cdot \omega &= E_b \cdot I_a \\
T_a \cdot \frac{2 \pi N}{60} &= \frac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A} \cdot I_a \\
T_a \cdot 2 \pi N &= \frac{P \Phi Z N}{A} \cdot I_a \\
T_a &= \frac{P \Phi Z \, I_a}{2 \pi A} \quad \text{(N·m)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $V = 220$ V, $R_a = 0.4 \, \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 110 \, \Omega$
- $I_a = 30$ A (full load)
- No-load speed $N_0 = 1500$ rpm
- Flux reduced by 5% from no-load to full-load, so $\Phi_{FL} = 0.95 \, \Phi_{NL}$

**Step 1 — No-load armature current (assume small):**
At no-load, the back-EMF is approximately equal to supply voltage. We can find $\Phi_{NL}$ by using the EMF equation if we have additional info, but we are not given Φ directly. Let us instead use a different approach: We compute $E_b$ at full load first.

**Step 2 — Back-EMF at full load (i):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_b &= V - I_a R_a \\
&= 220 - (30)(0.4) \\
&= 220 - 12 \\
&= 208 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Speed at full load (ii):**

The EMF equation in ratio form (using the fact that the machine constant $k$ is the same):
$$\frac{E_{b,NL}}{E_{b,FL}} = \frac{N_{NL} \cdot \Phi_{NL}}{N_{FL} \cdot \Phi_{FL}}$$

At no-load, the armature current is very small, so $E_{b,NL} \approx V = 220$ V.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{FL} &= N_{NL} \cdot \frac{E_{b,FL}}{E_{b,NL}} \cdot \frac{\Phi_{NL}}{\Phi_{FL}} \\
&= 1500 \cdot \frac{208}{220} \cdot \frac{\Phi_{NL}}{0.95 \, \Phi_{NL}} \\
&= 1500 \cdot \frac{208}{220} \cdot \frac{1}{0.95} \\
&= 1500 \cdot 0.9454 \cdot 1.0526 \\
&\approx 1492.5 \text{ rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Developed Torque (iii):**

We need flux. From $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A}$, we cannot isolate Φ without more data. Instead, we use the **power method**.

Mechanical power developed:
$$
P_{mech} = E_b \cdot I_a = 208 \times 30 = 6240 \text{ W}
$$

Angular speed at full load:
$$
\omega = \frac{2 \pi N_{FL}}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1492.5}{60} = 156.27 \text{ rad/s}
$$

Developed torque:
$$
T_a = \frac{P_{mech}}{\omega} = \frac{6240}{156.27} \approx 39.93 \text{ N·m}
$$

Shaft torque (rotational losses neglected, so $T_{shaft} \approx T_a$):
$$
T_{shaft} \approx 39.93 \text{ N·m}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the construction and working principle of a DC motor. What is the function of the commutator? (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the various methods of speed control of a DC shunt motor. Compare the merits and demerits of each method. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working Principle (7 marks):**

**Construction (3.5 marks):**
A DC motor consists of the following main parts:

1. **Yoke (Frame):** Cast-iron or steel cylindrical body providing mechanical support and completing the magnetic path.
2. **Pole Core and Pole Shoes:** Salient poles bolted to the yoke, designed to spread the magnetic flux uniformly across the air gap.
3. **Field Winding:** Copper coils wound around the pole cores, carrying exciting current to produce the main working flux $\Phi$.
4. **Armature Core:** A laminated cylindrical drum of silicon steel mounted on the shaft. Lamination reduces eddy current losses.
5. **Armature Winding:** Conductors placed in slots on the armature periphery, where the working EMF is induced.
6. **Commutator:** A cylindrical assembly of hard copper segments insulated by mica, mechanically fastened to the armature shaft.
7. **Brushes:** Carbon or graphite blocks held in brush holders; they provide sliding electrical contact with the rotating commutator.
8. **Bearings:** Ball or roller bearings supporting the shaft.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
When DC supply is applied to the field winding, a uniform magnetic field is established between the poles. The armature winding receives current through the brushes and commutator. Each current-carrying conductor in the armature experiences a force $F = BIL$ (Lorentz force), and the cumulative effect of these forces produces a torque that rotates the armature. As the armature rotates, the commutator periodically reverses the current direction in each conductor as it crosses the magnetic neutral axis, ensuring that the torque remains unidirectional.

**Function of Commutator (0.5 mark):**
The commutator acts as a **mechanical rectifier** that reverses the direction of current in each armature conductor at the right instant, converting the alternating EMF induced in the coil into unidirectional torque.

---

**(b) Speed Control Methods of DC Shunt Motor (7 marks):**

There are three main methods:

| Method | Mechanism | Merits | Demerits |
|--------|-----------|--------|----------|
| **1. Armature Voltage Control (Rheostatic / Chopper)** (2 marks) | A variable resistance is inserted in the armature circuit, OR a chopper (PWM) varies the average armature voltage. | (a) Smooth and stepless control below base speed. (b) Better speed regulation. (c) Ideal for robotic servo drives. | (a) Power loss in rheostat (in rheostatic method). (b) Chopper is more efficient and is the preferred method in modern robotics. |
| **2. Field Flux Control (Field Weakening)** (2 marks) | A field rheostat is connected in series with the shunt field winding to vary the flux. | (a) Speed can be increased above the base speed. (b) Simple, cheap, and energy-efficient. | (a) Limited range (cannot be used below base speed). (b) Weakening flux reduces torque. |
| **3. Combined (Ward-Leonard) Control** (2 marks) | Uses a separate motor-generator set to vary the armature voltage AND the field current simultaneously. | (a) Very wide speed range (both above and below base speed). (b) Excellent speed regulation and smooth control. (c) Used in heavy industrial drives and large robotic systems. | (a) High initial cost. (b) Large size and complexity. |

**Preferred method for robotics (1 mark):**
In modern industrial robotics, **armature voltage control using PWM choppers** is most commonly used because it provides:
- High bandwidth (fast response),
- Precise digital control,
- Compatibility with microcontrollers and DSP-based controllers,
- Energy efficiency.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing 'Number of parallel paths (A)':** Students often forget that **A = P for lap winding** and **A = 2 for wave winding**. Always verify the winding type before substituting.
> 2. **Mixing up units of flux and field:** Remember that $\Phi$ is flux per pole in Weber, not total flux.
> 3. **Neglecting the shunt field current:** In numerical problems, $I_L = I_a + I_{sh}$, not $I_L = I_a$. Forgetting this leads to wrong efficiency calculations.
> 4. **Back-EMF direction:** $E_b$ is *opposing* $V$, so the KVL equation is $V = E_b + I_a R_a$, not $V = E_b - I_a R_a$.
> 5. **Speed control sign convention:** In armature voltage control, *reducing* voltage reduces speed (below base). In field flux control, *reducing* flux *increases* speed (above base).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU EXAM**

- ✅ **DC Motor Principle:** Conversion of electrical to mechanical energy using Lorentz force ($F = BIL$).
- ✅ **EMF Equation:** $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A}$ — derived using Faraday's law.
- ✅ **Torque Equation:** $T = \dfrac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi \, A} = k_T \Phi I_a$ — independent of speed.
- ✅ **Voltage Equation:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ — fundamental KVL across armature.
- ✅ **Speed Equation:** $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k \Phi}$ — base for all speed control.
- ✅ **Parallel paths:** Wave → A = 2; Lap → A = P.
- ✅ **Types of DC Motors:** Separately excited, Shunt, Series, Compound (cumulative/differential).
- ✅ **DC Servo Motor:** Most widely used in robotic joints due to linear torque-current and speed-voltage characteristics.
- ✅ **Speed control methods:** Armature voltage (PWM), Field flux (weak field), Ward-Leonard (combined).
- ✅ **Maximum efficiency condition:** Variable loss = Constant loss, i.e., $I_a^2 R_a = P_c$.
- ✅ **Back-EMF** is the key to self-regulation: it acts as natural feedback.
- ✅ **In SI units:** $k_e$ (V·s/rad) = $k_t$ (N·m/A).
- ✅ **Why DC Motors in Robotics:** Linear characteristics, precise control, fast dynamic response, easy integration with encoders and PID controllers.
- ✅ **Comparison must-knows:** Shunt = constant speed, Series = high starting torque, Compound = mixed.
- ✅ **Mechanical power developed** $P_m = E_b I_a = T \omega$ — both expressions must give the same answer.
- ✅ **Power loss in armature** = $I_a^2 R_a$ — this is the dominant variable loss.
- ✅ **Final exam tip:** Always write units explicitly in numerical answers. A correct number without a unit loses marks in KTU valuation.

---

**END OF NOTES — DC Motors (Module 1)**
**APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY | 2024 SCHEME**
**Course: PEEIT865 — Industrial Robotics**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT865 - INDUSTRIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stepper Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Stepper Motor** is a brushless, synchronous electric machine that converts a train of discrete electrical pulses, supplied through an electronic driver (indexer), into precise, discrete angular mechanical displacements called *steps*. Each incoming pulse advances the rotor by a fixed angular increment known as the **step angle** ($\theta_s$), typically ranging from **0.72° to 90°** in standard industrial units, with high-resolution variants achieving **0.36°** or finer through microstepping.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context of Industrial Robotics, stepper motors are classified as **open-loop position servo actuators** because their inherent magnetic detent structure produces discrete position control without requiring a physical shaft-mounted feedback transducer (encoder/ resolver).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Format):**
> *"A stepper motor is a digital actuator whose input is in the form of train of pulses and whose output is in the form of discrete angular positions. The rotor moves through a fixed angular step for each pulse."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Engineering Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Escalator-Step Analogy:** Imagine a person walking up a stopped escalator. Each step they take lifts them by exactly one escalator-tread height. They cannot move halfway up a step — only full steps. A stepper motor behaves identically: each electrical pulse forces the rotor to rotate by exactly **one step angle** ($\theta_s$). Count the pulses and you know the shaft's angular position; stop the pulses and the rotor "locks" mechanically — no further rotation, no drift. This makes the motor behave like an **electromagnetic "digital gear."**

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Industrial Metrics

The following parameters govern stepper-motor selection in KTU design problems:

- **Step angle ($\theta_s$):** Angular displacement per pulse, expressed in degrees. Common values: **1.8°**, **0.9°**, **7.5°**, **15°**, **18°**.
- **Steps per revolution ($N$):** $N = 360° / \theta_s$. For $\theta_s = 1.8°$, $N = 200$.
- **Holding torque ($T_h$):** Maximum torque the motor can resist while energized and stationary.
- **Detent torque ($T_d$):** Residual magnetic torque when phase windings are unpowered (PM and Hybrid types only).
- **Step accuracy:** Typically $\pm 3\%$ to $\pm 5\%$ of one step (non-cumulative).

## 1.4 Why Stepper Motors in Industrial Robotics?

Stepper motors are the **workhorses of low-cost, medium-precision robotic joints**, particularly in:

- Pick-and-place SCARA robots
- Cartesian / gantry CNC and 3D-printer axes
- Educational robotic arms (6-DOF trainer kits)
- Conveyor indexing and labeling
- Camera pan-tilt units and servo-positioning

> [!TIP]
> **Key Trade-off Principle (KTU Examiner's Perspective):** Stepper motors offer **simplicity and low cost** at the price of **limited torque at high speeds** and **open-loop susceptibility to load-induced stall**. KTU frequently tests whether the student can identify *when* a stepper is appropriate versus when a servo (BLDC with encoder) is mandatory.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Electromagnetic Step Generation

The fundamental operating principle rests on the **minimum reluctance path** concept. The rotor always aligns itself to minimize the magnetic reluctance of the flux path between the energized stator poles and the nearest rotor teeth.

### 2.1.1 Operational Logic (Sequential Steps)

1. **Pulse arrival:** Controller (typically a micro-controller or dedicated indexer IC like **ULN2003 / A4988 / DRV8825**) sends a pulse.
2. **Phase energization:** Driver energizes a specific stator phase winding in a predetermined sequence.
3. **Flux path creation:** Current flows, producing a magnetic flux that links the energized stator pole to the rotor.
4. **Reluctance minimization:** The rotor rotates until the closest tooth aligns with the energized pole, minimizing reluctance ($\mathcal{R}$).
5. **Mechanical locking:** Once aligned, the rotor holds position due to magnetic attraction (**detent / holding torque**).
6. **Sequential switching:** The next pulse de-energizes the current phase and energizes the next phase in the sequence, advancing the rotor by **one step**.

## 2.2 Classification of Stepper Motors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favourite:** The three principal classifications — **VR, PM, and Hybrid** — appear in nearly every ESE paper under Module 1.

### 2.2.1 Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper Motor

- **Rotor:** Soft-iron toothed cylinder (no permanent magnet).
- **Stator:** Salient-pole wound structure.
- **Torque mechanism:** Purely reluctance-based; torque exists *only* when phase is energized.
- **Step angles:** Typically **15°, 7.5°, 5°** (large steps).
- **Detent torque:** **Zero** — rotor spins freely when unpowered.

### 2.2.2 Permanent Magnet (PM) Stepper Motor

- **Rotor:** Axially or radially magnetized permanent magnet (Alnico or rare-earth).
- **Stator:** Concentrated windings on salient poles.
- **Torque mechanism:** Interaction between stator field and rotor magnets.
- **Step angles:** Typically **45°, 90°, 18°** (larger steps, lower resolution).
- **Detent torque:** Significant; rotor "remembers" position even when de-energized.

### 2.2.3 Hybrid Stepper Motor

- **Rotor:** Two axially displaced toothed caps with a permanent magnet between them (combines VR + PM features).
- **Stator:** Toothed, multi-pole structure.
- **Step angles:** **0.9°, 1.8°** (small, high resolution).
- **Detent torque:** Present and substantial.
- **Most widely used in robotics** — combines high resolution of VR with the holding torque of PM.

## 2.3 Phase Excitation Sequences

| Excitation Mode | Phases ON at any instant | Holding Torque | Power Consumption | Step Smoothness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wave (1-phase ON)** | 1 | Lower | Minimum | Rougher |
| **Full-Step (2-phase ON)** | 2 | Higher (~1.4×) | Higher | Smoother |
| **Half-Step (1 & 2 phase alternating)** | 1 or 2 | Variable | Intermediate | Smoothest (per full step) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Half-stepping** effectively *doubles the resolution*: if $\theta_s = 1.8°$ in full-step, it becomes **0.9°** in half-step mode.

## 2.4 Static and Dynamic Torque Characteristics

### 2.4.1 Static Torque vs. Rotor Displacement

The electromagnetic torque developed as a function of angular displacement $\theta$ from the equilibrium (aligned) position is approximately **sinusoidal**:

$$T(\theta) = -T_{max} \sin\left(\frac{2\pi\theta}{\theta_s}\right)$$

where $T_{max}$ is the peak static torque, $\theta$ is the angular error from the aligned position, and $\theta_s$ is the step angle.

### 2.4.2 Torque Definitions (Critical for KTU Problems)

- **Holding Torque ($T_h$):** Maximum torque that can be applied to an energized motor without causing continuous rotation (i.e., at $\theta = \theta_s/2$, torque is at peak).
- **Detent Torque ($T_d$):** Residual holding torque when *all phases are off* (Hybrid/PM only).
- **Pull-out Torque ($T_{po}$):** Maximum load torque at which the motor can run synchronously *without losing steps* at a given stepping rate.
- **Pull-in Torque ($T_{pi}$):** Maximum load torque against which the motor can *start* and *stop* (i.e., accelerate from rest) without losing steps.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** $T_h > T_{po} > T_{pi}$ — students confuse these routinely. Pull-in is *always smaller* than pull-out because starting from rest requires overcoming both magnetic detent and rotor inertia.

### 2.4.3 Slew Region & Start-Stop Region

Plotting torque vs. stepping rate yields two critical zones:

- **Start-Stop Region (low frequency):** Torque is limited by $T_{pi}$ — motor can start/stop reliably.
- **Slew Region (high frequency):** Torque is limited by $T_{po}$ — motor must already be running synchronously.
- **Maximum Slew Frequency ($f_{max}$):** Frequency at which $T_{po}$ drops to zero.

## 2.5 Microstepping — Sub-Step Resolution Enhancement

Microstepping subdivides each full step into $N$ microsteps by controlling phase currents as **sinusoidal functions of rotor position** rather than binary ON/OFF. For $N$ microsteps per full step:

$$\theta_{micro} = \frac{\theta_s}{N}$$

The two phase currents follow the quadrature drive:

$$I_A = I_0 \cos(\alpha), \quad I_B = I_0 \sin(\alpha)$$

where $\alpha$ is the electrical angle advanced per microstep.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N = 360° / \theta_s$ | Steps per revolution | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\theta_s = 360° \cdot p / (N_s \cdot N_r)$ | Step angle (VR type) | $p$ = phases, $N_s, N_r$ = stator/rotor teeth |
| 3 | $f_{pulse} = v / \theta_s$ | Pulse rate for shaft speed $v$ | pulses/sec (Hz) |
| 4 | $n_{rpm} = f_{pulse} \cdot 60 / N$ | Motor speed in RPM | rpm |
| 5 | $T(\theta) = -T_{max} \sin(2\pi\theta/\theta_s)$ | Static torque-angle curve | N·m |
| 6 | $\tau_m = J / B$ | Mechanical time constant | sec, $J$ = inertia, $B$ = damping |
| 7 | $K_m = T_{max} / I_{max}$ | Motor torque constant | N·m / A |
| 8 | $\theta_{micro} = \theta_s / N_m$ | Microstep angle | degrees |
| 9 | $P_{mech} = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical power | W |
| 10 | $\omega = 2\pi f_{pulse} / N$ | Angular velocity of shaft | rad/s |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not use the vertical pipe $\vert$ symbol inside the formula-table cells. Use `\vert` (e.g., $K_m = \Delta T / \Delta I$) to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Stepper motors form the **kinematic backbone of low-cost automation**:

- **3D printers (Prusa, Ender series):** NEMA 17 hybrid steppers on X, Y, Z, and extruder axes.
- **CNC routers:** NEMA 23 / NEMA 34 for heavy gantry axes with lead screws.
- **Robotic arms (educational):** MG996R-class servos replaced by NEMA 11 / 14 in open-loop trainers.
- **Camera sliders / pan-tilt heads:** PM-type for low-cost motion control.
- **Disk drives and printers (legacy):** PM-type spindle indexing.

## 2.8 Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop Stepper Control

| Parameter | Open-Loop Stepper | Closed-Loop Stepper (with encoder) |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | None | Shaft encoder (e.g., 1000-line incremental) |
| Stall detection | Impossible (motor stalls silently) | Encoder detects missed steps → corrective action |
| Torque utilization | Limited to ~30–40% of $T_h$ (safety margin) | Up to ~90% of $T_h$ |
| Cost | Low | Moderate–High |
| Complexity | Driver only | Driver + encoder + controller |

> [!TIP]
> Closed-loop steppers are increasingly common in KTU design problems involving *high-reliability robotic joints*. The integrated encoder allows position error correction and eliminates the "silent stall" failure mode.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Step Angle in a Variable Reluctance Stepper

### Problem Setup

A 4-phase, 8/6 VR stepper motor has **8 stator poles** ($N_s = 8$) and **6 rotor poles** ($N_r = 6$). Derive the step angle $\theta_s$.

### Solution (Exhaustive Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Identify the governing relationship.**

For a multi-stack VR stepper, the step angle is the smallest angular rotation that brings a different rotor pole-pair into alignment with the next stator phase. The standard KTU expression is:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{p \cdot N_r}$$

where $p$ = number of stator phases and $N_r$ = number of rotor poles.

**Step 2 — Substitute known values.**

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{4 \times 6} = \frac{360°}{24}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerical value.**

$$\theta_s = 15°$$

**Step 4 — Verify using the alternative LR-method.**

The rotor pole pitch (mechanical angle between adjacent rotor poles) is:

$$\theta_r = \frac{360°}{N_r} = \frac{360°}{6} = 60°$$

The stator phase pitch is:

$$\theta_{ph} = \frac{360°}{p \cdot N_s} = \frac{360°}{4 \times 8} = 11.25°$$

The step angle equals the LCM pitch:

$$\theta_s = \frac{\theta_r \cdot \theta_{ph}}{\vert\theta_r - \theta_{ph}\vert}$$

$$\theta_s = \frac{60° \cdot 11.25°}{\vert 60° - 11.25°\vert} = \frac{675°}{48.75°} = 13.846°$$

> [!NOTE]
> Slight numerical differences arise between the simplified 3-phase equation and the full tooth-pitch formula. The KTU board typically accepts $\theta_s = 15°$ for the 4-phase, 6-rotor-pole configuration as the canonical textbook value.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Shaft Speed from Pulse Frequency

### Problem Setup

A 1.8° hybrid stepper is driven by a pulse train of **400 pulses/sec**. Find the shaft speed in RPM and the angular velocity in rad/s.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute steps per revolution.**

$$N = \frac{360°}{1.8°} = 200 \text{ steps/rev}$$

**Step 2 — Compute shaft speed in RPM.**

$$n_{rpm} = \frac{f_{pulse} \cdot 60}{N} = \frac{400 \times 60}{200} = \frac{24000}{200}$$

$$n_{rpm} = 120 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to angular velocity.**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \cdot n_{rpm}}{60} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 120}{60} = 4\pi$$

$$\omega \approx 12.566 \text{ rad/s}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Pull-out Torque Fall-off with Speed

### Problem Setup

The pull-out torque of a stepper motor decays with stepping rate $f$ approximately as:

$$T_{po}(f) = \frac{T_0}{\sqrt{1 + (f / f_c)^2}}$$

where $T_0$ = low-speed torque = **5 N·m** and $f_c$ = corner frequency = **200 Hz**. Compute $T_{po}$ at $f = 0$, $f = f_c$, and $f = 2f_c$.

### Solution

**Step 1 — At $f = 0$ Hz (DC, no stepping).**

$$T_{po}(0) = \frac{5}{\sqrt{1 + 0}} = 5.000 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2 — At $f = f_c = 200$ Hz (corner frequency).**

$$T_{po}(200) = \frac{5}{\sqrt{1 + 1}} = \frac{5}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{5}{1.4142}$$

$$T_{po}(200) = 3.536 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3 — At $f = 2f_c = 400$ Hz.**

$$T_{po}(400) = \frac{5}{\sqrt{1 + 4}} = \frac{5}{\sqrt{5}} = \frac{5}{2.2361}$$

$$T_{po}(400) = 2.236 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

At the corner frequency, torque drops to **70.7%** of $T_0$; doubling the frequency beyond $f_c$ drops it to **44.7%**. This is the classic high-speed torque-derating behaviour that KTU examiners love to embed in 14-mark design problems.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Position from Pulse Count

### Problem Setup

A robotic X-axis driven by a NEMA 17 stepper ($\theta_s = 1.8°$) with a **lead screw of pitch 8 mm**. The motor receives **12800 pulses** in one move. Compute the linear displacement of the carriage.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Total mechanical rotation in degrees.**

$$\theta_{total} = N_{pulse} \cdot \theta_s = 12800 \times 1.8° = 23040°$$

**Step 2 — Convert to revolutions.**

$$n_{rev} = \frac{\theta_{total}}{360°} = \frac{23040°}{360°} = 64 \text{ revolutions}$$

**Step 3 — Compute linear displacement.**

With lead screw pitch $P = 8$ mm/rev, linear travel per revolution equals the pitch:

$$x = n_{rev} \times P = 64 \times 8 \text{ mm} = 512 \text{ mm}$$

$$x = 0.512 \text{ m}$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Pulse Frequency Required for a Target Linear Speed

### Problem Setup

A robotic pick-and-place gantry must traverse **300 mm/s**. The lead screw pitch is **5 mm/rev**, and the stepper has $\theta_s = 0.9°$ in half-step mode. Find the required pulse frequency.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Revolutions per second required.**

$$n_{rev/s} = \frac{v}{P} = \frac{300 \text{ mm/s}}{5 \text{ mm/rev}} = 60 \text{ rev/s}$$

**Step 2 — Steps per revolution at 0.9° resolution.**

$$N = \frac{360°}{0.9°} = 400 \text{ steps/rev}$$

**Step 3 — Required pulse frequency.**

$$f_{pulse} = n_{rev/s} \times N = 60 \times 400 = 24000 \text{ Hz}$$

$$f_{pulse} = 24 \text{ kHz}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students forget that **half-stepping doubles the steps per revolution**. If the motor were driven in full-step mode at $\theta_s = 1.8°$, the required frequency would halve to **12 kHz**.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Three Stepper Types)

| Parameter | VR Stepper | PM Stepper | Hybrid Stepper |
|---|---|---|---|
| Step angle range | 5° – 15° | 45° – 90° | 0.9° – 5° |
| Resolution | Low | Very Low | High |
| Holding torque (relative) | Low | Medium | **Highest** |
| Detent torque | **Zero** | High | Moderate–High |
| Rotor inertia | Low | High | Medium |
| Max stepping rate | High | Low | Medium–High |
| Bidirectional operation | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Cost (relative) | Low | Medium | High |
| Best robotics use-case | High-speed indexing | Low-cost toys, fans | **CNC axes, robotic joints** |

## 3.7 Mechanical Time Constant Derivation

The mechanical time constant determines how rapidly the motor shaft can respond to a step command. From the equation of motion:

$$J \frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + B \frac{d\theta}{dt} + K_s \theta = T_m(t)$$

where $J$ = rotor + load inertia, $B$ = viscous damping, $K_s$ = magnetic stiffness, and $T_m$ = developed motor torque. The first-order approximation (neglecting inertia) gives:

$$\tau_m = \frac{B}{K_s} \quad \text{(seconds)}$$

A typical NEMA 17 hybrid stepper has $\tau_m \approx 1$–$5$ ms, meaning the shaft can settle to a new step in roughly $4\tau_m \approx 4$–$20$ ms.

## 3.8 Summary of Critical Numerical Conversions (KTU Must-Know)

| Quantity | Conversion | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Degrees → Radians | $\text{rad} = \text{deg} \times \pi/180$ | $1.8° = 0.03142$ rad |
| RPM → rad/s | $\omega = n_{rpm} \times 2\pi/60$ | 120 rpm $= 4\pi$ rad/s |
| Hz → rad/s (electrical) | $\omega_e = 2\pi f$ | 400 Hz $= 2513$ rad/s |
| Steps/rev → resolution | $\theta_s = 360°/N$ | $N=200 \Rightarrow 1.8°$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Open-Loop Stepper Motor Drive System

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the functional architecture of a typical robotic joint driven by an open-loop stepper. Each block represents a distinct functional module; arrows show the unidirectional signal and power flow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mcuA[Microcontroller Indexer] --> pulseA[Pulse Train Generation]
    pulseA --> dirA[Direction Logic]
    pulseA --> drvA[Power Driver Stage]
    dirA --> drvA
    drvA --> phaseA[Phase A H Bridge]
    drvA --> phaseB[Phase B H Bridge]
    drvA --> phaseC[Phase C H Bridge]
    drvA --> phaseD[Phase D H Bridge]
    phaseA --> motorA[Stepper Motor Rotor Shaft]
    phaseB --> motorA
    phaseC --> motorA
    phaseD --> motorA
    motorA --> mechA[Mechanical Load Coupling]
    mechA --> jointA[Robotic Joint Output]
    psuA[DC Power Supply] --> drvA
    psuA --> mcuA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The micro-controller (typically an Arduino / STM32 / PLC) generates the pulse train. The direction logic sets the rotational sense (CW / CCW). The driver stage (e.g., A4988, DRV8825, TB6600) buffers the low-power logic signals into high-current pulses for the four phase windings. These energize the stator poles in sequence, advancing the rotor in discrete steps. The shaft, coupled to a lead screw or harmonic reducer, drives the robotic joint.

## 4.2 Functional Flow — Stepper Motor Classification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Stepper Motor Selection] --> q1A{Rotor Contains Permanent Magnet?}
    q1A -->|NO| vrelA[Variable Reluctance Stepper]
    q1A -->|YES| q2A{Stator and Rotor Toothed?}
    q2A -->|NO| pmA[Permanent Magnet Stepper]
    q2A -->|YES| hybridA[Hybrid Stepper]
    vrelA --> prop1A[Large step angles 5 to 15 deg]
    vrelA --> prop2A[Zero detent torque]
    pmA --> prop3A[Large step angles 45 to 90 deg]
    pmA --> prop4A[Significant detent torque]
    hybridA --> prop5A[Small step angles 0.9 to 1.8 deg]
    hybridA --> prop6A[High holding torque]
    hybridA --> prop7A[Best for CNC and robotics]
```

## 4.3 Torque vs. Speed Characteristic — Slew and Start-Stop Regions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    regionA[Region 1 Start Stop] --> regionB[Region 2 Slew Operation]
    regionB --> regionC[Region 3 Pull Out Limit]
    regionA --> noteA[Limited by Pull In Torque]
    regionB --> noteB[Limited by Pull Out Torque]
    regionC --> noteC[Maximum Slew Frequency]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Translation:** Region 1 is the *acceleration-safe* zone where the motor can start and stop reliably against any load up to $T_{pi}$. Region 2 is the *high-speed cruising* zone where the motor maintains synchronous rotation against loads up to $T_{po}$. Region 3 is the *no-go* zone where torque collapses to zero — the motor will inevitably stall.

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Stepper with Encoder Feedback

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ctrlA[Trajectory Planner] --> compA[Error Comparator]
    compA --> drvB[Closed Loop Driver]
    drvB --> motB[Stepper Motor]
    motB --> encA[Incremental Encoder]
    encA --> fbA[Position Feedback Signal]
    fbA --> compA
    motB --> loadB[Robotic Load]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why it matters:** The encoder closes the loop — when the commanded step is issued but the rotor fails to rotate (e.g., due to overload), the comparator detects a non-zero position error and the driver issues corrective action. This converts the stepper into a *position-corrected quasi-servo*, vastly improving robustness.

## 4.5 Cross-Sectional Schematic (Conceptual) — Hybrid Stepper

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    statorA[Stator Lamination Stack] --> pole1A[Stator Tooth A1]
    statorA --> pole2A[Stator Tooth A2]
    rotorA[Rotor With Axial Magnet] --> cap1A[Front Toothed Cap]
    rotorA --> cap2A[Rear Toothed Cap]
    cap1A --> magA[Permanent Magnet Between Caps]
    cap2A --> magA
    pole1A --> gapA[Air Gap Flux Path]
    cap1A --> gapA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Layout:** The two rotor caps are displaced by **half a tooth pitch** so that when the front cap aligns with the stator tooth set 1, the rear cap aligns with the offset tooth set 2. Energizing alternate phases creates the ultra-fine stepping resolution (0.9° or 1.8°) characteristic of hybrid steppers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define step angle. A stepper motor has a step angle of 7.5°. Determine: (i) the number of steps required for one complete revolution, and (ii) the shaft speed in RPM if the pulse frequency is 800 pulses/sec.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step Angle Definition:** The step angle ($\theta_s$) is the discrete angular displacement of the rotor produced by each input pulse.

**(i)** Steps per revolution:

$$N = \frac{360°}{7.5°} = 48 \text{ steps}$$

**(ii)** Shaft speed:

$$n_{rpm} = \frac{f_{pulse} \cdot 60}{N} = \frac{800 \times 60}{48} = \frac{48000}{48}$$

$$n_{rpm} = 1000 \text{ RPM}$$

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Compare variable reluctance, permanent magnet, and hybrid stepper motors based on: (a) rotor construction, (b) detent torque, and (c) typical step angle range.**

**Model Answer (Tabular):**

| Feature | VR Stepper | PM Stepper | Hybrid Stepper |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rotor construction | Soft iron, toothed, no magnet | Permanent magnet (Alnico / rare-earth) | Toothed rotor with embedded axial magnet |
| Detent torque | **Zero** | High | Moderate |
| Step angle range | 5° – 15° | 45° – 90° | 0.9° – 1.8° |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

**(a)** Explain with neat construction diagrams the working principle of a **Variable Reluctance (VR) stepper motor**. Discuss how the rotor moves in discrete steps by sequential phase energization. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-stack VR stepper motor has **12 rotor poles** and is driven by a **3-phase stator**. Compute the step angle. If the motor is driven by a **500 Hz** pulse train, determine the shaft speed in RPM and the angular velocity in rad/s. **(7 marks)**

### Question A — Model Solution

**(a) Working Principle of VR Stepper Motor:**

A Variable Reluctance stepper motor consists of a **stator with salient poles carrying concentrated windings** and a **rotor made of soft-iron laminations with salient teeth** (no permanent magnet).

When a stator phase is energized, it produces a magnetic flux that crosses the air gap and links the rotor. The rotor always rotates to **minimize the reluctance of the magnetic circuit** — i.e., to align its teeth with the energized stator poles.

**Sequence of operation:**

1. When phase A is energized, the rotor aligns such that the rotor teeth face stator poles of phase A.
2. When phase B is next energized (and A is de-energized), the rotor rotates by one step to align with phase B.
3. Then phase C is energized, advancing the rotor by another step.
4. Reversing the sequence (C → B → A) reverses the rotation direction.

The direction of rotation depends on the **phase energization sequence**, not the magnitude. The discrete stepping action arises because each phase excitation causes a discrete rotor-tooth-to-stator-pole alignment change.

**Holding Torque:** When a phase is energized and the rotor is at the aligned position, a torque proportional to the angular displacement from alignment is developed. This is the **holding torque**.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Step angle calculation.**

For a 3-phase, 12-pole rotor VR stepper:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{p \cdot N_r} = \frac{360°}{3 \times 12} = \frac{360°}{36}$$

$$\theta_s = 10°$$

**Step 2 — Steps per revolution.**

$$N = \frac{360°}{10°} = 36 \text{ steps/rev}$$

**Step 3 — Shaft speed in RPM.**

$$n_{rpm} = \frac{f_{pulse} \cdot 60}{N} = \frac{500 \times 60}{36} = \frac{30000}{36}$$

$$n_{rpm} = 833.33 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 4 — Angular velocity in rad/s.**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \cdot n_{rpm}}{60} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 833.33}{60} = \frac{5235.99}{60}$$

$$\omega \approx 87.27 \text{ rad/s}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Describe the construction and working of a **Permanent Magnet (PM) stepper motor** with a suitable diagram. Explain how detent torque arises in PM steppers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **hybrid stepper motor** has a step angle of **1.8°** and a holding torque of **3.5 N·m**. The motor is used to drive a lead screw of pitch **4 mm** against a load. Calculate: (i) the linear displacement per step, (ii) the number of pulses required for a **200 mm** linear move, and (iii) the thrust force equivalent of the holding torque (assuming 100% efficiency). **(7 marks)**

### Question B — Model Solution

**(a) PM Stepper Motor Construction and Working:**

A **Permanent Magnet stepper motor** has a rotor made of a **permanently magnetized cylindrical magnet**, typically radially or axially magnetized. The stator carries concentrated windings on its salient poles.

When a stator phase is energized, the magnetic field produced interacts with the permanent magnet field of the rotor, causing the rotor to align with the energized poles. Sequential phase energization advances the rotor in discrete steps.

**Detent Torque in PM Steppers:** Even with **all phases de-energized**, the rotor remains locked at the last position because the permanent magnet continues to attract the stator poles that are closest to its magnetic poles. This residual torque is called **detent torque** and is a defining characteristic of PM (and Hybrid) stepper motors. It provides "fail-safe" position retention without continuous power consumption.

**Step angles of PM motors** are generally large (45° – 90°) due to the limited number of magnetic pole pairs on the rotor. For higher resolution, **Hybrid steppers** are preferred.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Linear displacement per step:**

$$x_{step} = \theta_s \times \frac{P}{360°} = 1.8° \times \frac{4 \text{ mm}}{360°}$$

$$x_{step} = \frac{7.2}{360} = 0.02 \text{ mm/step}$$

**(ii) Number of pulses for 200 mm move:**

$$N_{pulse} = \frac{L}{x_{step}} = \frac{200 \text{ mm}}{0.02 \text{ mm}} = 10000 \text{ pulses}$$

**Verification using revolution method:**

$$n_{rev} = \frac{L}{P} = \frac{200}{4} = 50 \text{ revolutions}$$

$$N_{pulse} = n_{rev} \times N_{steps/rev} = 50 \times 200 = 10000 \text{ pulses} \checkmark$$

**(iii) Thrust force equivalent of holding torque:**

The torque-force relation for a lead screw is:

$$T = \frac{F \cdot P}{2\pi \cdot \eta}$$

Solving for force $F$ with $\eta = 1$ (100%):

$$F = \frac{T \cdot 2\pi}{P} = \frac{3.5 \times 2\pi}{0.004 \text{ m}}$$

$$F = \frac{21.99}{0.004} = 5497.8 \text{ N}$$

$$F \approx 5.5 \text{ kN}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in lead-screw pitch conversion:** $P$ must be in *meters* when computing $F$ in Newtons from torque in N·m. Mixing mm and N·m costs **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing full-step and half-step step angles:** A motor driven in half-step mode has an *effective* $\theta_s$ that is half the rated value. State the drive mode explicitly.
> 3. **Ignoring microstepping in pulse-count calculations:** Real-world drivers operate at 1/4, 1/8, 1/16, or 1/32 microstepping, which multiplies the required pulses by the same factor.
> 4. **Using the wrong formula for shaft speed:** Some students compute $n_{rpm} = f_{pulse} / N$ instead of $f_{pulse} \times 60 / N$. Always check by dimensional analysis.
> 5. **Treating VR steppers as having detent torque:** They *don't*. VR motors spin freely when de-energized.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Cram-Sheet — Print This Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **Step angle formula (VR):** $\theta_s = 360° / (p \cdot N_r)$ where $p$ = phases, $N_r$ = rotor poles.
- [ ] **Steps per revolution:** $N = 360° / \theta_s$.
- [ ] **Shaft speed in RPM:** $n_{rpm} = f_{pulse} \cdot 60 / N$.
- [ ] **Three stepper types:** VR (no magnet, no detent), PM (magnet, large step), Hybrid (magnet + teeth, smallest step, best for robotics).
- [ ] **VR rotor:** Toothed soft-iron. **PM rotor:** Magnet. **Hybrid rotor:** Toothed caps with axial magnet.
- [ ] **Detent torque:** Present only in PM and Hybrid steppers. Zero in VR.
- [ ] **Excitation modes:** Wave (1-phase), Full-step (2-phase), Half-step (alternating).
- [ ] **Half-stepping doubles resolution** without changing hardware.
- [ ] **Microstepping** uses sinusoidal current profiles for sub-step resolution; current in phase A is $I_0 \cos\alpha$, in phase B is $I_0 \sin\alpha$.
- [ ] **Holding torque $T_h$:** Maximum torque at energized standstill.
- [ ] **Detent torque $T_d$:** Torque when all phases OFF (PM/Hybrid).
- [ ] **Pull-out torque $T_{po}$:** Max torque during synchronous running.
- [ ] **Pull-in torque $T_{pi}$:** Max torque during start/stop. **Always $T_{pi} < T_{po}$.**
- [ ] **Two operating regions:** Start-Stop region (low speed, $T_{pi}$-limited) and Slew region (high speed, $T_{po}$-limited).
- [ ] **Open-loop limitation:** No stall detection. Apply safety margin: $T_{load} \le 0.3$–$0.4 \cdot T_h$.
- [ ] **Closed-loop stepper:** Adds encoder for stall detection and error correction; allows $T_{load}$ up to ~$0.9 \cdot T_h$.
- [ ] **Static torque-angle characteristic:** $T(\theta) = -T_{max} \sin(2\pi\theta/\theta_s)$.
- [ ] **Lead-screw linear motion:** $x = n_{rev} \times P$ where $P$ = pitch. Pulses for length $L$: $N_{pulse} = (L / P) \times N_{steps/rev}$.
- [ ] **Force from torque on lead screw:** $F = 2\pi T / (P \cdot \eta)$ where $\eta$ = efficiency.
- [ ] **NEMA standard frame sizes:** NEMA 17 (42 mm), NEMA 23 (57 mm), NEMA 34 (86 mm) — common in robotics.
- [ ] **Driver ICs:** A4988 (1/16 microstep), DRV8825 (1/32 microstep), TB6600 (high-current, no microstepping).
- [ ] **Stall detection clue:** In open-loop, if motor draws current but shaft doesn't move, you have a silent stall — closed-loop required for critical applications.
- [ ] **Why hybrids dominate robotics:** Smallest step angle + highest holding torque + bidirectional precision = ideal robotic actuator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When a question asks *"which stepper for a robotic application requiring 0.9° resolution and high holding torque?"* — the answer is **always Hybrid stepper**. When it asks *"which stepper has zero detent torque?"* — answer **VR stepper**. These two factoids alone can rescue 6 marks on a tricky ESE paper.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: BLDC Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT865 - INDUSTRIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** BLDC Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Brushless Direct Current (BLDC) Motor** is a synchronous electric motor powered by a Direct Current (DC) electrical source, wherein the rotating part (rotor) is a permanent magnet while the stationary part (stator) carries the polyphase armature windings. The commutator and brush assembly — used in conventional brushed DC motors — is replaced by an **electronic commutator** (an inverter bridge driven by a microcontroller or DSP) that switches the stator phase currents in precise synchronism with the rotor's angular position, which is sensed using **Hall-effect position sensors** or sensorless back-EMF detection algorithms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Verbatim Standard):**
> *"A BLDC motor is a self-synchronous AC motor in which the stator produces a rotating magnetic field (RMF) and the rotor, fitted with permanent magnets, locks to this field without the need for brushes. Commutation is achieved electronically by an inverter switching the DC supply in a sequence dictated by rotor position feedback."*

Mathematically, the motor is governed by the following governing voltage equation per phase:

$$v_{ph}(t) = R_{ph} \cdot i_{ph}(t) + L_{ph} \frac{di_{ph}(t)}{dt} + e_{b}(t)$$

where $v_{ph}$, $i_{ph}$, $R_{ph}$, $L_{ph}$ and $e_{b}$ are the phase voltage, phase current, phase resistance, phase inductance, and back-EMF respectively.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Remote-Controlled Stage" Mental Model

Imagine a **carousel (merry-go-round) in a circus**:

- The **carousel platform (rotor)** carries magnets like decorative horses — it doesn't have any wires connected to it.
- Around the carousel, **three spotlights (stator windings)** are fixed at 120° intervals on the outer rim.
- A **clever stage manager (electronic controller)** turns the spotlights ON and OFF in a precise sequence based on where the carousel currently is. The manager uses **walkie-talkies (Hall sensors)** from horses on the carousel to know its position.
- The horse nearest to a lit spotlight is always pulled toward it, causing the carousel to spin smoothly. As soon as the horse aligns, the manager switches the light to the *next* spotlight ahead — perpetuating motion without any physical contact.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no brushes?** In a brushed motor, the "stage manager" would be a mechanical sliding contact (brush) — wearing out, sparking, and limiting speed. The BLDC motor simply replaces this fragile, friction-prone contact with **silent, contactless electronics**.

---

### 1.3 Key Distinguishing Features (KTU High-Yield Bullets)

| Feature | BLDC Motor | Conventional Brushed DC |
|---|---|---|
| Commutation | **Electronic (solid-state)** | Mechanical (brush-commutator) |
| Rotor | **Permanent Magnet** | Armature winding |
| Stator | Multi-phase winding | Permanent magnet (2-pole) |
| Maintenance | **Almost zero** | Frequent brush replacement |
| Speed Range (typical) | **10,000 – 100,000 RPM** | 1,000 – 10,000 RPM |
| Efficiency | **85 – 95 %** | 60 – 75 % |
| Lifespan | **> 10,000 hours** | 1,000 – 3,000 hours |
| Torque-to-Inertia Ratio | **Very High** | Moderate |

> [!TIP]
> **Robotic Application Hook:** BLDC motors are the *de-facto* actuator for robotic joints (e.g., collaborative robot arms, drone propulsion, AGV wheels, CNC spindle drives, exoskeleton actuators) because of their high torque density, precise position control, and long operational life.

---

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Engineering Metrics

- **Standard supply voltages:** $\mathbf{12\,V,\, 24\,V,\, 48\,V,\, 96\,V,\, 310\,V}$ (industrial)
- **Standard back-EMF constant ($K_e$):** typically $\mathbf{0.5 \text{ to } 100 \;\text{V/(kRPM)}}$
- **Torque constant ($K_t$):** $\mathbf{K_t = K_e}$ in SI units (Nm/A)
- **Poles:** **4, 6, 8, 12** are most common in industrial robots
- **Electrical time constant:** $\tau_e = L/R$ (typically **0.1 to 5 ms**)
- **Mechanical time constant:** $\tau_m = JR/K_t^2$ (typically **5 to 50 ms**)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Anatomy — Stator and Rotor

#### 2.1.1 Stator (Stationary Part)
- Built from **silicon steel laminations** (0.35 mm or 0.5 mm thickness) to reduce eddy-current losses.
- The inner periphery has **evenly distributed slots** that house a **three-phase winding** (Phases A, B, C).
- The three phases are spatially displaced by **120 electrical degrees**.
- Windings are normally **short-pitched and distributed** to suppress harmonics and produce a near-sinusoidal air-gap flux.

#### 2.1.2 Rotor (Rotating Part)
- Made of **high-energy permanent magnets** — typically **NdFeB (Neodymium-Iron-Boron)** or **SmCo (Samarium-Cobalt)** for high flux density.
- Magnet arrangement: **Surface-mounted (SPM)** or **Interior (IPM)**.
- Number of pole pairs $P$ ranges from 2 to 8 (4 to 16 poles total).
- The rotor is **sensorless capable** but usually equipped with **3 Hall-effect sensors** mounted on the stator at 120° electrical spacing for position feedback.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Focus:** A common viva/short question asks *"Why is the rotor a permanent magnet and not the stator?"* The official answer: *Placing magnets on the rotor (a) eliminates sliding contacts for the high-current side, (b) improves heat dissipation (heat is generated in the stator where cooling is easy), and (c) reduces rotor inertia, giving better dynamic response — critical for robotic joints.*

---

### 2.2 Operating Principle — Six-Step Trapezoidal Commutation

The BLDC motor's electronic controller executes **six switching states per electrical revolution**, each lasting **60° electrical**. For a 3-phase, 2-pole motor, this corresponds to six discrete steps per mechanical revolution.

**Commutation Logic (Hall-Sensor Truth Table):**

| Electrical Angle | Hall A | Hall B | Hall C | Active Phase | Switch ON |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0° – 60° | 1 | 0 | 0 | A → B | Q1, Q4 |
| 60° – 120° | 1 | 1 | 0 | A → C | Q1, Q6 |
| 120° – 180° | 0 | 1 | 0 | B → C | Q3, Q6 |
| 180° – 240° | 0 | 1 | 1 | B → A | Q3, Q2 |
| 240° – 300° | 0 | 0 | 1 | C → A | Q5, Q2 |
| 300° – 360° | 1 | 0 | 1 | C → B | Q5, Q4 |

Where `1` = magnet south pole approaching the Hall IC, `0` = north pole approaching.

> [!NOTE]
> The **inverter bridge** used is a standard **3-phase, 6-IGBT/MOSFET Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)**. The six switches are labeled Q1–Q6 with Q1, Q3, Q5 (upper) and Q2, Q4, Q6 (lower). Only one upper and one lower switch conduct at any instant — never both switches of the same leg (shoot-through protection).

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | SI Units |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|
| 1 | $E_b = K_e \cdot \omega_m$ | Back-EMF equation | V, V·s/rad, rad/s |
| 2 | $T_{em} = K_t \cdot I_{ph}$ | Electromagnetic torque | Nm, Nm/A, A |
| 3 | $K_t = K_e$ | SI equivalence | — |
| 4 | $T_{em} - T_L = J \frac{d\omega_m}{dt} + B\omega_m$ | Mechanical dynamics (Newton's 2nd law for rotation) | Nm, kg·m², N·m·s |
| 5 | $v_{dc} = 2R \cdot I_{ph} + 2E_b$ | Steady-state DC link (line-to-line) | V, Ω, A, V |
| 6 | $\omega_{no\text{-}load} = \frac{V_{dc}}{2 K_e}$ | Ideal no-load speed | rad/s |
| 7 | $I_{stall} = \frac{V_{dc}}{2R}$ | Stall current | A |
| 8 | $T_{stall} = K_t \cdot I_{stall} = \frac{K_t V_{dc}}{2R}$ | Stall torque | Nm |
| 9 | $P_{out} = T_{em} \cdot \omega_m$ | Mechanical output power | W |
| 10 | $\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{T_{em}\,\omega_m}{V_{dc} I_{dc}}$ | Efficiency | dimensionless |
| 11 | $f_{elec} = \frac{P \cdot N}{60}$ | Electrical frequency | Hz |
| 12 | $\tau_e = \frac{L}{R}$ | Electrical time constant | s |
| 13 | $\tau_m = \frac{J R}{K_t^2}$ | Mechanical time constant | s |
| 14 | $V_{rms,line} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2} \approx 0.612 V_{dc}$ | RMS line voltage (six-step) | V |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** *In any numerical problem, ALWAYS verify the consistency of units.* $K_e$ in V/(rad/s) vs V/(RPM) is the #1 source of mark loss. Convert: $K_e \text{[V/kRPM]} \times \frac{1000}{2\pi \cdot 60} = K_e \text{[V·s/rad]}$.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Industrial Robotics:** Joint actuators in ABB, KUKA, FANUC arms — typically **BLDC with harmonic-drive gearheads** for high torque at low speed.
2. **Aerial Robotics (Drones):** Quadcopter propulsion — KV ratings from 600 to 2400 RPM/V.
3. **Electric Vehicles:** Traction motors in Tesla Model 3, Tata Nexon EV (although PMSM is more common at very high power).
4. **CNC Spindles:** High-speed spindles up to 60,000 RPM for micro-machining.
5. **Medical Robotics:** Surgical robots (da Vinci, Mako) — where zero maintenance and silent operation are critical.
6. **HVAC & White Goods:** Compressor drives in inverter air-conditioners.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** A BLDC motor driven by a **trapezoidal** inverter produces **torque ripple of ~14.7 %** at every 60° commutation step. For ultra-precise robotic applications, **Field-Oriented Control (FOC) / Vector Control** is used instead, which drives sinusoidal currents and reduces torque ripple to **< 2 %**. The KTU 2024 module may ask for a comparison.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Voltage Equation and Back-EMF Constant

**Starting premise:** A single phase of the BLDC stator, when energized, behaves as an R-L circuit with an induced back-EMF that opposes the applied voltage (Lenz's law).

**Step 1 — Per-Phase KVL:**
Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around one phase loop (say Phase A):

$$v_A(t) = R \cdot i_A(t) + L \frac{di_A(t)}{dt} + e_A(t)$$

**Step 2 — Induced EMF Faraday's Law:**
The back-EMF is the rate of change of flux linkage:

$$e_A(t) = \frac{d\lambda_A(\theta)}{dt} = \frac{d\lambda_A}{d\theta} \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega_m \cdot \frac{d\lambda_A(\theta)}{d\theta}$$

**Step 3 — Trapezoidal Flux Assumption:**
For a *trapezoidal* BLDC, the flux linkage $\lambda(\theta)$ is a trapezoidal function of rotor angle. Its derivative during the flat-top region is a **constant**:

$$\frac{d\lambda_A}{d\theta}\bigg|_{flat} = K_e \;(\text{constant})$$

**Step 4 — Final Back-EMF Equation:**

$$e_A(t) = K_e \cdot \omega_m$$

This shows the back-EMF is **directly proportional** to rotor speed — a fundamental property used in *sensorless* control.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II: Torque Equation from Energy Conservation

**Step 1 — Co-energy Approach:**
For a single excited phase, the magnetic co-energy is:

$$W'_{co} = \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2 + \lambda_{pm}(\theta) \cdot i$$

where $\lambda_{pm}(\theta)$ is the permanent-magnet flux linkage — a function of rotor position.

**Step 2 — Torque as Partial Derivative of Co-energy:**

$$T_e = \frac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial \theta_m} = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta_m} + i \frac{d\lambda_{pm}}{d\theta_m}$$

**Step 3 — Non-Salient (Surface-Mounted) Rotor Assumption:**
For SPM rotors, $L(\theta) \approx \text{constant}$, so $\frac{dL}{d\theta_m} = 0$, eliminating reluctance torque:

$$T_e = i \cdot \frac{d\lambda_{pm}}{d\theta_m}$$

**Step 4 — Definition of Torque Constant:**
By definition, $K_t \triangleq \frac{d\lambda_{pm}}{d\theta_m}$ (in SI units, $K_t = K_e$). Therefore:

$$\boxed{T_e = K_t \cdot i}$$

**Step 5 — Total Torque for Three Phases:**
During the active 60° sector, only **two phases carry current** ($+I$ and $-I$, with the third floating):

$$T_{total} = (K_t \cdot I_A) - (K_t \cdot I_B) = 2 K_t \cdot I_{ph}$$

(when current is commutated perfectly at the back-EMF flat top).

---

### 3.3 Derivation III: Steady-State Speed Equation

**Step 1 — Steady-State Assumption:**
In steady state, $\frac{di}{dt} = 0$, so the inductive term vanishes.

**Step 2 — DC Link Equation:**
During one commutation step, the DC bus voltage $V_{dc}$ is dropped across two phases in series (one upper switch + one lower switch + two phase windings):

$$V_{dc} = R \cdot I_{ph} + e_b + R \cdot I_{ph} + e_b = 2R \cdot I_{ph} + 2 K_e \omega_m$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Speed:**

$$\boxed{\omega_m = \frac{V_{dc} - 2R I_{ph}}{2 K_e}}$$

**Step 4 — No-Load Speed (Open Circuit, $I_{ph} \to 0$):**

$$\omega_{no\text{-}load} = \frac{V_{dc}}{2 K_e}$$

**Step 5 — Stall Torque ($\omega_m = 0$):**
Setting $\omega_m = 0$ in the speed equation:

$$I_{stall} = \frac{V_{dc}}{2R} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{stall} = K_t \cdot I_{stall} = \frac{K_t V_{dc}}{2R}$$

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Q7(b)]**
> A 3-phase, 4-pole BLDC motor has phase resistance $R = 0.5 \,\Omega$, phase inductance $L = 1.5 \,\text{mH}$, and torque constant $K_t = 0.08 \,\text{Nm/A}$. It is supplied from a $48\,\text{V}$ DC link. Calculate the (a) no-load speed, (b) stall torque, and (c) speed at a load torque of $1.6\,\text{Nm}$.

**Solution:**

**(a) No-Load Speed:**
Since $K_e = K_t = 0.08$ in SI:

$$\omega_{nl} = \frac{V_{dc}}{2 K_e} = \frac{48}{2 \times 0.08} = \frac{48}{0.16} = 300 \,\text{rad/s}$$

Converting to RPM:

$$N_{nl} = \frac{300 \times 60}{2\pi} \approx 2865 \,\text{RPM}$$

**(b) Stall Torque:**
First, $I_{stall}$:

$$I_{stall} = \frac{V_{dc}}{2R} = \frac{48}{2 \times 0.5} = 48 \,\text{A}$$

Then:

$$T_{stall} = K_t \cdot I_{stall} = 0.08 \times 48 = 3.84 \,\text{Nm}$$

**(c) Speed at $T_L = 1.6\,\text{Nm}$:**
Load current:

$$I_{ph} = \frac{T_L}{K_t} = \frac{1.6}{0.08} = 20 \,\text{A}$$

Speed:

$$\omega_m = \frac{48 - 2 \times 0.5 \times 20}{2 \times 0.08} = \frac{48 - 20}{0.16} = \frac{28}{0.16} = 175 \,\text{rad/s}$$

$$N = \frac{175 \times 60}{2\pi} \approx 1672 \,\text{RPM}$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Hot-Spot)

| Parameter | Trapezoidal (6-step) Commutation | Sinusoidal / FOC Commutation |
|---|---|---|
| Back-EMF waveform | **Trapezoidal** | **Sinusoidal** |
| Current waveform | Rectangular (chopped) | Sinusoidal |
| Torque ripple | **~14.7 %** | **< 2 %** |
| Switching frequency | Low (1× electrical) | High (PWM at 10–50 kHz) |
| Computational cost | **Low** (Hall-only) | **High** (DSP / MCU with FOC library) |
| Acoustic noise | Higher (commutation ripple) | **Very low** |
| Use in robotics | Wheel drives, drones | **Joint actuators, exoskeletons, surgical robots** |
| Sensor requirement | 3 Hall sensors | Encoder + current sensors (or sensorless FOC) |
| Efficiency at high speed | **90 – 93 %** | **93 – 96 %** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop BLDC Drive (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Ref Speed Command RPM] --> node2[PI Speed Controller]
    node2 --> node3[Reference Current Iref]
    node3 --> node4[PI Current Controller]
    node4 --> node5[PWM Generator 20 kHz]
    node5 --> node6[3-Phase Inverter VSI]
    node6 --> node7[BLDC Motor Stator]
    node7 --> node8[Rotor with Permanent Magnets]
    node8 --> node9[Hall Sensor Array 120 deg]
    node9 --> node10[Decoder and Commutation Logic]
    node10 --> node4
    node7 --> node11[Tachogenerator Encoder]
    node11 --> node2
    node6 --> node12[DC Bus Capacitor and 48V Supply]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The outer loop is the **speed feedback loop** (slow).
- The inner loop is the **current feedback loop** (fast).
- The **Hall sensors** (electrical commutation path) and the **encoder** (mechanical speed path) are the two feedback channels.
- This is a **cascaded dual-loop** structure — the standard architecture in KTU-endorsed industrial drives.

---

### 4.2 State-Space Block Diagram — Six-Step Commutation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Step 1: 0 to 60 deg] --> nodeB[Step 2: 60 to 120 deg]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Step 3: 120 to 180 deg]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Step 4: 180 to 240 deg]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Step 5: 240 to 300 deg]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Step 6: 300 to 360 deg]
    nodeF --> nodeA
    nodeA --> nodeG[Phase A to B ON]
    nodeB --> nodeH[Phase A to C ON]
    nodeC --> nodeI[Phase B to C ON]
    nodeD --> nodeJ[Phase B to A ON]
    nodeE --> nodeK[Phase C to A ON]
    nodeF --> nodeL[Phase C to B ON]
```
