**Reading the diagram:** Each "Step" represents a 60° electrical sector where a specific pair of MOSFETs/IGBTs is ON. The rotor completes one electrical cycle in 360°, after which the pattern repeats indefinitely.

---

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Electronic Commutation Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeMCU[DSP or MCU Controller] --> nodeGate[Gate Driver IC IR2110]
    nodeGate --> nodeHS1[High-Side Switch Q1]
    nodeGate --> nodeHS2[High-Side Switch Q3]
    nodeGate --> nodeHS3[High-Side Switch Q5]
    nodeGate --> nodeLS1[Low-Side Switch Q4]
    nodeGate --> nodeLS2[Low-Side Switch Q6]
    nodeGate --> nodeLS3[Low-Side Switch Q2]
    nodeHS1 --> nodePHASEA[Phase A Winding]
    nodeHS2 --> nodePHASEB[Phase B Winding]
    nodeHS3 --> nodePHASEC[Phase C Winding]
    nodeLS1 --> nodePHASEA
    nodeLS2 --> nodePHASEB
    nodeLS3 --> nodePHASEC
    nodeHALL[Hall Sensors H1 H2 H3] --> nodeMCU
    nodeCUR[Current Shunt Resistor] --> nodeADC[ADC Channel]
    nodeADC --> nodeMCU
    nodeDC[48V DC Supply] --> nodeBULK[Bulk Capacitor]
    nodeBULK --> nodeHS1
    nodeBULK --> nodeHS2
    nodeBULK --> nodeHS3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Viva Question:** *"What is the role of the bulk capacitor?"* — **Answer:** It acts as a local energy reservoir to supply the high peak currents demanded during MOSFET commutation (each switching event is a ~100 A/µs transient). Without it, the DC bus voltage would droop and the motor would produce torque ripple.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is a BLDC motor? Differentiate it from a conventional DC motor.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Brushless DC (BLDC) motor** is a synchronous motor in which the rotor carries permanent magnets and the stator carries polyphase armature windings. Commutation is achieved **electronically** using an inverter and rotor-position sensors, eliminating the need for brushes and a mechanical commutator.

| Aspect | Brushed DC | BLDC |
|---|---|---|
| Commutation | Mechanical (brush-commutator) | Electronic (inverter + sensors) |
| Rotor type | Armature winding | Permanent magnet |
| Maintenance | High | Negligible |
| Speed range | Limited by brush arcing | Up to 100,000 RPM |
| Efficiency | 60 – 75 % | 85 – 95 % |
| Life | ~3,000 hours | >10,000 hours |

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for table, 1 mark for the implication — brush elimination gives long life and high speed.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*List the function of Hall sensors in a BLDC motor. What happens if all three Hall sensors fail simultaneously?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Function (2 marks):**
1. **Rotor position feedback** — Hall sensors detect the instantaneous angular position of the rotor magnet by sensing the polarity of the magnetic field.
2. **Commutation triggering** — the controller uses the 3-bit Hall code (A, B, C) to determine which pair of stator phases must be energized next, ensuring the rotating magnetic field leads the rotor by the correct torque-producing angle.
3. **Speed estimation** — by counting transitions per second, the controller can estimate rotor speed in real time.

**Failure scenario (1 mark):** If all three Hall sensors fail, the controller loses rotor position information, and commutation becomes impossible. The motor will either stop abruptly (safe state) or — if the controller attempts blind commutation — may **lock, vibrate violently, or draw destructive currents**, potentially burning the windings. *This is why industrial BLDC drives include sensor-failure diagnostics that default to a safe stop.*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 1, Q7]**
> *With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a Brushless DC motor. Derive the expressions for back-EMF and electromagnetic torque. A 3-phase, 6-pole BLDC motor with $K_e = 0.05\,\text{V·s/rad}$ and $R_{ph} = 0.6\,\Omega$ is fed from a $60\,\text{V}$ DC link. Find the (a) no-load speed, (b) stall current, and (c) maximum mechanical power output assuming linear torque-speed characteristics.*

**Model Solution (14 marks):**

**(a) Construction and Working — 5 marks**

The BLDC motor has two main parts: **stator** and **rotor**.

- **Stator:** Laminated silicon steel core with 3-phase distributed windings (A, B, C) placed in slots. The three phases are spatially displaced by 120° electrical. Each phase has resistance $R$ and inductance $L$.
- **Rotor:** Surface-mounted (or interior) **NdFeB permanent magnets** with alternating N-S poles. For a 6-pole motor, $P = 3$ pole pairs.
- **Position Sensors:** Three Hall-effect sensors mounted on the stator at 120° electrical intervals detect rotor position.
- **Electronic Inverter:** A 3-phase, 6-switch Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) drives the stator windings from a DC source.

**Working (3 marks):**
The controller reads the Hall sensor code (3-bit digital word), determines the rotor's angular sector, and energizes two of the three phases with currents of opposite polarity. This produces a **stator magnetomotive force (MMF)** that leads the rotor magnet by an optimal torque angle (~90°). The rotor is pulled to align with the stator MMF. Every 60° electrical, the Hall code changes, the controller switches to the next phase pair, and the rotor continues to rotate. This six-step sequence repeats, producing smooth, brushless rotation.

**(b) Derivation of Back-EMF — 3 marks:**

$$e(t) = \frac{d\lambda(\theta)}{dt} = \frac{d\lambda}{d\theta} \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} = K_e \cdot \omega_m$$

where $K_e = \frac{d\lambda}{d\theta}$ is constant over the flat-top region of the trapezoidal flux linkage.

**(c) Derivation of Torque — 2 marks:**

Using the co-energy method and assuming a non-salient rotor:

$$T_e = \frac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial \theta_m} = i \cdot \frac{d\lambda_{pm}}{d\theta_m} = K_t \cdot i$$

In SI units, $K_t = K_e$.

**(d) Numerical Computations — 4 marks:**

Given: $K_e = 0.05\,\text{V·s/rad}$, $R_{ph} = 0.6\,\Omega$, $V_{dc} = 60\,\text{V}$.

**(i) No-load speed:**

$$\omega_{nl} = \frac{V_{dc}}{2K_e} = \frac{60}{2 \times 0.05} = 600 \,\text{rad/s}$$

Converting to RPM: $N_{nl} = \frac{600 \times 60}{2\pi} \approx 5730\,\text{RPM}$

**(ii) Stall current:**

$$I_{stall} = \frac{V_{dc}}{2R_{ph}} = \frac{60}{2 \times 0.6} = 50\,\text{A}$$

**(iii) Maximum mechanical power:**
Under a linear torque-speed characteristic, maximum power is delivered at $\omega = \frac{\omega_{nl}}{2}$ and $T = \frac{T_{stall}}{2}$:

$$T_{stall} = K_t \cdot I_{stall} = 0.05 \times 50 = 2.5\,\text{Nm}$$

$$P_{max} = \frac{T_{stall} \cdot \omega_{nl}}{4} = \frac{2.5 \times 600}{4} = 375\,\text{W}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 1, Q7 — Alternative]**
> *Compare trapezoidal and sinusoidal commutation techniques for a BLDC motor in the context of industrial robotic actuators. With a suitable block diagram, explain the closed-loop speed control of a BLDC motor using a PI controller. A robot joint uses a BLDC motor with $V_{dc} = 24\,\text{V}$, $K_e = 0.03\,\text{V·s/rad}$, and $R = 0.4\,\Omega$. The motor drives a 5 kg payload with a gear ratio of 20:1. Calculate the no-load speed at the joint (after gearing) and the joint's stall torque, assuming the rotor inertia is negligible.*

**Model Solution (14 marks):**

**(a) Comparison: Trapezoidal vs Sinusoidal Commutation — 5 marks**

| Aspect | Trapezoidal (Six-step) | Sinusoidal (FOC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Back-EMF shape** | Trapezoidal | Sinusoidal |
| **Current shape** | Rectangular (chopped PWM) | Sinusoidal PWM |
| **Torque ripple** | ~14.7 % | < 2 % |
| **Position sensor** | 3 Hall sensors (low resolution) | Encoder + current sensors |
| **Computational load** | Minimal (look-up table) | High (Park/Clarke transforms) |
| **Switching losses** | Lower | Higher (continuous PWM) |
| **Suitability for robotics** | Wheel drives, drones | Precision joints, exoskeletons, surgical robots |

For **industrial robotic joints** where smooth torque, low acoustic noise, and high bandwidth are required, **sinusoidal commutation / FOC** is preferred despite the higher cost.

**(b) Closed-Loop PI Speed Control — 5 marks**

A dual-loop (cascaded) structure is used:
1. **Outer speed loop:** The reference speed $\omega^*$ is compared with the measured speed $\omega$ from an encoder. The error $e_\omega = \omega^* - \omega$ is fed to a PI controller producing the reference current $I^*$.
2. **Inner current loop:** The reference current $I^*$ is compared with the measured phase current $I$. The error is fed to a second PI controller, which generates the PWM duty cycle.
3. **PWM Inverter:** Generates the 3-phase voltages that drive the BLDC.
4. **Hall sensors + encoder:** Provide commutation signals (electrical position) and speed (mechanical feedback).

PI tuning: $K_p$ determines the response speed; $K_i$ eliminates steady-state error. For robotic joints, the speed-loop bandwidth is typically 50–200 Hz, while the current-loop bandwidth is 1–5 kHz.

**(c) Numerical Computations — 4 marks**

Given: $V_{dc} = 24\,\text{V}$, $K_e = 0.03\,\text{V·s/rad}$, $R = 0.4\,\Omega$, gear ratio $G = 20$.

**(i) Joint no-load speed (after gearing):**

$$\omega_{rotor,nl} = \frac{V_{dc}}{2K_e} = \frac{24}{2 \times 0.03} = 400\,\text{rad/s}$$

After 20:1 gear reduction:

$$\omega_{joint,nl} = \frac{\omega_{rotor,nl}}{G} = \frac{400}{20} = 20\,\text{rad/s}$$

Converting: $N_{joint,nl} = \frac{20 \times 60}{2\pi} \approx 191\,\text{RPM}$ (joint revolution speed).

**(ii) Joint stall torque:**

Rotor stall current: $I_{stall} = \frac{V_{dc}}{2R} = \frac{24}{2 \times 0.4} = 30\,\text{A}$

Rotor stall torque: $T_{stall,rotor} = K_t \cdot I_{stall} = 0.03 \times 30 = 0.9\,\text{Nm}$

Joint stall torque (after gear-up, torque is multiplied by gear ratio):

$$T_{stall,joint} = T_{stall,rotor} \times G = 0.9 \times 20 = 18\,\text{Nm}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in BLDC Motor Problems:**
> 1. **Unit mix-up between $K_e$ in V/(krpm) and V·s/rad** — causes all subsequent numerical answers to be off by a factor of ~1047. Always convert explicitly. *(–2 to –4 marks in a 14-mark question)*
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the DC-link equation** — the voltage equation is $V_{dc} = 2R I + 2K_e \omega$ (two phases in series), not $V_{dc} = R I + K_e \omega$. *(–2 marks)*
> 3. **Confusing electrical and mechanical degrees** — for a 6-pole motor, 360° electrical = 120° mechanical. Use $f_{elec} = \frac{P \cdot N_{mech}}{60}$. *(–2 marks)*
> 4. **Stating "BLDC has no torque ripple"** — *Wrong.* Six-step commutation has ~14.7 % ripple. Sinusoidal FOC has < 2 %. Examiners expect this distinction. *(–1 mark)*
> 5. **Wrong definition of "sensorless control"** — it is *not* "no sensors." It uses **back-EMF zero-crossing detection** or **observer-based** rotor estimation, requiring no *external* position sensors.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **BLDC motor:** Synchronous, electronically commutated, with rotor-mounted permanent magnets and stator-mounted polyphase windings. *(1 line for the board exam)*
- **Key relationship:** $K_t = K_e$ in SI units; back-EMF $E_b = K_e \omega$; torque $T = K_t I$.
- **Six-step commutation** is the simplest control scheme using **3 Hall sensors**; rotor advances 60° electrical per step.
- **Steady-state speed equation:** $\omega_m = \frac{V_{dc} - 2RI_{ph}}{2K_e}$ — this is the *single most important formula* for KTU 2024 numerical problems.
- **No-load speed:** $\omega_{nl} = \frac{V_{dc}}{2K_e}$ — at $I_{ph} = 0$.
- **Stall torque:** $T_{stall} = \frac{K_t V_{dc}}{2R}$ — at $\omega_m = 0$.
- **Maximum power point** of a linear torque-speed curve is at $\frac{\omega_{nl}}{2}$ and $\frac{T_{stall}}{2}$ giving $P_{max} = \frac{V_{dc}^2}{16R}$.
- **Comparison pair to remember:** Trapezoidal (high ripple, simple) vs Sinusoidal/FOC (low ripple, complex). Examiners love this contrast.
- **Robotic relevance:** BLDC motors are the *dominant* robotic joint actuator due to high torque density, low maintenance, and precise controllability. Always cite at least one robotic example (e.g., KUKA KR 16 R2010, drone propulsion) for full marks on "application" sub-questions.
- **Gearing effect:** Speed is divided by gear ratio $G$, torque is multiplied by $G$ — energy is conserved.
- **Sensor failure mode:** A BLDC *cannot* run without rotor position information — this is its Achilles' heel, motivating sensorless FOC algorithms.
- **Efficiency benchmark:** Industrial BLDC drives reach 93–96 % at rated load — substantially higher than brushed DC (60–75 %) and induction motors (85–90 %).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synchronous AC Servo Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT865 - INDUSTRIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Synchronous AC Servo Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Synchronous AC Servo Motor** is a specialized rotary actuator that operates at a constant speed strictly synchronized with the frequency of the supply current, while employing a closed-loop feedback control system (typically via an encoder or resolver) to achieve precise control over **angular position, velocity, and torque**. It is a permanent-magnet (PM) or reluctance-based machine whose rotor mechanically locks in synchronism with the rotating stator magnetic field, and is the workhorse prime-mover of modern industrial robotics, CNC machinery, and automated assembly lines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Synchronous AC Servo Motors are the **de-facto standard** for robotic joint actuation in KTU 2024 Scheme industrial robotics, replacing DC and stepper motors in high-performance applications due to their superior **torque-to-inertia ratio**, **bandwidth**, and **thermal dissipation**.

## 1.2 Key Identification Markers of a Synchronous AC Servo Motor

| Marker | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Rotor Type** | Permanent Magnet (PM) or Reluctance type — *no slip rings for excitation* |
| **Speed Equation** | $N_s = \frac{120 f}{P}$ — speed is rigidly tied to supply frequency |
| **Feedback Element** | Incremental/Absolute Encoder, Resolver, or Hall sensors |
| **Drive Topology** | Three-phase PWM inverter (VFD/Variable Frequency Drive) |
| **Commutation** | Electronic (brushless) — *no mechanical commutator* |
| **Key Constant** | **Torque constant $K_t$** (Nm/A) and **Back-EMF constant $K_e$** (V·s/rad) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Dancer in Perfect Sync"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a ballroom dancer (the **rotor**) locked in an embrace with the music's tempo (the **rotating stator field**). The dancer does **not** slip or fall behind the beat — every step (electrical cycle) is matched. A coach standing at the side (the **encoder + controller**) constantly whispers corrections: *"turn 30° more," "slow down 5 rpm."* The dancer obeys instantly because he trusts the coach's feedback.

In this analogy:
- The **music tempo** = the stator's rotating magnetic field (set by VFD frequency $f$).
- The **dancer** = the permanent-magnet rotor, magnetically locked to the field.
- The **coach's whistle** = the encoder pulses feeding position error to the controller.
- The **choreography** = the reference trajectory commanded by the robot's motion planner.

> [!TIP]
> This is why it is called a **SERVO** motor: it **SERVES** a commanded position/velocity profile with high fidelity, unlike an induction motor which simply runs at a speed dictated by the load.

## 1.4 Why Industrial Robotics Prefers Synchronous AC Servos

1. **Zero steady-state error** in position when in closed-loop.
2. **High peak torque** for short durations (acceleration/deceleration of robot joints).
3. **Excellent heat dissipation** — heat is generated in the stationary stator, not the rotating rotor.
4. **High power density** — small frame size, high torque output (critical for robot arms).
5. **Smooth torque ripple** when properly wound (sinusoidal back-EMF design).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Architecture

A synchronous AC servo motor consists of two main sub-assemblies:

### A. The Stator (Stationary Member)
- Laminated silicon-steel core with **three-phase sinusoidally distributed windings** (U, V, W) physically displaced by **120° electrical** in space.
- The windings are star-connected (wye) and fed by a 3-phase PWM inverter.
- Slots are often **semi-closed** to reduce cogging torque and slot harmonics.

### B. The Rotor (Rotating Member)
Two principal constructions exist:

**(i) Surface-Mounted Permanent Magnet (SPM) Rotor**
- Magnets (typically NdFeB — Neodymium Iron Boron, with $B_r \approx 1.2$ T) are glued to the outer surface of a ferromagnetic hub.
- Produces a **sinusoidal air-gap flux distribution** → sinusoidal back-EMF → smooth low-speed torque.

**(ii) Interior Permanent Magnet (IPM) Rotor**
- Magnets are embedded *inside* the rotor lamination pockets.
- Generates **reluctance torque** in addition to magnet torque → higher power density.
- Mechanical robustness is superior at high speeds (magnets don't fly off due to centrifugal force).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** Robot joint motors (e.g., in 6-axis arms like KUKA, FANUC, ABB) predominantly use **IPM synchronous servo motors** because of their high-speed safety and field-weakening capability.

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step

1. **Three-phase balanced sinusoidal currents** $i_a, i_b, i_c$ are injected by the inverter into the stator windings, separated in time by 120°.
2. These currents produce a **rotating magnetic field** in the air gap, rotating at synchronous speed:
$$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P}$$
where $f$ is supply frequency in Hz and $P$ is the number of poles.
3. The rotor's permanent magnets experience a force/torque trying to align their magnetic axis with the stator field axis (minimum reluctance path).
4. Due to rotor inertia, the rotor "chases" the stator field but **cannot catch up and slip** — it locks magnetically and rotates at exactly $N_s$.
5. The shaft-mounted **encoder** continuously reports the actual rotor angle $\theta_{actual}$ to the digital controller (DSP/FPGA-based servo drive).
6. The controller computes the **position error** $e = \theta_{ref} - \theta_{actual}$ and modulates the inverter PWM duty cycles to minimize it via **PI/PID control loops** (current loop → velocity loop → position loop, cascaded).

## 2.3 Control Loop Hierarchy (Cascaded Structure)

Modern servo drives use **three nested control loops**, executed at different sampling rates:

| Loop | Inner/Outer | Typical Bandwidth | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Current (Torque) Loop** | Innermost | 1–4 kHz | Regulates $i_q$ and $i_d$ (torque & flux producing currents) |
| **Velocity Loop** | Middle | 200–500 Hz | Controls shaft speed $\omega_m$ via PI on speed error |
| **Position Loop** | Outermost | 50–100 Hz | Drives position error to zero, often P or P/feed-forward |

> [!TIP]
> **Field-Oriented Control (FOC) / Vector Control:** The current loop operates in the **d-q reference frame** rotating with the rotor, decoupling torque-producing ($q$-axis) and flux-producing ($d$-axis) currents. This is the **mathematical heart** of every modern AC servo drive.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM ALERT:** Memorize the following equations. They form the spine of virtually every numerical problem on this topic in KTU ESE.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed | **rpm** |
| 2 | $\omega_s = \frac{2 \pi f}{P/2} = \frac{4 \pi f}{P}$ | Synchronous mechanical angular speed | **rad/s** |
| 3 | $E_b = K_e \, \omega_m$ | Back-EMF (RMS) | **V** |
| 4 | $T_e = K_t \, I_a$ | Electromagnetic torque | **Nm** |
| 5 | $K_e = K_t$ (in SI units) | Torque-BackEMF equivalence | $K_e$ in V·s/rad, $K_t$ in Nm/A |
| 6 | $P_{mech} = T_e \, \omega_m$ | Mechanical power developed | **W** |
| 7 | $P_{in,elec} = 3 \, V_{ph} \, I_{ph} \cos\phi$ | Three-phase input power | **W** |
| 8 | $\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Efficiency | **%** |
| 9 | $T_{starting} \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 3 \times T_{rated}$ | Starting torque capability | **Nm** |
| 10 | $J \, \frac{d\omega_m}{dt} = T_e - T_L - B \omega_m$ | Mechanical (rotational) dynamics | **SI base units** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT use the vertical bar `\|x\|` inside tables** (it breaks markdown). Always write $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value in KTU notes.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Synchronous AC servo motors drive:
- **6-axis articulated robot arms** (KUKA KR, ABB IRB, FANUC M-series).
- **SCARA and Delta robots** for pick-and-place on assembly lines.
- **CNC machine tool feed axes** (X, Y, Z servo drives).
- **AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) traction wheels.**
- **Semiconductor wafer-handling robots** (cleanroom-rated, low particulate emission).

Their unmatched **dynamic response** (acceleration up to **10 g** in some robot joints) makes them irreplaceable in modern manufacturing automation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Synchronous Speed from Supply Frequency

**Statement to Prove:**
The mechanical synchronous speed of a rotating magnetic field produced by a three-phase, $P$-pole winding supplied at frequency $f$ is given by:
$$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P} \quad \text{(in rpm)}$$

**Step 1 — Define the poles.**
Let the machine have $P$ magnetic poles (always an even integer: 2, 4, 6, 8, ...). Each pair of poles (North + South) covers 360° electrical but only 360°/($P/2$) mechanical.

**Step 2 — Electrical cycles per mechanical revolution.**
For one complete mechanical revolution of the rotor, the magnetic field pattern must pass through $P/2$ complete North-South pairs. Each pair corresponds to one electrical cycle. Therefore, the number of electrical cycles per mechanical revolution is:
$$n_{elec} = \frac{P}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Time per mechanical revolution.**
If the supply frequency is $f$ Hz (electrical cycles per second), the time for one electrical cycle is $1/f$ seconds. The time for one mechanical revolution is therefore:
$$T_{mech} = \frac{P/2}{f} = \frac{P}{2 f} \quad \text{(seconds)}$$

**Step 4 — Convert time to speed.**
Speed in rpm is revolutions per minute:
$$N_s = \frac{1}{T_{mech}} \times 60 = \frac{60 \times 2 f}{P} = \frac{120 f}{P}$$

Hence proved. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Electromagnetic Torque from Energy Conversion

**Statement to Prove:**
The average electromagnetic torque developed by a synchronous AC servo motor under sinusoidal excitation is:
$$T_e = \frac{P_{mech}}{\omega_m} = \frac{3 \, E_b \, I_a \cos\phi}{\omega_s} = K_t \, I_a$$

**Step 1 — Air-gap power transfer.**
In a synchronous machine, power is transferred across the air gap from the stator to the rotor purely through the magnetic field. The power converted from electrical to mechanical form (the **air-gap power** $P_g$) is:
$$P_g = 3 \, E_b \, I_a \cos\phi$$
where $E_b$ is the back-EMF per phase, $I_a$ is the armature current per phase, and $\cos\phi$ is the power-factor angle between them.

**Step 2 — Mechanical power at the shaft.**
For a synchronous motor (with no rotor copper losses, since the rotor is excited by permanent magnets or DC and carries no AC), the entire air-gap power is converted to mechanical power (ignoring friction and windage for now):
$$P_{mech} = P_g = 3 \, E_b \, I_a \cos\phi$$

**Step 3 — Torque = Power / Angular velocity.**
The electromagnetic torque is mechanical power divided by mechanical angular velocity:
$$T_e = \frac{P_{mech}}{\omega_s} = \frac{3 \, E_b \, I_a \cos\phi}{\omega_s}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the back-EMF expression.**
From Faraday's law, the back-EMF is proportional to speed:
$$E_b = K_e \, \omega_m$$
Substituting:
$$T_e = \frac{3 \, (K_e \omega_m) \, I_a \cos\phi}{\omega_s}$$

For a synchronous motor, $\omega_m = \omega_s$ (rotor runs at synchronous speed). Also, under **optimal torque-per-ampere control (MTPA)**, $\cos\phi = 1$ and $i_d = 0$. Thus:
$$T_e = 3 \, K_e \, I_a$$

By redefining the constant $K_t = 3 K_e$ (or equivalently absorbing the 3-phase factor into the constant), we obtain the canonical linear torque-current relationship:
$$T_e = K_t \, I_a \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Equivalent of $K_e$ and $K_t$ in SI Units

**Statement to Prove:**
In SI units, the back-EMF constant $K_e$ (V·s/rad) is numerically equal to the torque constant $K_t$ (Nm/A).

**Step 1 — Energy conservation.**
The electrical power input to the motor (which becomes mechanical output) is, for a single phase, $P = e \cdot i = (K_e \omega) \cdot i$. The mechanical power produced is $P = T \cdot \omega = (K_t i) \cdot \omega$.

**Step 2 — Equate the two expressions.**
Setting electrical input = mechanical output (per phase, lossless assumption):
$$(K_e \, \omega) \cdot i = (K_t \, i) \cdot \omega$$

**Step 3 — Cancel common terms.**
$$K_e \, \omega \, i = K_t \, i \, \omega$$
$$K_e = K_t \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** This is a direct consequence of the **reciprocity theorem** in electromechanical energy conversion. The same magnetic field geometry that produces torque for a given current also generates back-EMF for a given speed.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Problem — Solved Example (KTU-Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Numerical:** Practice this exact style of problem for KTU ESE — partial credit is awarded for every correct step.

**Problem Statement:**
A 4-pole, 3-phase synchronous AC servo motor is supplied by a VFD at 50 Hz. The motor develops a torque of **12 Nm** at a current of **6 A** per phase. Compute:
1. The synchronous speed in rpm.
2. The mechanical power developed.
3. The torque constant $K_t$.
4. The back-EMF constant $K_e$.

**Solution:**

**Part (1) — Synchronous Speed:**
$$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4}$$
$$N_s = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$

**Part (2) — Mechanical Power:**
First, compute the angular velocity:
$$\omega_m = \frac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1500}{60}$$
$$\omega_m = 50 \pi \approx 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$
Now compute the power:
$$P_{mech} = T_e \times \omega_m = 12 \times 50 \pi$$
$$P_{mech} = 600 \pi \approx 1884.96 \text{ W} \approx 1.885 \text{ kW}$$

**Part (3) — Torque Constant:**
$$K_t = \frac{T_e}{I_a} = \frac{12}{6} = 2.0 \text{ Nm/A}$$

**Part (4) — Back-EMF Constant:**
In SI units:
$$K_e = K_t = 2.0 \text{ V·s/rad}$$
The back-EMF at this speed is:
$$E_b = K_e \times \omega_m = 2.0 \times 50 \pi \approx 314.16 \text{ V (peak, per phase)}$$

For RMS (line-neutral), divide by $\sqrt{2}$:
$$E_{b,RMS} = \frac{314.16}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 222.14 \text{ V}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Mechanical Dynamic Equation of the Servo

The rotor's rotational motion is governed by Newton's second law for rotation:
$$J \, \frac{d\omega_m}{dt} + B \, \omega_m + T_L = T_e$$

where:
- $J$ = total moment of inertia at the motor shaft (kg·m²)
- $B$ = viscous friction coefficient (Nm·s/rad)
- $T_L$ = load torque (Nm)
- $T_e$ = electromagnetic torque developed (Nm)

**Derivation from Power Balance:**
Multiplying both sides by $\omega_m$:
$$J \, \omega_m \frac{d\omega_m}{dt} + B \, \omega_m^2 + T_L \, \omega_m = T_e \, \omega_m$$
$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{1}{2} J \omega_m^2\right) + P_{friction} + P_{load} = P_{mech}$$

This represents the **kinetic energy storage rate** + **friction loss** + **useful mechanical power** = **electromagnetic power converted from electrical input**. $\blacksquare$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Servo Drive System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Command: theta_ref, omega_ref] --> B[Position Controller P or PID]
    B --> C[Velocity Controller PI Loop]
    C --> D[Current Controller PI Loop d-q Axis]
    D --> E[SVPWM Modulator]
    E --> F[3-Phase PWM Inverter IGBT Bridge]
    F --> G[Synchronous AC Servo Motor Stator + PM Rotor]
    G --> H[Encoder or Resolver on Shaft]
    H --> I[Feedback Signal Processor]
    I --> B
    G --> J[Robot Joint Mechanical Load]
    J --> K[Torque T_e delivered to link]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The signal flow is **clockwise**: reference enters the cascade at top-left, propagates through the nested PI controllers, drives the inverter, actuates the motor, and the encoder (right side) closes the loop back to the position controller. This is the textbook **cascade control architecture** for industrial servo drives.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of a Synchronous AC Servo Motor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph statorAssembly[Stator Assembly]
        s1[Laminated Si Steel Core]
        s2[3-Phase Sinusoidal Windings U V W]
        s3[120 Degree Spatial Distribution]
    end
    subgraph rotorAssembly[Rotor Assembly]
        r1[Permanent Magnets NdFeB]
        r2[Ferromagnetic Hub Shaft]
        r3[Optional IPM Flux Barriers]
    end
    subgraph feedbackAssembly[Feedback Assembly]
        f1[Incremental Encoder 2 Channels A B]
        f2[Optional Index Z Pulse]
        f3[Resolver or Hall Sensors for Commutation]
    end
    s2 -->|produces| field[Rotating Air Gap Magnetic Field]
    field -->|aligns with| r1
    r2 -->|drives| shaft[Mechanical Shaft Output]
    f1 -->|position feedback| ctl[DSP or FPGA Servo Controller]
    f3 -->|rotor angle theta| ctl
    ctl -->|3-phase PWM| s2
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cascaded Control Loop (Bandwidth View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    posIn[Position Reference theta_ref] --> posErr[Subtractor: e_pos]
    posErr --> pCtrl[P-Controller Kp]
    pCtrl --> velRef[Velocity Reference omega_ref]
    velRef --> velErr[Subtractor: e_vel]
    velErr --> piVel[PI Velocity Controller Kpv Kiv]
    piVel --> iqRef[Q-axis Current Reference iq_ref]
    iqRef --> iqErr[Subtractor: e_iq]
    iqErr --> piCur[PI Current Controller Kpc Kic]
    piCur --> svpwm[SVPWM Duty Cycle Generator]
    svpwm --> inv[3-Phase Inverter]
    inv --> motor[AC Servo Motor]
    motor --> enc[Encoder]
    enc --> velFB[Velocity Feedback omega_m]
    enc --> posFB[Position Feedback theta_m]
    enc --> iqFB[Current Feedback iq measured]
    posFB --> posErr
    velFB --> velErr
    iqFB --> iqErr
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid — Power Conversion Signal Chain (End-to-End)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mains[3-Phase AC Mains 415V 50Hz] --> rectifier[Diode Bridge Rectifier]
    rectifier --> dcBus[DC Link Bus with Capacitor]
    dcBus --> igbt[IGBT Inverter Bridge 6 Switches]
    igbt --> motor[Synchronous AC Servo Motor]
    motor --> load[Robot Joint or CNC Axis]
    load --> motion[Precise Linear or Rotary Motion]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The **DC link capacitor** is critical — it acts as an energy buffer that absorbs regenerative energy when the motor decelerates a load (braking), preventing over-voltage destruction of the IGBTs.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]

**Q: Define a synchronous AC servo motor. Why is it preferred over a DC servo motor in modern industrial robotic applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **synchronous AC servo motor** is a brushless, permanent-magnet (or reluctance) AC machine whose rotor speed is locked in synchronism with the supply frequency, and which operates within a closed-loop feedback system (encoder-based) to deliver precise control of position, velocity, and torque. **(1 mark)**

It is preferred over a DC servo motor because:
1. **Higher power density** — no commutator/brushes; more compact for the same torque. **(1 mark)**
2. **Better heat dissipation** — losses occur in the stator (stationary) which can be water- or air-cooled more effectively than the rotor. **(0.5 marks)**
3. **Lower maintenance, longer life** — absence of brushes means no commutator wear, no brush dust (critical in cleanrooms like semiconductor fabs). **(0.5 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: What is meant by "field-oriented control" (FOC) in a synchronous AC servo motor? State its main advantage.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Field-Oriented Control (FOC), also called **vector control**, is a control technique in which the three-phase stator currents are mathematically transformed (via the Park and Clarke transforms) into a two-axis rotating reference frame (d-q frame) that is aligned with the rotor's magnetic field. **(2 marks)**

**Main advantage:** It allows **independent control of the torque-producing current ($i_q$) and the flux-producing current ($i_d$)**, just like in a separately-excited DC motor. This results in **fast and accurate torque response** with a linear $T \propto i_q$ relationship, enabling high dynamic performance essential for robotic joint actuation. **(1 mark)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Q: (a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the construction and working principle of a 3-phase synchronous AC servo motor used in industrial robots. Discuss the role of the encoder in the closed-loop control. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6-pole, 3-phase synchronous AC servo motor operates at 60 Hz supply. It develops a torque of 25 Nm at a current of 8 A per phase. Calculate the synchronous speed, mechanical power output, torque constant, and back-EMF constant. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

A **3-phase synchronous AC servo motor** consists of:

**Construction:**
- **Stator:** A laminated silicon-steel core carrying a **3-phase distributed winding** (U, V, W) physically displaced by **120° electrical** in space. The windings are star-connected and terminated at a terminal box. **(1 mark)**
- **Rotor:** A **permanent-magnet (PM)** rotor — either surface-mounted (SPM) for smooth low-speed torque, or interior-mounted (IPM) for high-speed operation with reluctance-torque contribution. Modern servo motors almost always use **IPM** with NdFeB magnets. **(1 mark)**
- **Encoder/Resolver:** A high-resolution optical encoder (typically 17-bit or 23-bit, giving 131072 or 8388608 counts/rev) is mounted on the non-drive end of the shaft. It generates two quadrature signals (A, B) and an index pulse (Z) for absolute reference. **(1 mark)**

**Working Principle:**
The 3-phase VFD supplies **sinusoidal currents** to the stator, creating a **rotating magnetic field** at synchronous speed $N_s = 120f/P$. The PM rotor experiences a torque due to the tendency of its magnetic axis to align with the stator field axis. Due to rotor inertia, the rotor cannot "catch up" but locks magnetically and rotates at exactly $N_s$. **(2 marks)**

**Role of Encoder in Closed-Loop Control:**
The encoder continuously measures the actual rotor angular position $\theta_{actual}$ and feeds it to the digital servo controller (DSP/FPGA). The controller compares $\theta_{actual}$ with the reference $\theta_{ref}$, computes a position error $e = \theta_{ref} - \theta_{actual}$, and through cascaded P/PI controllers modulates the inverter PWM signals to drive the error to zero. This achieves **zero steady-state position error**, **high dynamic stiffness** (resistance to disturbance torques), and **accurate trajectory tracking** — all essential for robotic joint control. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Given:**
- Number of poles, $P = 6$
- Supply frequency, $f = 60$ Hz
- Torque developed, $T_e = 25$ Nm
- Phase current, $I_a = 8$ A

**Step 1 — Synchronous Speed:**
$$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 60}{6}$$
$$N_s = 1200 \text{ rpm} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 2 — Angular Velocity:**
$$\omega_m = \frac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1200}{60} = 40 \pi \approx 125.66 \text{ rad/s} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3 — Mechanical Power Output:**
$$P_{mech} = T_e \times \omega_m = 25 \times 40 \pi = 1000 \pi$$
$$P_{mech} \approx 3141.59 \text{ W} \approx 3.14 \text{ kW} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 4 — Torque Constant:**
$$K_t = \frac{T_e}{I_a} = \frac{25}{8} = 3.125 \text{ Nm/A} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 5 — Back-EMF Constant (in SI):**
$$K_e = K_t = 3.125 \text{ V·s/rad} \quad \textbf{(0.5 marks)}$$

**Step 6 — Back-EMF at operating speed:**
$$E_b = K_e \times \omega_m = 3.125 \times 40 \pi = 125 \pi \approx 392.70 \text{ V (peak, per phase)} \quad \textbf{(0.5 marks)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to convert $N_s$ (in rpm) to $\omega_m$ (in rad/s) before computing power. This is the **single most common error** in this type of problem, costing **2 marks**. Always write the unit conversion step explicitly: $\omega_m = 2\pi N_s/60$.

---

### Question B [14 Marks — Alternative Choice] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Q: (a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **cascaded control structure** (current → velocity → position loops) of an industrial AC servo drive. Why is the current loop the innermost and fastest? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **electromagnetic torque** of a synchronous AC servo motor from first principles using the air-gap power balance. Show that $K_e = K_t$ in SI units. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

The **cascaded control structure** of an industrial AC servo drive consists of three nested feedback loops, with bandwidth increasing as we move from the outer to the inner loop:

**Block Diagram Explanation (refer to SECTION 4.3 above for full mermaid):**

1. **Position Loop (Outermost, lowest bandwidth, ~50–100 Hz):** Generates a velocity reference $\omega_{ref}$ from position error. **(1.5 marks)**
2. **Velocity Loop (Middle, ~200–500 Hz):** Receives $\omega_{ref}$ and outputs a torque-producing current reference $i_{q,ref}$ (and a flux reference $i_{d,ref}$). **(1.5 marks)**
3. **Current Loop (Innermost, highest bandwidth, ~1–4 kHz):** Regulates the actual d-q axis currents $i_d, i_q$ in the rotor reference frame via fast PI control. The output of the current controller is the voltage reference fed to the SVPWM modulator and inverter. **(2 marks)**

**Why the current loop is innermost and fastest:**
The current loop is the innermost because the **electrical time constant** of the motor winding ($L/R$) is much smaller (typically < 1 ms) than the **mechanical time constant** of the rotor-load system ($J/B$, often tens of ms). Since the current loop must be at least 5–10× faster than the velocity loop, and the velocity loop 5–10× faster than the position loop, a cascade with progressively decreasing bandwidths from inner to outer is the natural architecture. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Derivation of Electromagnetic Torque from Air-Gap Power:**

**Step 1 — Identify power input to stator.**
The 3-phase electrical power input to the stator windings is:
$$P_{in} = 3 \, V_{ph} \, I_{ph} \cos\phi$$
where $V_{ph}$ is phase voltage, $I_{ph}$ is phase current, $\phi$ is the power-factor angle. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Stator copper loss (negligible for the model):**
Stator copper loss $P_{cu} = 3 I_{ph}^2 R_s$. Subtracting gives air-gap power $P_g$:
$$P_g = 3 \, V_{ph} I_{ph} \cos\phi - 3 I_{ph}^2 R_s = 3 \, E_b \, I_{ph} \cos\phi$$
where $E_b$ is the back-EMF per phase. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Convert to mechanical power.**
In a synchronous machine, the rotor has no AC copper losses (PM rotor has no field winding current; or if wound rotor, DC excitation). All air-gap power is converted to mechanical form:
$$P_{mech} = P_g = 3 \, E_b \, I_{ph} \cos\phi \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 4 — Express torque as power/speed.**
$$T_e = \frac{P_{mech}}{\omega_s} = \frac{3 \, E_b \, I_{ph} \cos\phi}{\omega_s} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 5 — Use $E_b = K_e \omega_s$ (back-EMF proportional to speed).**
$$T_e = \frac{3 \, K_e \omega_s \, I_{ph} \cos\phi}{\omega_s} = 3 K_e I_{ph} \cos\phi$$

Under optimal MTPA control, $\cos\phi = 1$ and define $K_t = 3 K_e$:
$$\boxed{T_e = K_t \, I_a} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 6 — Prove $K_e = K_t$ in SI units:**
Equating electrical input power (per phase) with mechanical output power (per phase, lossless):
$$K_e \, \omega \, i = K_t \, i \, \omega$$
Canceling $i \omega$:
$$\boxed{K_e = K_t} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Students often forget to state the **lossless assumption** before equating input and output power. This is mandatory for **1 mark** under "physical reasoning." Also, do NOT confuse $K_e$ (V·s/rad, SI) with $K_e$ (V/krpm, often used by manufacturers in datasheets) — the conversion factor is $\frac{60}{2\pi} = 9.5493$. Always specify the unit system explicitly.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Print This Section Before Exam:**

- [ ] **Definition:** A synchronous AC servo motor is a **PM/Reluctance** AC machine with rotor locked to stator field frequency, operating in **closed-loop** with encoder feedback.
- [ ] **Speed Equation:** $N_s = \frac{120f}{P}$ — speed is a function of frequency only (for a given pole count), independent of load.
- [ ] **No slip:** Unlike induction motors, there is **no slip** ($s = 0$); rotor speed = synchronous speed.
- [ ] **Key constants:** $K_t$ (Nm/A) and $K_e$ (V·s/rad), and they are **equal in SI units**.
- [ ] **Torque equation:** $T_e = K_t \, I_a$ — linear, controllable via current.
- [ ] **Power:** $P_{mech} = T_e \, \omega_m$ (W).
- [ ] **Control technique:** **FOC / Vector Control** in the d-q frame — separates torque ($i_q$) and flux ($i_d$) control.
- [ ] **Cascade:** Current (innermost, fastest) → Velocity → Position (outermost, slowest). Bandwidth ratio ~5–10× between adjacent loops.
- [ ] **PM materials:** NdFeB (Neodymium Iron Boron), $B_r \approx 1.2$ T — used in modern IPM rotors.
- [ ] **Rotor types:** **SPM** (sinusoidal back-EMF, smooth torque) vs **IPM** (reluctance + magnet torque, high speed, robust).
- [ ] **Feedback:** Encoder (incremental 17-bit/23-bit standard), Resolver (for harsh environments), Hall sensors (for commutation only).
- [ ] **Advantage over DC servo:** No brushes, no commutator, better heat dissipation, higher power density, lower maintenance.
- [ ] **Disadvantages:** Requires sophisticated drive (VFD/Vector Drive), encoder failure can cause loss of commutation, demagnetization risk at high temperatures.
- [ ] **Application in robotics:** All modern 6-axis robot joint drives, CNC feed axes, AGV wheels, semiconductor handlers.
- [ ] **Remember:** The DC link capacitor in the inverter is essential for absorbing regenerative braking energy.

---

> **END OF NOTES — PEEIT865 Module 1: Synchronous AC Servo Motors**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
