<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Servo Drive (KTU Definition):**
> An *Industrial Servo Drive* is a closed-loop, high-performance **electromechanical motion-control subsystem** that precisely regulates the **position**, **velocity**, and **torque** of a rotating shaft by continuously comparing a commanded reference signal with a feedback signal (from an encoder or resolver) and applying a corrective actuation through a power electronic converter to a servo-grade motor (DC, Brushless DC, or AC synchronous/induction).

In the context of the **PEEIT865 – Industrial Robotics** course, the servo drive is the *muscular actuation unit* of every robotic joint — it converts the low-power digital command pulses from the robot controller into the high-power, precisely modulated shaft motion demanded by the kinematics of the manipulator.

## 1.2 Key Functional Building Blocks

A standard industrial servo drive is composed of **four mandatory functional blocks**:

| # | Block | Engineering Role |
|---|-------|------------------|
| 1 | **Servomotor** | Energy conversion (electrical → mechanical torque) |
| 2 | **Feedback Sensor** | Real-time measurement of position / speed / torque |
| 3 | **Controller** | Error computation & law-of-control execution (P/PI/PID/Fuzzy) |
| 4 | **Power Amplifier** | PWM/IGBT-based current/voltage boosting stage |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** A *servo drive* is **not** just a motor and **not** just an amplifier. The defining attribute is the **closed feedback loop** — without feedback, the system degrades to an open-loop *stepper drive*, which is a fundamentally inferior architecture for robotics.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The Human Eye–Hand System:**
> Imagine you are driving a car and trying to park it **exactly** 5 cm from the wall.
> - Your **brain** sees the gap (feedback) → computes the error → decides "turn wheel 3° left, press accelerator lightly" → this is the **controller**.
> - Your **arm and leg muscles** execute the command with amplified force → this is the **power amplifier + servomotor**.
> - Your **eyes** continuously re-measure the distance → this is the **encoder/resolver**.
>
> A servo drive does *exactly* this — thousands of times per second — but in a robot joint instead of a car.

## 1.4 Critical Performance Metrics (KTU Board-Weighted)

The following metrics are routinely asked in KTU exams and **must be memorized in bold**:

- **Bandwidth** — typically **100 Hz to 1 kHz** for industrial servo drives
- **Positioning Accuracy** — **±1 µm to ±10 µm** in high-precision CNC-grade drives
- **Resolver/Encoder Resolution** — **2¹⁷ to 2²⁰ counts/revolution** (≈ 131072 to 1048576 CPR)
- **Torque Range** — **0.1 Nm to 500 Nm** (industrial robotics span)
- **Speed Range** — **0 to 6000 RPM** (with field weakening up to **12000 RPM**)
- **Settling Time** — typically **< 10 ms** for a step command

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The servo drive operates on the **closed-loop negative feedback principle**. The following 6-step causal chain constitutes the operational heartbeat:

1. **Command Generation**: The robot controller (PLC / CNC / motion controller) issues a *reference signal* $r(t)$ — typically a train of step pulses (for position), an analog voltage (for velocity), or a current set-point (for torque).
2. **Error Detection**: The feedback sensor (encoder/resolver) measures the actual shaft variable $y(t)$ and the controller subtracts it: $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$.
3. **Control Law Computation**: The error $e(t)$ is processed through a control algorithm — most commonly a **PID controller** — producing a control signal $u(t)$.
4. **Power Amplification**: The low-power $u(t)$ is fed to a PWM/IGBT inverter which boosts it to a high-current, high-voltage drive signal applied to the motor windings.
5. **Electromechanical Conversion**: The motor produces a torque $T_m$ proportional to the armature current, accelerating the shaft and the mechanical load.
6. **Continuous Re-Feedback**: The encoder/resolver instantly re-samples the new shaft position, and the loop iterates at a loop-rate of typically **1–16 kHz**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the loop so fast?** Because at every iteration, the error is forced toward zero. This gives servo drives their hallmark attributes: *zero steady-state error*, *disturbance rejection*, and *high dynamic stiffness*.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** the KTU examiner is known to test under Module 1. Memorize the symbols and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Engineering Use | Units |
|---|--------------------|-------------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $T_m = K_t \cdot I_a$ | Motor torque is proportional to armature current | Nm |
| 2 | $E_b = K_e \cdot \omega$ | Back-EMF proportional to angular velocity | V |
| 3 | $V_a = I_a R_a + L_a \dfrac{dI_a}{dt} + E_b$ | Armature voltage KVL equation | V |
| 4 | $T_m - T_L = J \dfrac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + B \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$ | Mechanical Newton-Euler equation | Nm |
| 5 | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t) dt + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | **PID control law** (the heart of the drive) | — |
| 6 | $G(s) = \dfrac{\theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_t}{s \left[ (L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_t K_e \right]}$ | **Transfer function of armature-controlled DC servo motor** | rad/V |
| 7 | $\omega(s) = \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K_e}$ | Steady-state speed-torque relationship | rad/s |
| 8 | $e_{ss}(\text{step}) = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p}$ | Steady-state error for proportional position control | — |
| 9 | $\tau_e = \dfrac{L_a}{R_a}$ | Electrical time-constant of armature | s |
| 10 | $\tau_m = \dfrac{J R_a}{K_t K_e}$ | Mechanical time-constant | s |
| 11 | $K = \dfrac{K_t}{R_a B + K_t K_e}$ | DC motor gain constant (steady-state) | rad/(V·s) |
| 12 | $f_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{T_{sw}}$ | PWM switching frequency (typically **4–16 kHz**) | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Symbols Legend:**
> $K_t$ = torque constant (Nm/A), $K_e$ = back-EMF constant (V·s/rad), $R_a$ = armature resistance (Ω), $L_a$ = armature inductance (H), $J$ = moment of inertia (kg·m²), $B$ = viscous friction coefficient (N·m·s/rad), $\theta$ = angular position (rad), $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotic Manipulators** (KUKA, FANUC, ABB arms): each of the 6 joints is driven by an independent servo drive — the entire motion envelope of the robot depends on the bandwidth and torque density of these drives.
- **CNC Machining Centers**: servo drives enable contouring accuracy of **±2 µm** on 3-axis milling machines.
- **Semiconductor Lithography (ASML)**: uses nano-precision linear servo drives for wafer-stage positioning with sub-nanometer resolution.
- **Packaging & Printing**: web-feed tension control relies on torque-mode servo drives.
- **Pick-and-Place Machines (SMT)**: placement rates of **> 50,000 CPH** are achievable only with high-bandwidth servo drives.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Transfer Function of an Armature-Controlled DC Servo Motor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is a **guaranteed 7–10 mark question** in any KTU Module-1 ESE. Memorize the logical steps, not just the final result.

### Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) to the Armature Circuit

The armature winding is modelled as a series combination of resistance $R_a$ and inductance $L_a$, with back-EMF $E_b$ opposing the applied voltage $V_a$:

$$
V_a(t) = I_a R_a + L_a \dfrac{d I_a(t)}{dt} + E_b(t)
$$

**Logic:** The applied voltage must overcome (a) the resistive drop, (b) the inductive drop (Lenz's law), and (c) the back-EMF induced by the rotating armature (Faraday's law).

### Step 2 — Apply Newton's 2nd Law to the Rotating Shaft

The motor produces torque $T_m$, the load applies a counter-torque $T_L$, and inertia $J$ plus viscous friction $B$ resist motion:

$$
T_m(t) - T_L(t) = J \dfrac{d^2 \theta(t)}{dt^2} + B \dfrac{d \theta(t)}{dt}
$$

**Logic:** Net torque equals the rate of change of angular momentum plus viscous losses.

### Step 3 — Constitutive Relations of the DC Machine

$$
T_m(t) = K_t \cdot I_a(t) \qquad \text{and} \qquad E_b(t) = K_e \cdot \omega(t) = K_e \cdot \dfrac{d\theta(t)}{dt}
$$

**Logic:** The torque constant $K_t$ converts current to mechanical torque. The same constant $K_e$ (in SI units $K_t = K_e$) converts angular velocity into a back-EMF that opposes the applied voltage.

### Step 4 — Take the Laplace Transform (Zero Initial Conditions)

Transforming all three equations into the s-domain:

$$
V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) I_a(s) + K_e \, s \, \theta(s)
$$

$$
\left[ K_t I_a(s) - T_L(s) \right] = \left( J s^2 + B s \right) \theta(s)
$$

**Logic:** Differentiation in time-domain $\rightarrow$ multiplication by $s$ in s-domain.

### Step 5 — Eliminate the Internal Variable $I_a(s)$

From the torque equation:

$$
I_a(s) = \dfrac{(J s^2 + B s) \theta(s) + T_L(s)}{K_t}
$$

Substituting into the KVL equation:

$$
V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) \cdot \dfrac{(J s^2 + B s) \theta(s) + T_L(s)}{K_t} + K_e s \, \theta(s)
$$

### Step 6 — Collect the $\theta(s)$ Terms

$$
V_a(s) = \theta(s) \left[ \dfrac{(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B) s}{K_t} + K_e s \right] + \dfrac{(R_a + L_a s)}{K_t} T_L(s)
$$

### Step 7 — Define the Transfer Function with $T_L = 0$ (No-Load)

Setting $T_L(s) = 0$ and solving for $G(s) = \theta(s) / V_a(s)$:

$$
G(s) = \dfrac{K_t}{s \left[ (L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_t K_e \right]}
$$

This is the **canonical KTU transfer function** of an armature-controlled DC servo motor.

### Step 8 — Special Case (Neglecting $L_a$)

If armature inductance is small (a valid assumption when $\tau_e \ll \tau_m$):

$$
G(s) \approx \dfrac{K_t}{s \left[ R_a (J s + B) + K_t K_e \right]} = \dfrac{K_t / R_a}{s \left[ J s + B + \dfrac{K_t K_e}{R_a} \right]}
$$

Defining $K = \dfrac{K_t}{R_a B + K_t K_e}$:

$$
G(s) \approx \dfrac{K}{s (\tau_m s + 1)} \quad \text{where} \quad \tau_m = \dfrac{J R_a}{K_t K_e}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Physical interpretation:** The motor behaves as a *first-order lag* $\dfrac{1}{\tau_m s + 1}$ cascaded with a *pure integrator* $\dfrac{1}{s}$ (since voltage is the integral of acceleration that produces velocity, which integrates to position).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Steady-State Speed

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This numerical problem appears almost verbatim in KTU previous papers.

**Problem:** A DC servomotor has $R_a = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $K_e = 0.05 \, \text{V·s/rad}$. If the applied armature voltage is $V_a = 100 \, \text{V}$ and the armature current at load is $I_a = 10 \, \text{A}$, find the steady-state angular velocity.

**Solution:**

$$
\omega = \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K_e}
$$

$$
\omega = \dfrac{100 - (10)(0.5)}{0.05} = \dfrac{100 - 5}{0.05} = \dfrac{95}{0.05}
$$

$$
\boxed{\omega = 1900 \, \text{rad/s} \approx 18{,}144 \, \text{RPM}}
$$

**Engineering insight:** Notice how a small armature drop (only 5 V) causes a 5% reduction in speed — this is why *load disturbances* in open-loop drives cause large speed errors, motivating the need for *closed-loop velocity feedback*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Industrial Servo Drive

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete closed-loop architecture of an industrial servo drive. It is the diagram most frequently requested by KTU examiners.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Controller CNC Or PLC] --> B[Reference Signal r t]
    B --> C[Comparator Subtractor Node]
    G[Encoder Or Resolver] --> H[Feedback Signal y t]
    H --> C
    C --> D[PID Controller Error Processor]
    D --> E[PWM Modulator Pulse Width Generator]
    E --> F[Power Amplifier IGBT Inverter Stage]
    F --> I[Servomotor DC Or AC Or BLDC]
    I --> J[Mechanical Load Robot Joint Coupling]
    I --> K[Shaft Speed and Position]
    K --> G
    J --> L[Robot End Effector Motion]
```

### Reading the Diagram

- **A → B**: The host controller issues a digital or analog command (e.g., "move to $\theta = 45°$").
- **G → H**: The encoder/resolver continuously emits the actual shaft position.
- **C**: The comparator computes the error $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$.
- **D**: The PID controller shapes the dynamic response.
- **E → F**: The PWM modulator + IGBT inverter amplifies the control signal to the kW range.
- **I**: The servomotor converts electrical power to mechanical torque.
- **K → G**: This feedback path is the **defining feature** of a servo drive.

## 4.2 Classification Tree of Industrial Servo Drives

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Industrial Servo Drives] --> B[DC Servo Drives]
    A --> C[AC Servo Drives]
    A --> D[Brushless DC BLDC Servo Drives]
    A --> E[Stepper Servo Closed Loop Stepper]

    B --> B1[Armature Controlled]
    B --> B2[Field Controlled]

    C --> C1[Induction Motor Based]
    C --> C2[PMSM Permanent Magnet Synchronous]

    D --> D1[Trapezoidal BLDC]
    D --> D2[Sinusoidal BLDC]

    E --> E1[Encoder Closed Stepper]
    E --> E2[Servo Stepper Hybrid]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** PMSM (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor) based servo drives are now the **industry default** for 6-axis industrial robots due to their superior torque density, efficiency (> 95%), and absence of rotor copper losses.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper Style**

### Q1. Define an industrial servo drive. List any four of its essential components. `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
An industrial servo drive is a closed-loop electromechanical motion-control system that precisely regulates the position, velocity, and torque of a motor shaft by continuously comparing a reference command with a feedback signal.

The four essential components are:
1. **Servomotor** (DC / AC / BLDC)
2. **Feedback sensor** (encoder or resolver)
3. **Controller** (PID-based)
4. **Power amplifier** (PWM/IGBT-based inverter)

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Insight:** Award full marks only if the student explicitly mentions *closed-loop feedback*. A motor alone is *not* a servo drive.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between an open-loop stepper drive and a closed-loop servo drive. `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Open-Loop Stepper | Closed-Loop Servo Drive |
|-----------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| Feedback | Absent | Present (encoder/resolver) |
| Position error on disturbance | High (loses steps) | Auto-corrected to zero |
| Torque at high speed | Drops sharply | Maintained constant |
| Efficiency | Low (current always ON) | High (current only as needed) |
| Heat dissipation | High | Low |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **Choose either Question A or Question B. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.**

---

### ❓ QUESTION A — `[14 Marks]`

#### (a) Explain the construction and working of an AC servo drive with a block diagram. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
An AC servo drive consists of:
1. A **two-phase induction motor** (or PMSM) with two stator windings — a *reference winding* and a *control winding* — physically displaced by 90° in space.
2. A **fixed-phase capacitor** in series with the reference winding to generate a 90° phase shift, producing a **rotating magnetic field**.
3. A **shaft-mounted encoder** providing position/speed feedback.
4. An **electronic controller** (often DSP-based) executing vector-control algorithms.

**Working Principle:**
- A fixed-amplitude AC voltage $V_f$ is applied to the reference winding.
- A variable-amplitude AC voltage $V_c$ (from the controller) is applied to the control winding, *90° out of phase* with $V_f$.
- The two fluxes combine to produce a **rotating magnetic field** whose magnitude is proportional to $V_c$.
- The rotor (squirrel-cage or permanent magnet) follows this rotating field, producing torque proportional to $V_c$.
- When $V_c = 0$, the rotating field collapses to a *pulsating field*, producing zero net torque — the motor naturally stops (a key safety feature of AC servos).
- The encoder continuously closes the loop, and the PID controller modulates $V_c$ in real time to track the reference.

**Key Equation:**

$$
T \propto V_c \cdot \sin(\phi), \quad \text{where } \phi = 90° \Rightarrow T \propto V_c
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why AC servos dominate robotics:** Higher power-to-weight ratio, no brush wear (longer life, typically > 20,000 hours), better heat dissipation from the stator, and superior high-speed torque characteristics.

---

#### (b) Derive the transfer function of an armature-controlled DC servomotor. State all assumptions. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

The transfer function derivation is presented exhaustively in **Section 3.1** above. Students should reproduce the following final form:

$$
G(s) = \dfrac{\theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_t}{s \left[ (L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_t K_e \right]}
$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Magnetic linearity (no saturation).
2. Constant field flux (separately excited motor).
3. Negligible armature reaction.
4. Linear viscous friction proportional to velocity.
5. Rigid coupling between motor and load (no shaft compliance).

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to *square* the $s$ in the mechanical equation. The inertia term is $J s^2$ (second derivative of position), **not** $J s$.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B — `[14 Marks]` (Alternative)

#### (a) Explain the various feedback devices used in industrial servo drives. Compare them. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

The three principal feedback devices are:

**1. Optical Encoder (Incremental)**
- A slotted disc rotates between an LED and a photodetector, generating two quadrature square waves (A and B channels) plus an index pulse Z.
- Resolution: **100 to 10,000 CPR** (counts per revolution).
- Limitation: Loses position on power-failure (no absolute reference).

**2. Optical Encoder (Absolute)**
- Multi-track disc with unique Gray-coded pattern at every angle.
- Resolution: up to **23 bits** (≈ 8 million positions/rev).
- Advantage: Retains position on power-down. Used in high-end robots.

**3. Resolver**
- A rotary transformer with two stator windings (sine and cosine) and a rotor winding excited by an AC carrier.
- The induced voltages are: $V_s = V_0 \sin(\omega_c t) \sin(\theta)$, $V_c = V_0 \sin(\omega_c t) \cos(\theta)$.
- Demodulation yields an absolute angle via $\theta = \arctan(V_s / V_c)$.
- **Rugged**, immune to oil/dust/vibration — preferred in heavy industrial robots and aerospace.

**4. Hall-Effect Sensors** (used in BLDC drives only) — give 6 commutation pulses per electrical cycle.

| Device | Resolution | Ruggedness | Cost | Application |
|--------|-----------|------------|------|-------------|
| Incremental Encoder | Medium | Medium | Low | CNC, low-cost robots |
| Absolute Encoder | High | Medium | High | Semiconductor, medical |
| Resolver | Medium | **Very High** | Medium | Aerospace, heavy industry |
| Hall Sensor | Low (60°/step) | High | Low | BLDC commutation |

---

#### (b) A DC servomotor has the following parameters: $R_a = 1 \, \Omega$, $L_a = 5 \, \text{mH}$, $K_t = 0.1 \, \text{Nm/A}$, $K_e = 0.1 \, \text{V·s/rad}$, $J = 0.02 \, \text{kg·m}^2$, $B = 0.005 \, \text{N·m·s/rad}$. Determine the transfer function $\theta(s)/V_a(s)$. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Substitute the given values into the canonical transfer function:

$$
G(s) = \dfrac{K_t}{s \left[ (L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_t K_e \right]}
$$

**Step 2:** Expand the bracket term by term:

$$
(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) = (0.005 s + 1)(0.02 s + 0.005)
$$

$$
= (0.005)(0.02) s^2 + (0.005)(0.005) s + (1)(0.02) s + (1)(0.005)
$$

$$
= 0.0001 \, s^2 + 0.000025 \, s + 0.02 \, s + 0.005
$$

$$
= 0.0001 \, s^2 + 0.020025 \, s + 0.005
$$

**Step 3:** Add the $K_t K_e$ term:

$$
K_t K_e = (0.1)(0.1) = 0.01
$$

$$
0.0001 \, s^2 + 0.020025 \, s + 0.005 + 0.01 = 0.0001 \, s^2 + 0.020025 \, s + 0.015
$$

**Step 4:** Assemble the final transfer function:

$$
\boxed{G(s) = \dfrac{\theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{0.1}{s \left( 0.0001 \, s^2 + 0.020025 \, s + 0.015 \right)}}
$$

**Step 5 (Optional — Engineering Insight):** Compute the time-constants:

$$
\tau_e = \dfrac{L_a}{R_a} = \dfrac{0.005}{1} = 5 \, \text{ms}, \quad \tau_m = \dfrac{J R_a}{K_t K_e} = \dfrac{(0.02)(1)}{(0.1)(0.1)} = 2 \, \text{s}
$$

Since $\tau_e \ll \tau_m$, the inductance can often be neglected, simplifying to a first-order system.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most-commonly lose marks in Industrial Servo Drive questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the back-EMF term $K_e \omega$** in the KVL equation — this is the single most common error. Without $E_b$, the motor appears to have unlimited acceleration (physically impossible).
> 2. **Mixing up the two K constants** ($K_t$ for torque, $K_e$ for back-EMF). In SI units they are numerically equal, but *conceptually distinct*.
> 3. **Treating the armature as purely resistive** — neglecting $L_a$ is only valid when $\tau_e \ll \tau_m$. The examiner will deduct 1–2 marks if the assumption is not stated.
> 4. **Writing transfer function as $\theta / I_a$ instead of $\theta / V_a$** — the input variable matters; $V_a$ is the *armature voltage input*, not the *current input*.
> 5. **Drawing block diagrams without arrowheads** showing the direction of signal flow — KTU examiners explicitly award 1 mark for proper block diagram conventions.
> 6. **Using stepper-motor terminology** (steps, detent torque) in servo-drive answers — these are *not* synonyms and signal weak conceptual grasp.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **servo drive = motor + feedback sensor + controller + amplifier** operating in a **closed loop**.
- ✅ The **defining equation** is $T_m = K_t I_a$ and the **back-EMF equation** is $E_b = K_e \omega$.
- ✅ The **canonical transfer function** is $G(s) = \dfrac{K_t}{s[(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_t K_e]}$ — *memorize this verbatim*.
- ✅ Three loop rates matter: **electrical time-constant $\tau_e = L_a / R_a$**, **mechanical time-constant $\tau_m = J R_a / K_t K_e$**, and **switching frequency $f_{sw}$**.
- ✅ **PID controller** has three terms: Proportional (K_p — speed of response), Integral (K_i — eliminates steady-state error), Derivative (K_d — damps overshoot).
- ✅ **AC servo drives** (especially PMSM) dominate modern industrial robotics.
- ✅ **Brushless DC (BLDC)** is preferred over brushed DC for longevity (> 20,000 hours) and lower maintenance.
- ✅ **Resolvers** are the most rugged feedback device; **absolute encoders** provide the highest resolution.
- ✅ **PWM switching frequency** is typically **4–16 kHz** — higher gives smoother current but more switching losses.
- ✅ **Bandwidth** of an industrial servo drive is typically **100 Hz – 1 kHz**; robotic joints demand *high bandwidth* for trajectory tracking.
- ✅ In SI units, $K_t = K_e$ (numerically), but their *physical dimensions* are different (Nm/A vs V·s/rad).
- ✅ The **steady-state speed** under load: $\omega = (V_a - I_a R_a) / K_e$ — load current *reduces* speed in open loop; closed loop *eliminates* this drop.
- ✅ KTU favourite numerical: substitution into the canonical transfer function with $L_a$ neglected to obtain a first-order lag + integrator.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hardware Controllers and Interfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT865 - INDUSTRIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hardware Controllers and Interfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Industrial Robotics**, a **Hardware Controller** is the dedicated electronic computational system responsible for executing the real-time motion-control, trajectory-generation, sensor-fusion, and task-sequencing functions of a robotic manipulator. It acts as the central nervous system that bridges the **high-level planning software** (offline programming, task description) with the **low-level electromechanical hardware** (motors, drives, encoders, end-effectors, and safety circuits).

An **Interface**, in the robotics domain, refers to the standardized electrical, electronic, and protocol-level boundary that enables deterministic, noise-immune, and time-bounded data exchange between the controller and its peripheral subsystems — including sensors, actuators, communication networks, and Human-Machine Interfaces (HMIs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology:** The course PEEIT865 groups controllers into the following architectural layers:
> - **Joint-Level Controllers** (inner loop — current/torque/velocity)
> - **Servo-Level Controllers** (mid loop — position)
> - **Task-Level / Supervisory Controllers** (outer loop — trajectory & coordination)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a human body performing a precise task like threading a needle:

- **Brain** = Task-Level Controller (decides *what* to do and *why*)
- **Cerebellum & Spinal Cord** = Servo-Level Controllers (coordinate the limbs)
- **Motor Neurons & Muscles** = Joint-Level Controllers + Power Electronics (execute *how* to move)
- **Eyes, Skin, Proprioceptors** = Sensors feeding back through the **interfaces** (the nervous system wiring)

Similarly, the robot hardware controller orchestrates a hierarchy of decisions, and **interfaces** are the standardized "nerves" through which sensor feedback (vision, force, position) and motor commands flow. Without robust interfaces, even the smartest controller becomes blind and paralyzed.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Constants

| Metric / Constant | Symbol | Typical Industrial Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Control Loop Update Rate | $f_s$ | **1 kHz – 32 kHz** | Deterministic real-time servo execution |
| Servo Cycle Time | $T_s$ | **0.5 ms – 1 ms** | Period between successive PID corrections |
| Encoder Resolution | $N$ | **10,000 – 1,000,000 CPR** | Position feedback granularity |
| Bus Latency (Industrial Ethernet) | $\tau_{lat}$ | **≤ 1 ms (EtherCAT)** | Distributed synchronization |
| Safety Integrity Level | SIL | **SIL 2 / SIL 3 (ISO 13849)** | Functional safety category |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Nyquist–Shannon sampling criterion** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ is non-negotiable in robotics — violating it produces aliasing-induced servo instability and audible/visible jitter even on perfectly tuned hardware.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Architecture of a Robot Controller

A modern industrial robot controller is **not a single processor** but a layered, distributed architecture. The breakdown is:

### A. Task / Supervisory Layer (Top)
- Runs a **real-time operating system (RTOS)** — VxWorks, QNX, Xenomai, RT-Linux.
- Handles job scheduling, I/O coordination, language interpretation (RAPID, KRL, KAREL).
- Communicates with factory networks: **Profinet, EtherNet/IP, Modbus TCP**.

### B. Servo / Motion Planning Layer (Middle)
- Performs **trajectory interpolation** (linear, circular, spline).
- Converts Cartesian path points $\mathbf{X}(t) \in \mathbb{R}^6$ into joint-space setpoints $\mathbf{q}(t) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ via **inverse kinematics**.
- Runs at **250 Hz – 1 kHz** typical.

### C. Joint / Loop Layer (Bottom)
- One controller per joint (or axis).
- Executes the **PID / state-feedback law** at **1 kHz – 32 kHz**.
- Drives the **power converter** (PWM inverter / H-bridge).

## 2.2 Types of Robot Controllers

### 1. **Open-Loop Controllers** (Non-Servo)
- Used with **stepper motors** or **hydraulic/pneumatic on-off valves**.
- No feedback; accuracy depends on mechanical precision.
- Application: low-cost pick-and-place, educational robots.

### 2. **Closed-Loop (Servo) Controllers**
- Use feedback from **encoders, resolvers, tachometers, or IMUs**.
- Sub-categories:
  - **PID Controller** — Proportional + Integral + Derivative.
  - **State-Space / Pole-Placement Controller** — uses full state vector $\mathbf{x}$.
  - **Adaptive Controller** — gains update online (e.g., MRAC).
  - **Robust $H_{\infty}$ Controller** — guarantees performance under uncertainty.
  - **Computed-Torque Controller** — cancels nonlinear dynamics via feedback linearization.

### 3. **PLC-Based Controllers**
- Industrial Programmable Logic Controllers (Siemens S7, Allen-Bradley ControlLogix).
- Best for **cell-level coordination**, not sub-millisecond joint control.

### 4. **PC-Based / Soft-PLC Controllers**
- Industrial PC running real-time kernel (Beckhoff CX-series).
- Combine high-level vision processing with motion control.

### 5. **Distributed / Modular Controllers**
- Multiple intelligent drives communicate via **EtherCAT, CANopen, Profibus**.
- Each drive has its own local controller — "smart actuator" concept.

## 2.3 Hardware Interfaces — Taxonomy

### A. **Signal-Level Interfaces**
| Type | Voltage / Standard | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Digital I/O | **24 V DC (IEC 61131-2)** | Limit switches, grippers, conveyors |
| Analog I/O | $\pm 10$ V or **4–20 mA** | Force sensors, pressure transducers |
| Encoder | RS-422 differential, **5 V TTL** | Position feedback |
| Resolver | Sinusoidal excitation, carrier **5–10 kHz** | Harsh-environment position sensing |

### B. **Fieldbus & Network Interfaces**
| Bus / Protocol | Topology | Bandwidth | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| CANopen | Multi-drop | **1 Mbit/s** | Mobile robots, distributed I/O |
| Profibus DP | Multi-drop | **12 Mbit/s** | Legacy factory automation |
| DeviceNet | Multi-drop | **500 kbit/s** | Sensor/actuator networks |
| EtherCAT | Daisy-chain | **100 Mbit/s** | High-speed motion control |
| Profinet IRT | Star | **100 Mbit/s** | Real-time Ethernet |
| EtherNet/IP | Star | **1 Gbit/s** | Plant-wide integration |
| RS-232 / RS-485 | Point-to-point | $\leq 115.2$ kbit/s | Sensors, low-speed peripherals |
| USB 3.0 | Point-to-point | **5 Gbit/s** | Vision systems, firmware update |

### C. **Sensor Interfaces**
- **Position**: Optical/ magnetic encoders, resolvers, LVDTs.
- **Velocity**: Tachogenerators, Hall sensors.
- **Force/Torque**: 6-DOF F/T sensors with strain-gauge bridges; signal conditioning via **Wheatstone bridge + instrumentation amplifier (INA128)**.
- **Vision**: GigE Vision, USB3 Vision, CoaXPress, Camera Link.
- **Proximity / Range**: Inductive, capacitive, LiDAR (Ethernet/RS-422).

### D. **Actuator (Power) Interfaces**
- **PWM Drives** — switching frequency $f_{sw}$ typically **8 kHz – 16 kHz**.
- **H-Bridge / 4-Quadrant Choppers** — for DC servo motors.
- **VFD (Variable Frequency Drive)** — for AC induction/PM synchronous motors.
- **Servo Amplifier** — closed-loop current control within torque bandwidth $\geq 1$ kHz.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

Hardware controllers and interfaces form the **backbone of every modern robotic cell**. They enable:
- **Automotive assembly**: 6-DOF welding robots using EtherCAT-synced servo drives.
- **Semiconductor cleanrooms**: Vision-guided pickers using GigE Vision cameras over PoE+ interfaces.
- **Collaborative robots (cobots)**: Real-time torque sensing via high-speed CAN-FD and SIL-3 safe torque-off (STO) digital interfaces.
- **Warehouse AGVs**: CANopen-controlled wheel drives with LiDAR over Ethernet.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t) dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$ | Continuous-time PID control law |
| 2 | $G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ | PID controller transfer function |
| 3 | $U(z) = K_p E(z) + K_i T_s \frac{E(z)}{1-z^{-1}} + \frac{K_d}{T_s}\big(E(z) - z^{-1}E(z)\big)$ | Discrete (velocity-form) PID |
| 4 | $\theta = \frac{2\pi}{N_{ppr}}$ | Encoder resolution per pulse (rad) |
| 5 | $\Delta V = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ | ADC quantization step (volts) |
| 6 | $e_q = \pm \frac{\Delta V}{2} = \pm \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}$ | Maximum quantization error |
| 7 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist) | Minimum sampling frequency |
| 8 | $\tau_{lat} = \frac{N_{nodes} \cdot t_{bit}}{N_{bytes}} \cdot N_{bytes}$ | Bus cycle time for EtherCAT-style ring |
| 9 | $J_{eff} = J_m + \frac{J_L}{N^2}$ | Reflected load inertia through gear ratio $N$ |
| 10 | $PWM_{duty} = \frac{V_{desired}}{V_{DC}}$ | PWM modulation index for DC motor drive |
| 11 | $\omega_m(s) = \frac{K_t}{(Js + B)(Ls + R) + K_t K_b} \cdot V(s)$ | DC motor angular velocity TF |
| 12 | $\mathbf{M}(q)\ddot{q} + \mathbf{C}(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + \mathbf{G}(q) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$ | Manipulator equation (controller must compensate) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Continuous-Time PID Control Law

**Goal:** Derive the standard PID control signal $u(t)$ from a generic error-driven feedback law.

The error signal at time $t$ is defined as the difference between the **desired (reference) signal** $r(t)$ and the **measured (feedback) signal** $y(t)$:

$$
e(t) = r(t) - y(t)
$$

A controller seeks to produce an actuation $u(t)$ such that $e(t) \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$. The most general **linear error-driven controller** can be written as a convolution integral:

$$
u(t) = \int_{0}^{t} k(t-\tau)\, e(\tau)\, d\tau
$$

where $k(\cdot)$ is the controller kernel. Expanding $k$ using a Taylor series about $\tau = t$ (the present instant) gives:

$$
k(t-\tau) = k(0) - \dot{k}(0)(t-\tau) + \tfrac{1}{2}\ddot{k}(0)(t-\tau)^2 - \cdots
$$

Truncating after the first derivative and substituting yields:

$$
u(t) = k(0)\int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau \;-\; \dot{k}(0)\int_{0}^{t}(t-\tau)e(\tau)\,d\tau
$$

Using the **Leibniz integral rule** on the second term to extract a time-derivative form:

$$
\int_{0}^{t}(t-\tau)e(\tau)\,d\tau \;\equiv\; \int_{0}^{t}\!\left(\int_{\tau}^{t} e(\sigma)\, d\sigma\right) d\tau \;\to\; \text{(after differentiation w.r.t. } t\text{)} \; e(t)
$$

Therefore the proportional, integral, and derivative components emerge naturally with the identifications:

$$
K_p = \dot{k}(0), \qquad K_i = k(0), \qquad K_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\ddot{k}(0)
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\boxed{\,u(t) = K_p\, e(t) \;+\; K_i\!\int_{0}^{t}\! e(\tau)\, d\tau \;+\; K_d\, \frac{de(t)}{dt}\,}
$$

**Engineering meaning:**
- $K_p$ provides an **immediate corrective force** proportional to current error (rigidity).
- $K_i$ **eliminates steady-state offset** by accumulating past error.
- $K_d$ **damps rapid oscillations** by reacting to the rate of change of error.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Discrete-Time PID (Velocity Form) for Digital Controllers

**Goal:** Convert continuous PID into a form executable by a microcontroller sampling every $T_s$ seconds.

Step 1: Discretize the integral using the **backward-rectangle rule** (causal, stable):

$$
\int_{0}^{t_k} e(\tau)d\tau \;\approx\; T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(jT_s) = T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e_j
$$

Step 2: Discretize the derivative using the **backward finite difference**:

$$
\frac{de(t)}{dt}\bigg|_{t=kT_s} \;\approx\; \frac{e_k - e_{k-1}}{T_s}
$$

Step 3: Substitute into the continuous PID and compute the **incremental (velocity) output** $\Delta u_k = u_k - u_{k-1}$:

$$
u_k = K_p e_k + K_i T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e_j + \frac{K_d}{T_s}(e_k - e_{k-1})
$$

Subtracting $u_{k-1}$ (with $u_{-1}=0$) cancels the summation, leaving:

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta u_k = K_p(e_k - e_{k-1}) + K_i T_s\, e_k + \frac{K_d}{T_s}(e_k - 2e_{k-1} + e_{k-2})\,}
$$

This is the **velocity-form PID** — preferred in robotics because it produces the *change* in command (bumpless transfer, no integral windup on startup).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Encoder Angular Resolution

A quadrature encoder produces $N_{ppr}$ pulses per revolution on each of two channels (A and B), where reading both edges of both channels yields **four counts per pulse**:

$$
N_{counts} = 4 \cdot N_{ppr}
$$

The angular resolution per count is therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,\theta_{res} = \frac{2\pi}{4 N_{ppr}} = \frac{\pi}{2 N_{ppr}} \;\; \text{rad/count}\,}
$$

**Worked numerical example (KTU-style):**
A robot joint uses an encoder with $N_{ppr} = 2500$. Compute the resolution:

$$
\theta_{res} = \frac{\pi}{2 \cdot 2500} = \frac{3.14159}{5000} \approx 6.2832 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{rad}
$$

Converting to degrees:

$$
\theta_{res} = 6.2832 \times 10^{-4} \cdot \frac{180}{\pi} \approx 0.036^{\circ}
$$

After reduction through a gear ratio $N = 100$, the **output-shaft resolution** becomes:

$$
\theta_{out} = \frac{\theta_{res}}{N} = 3.6 \times 10^{-4 \circ}
$$

This is sub-arcsecond precision — essential for precision assembly and welding.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Reflected Inertia and Motor Sizing

For a motor driving a load through a gear train of ratio $N = \omega_m / \omega_L$, the **energy equivalence principle** gives the reflected inertia:

$$
\tfrac{1}{2} J_m \omega_m^2 + \tfrac{1}{2} J_L \omega_L^2 \equiv \tfrac{1}{2} J_{eff}\, \omega_m^2
$$

Substituting $\omega_L = \omega_m / N$ and matching coefficients:

$$
J_{eff} = J_m + \frac{J_L}{N^2}
$$

**Why it matters:** Choosing $N$ large reduces reflected load inertia, improving acceleration bandwidth, but it multiplies the reflected friction torque by $N$ — a fundamental trade-off.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Controller Selection

| Application / Criterion | Open-Loop Stepper | PID Servo | State-Space | Adaptive |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cost | Low | Medium | High | High |
| Accuracy | Low (no feedback) | High | Very High | Very High |
| Tuning Effort | None | Low | Moderate | High |
| Nonlinearity Handling | None | Poor | Good | Excellent |
| Use Case | Educational, low-cost | **Most industrial robots** | High-performance CNC | Varying payloads |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Robot Hardware Controller

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    HMI --> SUP
    EXT --> SUP
    SUP --> MOT
    MOT --> J1
    MOT --> J2
    MOT --> J3

    J2 --> PWR
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Hardware Interface Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CTRL["Robot Controller CPU DSP FPGA"]
    ENC["Quadrature Encoder RS422"]

    ENC --> CTRL
    RES --> CTRL

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** In Mermaid `flowchart`, edges represent **real-time signal or data flow** between layers. The controller sits at the centre, with sensors feeding *upward* and power actuators being driven *downward*. This bi-directional topology is the defining feature of every modern industrial robot.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define a robot controller. Differentiate between an open-loop and a closed-loop controller with one industrial example for each.

**Model Answer:**

A **robot controller** is the dedicated electronic computational system that generates and supervises real-time motion commands, executes the servo loop, performs trajectory interpolation, and interfaces with sensors and actuators of a robotic manipulator.

**Open-loop controller:** The control action has *no feedback* from the output. The command is sent and assumed executed. Example: a **stepper-motor-driven pick-and-place gantry** where each pulse advances the motor by a fixed step angle without verification.

**Closed-loop (servo) controller:** Uses **feedback** (position, velocity, or torque) to continuously correct the output. Example: a **6-DOF articulated welding robot** where each joint encoder feeds back to a PID controller to correct tracking error in real time.

**Key distinction:** Closed-loop controllers achieve higher accuracy, reject disturbances, and compensate for nonlinearities; open-loop systems are simpler, cheaper, and suitable only when the load is predictable.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** List any four industrial fieldbus protocols used in robotic controllers and state one distinguishing feature of each.

**Model Answer:**

| # | Protocol | Distinguishing Feature |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **EtherCAT** | Sub-millisecond deterministic cycle time via "processing on the fly" |
| 2 | **CANopen** | Robust multi-drop bus for distributed I/O; dominant in mobile robotics |
| 3 | **Profinet IRT** | Isochronous Real-Time Ethernet; integrates with Siemens PLC ecosystem |
| 4 | **DeviceNet** | Based on CAN; widely used for sensor/actuator networks in legacy plants |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the hierarchical architecture of an industrial robot controller. Identify the functions performed at each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the continuous-time PID control law from first principles and write its velocity-form discrete equivalent. Explain why the velocity form is preferred in digital servo controllers. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Hierarchical Architecture of an Industrial Robot Controller**

A modern robot controller is organized into **three nested layers**, each operating at a different sampling rate and abstraction level:

**1. Supervisory / Task Layer:**
- Runs an **RTOS** (VxWorks, QNX, RT-Linux).
- Handles job sequencing, language interpretation (RAPID, KRL), I/O coordination.
- Communicates with the **factory network** (Ethernet/IP, OPC-UA).
- Cycle time: **10 ms – 100 ms**.

**2. Motion Planning / Servo Layer:**
- Performs **trajectory interpolation** (linear, circular, spline).
- Converts Cartesian path $\mathbf{X}(t)$ to joint-space $\mathbf{q}(t)$ via **inverse kinematics**.
- Cycle time: **1 ms – 4 ms**.

**3. Joint / Loop Layer:**
- One independent controller per joint.
- Implements **PID / state-feedback** at **0.5 ms – 1 ms**.
- Drives the **power converter** (PWM inverter, H-bridge).

**Block diagram reference:** See Section 4.1.

The hierarchical structure ensures that high-level planning is decoupled from low-level servo execution, enabling **modularity, fault isolation, and deterministic timing**.

**(b) PID Derivation and Discrete Velocity Form**

**Step 1:** Define the tracking error as $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$.

**Step 2:** Express the most general linear error-driven controller as a convolution:
$$
u(t) = \int_{0}^{t} k(t-\tau) e(\tau)\, d\tau
$$

**Step 3:** Taylor-expand the kernel $k(t-\tau)$ about $\tau = t$ and truncate after the second-order term.

**Step 4:** Apply Leibniz rule to identify the integral as the integral term and the derivative of the kernel-product as the derivative term.

**Step 5:** Final continuous PID:
$$
u(t) = K_p\, e(t) + K_i \!\int_{0}^{t}\! e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d\, \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 6:** Discretize using backward-rectangle (integral) and backward-difference (derivative):
$$
u_k = K_p e_k + K_i T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e_j + \frac{K_d}{T_s}(e_k - e_{k-1})
$$

**Step 7:** Take $\Delta u_k = u_k - u_{k-1}$ to eliminate the summation and yield the velocity form:
$$
\Delta u_k = K_p(e_k - e_{k-1}) + K_i T_s\, e_k + \frac{K_d}{T_s}(e_k - 2e_{k-1} + e_{k-2})
$$

**Why velocity form is preferred in robotics:**
- **Bumpless transfer** when switching from manual to auto mode.
- **Anti-windup**: since output is *change*, large accumulated error does not cause huge jumps.
- Naturally suited to motor drives that accept incremental torque/velocity commands.
- Re-initialization is straightforward after an emergency stop.

---

### Question B — (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Compare the following robot controller types with respect to cost, accuracy, complexity, and typical application: (i) Open-loop stepper, (ii) PID servo, (iii) PLC-based, (iv) PC-based soft-PLC. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **EtherCAT fieldbus architecture**. Derive an expression for its cycle time in terms of the number of slave nodes and per-node propagation delay. Why is EtherCAT preferred for high-speed robotic motion control? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison of Robot Controller Types**

| Criterion | Open-Loop Stepper | PID Servo | PLC-Based | PC Soft-PLC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cost** | Very Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| **Accuracy** | Low (no feedback) | High (sub-mm) | Medium | High |
| **Complexity** | Very Low | Moderate | Moderate | High |
| **Tuning** | None | Easy (Ziegler-Nichols) | Ladder logic | Code + tuning |
| **Application** | 3-DOF printers, lab demos | 6-DOF industrial arms | Cell coordination | Vision-guided robots |
| **Feedback** | None | Encoder/Resolver | Limited I/O | Full sensor suite |

**(b) EtherCAT Architecture and Cycle Time**

**Architecture:** EtherCAT (Ethernet for Control Automation Technology) uses a **logical ring** topology on a physical **daisy-chain**. The master sends a single Ethernet frame that *passes through* every slave node. Each slave reads its assigned data on the fly and inserts its response in the same frame — a technique called "processing on the fly."

**Cycle-time derivation:**

Let:
- $N$ = number of slave nodes
- $t_{node}$ = processing delay per slave (typically $\leq 1 \mu s$)
- $t_{frame}$ = frame transmission time for a 1518-byte frame at 100 Mbit/s:

$$
t_{frame} = \frac{1518 \times 8}{100 \times 10^{6}} \approx 121.4 \,\mu s
$$

Total cycle time:

$$
\boxed{\,T_{cycle} = t_{frame} + N \cdot t_{node} + t_{prop}\,}
$$

where $t_{prop}$ is the cable propagation delay (negligible for sub-100 m runs).

For $N = 50$ slaves and $t_{node} = 1 \mu s$:

$$
T_{cycle} \approx 121.4 + 50 \times 1 = 171.4 \,\mu s \approx 0.17 \,\text{ms}
$$

This sub-millisecond cycle time — orders of magnitude faster than conventional Ethernet — is why EtherCAT dominates **high-speed multi-axis motion control**.

**Why EtherCAT is preferred:**
- **Sub-millisecond deterministic cycle** achievable.
- **Synchronization** via distributed clocks with sub-microsecond jitter.
- **Bandwidth efficiency**: one frame carries data for all slaves.
- **Scalability** — supports up to 65,535 slaves on a single segment.
- **Open standard** — vendor-independent.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing PID transfer function with the control law.** Write the *time-domain law* $u(t)$ separately from the *Laplace-domain TF* $G_c(s)$ — students who skip this lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the sampling time $T_s$** in the discrete PID equation. Omitting it makes the integral term dimensionally wrong.
> 3. **Mixing up encoder counts vs pulses per revolution.** Quadrature gives 4× more counts than pulses. Always state $N_{counts} = 4 N_{ppr}$.
> 4. **In EtherCAT cycle-time problems,** many students ignore the frame transmission time and only multiply $N \cdot t_{node}$. Include both — the frame time dominates.
> 5. **Do not write "PID increases accuracy"** without explaining the *mechanism* (closed-loop correction of error). Vague statements lose marks under KTU's rubric.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **robot controller** is hierarchical: **Supervisory → Motion Planning → Joint Servo**.
- **Open-loop** uses no feedback (steppers); **closed-loop** uses encoders/resolvers and PID/state-feedback.
- The **continuous PID law** is $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \!\int e\, dt + K_d \dot{e}$.
- The **velocity-form discrete PID** is the standard implementation on digital servo controllers.
- **Encoder resolution** is $\theta_{res} = \pi / (2 N_{ppr})$ rad/count; quadrature gives 4× counts/pulse.
- **Reflected inertia** through gear ratio $N$ is $J_{eff} = J_m + J_L/N^2$.
- **EtherCAT** delivers sub-millisecond deterministic cycle time via "processing on the fly."
- **Safety layer** (STO, SIL 2/3 per ISO 13849) is mandatory in industrial robots.
- **Industrial I/O standards:** 24 V DC digital, $\pm 10$ V or 4–20 mA analog.
- **ADC quantization error** is $\pm V_{FS}/2^{n+1}$ — relevant when selecting encoder/sensor resolution.
- **Nyquist criterion** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ must always be satisfied when sampling sensor signals.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT865 - INDUSTRIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Selection of Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Motor Selection in Industrial Robotics** is the systematic engineering process of evaluating, comparing, and choosing the most appropriate electromechanical actuator (DC servomotor, AC servomotor, stepper motor, brushless DC motor, or hydraulic/pneumatic actuator) for a specific joint or axis of a robotic manipulator, based on quantitative performance criteria such as **torque requirement**, **speed range**, **inertia matching ratio**, **power dissipation**, **positional accuracy**, **repeatability**, and **duty cycle**.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme, motor selection is treated as a **design-time decision** that bridges the kinematic specifications of the robot (payload, workspace, acceleration, cycle time) with the dynamic specifications of the joint drive system.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Choosing an Engine for a Vehicle:**
> Think of a robotic joint as the **wheel of a vehicle**. The wheel needs a "muscle" (motor) to turn it. If you put a tiny engine in a truck, it stalls. If you put a massive racing engine in a toy car, it tears the chassis apart. Motor selection is exactly this — matching the **"muscle size"** to the **"load weight"** and **"speed required"**.

A 6-axis industrial robot arm is essentially six such "wheels" stacked together. Each wheel has a different load (due to gravity, acceleration, and the weight of subsequent links), so each needs a carefully chosen motor.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard engineering constants and metrics govern motor selection in robotics:

- **Gravity** $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$
- **Peak Torque** $T_{peak}$ (Nm)
- **RMS Torque** $T_{rms}$ (Nm)
- **Motor Inertia** $J_m$ (kg·m²)
- **Load Inertia** $J_L$ (kg·m²)
- **Inertia Ratio (Mismatch Ratio)** $J_L / J_m$ — recommended $\leq 10:1$ for servos
- **Back-EMF Constant** $K_b$ (V·s/rad)
- **Torque Constant** $K_t$ (Nm/A)
- **Power Rating** $P$ (W or kW)
- **Duty Cycle** (% of operating time)
- **Service Factor** — typically **1.0 to 1.5**

## 1.4 Classification of Motors Used in Robotics

Robotic actuators are broadly classified into three families:

| Family | Sub-Types | Typical Use in Robotics |
|--------|----------|------------------------|
| **Electrical** | DC Servo, AC Servo, Brushless DC (BLDC), Stepper (PM/VR/Hybrid) | Most common — joint drives |
| **Hydraulic** | Linear/Rotary hydraulic actuators | Heavy-payload robots, mining, space |
| **Pneumatic** | Air cylinders, pneumatic motors | Pick-and-place, low-precision, low-cost |

---

> **End of SECTION 1 — Continue to SECTION 2 for Theoretical Depth and Formula Sheet**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Motor Selection Philosophy — The "Five-Spec" Framework

Every motor selection problem in robotics can be reduced to matching **five quantitative specifications** between the load and the actuator:

1. **Torque Demand** — Can the motor deliver the required instantaneous (peak) and continuous (RMS) torque?
2. **Speed Demand** — Is the no-load speed and the speed-at-load within the robot's motion profile?
3. **Inertia Match** — Is the load-to-motor inertia ratio within the controllable band (typically $\leq 10:1$)?
4. **Power Demand** — Does the motor's continuous power rating exceed the average power dissipation?
5. **Precision/Duty** — Does the motor's resolution, repeatability, and thermal class match the application's duty cycle?

## 2.2 Load Torque Analysis at a Robotic Joint

The total torque required at any joint of a manipulator is the sum of four physically distinct components:

$$
T_{total} = T_{static} + T_{friction} + T_{acceleration} + T_{gravity}
$$

Where:

- $T_{static}$ = torque due to steady-state payload reaction
- $T_{friction}$ = Coulomb + viscous friction torque
- $T_{acceleration}$ = torque to accelerate the link + payload inertia
- $T_{gravity}$ = torque due to gravitational loading on the link

The **acceleration torque** component is the most critical during high-speed motion. It is given by:

$$
T_{acceleration} = (J_m + J_L) \cdot \alpha
$$

Where $\alpha$ is the angular acceleration (rad/s²) required by the motion profile.

## 2.3 RMS (Continuous) Torque Calculation

Because motors are thermally limited, the **average heating** must be calculated using the RMS method:

$$
T_{rms} = \sqrt{ \frac{1}{T} \int_0^{T} T^2(t) \, dt }
$$

For a typical trapezoidal velocity profile (constant acceleration, constant velocity, constant deceleration):

$$
T_{rms} = \sqrt{ \frac{T_{acc}^2 \cdot t_a + T_{const}^2 \cdot t_v + T_{dec}^2 \cdot t_d}{t_a + t_v + t_d} }
$$

## 2.4 Inertia Matching — The "Reflected Inertia" Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When a gear reducer with ratio $N$ is placed between the motor and the load, the load inertia **as seen by the motor** (the **reflected inertia**) is divided by $N^2$:

$$
J_{L,\text{reflected}} = \frac{J_L}{N^2}
$$

This is the cornerstone of inertia matching. The goal is:

$$
1 \leq \frac{J_L}{J_m \cdot N^2} \leq 10
$$

> **Optimal inertia match** is typically achieved at $J_L / J_m \approx 1$ to $3$ (with gearing), giving the best compromise between **bandwidth**, **stability**, and **disturbance rejection**.

## 2.5 Power & Speed Relationship

The mechanical power delivered by a rotating shaft is:

$$
P = T \cdot \omega
$$

Where $\omega$ is in rad/s. Converting from RPM:

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi \cdot n}{60} \quad \text{[rad/s]}
$$

## 2.6 Motor Constant — The "Figure of Merit"

The **motor constant** $K_m$ combines torque constant and motor resistance to indicate intrinsic quality:

$$
K_m = \frac{K_t}{\sqrt{R_m}} \quad \text{[Nm / }\sqrt{\text{W}}\text{]}
$$

A **higher** $K_m$ indicates a more efficient motor for a given power dissipation.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $T = F \cdot r$ | Torque from force and lever arm | Nm |
| 2 | $T_{total} = T_s + T_f + T_a + T_g$ | Total joint torque | Nm |
| 3 | $T_{acc} = (J_m + J_L) \cdot \alpha$ | Acceleration torque | Nm |
| 4 | $J_{L,\text{ref}} = J_L / N^2$ | Reflected load inertia | kg·m² |
| 5 | $J_L / (J_m \cdot N^2) \leq 10$ | Inertia mismatch limit | dimensionless |
| 6 | $T_{rms} = \sqrt{ \frac{1}{T} \int T^2 dt }$ | Continuous (heating) torque | Nm |
| 7 | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical power | W |
| 8 | $\omega = 2\pi n / 60$ | RPM → rad/s | rad/s |
| 9 | $K_m = K_t / \sqrt{R_m}$ | Motor constant (figure of merit) | Nm/√W |
| 10 | $\tau_{mech} = J \cdot \alpha / T_{applied}$ | Mechanical time constant | s |
| 11 | $V_{back} = K_b \cdot \omega$ | Back-EMF voltage | V |
| 12 | $T_{stall} = V_{supply} \cdot K_t / R_m$ | Stall torque | Nm |
| 13 | $\eta = P_{out} / P_{in}$ | Efficiency | dimensionless |
| 14 | $T_{gravity} = m \cdot g \cdot r \cdot \cos\theta$ | Gravity torque at joint | Nm |

> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> This formula set is used daily by **robot design engineers** at firms like KUKA, ABB, FANUC, and Yaskawa to size joint drives for automotive welding lines, palletizing cells, and surgical robots. It is also the basis of the **motor sizing software** (e.g., Kollmorgen Motioneering, Maxon Selector) used in industrial practice.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Trapezoidal Velocity Profile Torque Equation

### Problem Setup
A robotic joint executes a move in time $t_{total}$ using a trapezoidal velocity profile: acceleration time $t_a$, constant-velocity time $t_v$, deceleration time $t_d$, such that $t_{total} = t_a + t_v + t_d$. The required angular displacement is $\theta_{total}$.

**Given:** $\theta_{total}$, $t_a = t_d$, $J_{eq}$ (effective inertia at motor shaft), $T_f$ (constant friction torque).

**Find:** RMS torque and Peak torque expressions.

### Step 1 — Velocity at End of Acceleration Phase
During acceleration (uniform), by kinematics:

$$
\theta_{a} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \omega_{peak} \cdot t_a
$$

By symmetry of the trapezoid, the constant-velocity region sweeps an angle:

$$
\theta_{v} = \theta_{total} - 2\theta_{a} = \theta_{total} - \omega_{peak} \cdot t_a
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for Peak Velocity
The constant velocity time is:

$$
t_v = \frac{\theta_v}{\omega_{peak}} = \frac{\theta_{total}}{\omega_{peak}} - t_a
$$

Therefore:

$$
\omega_{peak} = \frac{\theta_{total}}{t_a + t_v} = \frac{\theta_{total}}{t_{total} - t_d}
$$

(For symmetric profiles, $t_a = t_d$, giving $\omega_{peak} = \theta_{total} / (t_{total} - t_a)$.)

### Step 3 — Peak Acceleration
$$
\alpha = \frac{\omega_{peak}}{t_a}
$$

### Step 4 — Peak Torque (Occurs During Acceleration)
$$
T_{peak} = T_f + J_{eq} \cdot \alpha = T_f + J_{eq} \cdot \frac{\omega_{peak}}{t_a}
$$

### Step 5 — RMS Torque (Three-Region Average)
During the constant-velocity region, only friction is overcome, so $T_{const} = T_f$.

$$
T_{acc} = T_{dec} = T_f + J_{eq} \cdot \alpha
$$

Therefore:

$$
T_{rms} = \sqrt{ \frac{ (T_f + J_{eq}\alpha)^2 \cdot t_a + T_f^2 \cdot t_v + (T_f + J_{eq}\alpha)^2 \cdot t_d }{t_a + t_v + t_d} }
$$

Factoring, with $t_a = t_d$:

$$
T_{rms} = \sqrt{ \frac{ 2 t_a (T_f + J_{eq}\alpha)^2 + t_v T_f^2 }{t_{total}} }
$$

> **Interpretation:** The RMS torque is **always less** than the peak torque, but it must be **less than** the motor's continuous torque rating for safe long-term operation.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Sizing a Motor for a Palletizing Robot Joint

### Given
- Payload mass $m = 50$ kg, link length $r = 0.4$ m
- Joint angle (worst case) $\theta = 0°$ (horizontal)
- Required angular acceleration $\alpha = 5$ rad/s²
- Total inertia at joint $J_{eq} = 2.5$ kg·m² (after gear reduction)
- Friction torque $T_f = 1.5$ Nm
- Gear ratio $N = 50$ (motor to joint)
- Motor inertia $J_m = 1.2 \times 10^{-4}$ kg·m²

### Step 1 — Gravity Torque

$$
T_g = m \cdot g \cdot r \cdot \cos\theta = 50 \times 9.81 \times 0.4 \times 1 = 196.2 \, \text{Nm}
$$

### Step 2 — Acceleration Torque (Reflected to Motor Shaft)
Reflected inertia:

$$
J_{L,\text{ref}} = \frac{J_{eq}}{N^2} = \frac{2.5}{50^2} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{kg·m}^2
$$

Total inertia at motor:

$$
J_{motor,\text{total}} = J_m + J_{L,\text{ref}} = 1.2 \times 10^{-4} + 1.0 \times 10^{-3} = 1.12 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{kg·m}^2
$$

Note: Gravity torque must also be reflected to the motor shaft:

$$
T_{g,\text{motor}} = \frac{T_g}{N \cdot \eta_{gear}} = \frac{196.2}{50 \times 0.9} = 4.36 \, \text{Nm}
$$

(assuming $\eta_{gear} = 0.9$)

### Step 3 — Peak Torque at Motor Shaft
Required angular acceleration at motor: $\alpha_m = N \cdot \alpha = 50 \times 5 = 250$ rad/s²

$$
T_{peak,\text{motor}} = T_{g,\text{motor}} + T_{f,\text{motor}} + J_{motor,\text{total}} \cdot \alpha_m
$$

$$
T_{peak,\text{motor}} = 4.36 + \frac{1.5}{50 \times 0.9} + (1.12 \times 10^{-3}) \times 250
$$

$$
T_{peak,\text{motor}} = 4.36 + 0.033 + 0.28 = 4.67 \, \text{Nm}
$$

### Step 4 — Selection Verdict
A motor with **continuous torque rating $\geq 1.5$ Nm** and **peak torque rating $\geq 5$ Nm** (with 1.2× safety factor) would suffice. Example: A **400 W AC servomotor** with $T_{cont} = 1.91$ Nm, $T_{peak} = 5.7$ Nm is a valid selection.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Motor Types vs. Selection Criteria

| Selection Criterion | DC Servo | AC Servo | Stepper (Hybrid) | BLDC | Hydraulic |
|---------------------|----------|----------|------------------|------|-----------|
| **Torque Density** | Medium | High | Medium | High | Very High |
| **Speed Range** | Medium | Very High | Low–Medium | High | Medium |
| **Position Accuracy** | High ($\pm 0.01°$) | Very High ($\pm 0.001°$) | High (no feedback) | Very High | Medium |
| **Closed-Loop Control** | Yes | Yes | Optional (open-loop) | Yes | Yes |
| **Cost** | Medium | High | Low | High | Very High |
| **Maintenance** | Brush wear | Brushless | Brushless | Brushless | High (seals, fluid) |
| **Power-to-Weight** | Medium | High | Medium | High | Low |
| **Heat Dissipation** | Difficult (rotor) | Easy (stator) | Easy | Easy | Excellent (fluid) |
| **Best Application** | Educational robots, small CNC | Industrial 6-axis arms | Pick-and-place, 3D printers | Drones, AGVs, medical | Heavy forging, mining |

## 3.4 Selection Decision Tree (Algorithmic Logic)

The KTU examination may test the **decision flow** for selecting a motor. The logic is:

1. **Compute** $T_{total}$ (static + dynamic + gravity + friction).
2. **Compute** $T_{rms}$ over a full duty cycle.
3. **Compute** $J_L$ and the **inertia mismatch ratio** $J_L / (J_m \cdot N^2)$.
4. **If** $T_{rms} \leq T_{cont,\text{motor}}$ **and** $T_{peak} \leq T_{peak,\text{motor}}$ **and** inertia ratio $\leq 10:1$ → **Motor acceptable**.
5. **Else** → Resize gear ratio, re-select motor, or add a second-stage reducer.

> **Note:** Hydraulic actuators are selected when the **power density** requirement exceeds what electric motors can deliver — typically for payloads above **500 kg** or torques above **1000 Nm**.

```mermaid
    C --> D[Compute Peak Torque T_peak]

    D --> F[Select Gear Ratio N]

    G --> H{Is Inertia Ratio <= 10:1?}

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List the factors to be considered in the selection of a motor for a robotic joint.**

**Model Answer:**

The key factors considered while selecting a motor for a robotic joint are:

1. **Torque requirement** — peak torque, RMS (continuous) torque, and stall torque.
2. **Speed range** — maximum no-load speed and operating speed range.
3. **Inertia matching** — load-to-motor inertia ratio should ideally be $\leq 10:1$ (with gearing).
4. **Power rating** — continuous and peak power dissipation limits.
5. **Accuracy and resolution** — encoder resolution and positioning repeatability.
6. **Duty cycle and thermal class** — heat dissipation capability.
7. **Physical size, weight, and cost** — important for compact/lightweight robots.
8. **Type of control** — open-loop (stepper) vs. closed-loop (servo).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the significance of the inertia ratio in motor selection? State its recommended range.**

**Model Answer:**

The inertia ratio $J_L / J_m$ (or $J_L / (J_m N^2)$ when gearing is used) is a measure of how well the motor's rotor inertia matches the load inertia as seen at the motor shaft.

- A **high** inertia ratio causes poor dynamic response, overshoot, and instability in servo control.
- A **low** inertia ratio wastes motor capability and may cause hunting oscillations.

**Recommended range:** $1:1$ to $10:1$ (with gearing), with the **optimal** being approximately $1:1$ to $3:1$ for high-performance servos.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part A:** Students often write only "$\leq 10:1$" without mentioning the optimal range (1:1 to 3:1). The examiner expects both — full marks require the **band** and the **optimum**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Part (a)** — 7 Marks: **Explain the various types of motors used in industrial robotics. Compare their characteristics in a tabular form.**

**Part (b)** — 7 Marks: **A robotic joint has to lift a payload of 20 kg at an arm length of 0.5 m. The joint must accelerate the load at 4 rad/s². Friction torque is 2 Nm. The gear ratio between motor and joint is 40, with 90% efficiency. The motor's rotor inertia is $1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ kg·m². Determine the peak torque required at the motor shaft. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s².**

### Model Solution — Part (a)

The four major motor categories used in industrial robotics are:

1. **DC Servo Motors** — Use permanent-magnet field; have a linear torque-speed relationship; require brushes (maintenance); suitable for small-to-medium robotic joints with high precision.

2. **AC Servo Motors** — Brushless, use permanent-magnet rotor and stator windings; very high torque density, high speeds, high efficiency; used in modern industrial robots (FANUC, KUKA, ABB).

3. **Stepper Motors** — Rotate in discrete steps; can run open-loop; lower cost; used in low-speed pick-and-place and 3D-printer-style machines. Hybrid steppers combine PM and VR principles for finer resolution.

4. **Brushless DC (BLDC) Motors** — Electronic commutation, high reliability, high efficiency; widely used in collaborative robots, AGVs, drones, and surgical robots.

5. **Hydraulic Actuators** — Use pressurized fluid; deliver extremely high force/torque; used in heavy-payload robots (forging, mining, underwater).

6. **Pneumatic Actuators** — Use compressed air; low cost, low precision; used in simple pick-and-place mechanisms.

**Comparative Table (reproduced from SECTION 3.3):**

| Criterion | DC Servo | AC Servo | Stepper | BLDC | Hydraulic |
|-----------|----------|----------|---------|------|-----------|
| Torque Density | Medium | High | Medium | High | Very High |
| Speed Range | Medium | Very High | Low–Medium | High | Medium |
| Position Accuracy | High | Very High | High | Very High | Medium |
| Cost | Medium | High | Low | High | Very High |
| Maintenance | Brush wear | Brushless | Brushless | Brushless | High |
| Best Application | Educational | 6-axis arms | Pick-and-place | AGV/Medical | Heavy payload |

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $m = 20$ kg, $r = 0.5$ m, $\alpha = 4$ rad/s², $T_f = 2$ Nm
- $N = 40$, $\eta = 0.9$, $J_m = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ kg·m², $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Gravity Torque at Joint (worst case, horizontal):**

$$
T_g = m \cdot g \cdot r = 20 \times 9.81 \times 0.5 = 98.1 \, \text{Nm}
$$

**Step 2 — Acceleration Torque at Joint:**

$$
T_{acc,\text{joint}} = J_{joint} \cdot \alpha
$$

Assuming the load inertia dominates (or given as $J_{joint} = m r^2$ for a point mass):

$$
J_{joint} = 20 \times 0.5^2 = 5 \, \text{kg·m}^2
$$

$$
T_{acc,\text{joint}} = 5 \times 4 = 20 \, \text{Nm}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Joint Torque:**

$$
T_{total,\text{joint}} = T_g + T_{acc,\text{joint}} + T_f = 98.1 + 20 + 2 = 120.1 \, \text{Nm}
$$

**Step 4 — Reflect to Motor Shaft (divide by $N \cdot \eta$):**

$$
T_{peak,\text{motor}} = \frac{T_{total,\text{joint}}}{N \cdot \eta} = \frac{120.1}{40 \times 0.9} = \frac{120.1}{36} \approx 3.34 \, \text{Nm}
$$

**Note:** The motor's own rotor inertia contribution is:

$$
T_{acc,\text{motor}} = J_m \cdot (N \alpha) = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times (40 \times 4) = 0.024 \, \text{Nm}
$$

This is **negligible** compared to the load and gravity components, confirming the standard design practice of using $J_m$ as a "minor term."

**Final Answer:** **Peak torque required at the motor shaft $\approx 3.34$ Nm** (plus a 20% safety factor → select a motor with $T_{peak,\text{rating}} \geq 4$ Nm).

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Part (a)** — 7 Marks: **With a neat block diagram, explain the procedure for selection of a motor for a given robotic application.**

**Part (b)** — 7 Marks: **A pick-and-place robot joint uses a stepper motor with holding torque 3 Nm and step angle 1.8°. The load inertia reflected to the motor is $4 \times 10^{-5}$ kg·m² and the rotor inertia is $1 \times 10^{-5}$ kg·m². Determine: (i) the inertia ratio, (ii) whether the motor is suitable (assume the maximum permissible ratio is 10:1), and (iii) the positioning resolution achievable with a 10:1 microstepping drive.**

### Model Solution — Part (a)

The motor selection procedure for a robotic joint consists of the following sequential steps (refer to SECTION 4.1 block diagram):

1. **Define Application Specs** — payload, speed, accuracy, duty cycle, environment.
2. **Compute Load Torque** — gravity, friction, acceleration, payload reaction.
3. **Compute RMS Torque** — over the full motion cycle.
4. **Choose a Preliminary Gear Ratio** — based on speed-reduction requirements.
5. **Reflect Load Inertia to Motor Shaft** — $J_L / N^2$.
6. **Check Inertia Ratio** — must be $\leq 10:1$ (else, increase $N$).
7. **Verify Peak and Continuous Torque** — must be within motor ratings.
8. **Verify Speed** — required joint speed $\times N \leq$ motor's max no-load speed.
9. **Verify Power and Thermal Limits** — using $P = T \cdot \omega$ and RMS heating.
10. **Add Safety Factor** — typically 1.2× to 1.5× on torque.
11. **Finalize Motor and Gearbox Combination.**

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Holding torque = 3 Nm, step angle = 1.8°
- $J_L = 4 \times 10^{-5}$ kg·m², $J_m = 1 \times 10^{-5}$ kg·m²
- Max permissible inertia ratio = 10:1

**Part (i) — Inertia Ratio:**

$$
\frac{J_L}{J_m} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-5}}{1 \times 10^{-5}} = 4
$$

**Part (ii) — Suitability Check:**

The inertia ratio is **4:1**, which is **less than 10:1** → **Motor is suitable** for this application.

**Part (iii) — Positioning Resolution with 10:1 Microstepping:**

Effective step angle:

$$
\theta_{micro} = \frac{1.8°}{10} = 0.18° \, \text{per microstep}
$$

Converting to radians (for SI):

$$
\theta_{micro} = 0.18 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 3.14 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{rad}
$$

Converting to linear resolution at a typical gripper radius of 50 mm:

$$
\Delta x = r \cdot \theta = 0.05 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-3} = 1.57 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m} = 0.157 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Final Answer:** Inertia ratio = 4:1, motor is suitable, and positioning resolution = **0.157 mm** at the gripper.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part B Numerical:**
> - Students often forget to **divide by gear ratio and efficiency** when reflecting torque to the motor shaft (loses 2–3 marks).
> - In the inertia ratio problem, students sometimes write only the ratio without the **comparison and conclusion** ("less than 10:1, hence suitable") — examiner awards only partial marks.
> - Unit conversions (degrees to radians, RPM to rad/s) are frequently missed; mark deductions of 1 mark per missing unit are standard KTU practice.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Selection of Motors**

- **Actuator families** in robotics: **Electric** (DC servo, AC servo, stepper, BLDC), **Hydraulic**, **Pneumatic**.
- **Total joint torque** $T_{total} = T_{static} + T_{friction} + T_{acceleration} + T_{gravity}$.
- **Acceleration torque**: $T_{acc} = J_{eq} \cdot \alpha$ where $J_{eq}$ is the inertia at the joint or motor shaft.
- **RMS torque** governs continuous/thermal sizing; **peak torque** governs instantaneous capability.
- **Trapezoidal profile** gives $T_{rms} = \sqrt{ [2 t_a (T_f + J\alpha)^2 + t_v T_f^2] / t_{total} }$.
- **Reflected inertia** through a gear of ratio $N$: $J_{L,\text{ref}} = J_L / N^2$.
- **Inertia mismatch ratio** should be $\leq 10:1$ (optimum 1:1 to 3:1).
- **Motor constant** $K_m = K_t / \sqrt{R_m}$ — higher is better (figure of merit).
- **Power equation**: $P = T \cdot \omega$, with $\omega = 2\pi n / 60$ (RPM to rad/s).
- **Gravity torque**: $T_g = m \cdot g \cdot r \cdot \cos\theta$ — maximum at $\theta = 0°$ (horizontal).
- **Stall torque** $T_{stall} = V \cdot K_t / R_m$.
- **Stepper motor** uses open-loop control; suitable for low-cost, low-speed, medium-precision applications.
- **Servo motor** uses closed-loop control; required for high-precision, high-dynamic industrial robots.
- **Hydraulic actuators** are preferred when payload > 500 kg or torque demand > 1000 Nm.
- **Selection procedure** is a **5-spec match**: torque, speed, inertia, power, precision.
- **Safety factor** of **1.2 to 1.5** is standard on the final selected motor rating.
- **KTU Favorite Numerical:** Given a payload + arm length + acceleration → compute joint torque, reflect to motor, select rating with safety factor.
- **KTU Favorite Theory:** Tabular comparison of motor types + selection criteria (often asked for 7 marks in Part B).
