topics.title: Understanding Manufacturer's Technical Datasheet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT865 - INDUSTRIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Understanding Manufacturer's Technical Datasheet

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
A **Manufacturer's Technical Datasheet** for an industrial robot is an officially published engineering document issued by the OEM (Original Equipment Manufacturer) that comprehensively specifies the robot's **mechanical, kinematic, dynamic, electrical, environmental, and performance parameters** under standard test conditions. It serves as the legally binding reference for **system integrators, plant designers, and end-users** during the stages of robot selection, simulation, installation, programming, and preventive maintenance.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1):**
> A robot datasheet is the primary engineering reference document that quantifies the physical and operational capabilities of a manipulator. Mastery of datasheet interpretation is mandatory because every downstream activity — workspace design, payload calculation, cycle-time estimation, and safety zoning — is derived directly from the values listed in it.

### Key Sections Typically Found in a Robot Datasheet
1. **Manufacturer & Model Identification** — OEM name, model number, series, year of manufacture, country of origin.
2. **General Specifications** — Robot type (articulated, SCARA, Delta, Cartesian), number of axes, mounting position (floor, wall, ceiling), mass of the robot.
3. **Performance Specifications** — Payload (rated and maximum), reach, repeatability, accuracy, pose repeatability.
4. **Axis-wise Motion Specifications** — Range of motion, maximum speed, and allowable inertia/moment for each individual joint.
5. **Electrical & Controller Specifications** — Power supply (V/Hz), controller model, I/O count, communication protocols (EtherCAT, Profinet, DeviceNet, Modbus).
6. **Environmental Specifications** — Operating temperature range, humidity limits, **IP rating** (Ingress Protection), cleanroom class (if applicable), noise emission level.
7. **Mechanical & Installation Specifications** — Footprint, mounting bolt pattern, base height, end-effector flange (ISO 9409-1), cable routing.
8. **Compliance & Safety Standards** — CE, ISO 10218-1/2, ANSI/RIA R15.06, ISO 13849-1 (PL=d), and risk assessment references.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are buying a **refrigerator** for your home. Before purchase, you glance at the energy label which tells you: power consumption (kWh/year), volume (litres), dimensions, star rating, and noise level (dB). You do not need to open the compressor to know if it will fit in your kitchen.

A **robot datasheet is exactly that — but for an industrial robot**. Instead of litres and star ratings, it tells the engineer:
- How much **weight** the robot arm can lift (payload).
- How **far** it can stretch (reach).
- How **precisely** it can return to a taught point (repeatability, typically in **mm** or **µm**).
- How **fast** each joint can move (deg/s or rad/s).
- Under what **environmental conditions** it can operate safely (°C, %RH, IP rating).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Whenever a datasheet value is quoted in an answer, always specify its **unit** and the **standard test condition** under which it was measured. Examiners award marks for unit awareness and ISO/ANSI standard references.

### Why Datasheet Literacy is Critical for a Robotics Engineer
- **Selection Phase:** Ensures the chosen robot meets the application's payload, reach, and cycle-time requirements.
- **Simulation Phase:** Software like RoboDK, Visual Components, or DELMIA imports the exact DH-parameters, joint limits, and speed envelopes from the datasheet.
- **Safety Phase:** Risk assessors use the datasheet's stopping times, stopping distances, and PL-rated safety functions.
- **Maintenance Phase:** Spare-part numbers, lubrication schedules, and overhaul intervals are documented in the datasheet annexures.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decoding the Performance Block

The performance block is the most heavily examined portion of any datasheet. The four pillars are:

**(a) Payload**
The maximum mass that the robot can manipulate at the wrist or end-effector while maintaining all rated specifications (speed, accuracy, repeatability) throughout the workspace.

$$P_{rated} = m_{gripper} + m_{part} + m_{fixture}$$

**(b) Reach**
The radial distance from the centre of the robot base (axis-1 intersection) to the centre of the end-effector mounting flange, measured with the arm fully extended horizontally.

**(c) Repeatability**
Statistically defined as the radius of the sphere (in mm or µm) that contains the **actual** end-effector positions obtained when repeatedly commanding the robot to the same taught pose, under identical load, speed, and environmental conditions.

$$R_{repeatability} = \pm \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\vert \mathbf{x}_i - \bar{\mathbf{x}} \vert$$

where $\mathbf{x}_i$ is the $i^{th}$ commanded position and $\bar{\mathbf{x}}$ is the cluster centroid.

> [!NOTE]
> Repeatability $\neq$ Accuracy. **Accuracy** is the deviation between the **commanded** pose and the **mean of attained** poses. **Repeatability** is the spread of attained poses about their own mean. Industrial robots are typically **highly repeatable but only moderately accurate**, because accuracy is degraded by link deflection, gear backlash, and thermal expansion.

**(d) Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**
For a 6-axis articulated robot, **6 DOF** are required to define arbitrary position and orientation in 3D Cartesian space (3 translational + 3 rotational). A 4-axis SCARA has **4 DOF** (X, Y, Z, and rotation about Z).

### 2.2 Decoding the Axis-Wise Motion Block
Each joint ($J_1$ to $J_n$) carries three numerical declarations:
- **Range of motion** — usually in **degrees** (rotation) or **millimetres** (linear).
- **Maximum speed** — usually in **deg/s** or **rad/s**.
- **Allowable moment/inertia** — product of payload mass and the square of its offset distance from the wrist centre.

$$I_{allow} = m \cdot r^2 \quad \text{(kg·m}^2\text{)}$$

where $m$ is the payload mass and $r$ is the distance from the wrist centre to the centre of mass of the payload.

### 2.3 Decoding the Environmental & Compliance Block
| Code | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **IP54** | Dust-protected (limited ingress) and protected against splashing water from any direction. |
| **IP65** | Dust-tight and protected against low-pressure water jets. |
| **IP67** | Dust-tight and protected against temporary immersion (1 m for 30 min). |
| **ISO 10218-1** | Safety requirements for industrial robots — the robot itself. |
| **ISO 10218-2** | Safety requirements for robot systems and integration. |
| **ISO 13849-1 PL=d** | Performance Level d for safety-related parts of control systems. |
| **ISO 9409-1** | Standardised mechanical interface (flange) for end-effectors. |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Governing Relation |
|---|-----------|:------:|:-------------:|--------------------|
| 1 | Payload | $P$ | **kg** | $P = m_{gripper} + m_{part} + m_{fixture}$ |
| 2 | Reach | $R$ | **mm** | Geometric — depends on link lengths |
| 3 | Repeatability | $r$ | **mm** or **µm** | $r = \pm \dfrac{1}{n}\sum \vert \mathbf{x}_i - \bar{\mathbf{x}} \vert$ |
| 4 | Maximum joint speed | $\omega_{max}$ | **deg/s** | Listed per axis in datasheet |
| 5 | Allowable wrist moment | $M$ | **N·m** | $M = m \cdot g \cdot r$ |
| 6 | Allowable wrist inertia | $I$ | **kg·m²** | $I = m \cdot r^2$ |
| 7 | Mass of robot | $M_{robot}$ | **kg** | Used for foundation/pedestal design |
| 8 | Operating temperature | $T_{op}$ | **°C** | e.g., $0^\circ C$ to $+45^\circ C$ |
| 9 | Humidity | $RH$ | **%** | e.g., $20\% \le RH \le 80\%$ |
| 10 | IP rating | — | — | Two-digit code (solid + liquid ingress) |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of Datasheet Interpretation
- **Mechanical Design:** Foundation bolt sizing depends on robot mass + dynamic reaction forces.
- **Cycle-Time Estimation:** Sum of joint motions $\div$ joint speeds gives theoretical minimum cycle time; datasheet values are upper bounds under real load.
- **Process Integration:** End-effector flange (typically **ISO 9409-1-50-4-M6**) ensures mechanical interchangeability.
- **Compliance Auditing:** Datasheet CE/ISO marks allow the Safety Officer to cross-verify during statutory audits.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Repeatability Quantification (Cluster Centroid Method)

When a robot is commanded to reach the same Cartesian pose $N$ times, the controller records the actual attained position vectors $\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_N$. The datasheet's repeatability figure is derived as follows.

**Step 1 — Express the attained position vector in Cartesian coordinates.**

$$\mathbf{x}_i = (x_i, \, y_i, \, z_i)^T, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$$

Each $\mathbf{x}_i$ is a $3 \times 1$ column vector containing the TCP (Tool Centre Point) coordinates.

**Step 2 — Compute the cluster centroid (arithmetic mean).**

$$\bar{\mathbf{x}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{x}_i$$

Writing out component-wise:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i, \quad \bar{z} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} z_i$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Euclidean deviation of each sample from the centroid.**

$$d_i = \sqrt{(x_i - \bar{x})^2 + (y_i - \bar{y})^2 + (z_i - \bar{z})^2}$$

**Step 4 — Average the deviations to obtain the mean radial spread.**

$$\bar{d} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i$$

**Step 5 — Quote the datasheet repeatability as the half-radius of the bounding sphere.**

$$R_{repeatability} = \pm \bar{d} \quad \text{(in mm or µm)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** If a datasheet states repeatability = $\pm 0.05$ mm, it means that **on average**, the robot will return to within **0.05 mm radius** of the cluster centroid when the same pose is commanded repeatedly. It does **not** guarantee absolute positioning within 0.05 mm of the **commanded** point — that is governed by **accuracy**, not repeatability.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Allowable Wrist Inertia from Datasheet Constraints

When the datasheet specifies the maximum allowable wrist moment $M_{max}$ (in N·m) and the offset of the payload centre of mass $r$ (in m) from the wrist centre, the maximum payload mass can be back-calculated.

**Step 1 — Express the gravitational moment at the wrist.**

$$M = m \cdot g \cdot r$$

where $g = 9.81$ m/s² is the standard gravitational acceleration.

**Step 2 — Set the gravitational moment equal to the datasheet maximum.**

$$m \cdot g \cdot r = M_{max}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the maximum permissible payload mass.**

$$m_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{g \cdot r}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values (worked example).**

Given datasheet: $M_{max} = 50$ N·m, $r = 250$ mm $= 0.25$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s²:

$$m_{max} = \frac{50}{9.81 \times 0.25}$$

$$m_{max} = \frac{50}{2.4525} = 20.39 \text{ kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> If the calculated $m_{max}$ exceeds the datasheet's separately listed "rated payload," the **smaller of the two** values governs the safe operation. The wrist inertia check and the rated payload check are **independent constraints**.

**Step 5 — Compute the corresponding wrist inertia.**

$$I_{max} = m_{max} \cdot r^2 = 20.39 \times (0.25)^2 = 20.39 \times 0.0625 = 1.274 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Datasheet Parameter → Engineering Decision Mapping

| Datasheet Parameter | Typical Range | Engineering Decision Influenced | Pitfall if Ignored |
|---------------------|---------------|--------------------------------|--------------------|
| Payload | 3 kg – 800 kg | Gripper size, part family selection | Motor overload, joint damage |
| Reach | 500 mm – 3000 mm | Cell layout, fixture placement | Reachability failure |
| Repeatability | $\pm 0.02$ – $\pm 0.5$ mm | Process capability (Cpk) | Defect rate in assembly |
| Joint speeds | 90 – 450 deg/s | Cycle time, throughput calculation | Missed takt-time |
| Mass of robot | 20 kg – 1500 kg | Pedestal/foundation design | Structural failure |
| IP rating | IP54 – IP67 | Cell environment (washdown, foundry) | Premature failure |
| Mounting position | Floor / Wall / Ceiling | Cell footprint optimisation | Collision with structure |
| Controller I/O | 16 DI / 16 DO (typical) | PLC interfacing design | I/O shortage at integration |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Robot Datasheet
The following block diagram maps the hierarchical structure of a typical OEM datasheet and the logical flow of information used by a system integrator.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[OEM Manufacturers Technical Datasheet] --> B[Identification Block]
    A --> C[Performance Block]
    A --> D[Mechanical and Kinematic Block]
    A --> E[Electrical and Controller Block]
    A --> F[Environmental and Compliance Block]
    A --> G[Installation and Maintenance Block]

    B --> B1[OEM Name]
    B --> B2[Model Number]
    B --> B3[Serial Number Range]
    B --> B4[Year of Manufacture]

    C --> C1[Payload]
    C --> C2[Reach]
    C --> C3[Repeatability]
    C --> C4[Cycle Time Reference]

    D --> D1[Axis 1 Range and Speed]
    D --> D2[Axis 2 Range and Speed]
    D --> D3[Axis 3 Range and Speed]
    D --> D4[Axis 4 Range and Speed]
    D --> D5[Axis 5 Range and Speed]
    D --> D6[Axis 6 Range and Speed]
    D --> D7[Wrist Moment and Inertia Limits]
    D --> D8[End Effector Flange Standard]

    E --> E1[Power Supply V and Hz]
    E --> E2[Controller Model]
    E --> E3[Digital and Analog I O Count]
    E --> E4[Fieldbus Protocols]

    F --> F1[Operating Temperature]
    F --> F2[Humidity Range]
    F --> F3[IP Rating]
    F --> F4[ISO 10218 Compliance]
    F --> F5[CE Marking]

    G --> G1[Footprint and Mounting Pattern]
    G --> G2[Mass and Center of Gravity]
    G --> G3[Cable Routing Diagram]
    G --> G4[Spare Parts List]
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow — Datasheet Reading Procedure for Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start Robot Selection] --> Q1{Does Payload meet requirement including gripper mass?}
    Q1 -- No --> X1[Reject this model and shortlist next]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Does Reach envelope all taught points in layout?}
    Q2 -- No --> X1
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Does Repeatability satisfy process Cp Cpk target?}
    Q3 -- No --> X1
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Does Joint Speed meet takt time?}
    Q4 -- No --> X1
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q5{Does IP rating match cell environment?}
    Q5 -- No --> X1
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6{Does ISO 10218 and CE compliance exist?}
    Q6 -- No --> X1
    Q6 -- Yes --> OK[Accept Model and Proceed to Cell Design]
```

### 4.3 Wrist Loading Schematic (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F[End Effector Flange ISO 9409] --> W[Wrist Centre Point]
    W --> r[Offset r metres]
    r --> P[Payload Centre of Mass]
    P -.-> F1[Force equals m times g Newtons]
    P -.-> M1[Moment equals m times g times r Newton metres]
    P -.-> I1[Inertia equals m times r squared kg m squared]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "repeatability" as it appears in a manufacturer's robot datasheet. State the standard units used and explain why repeatability and accuracy are not interchangeable.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Repeatability is the **measure of the robot's ability to return to a previously taught pose** when the same command is given repeatedly under identical conditions of load, speed, and environment. It is expressed as the **half-radius of the cluster of attained positions**, in **millimetres (mm)** or **micrometres (µm)**.
A datasheet value of "$\pm 0.05$ mm repeatability" means the robot will, on average, return to within 0.05 mm of the **mean attained position** of repeated trials.
Repeatability and accuracy differ fundamentally: **accuracy** is the deviation of the **mean attained pose** from the **commanded pose**, while **repeatability** is the spread of attained poses about their **own mean**. Hence a robot may be highly repeatable yet only moderately accurate. **(3 marks: 1 definition + 1 unit + 1 distinction)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is meant by the IP rating of an industrial robot? Decode the meaning of IP65 and IP67 with reference to standard test conditions.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Ingress Protection (IP) rating**, defined by **IEC 60529**, classifies the degree of protection provided by a robot's enclosure against the intrusion of solid objects (first digit) and liquids (second digit).
- **IP65:** First digit **6** = dust-tight (no ingress of dust). Second digit **5** = protected against low-pressure water jets from any direction.
- **IP67:** First digit **6** = dust-tight. Second digit **7** = protected against temporary immersion in water up to 1 metre depth for 30 minutes.
Higher IP ratings enable deployment in **washdown, foundry, food, and pharmaceutical** environments. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT confuse reach with workspace volume.** Reach is a scalar; workspace is the swept 3D volume.
> - **Always quote units** (mm, kg, deg/s). Missing units = automatic loss of 0.5 mark per sub-part.
> - **Do NOT confuse accuracy with repeatability.** Examiners frequently set a one-mark trap question on this distinction.
> - **Always mention the governing ISO/ANSI standard** (e.g., ISO 9283 for performance testing, ISO 10218 for safety).
> - When listing datasheet parameters, group them under clear headings; bullet format is preferred over long prose.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Datasheet Parameter Interpretation and Wrist Loading
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled block diagram, explain the **hierarchical structure** of a typical industrial robot manufacturer's datasheet. Identify at least **six major blocks** and list the key parameters contained in each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 6-axis articulated robot datasheet lists the following wrist constraints: **maximum allowable wrist moment = 80 N·m** and **maximum allowable wrist inertia = 4 kg·m²**. The proposed gripper has a mass of 5 kg and the part to be handled has a mass of 8 kg. The centre of mass of the combined payload is offset **350 mm** from the wrist centre. Determine whether the application is safe. Assume $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hierarchical Datasheet Structure (7 Marks)**

The datasheet is organised into six major blocks (full block diagram already presented in Section 4.1 of these notes; the candidate should sketch it):

| Block | Key Parameters |
|-------|----------------|
| **1. Identification** | OEM, model, serial, year |
| **2. Performance** | Payload, reach, repeatability, cycle time |
| **3. Mechanical / Kinematic** | Per-axis range, speed, wrist moments, flange standard (ISO 9409-1) |
| **4. Electrical / Controller** | Power supply, controller type, I/O count, fieldbus |
| **5. Environmental / Compliance** | Temperature, humidity, **IP rating**, CE, ISO 10218, ANSI/RIA R15.06 |
| **6. Installation / Maintenance** | Footprint, mounting pattern, mass, CoG, cable routing, spares |

*(Award 1 mark per block well-explained; 1 mark for the diagram.)*

**(b) Wrist Loading Verification (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Total payload mass.**

$$m = m_{gripper} + m_{part} = 5 + 8 = 13 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Gravitational moment at the wrist.**

$$M = m \cdot g \cdot r = 13 \times 9.81 \times 0.350$$

$$M = 44.63 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with datasheet limit.**

$$M_{max} = 80 \text{ N·m} \quad \text{and} \quad M_{actual} = 44.63 \text{ N·m}$$

Since $44.63 < 80$, the moment constraint is **satisfied**. ✓

**Step 4 — Compute the wrist inertia.**

$$I = m \cdot r^2 = 13 \times (0.350)^2 = 13 \times 0.1225 = 1.5925 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Compare with datasheet inertia limit.**

$$I_{max} = 4 \text{ kg·m}^2 \quad \text{and} \quad I_{actual} = 1.5925 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

Since $1.5925 < 4$, the inertia constraint is **satisfied**. ✓

**Step 6 — Conclusion.**

The application is **safe** with respect to both the wrist moment and wrist inertia limits as defined in the manufacturer's datasheet. **(7 marks distributed across the 5 computational/conceptual steps plus the final conclusion.)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Repeatability Quantification and Standards
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Define repeatability and accuracy of an industrial robot. Explain the ISO 9283 test methodology used to derive the datasheet repeatability value, citing the governing equation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A robot is commanded to move to the same Cartesian pose **20 times**. The TCP coordinates recorded by the controller (in mm) are tabulated below. Compute the **cluster centroid**, the **mean radial deviation**, and the **quoted repeatability**. Comment on whether this is acceptable for a precision electronics assembly application that requires $\pm 0.05$ mm. **(7 Marks)**

| Trial | x (mm) | y (mm) | z (mm) |
|:-----:|:------:|:------:|:------:|
| 1 | 500.02 | 300.01 | 200.03 |
| 2 | 500.01 | 300.02 | 200.02 |
| 3 | 499.98 | 300.03 | 200.01 |
| 4 | 500.03 | 299.99 | 200.04 |
| 5 | 500.00 | 300.00 | 200.00 |

*(For brevity, only 5 of 20 trials shown in this model question; the full set in the exam paper will follow identical statistical trends.)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definitions and ISO 9283 (7 Marks)**

- **Repeatability (RP):** The closeness of agreement among repeated measurements of the same commanded pose under specified conditions. Expressed as $RP = \pm 3 \sqrt{s_x^2 + s_y^2 + s_z^2}$ per ISO 9283 (statistical variant), or the simpler mean-radial form quoted earlier.
- **Accuracy (AP):** The deviation between the **commanded pose** and the **mean of attained poses** over $N$ trials. Expressed as $AP = \sqrt{(\bar{x} - x_c)^2 + (\bar{y} - y_c)^2 + (\bar{z} - z_c)^2}$ where $(x_c, y_c, z_c)$ is the commanded pose.
- **ISO 9283 Test:** Specifies test conditions (payload = rated max, speed = 100 % rated, temperature = 20 ± 2 °C), the number of cycles (typically 30), and the bidirectional pose approach to eliminate backlash bias.

*(Award 3 marks for definitions, 2 marks for ISO 9283 explanation, 2 marks for the governing equation.)*

**(b) Numerical Repeatability Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Cluster centroid (using the 5 representative trials):**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{500.02+500.01+499.98+500.03+500.00}{5} = \frac{2500.04}{5} = 500.008 \text{ mm}$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{300.01+300.02+300.03+299.99+300.00}{5} = \frac{1503.05}{5} = 300.010 \text{ mm}$$

$$\bar{z} = \frac{200.03+200.02+200.01+200.04+200.00}{5} = \frac{1000.10}{5} = 200.020 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Per-trial Euclidean deviation:**

$$d_i = \sqrt{(x_i-\bar{x})^2 + (y_i-\bar{y})^2 + (z_i-\bar{z})^2}$$

| i | $x_i-\bar{x}$ | $y_i-\bar{y}$ | $z_i-\bar{z}$ | $d_i$ (mm) |
|---|--------------|--------------|--------------|-----------|
| 1 | +0.012 | 0.000 | +0.010 | 0.0156 |
| 2 | +0.002 | +0.010 | 0.000 | 0.0102 |
| 3 | −0.028 | +0.020 | −0.010 | 0.0358 |
| 4 | +0.022 | −0.020 | +0.020 | 0.0356 |
| 5 | −0.008 | −0.010 | −0.020 | 0.0234 |

**Step 3 — Mean radial deviation:**

$$\bar{d} = \frac{0.0156 + 0.0102 + 0.0358 + 0.0356 + 0.0234}{5} = \frac{0.1206}{5} = 0.0241 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Quoted datasheet repeatability:**

$$RP = \pm 0.024 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Comment on suitability:**

Since $RP = \pm 0.024$ mm is **less than** the application's requirement of $\pm 0.05$ mm, the robot is **acceptable** for this precision electronics assembly task. **(7 marks: 2 centroid + 3 deviation table/mean + 1 quoted value + 1 conclusion)**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **robot datasheet** is the OEM's authoritative engineering document — treat every value as a contractual limit under ISO 9283 test conditions.
- ✅ **Six major blocks** of a datasheet: Identification, Performance, Mechanical/Kinematic, Electrical/Controller, Environmental/Compliance, Installation/Maintenance.
- ✅ **Payload** = gripper + part + fixture mass. Always include the **gripper mass**; omitting it is a common error.
- ✅ **Reach** is the maximum radial distance from base centre to flange centre with the arm horizontal.
- ✅ **Repeatability** is a statistical measure of **return** to the same point; **accuracy** is the **deviation** from the commanded point. They are NOT the same.
- ✅ Repeatability is typically quoted in **mm or µm** and is computed from $N$ trials using cluster-centroid averaging.
- ✅ **Joint limits** are specified per axis — range (deg or mm), speed (deg/s or rad/s), and allowable wrist moment/inertia.
- ✅ **Wrist moment** $= m \cdot g \cdot r$ (N·m); **Wrist inertia** $= m \cdot r^2$ (kg·m²). Both must independently be within datasheet limits.
- ✅ **IP rating** (IEC 60529) uses two digits: solid ingress (0–6) + liquid ingress (0–9). IP54/IP65/IP67 are the common industrial ratings.
- ✅ **Compliance** references to memorise: ISO 10218-1/-2 (robot safety), ISO 13849-1 PL=d (safety circuits), ISO 9283 (performance testing), ISO 9409-1 (end-effector flange), ANSI/RIA R15.06 (US equivalent).
- ✅ **Cycle time** is workload-dependent; datasheet values are benchmarks under standard ISO 9283 payloads/paths — real-world cycle times may be 10–25 % higher.
- ✅ Always quote **units and standard test conditions** in board exam answers — missing units lead to automatic mark deductions.
- ✅ A datasheet is **not a guarantee of accuracy**; it is a guarantee of **repeatability** under stated conditions.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Contact and Non-Contact Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT865 - INDUSTRIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Contact and Non-Contact Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Industrial Robotics**, a **sensor** is a transduction device that detects a physical phenomenon (force, displacement, light, sound, magnetism, etc.) and converts it into a measurable electrical signal. The **2024 KTU scheme (PEEIT865 – Module 2)** classifies end-effector and environment sensors into two fundamental categories based on their **physical interaction principle** with the target object:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Contact Sensors** are devices that require **physical/mechanical touch** between the sensor element and the object being measured for detection to occur. The signal is generated only when there is direct mechanical coupling (pressure, deflection, strain, or electrical continuity through contact).
>
> **Non-Contact Sensors** are devices that detect the **presence, position, distance, or characteristics** of an object without any physical touch, using electromagnetic fields (inductive/capacitive), light (optical/photoelectric), sound waves (ultrasonic), or image processing (machine vision).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a **pitch-dark room** and you want to know whether a table is in front of you:

- **Contact Method (Blind man's stick):** You tap the room with a **white cane**. Only when the cane physically strikes the table do you "sense" it. You get reliable data but you may damage delicate objects, and your maximum sensing speed is slow. This is exactly how a **micro-switch or tactile sensor** works.

- **Non-Contact Method (Flashlight + Ears):** You shine a flashlight (optical sensor), send out a clap and listen for the echo (ultrasonic), or detect the metallic disturbance with your skin (inductive). No touch is needed — the object remains safe, sensing is **fast** (microseconds), and you can detect objects that are far away or moving rapidly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Engineering Metric:** Contact sensors are typically characterized by **Hysteresis (in %FS)**, **Repeatability (in mm)**, and **Actuation Force (in N)**, while non-contact sensors are rated by **Sensing Range (in mm)**, **Switching Frequency (in Hz)**, and **Response Time (in ms)**. In high-speed KTU exam problems, these are often quoted in **bold** units like **mm**, **Hz**, and **kHz**.

---

## 1.3 Classification Tree (At a Glance)

$$
\boxed{\text{Robot Sensors} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{Contact Sensors} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{Passive: Tactile arrays, Strain gauges} \\
\text{Active: Limit switches, Potentiometers}
\end{cases} \\[10pt]
\text{Non-Contact Sensors} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{Inductive} \\
\text{Capacitive} \\
\text{Photoelectric} \\
\text{Ultrasonic} \\
\text{Vision (CCD/CMOS)}
\end{cases}
\end{cases}}
$$

This binary split governs **robot gripper design, work-cell safety**, and **PLC integration logic** in modern **Industry 4.0 / IIoT** assembly lines.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Contact Sensors — Operating Principles

### 2.1.1 Mechanical Limit Switches & Micro-switches
- A **micro-switch** uses a pre-stressed spring mechanism.
- When a target physically deflects the actuator (plunger, lever, roller), the snap-action contacts close within a travel distance typically of **$\mathbf{0.1\text{–}1\ mm}$**.
- Operating force ranges from **$\mathbf{0.15\text{ N}}$ to $\mathbf{2\ N}$**.
- **KTU Key Term:** *Differential Travel* — the small distance between the *operating point* and the *releasing point*. It defines the **Hysteresis** $H$.

### 2.1.2 Tactile / Force Sensors
- Built from arrays of **conductive elastomers**, **piezoresistive polymers**, or **MEMS strain gauges**.
- Spatial resolution: **$\mathbf{1\text{–}10\ mm}$**.
- Used in robotic fingers for slip detection and gentle grasping of fragile objects.
- The resistance change is given by the piezoresistive relation.

### 2.1.3 Strain Gauge Force / Torque Sensor
- A strain gauge bonded to an elastic element changes its resistance $\Delta R$ when strained.
- Used in wrist force/torque sensors at the robot joint.
- **Gauge Factor** $G_F$ relates fractional resistance change to strain $\varepsilon$.

### 2.1.4 Potentiometric Position Sensor
- A wiper slides over a resistive element. The output is a voltage divider signal.
- Resolution is limited by wire-winding pitch (typically **$\mathbf{0.01\ mm}$**).

---

## 2.2 Non-Contact Sensors — Operating Principles

### 2.2.1 Inductive Proximity Sensor
- Generates a high-frequency **alternating electromagnetic field** ($f \approx 100\text{ kHz–1 MHz}$) via an LC oscillator coil.
- Eddy currents induced in a metallic target dissipate energy, dampening the oscillation amplitude.
- Detection occurs when the target enters the **rated operating distance** $s_r$.
- **Only detects metals**, immune to dust/water.
- Typical range: **$\mathbf{1\text{–}40\ mm}$**.

### 2.2.2 Capacitive Proximity Sensor
- Forms one plate of a capacitor with the target acting as the other plate.
- Detects **metals, plastics, liquids, powders, and wood**.
- Range: **$\mathbf{1\text{–}30\ mm}$**.
- Susceptible to humidity and temperature drift.

### 2.2.3 Photoelectric / Optical Sensor
Three modes:
1. **Diffuse Reflective** — Emitter + Receiver in one housing; detects light scattered from object.
2. **Retro-Reflective** — Uses a reflector; detects when the object *breaks* the beam.
3. **Through-Beam** — Separate Emitter + Receiver; highest range, up to **$\mathbf{50\ m}$**.

### 2.2.4 Ultrasonic Sensor
- Emits a **$\mathbf{40\text{ kHz}$** acoustic burst and measures the time-of-flight $\Delta t$ of the echo.
- Detection of clear, transparent, or mirrored objects (where optical fails).

### 2.2.5 Machine Vision (CCD/CMOS Camera)
- Digital image acquisition followed by edge detection, template matching, and blob analysis.
- Used for part identification, orientation, and quality inspection.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Sensor / Phenomenon | Governing Equation | Variables / Units | Key Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Strain Gauge (Gauge Factor)** | $G_F = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$ | $\varepsilon$ (strain, dimensionless); $G_F$ typically $\mathbf{2.0}$ for constantan | $\varepsilon \ll 1$ (small strain assumption) |
| 2 | **Hysteresis** | $H(\% FS) = \dfrac{x_{release} - x_{operate}}{x_{rated}} \times 100$ | $x$ in $\mathbf{mm}$ | $H \le 10\%$ for industrial-grade limit switches |
| 3 | **Ultrasonic Range** | $D = \dfrac{v \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | $v \approx 343 \text{ m/s}$ in air at $20^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $\Delta t \ge 2 D_{min} / v$ (dead-zone) |
| 4 | **Inductive Sensor Range** | $s_r = s_n \times k_{correction}$ | $s_n$ = nominal range; $k_{correction} \in [0.6, 1]$ for non-ferrous | $k_{correction} = 1.0$ for mild steel |
| 5 | **Capacitive Sensor Output** | $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \dfrac{A}{d}$ | $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$ | $d$ changes by $\Delta d \ll d$ |
| 6 | **Potentiometer Output** | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_x}{R_{total}}$ | $R_x$ = wiper resistance | Resolution $\le$ winding pitch |
| 7 | **Repeatability (KTU definition)** | $R_{rep} = \pm k \cdot \sigma$ | $k = 2$ for $95\%$ confidence | $\sigma$ = standard deviation of $N$ trials |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In **automotive welding lines** (e.g., KTU industrial visit case study), a hybrid arrangement is standard: **inductive sensors** confirm fixture presence, **photoelectric sensors** detect car-body arrival, and **tactile arrays** on grippers prevent crushing painted panels. This mix ensures both **speed (non-contact)** and **safety (contact confirmation)**.

---

## 2.4 The "Why and How" Logic Chain

- **Why use contact sensors?** → Highest **accuracy** at short range, immunity to surface color/reflectivity, ability to measure **force**, simple interfacing with PLC digital inputs.
- **Why use non-contact sensors?** → **Zero mechanical wear**, **very high switching frequency (up to $\mathbf{5\ kHz}$)**, **longer lifetime ($\mathbf{10^9}$ cycles)**, ability to detect **fragile / hot / moving / distant** targets.
- **How does the robot choose?** → Based on the **target material**, **required range**, **response time**, **environment (dust, oil, EMI)**, and **cost**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Ultrasonic Distance Measurement (Time-of-Flight)

The most common KTU numerical problem on non-contact sensors involves deriving distance from an ultrasonic echo.

**Given:** Speed of sound in air at room temperature, $v = 343 \text{ m/s}$. Time-of-flight measured by the sensor, $\Delta t = 6.0 \text{ ms}$.

**To Find:** Distance $D$ to the target object.

**Step 1 — Write the basic kinematic relation.**
The acoustic pulse travels from the transducer to the target and back. Total distance covered is $2D$, traversed in time $\Delta t$.

$$
2D = v \cdot \Delta t
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $D$.**

$$
D = \frac{v \cdot \Delta t}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**
Note: $\Delta t = 6.0 \text{ ms} = 6.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \frac{343 \text{ m/s} \times 6.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}}{2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2.058}{2} \text{ m} \\[6pt]
&= 1.029 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Express in mm (typical KTU unit).**

$$
D = 1029 \text{ mm} \approx 1.03 \text{ m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** Students often forget the factor of **2** and write $D = v \cdot \Delta t$. This is the most common 1-mark loss. Always state *"the wave travels to and fro"*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Hysteresis of a Limit Switch (Contact Sensor)

**Given:** A limit switch has:
- Operating point (NO contacts close): $x_{op} = 5.10 \text{ mm}$
- Release point (NO contacts open): $x_{rel} = 4.65 \text{ mm}$
- Rated travel (full stroke): $x_{rated} = 10.00 \text{ mm}$

**To Find:** Hysteresis $H$ in $\% \text{ FS}$ (percent of full scale).

**Step 1 — Compute the differential travel.**

$$
\Delta x = x_{rel} - x_{op} = 4.65 - 5.10 = -0.45 \text{ mm}
$$

Magnitude $\vert \Delta x \vert = 0.45 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 2 — Apply the hysteresis formula.**

$$
H(\% FS) = \frac{\vert x_{release} - x_{operate} \vert}{x_{rated}} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(\% FS) &= \frac{0.45 \text{ mm}}{10.00 \text{ mm}} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= 0.045 \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= 4.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation.**
Since $H = 4.5\% < 10\%$, the switch qualifies as an **industrial-grade** limit switch suitable for KTU-rated robotic end-stopper applications.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Strain Gauge Force Computation (Robot Wrist Sensor)

**Given:** A strain gauge with $G_F = 2.05$, $R = 350 \ \Omega$ is bonded to a steel cantilever of cross-section $A = 10 \text{ mm}^2$ and Young's modulus $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$. The measured resistance change is $\Delta R = 1.75 \ \Omega$.

**Step 1 — Compute strain $\varepsilon$.**

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta R / R}{G_F}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= \frac{1.75 / 350}{2.05} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{0.005}{2.05} \\[6pt]
&= 2.439 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute stress $\sigma$ using Hooke's law.**

$$
\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= 200 \times 10^{9} \text{ Pa} \times 2.439 \times 10^{-3} \\[6pt]
&= 487.8 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the applied force $F$.**

$$
F = \sigma \cdot A
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= 487.8 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \times 10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 \\[6pt]
&= 4878 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kgf for KTU-friendly reporting.**

$$
F \approx 4878 / 9.81 \approx 497 \text{ kgf}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Contact vs Non-Contact)

| Parameter | Contact Sensor | Non-Contact Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical Touch** | Required | Not required |
| **Lifetime** | $10^6 \text{–} 10^7$ cycles | $\mathbf{10^9}$ cycles |
| **Switching Frequency** | Up to **$\mathbf{200\ Hz}$** | Up to **$\mathbf{5\ kHz}$** |
| **Target Damage Risk** | Possible | **None** |
| **Harsh Environment Tolerance** | Medium | High (sealed) |
| **Cost per Unit** | Low (**INR 50–500**) | Medium–High (**INR 500–15000**) |
| **Typical Range** | $\mathbf{0\text{–}10\ mm}$ | $\mathbf{1\ mm\text{–}50\ m}$ |
| **Detects Material** | Almost all | Depends on type (metals-only for inductive) |
| **Repeatability** | $\pm 0.01 \text{ mm}$ | $\pm 0.1 \text{ mm}$ |
| **Response Time** | $\mathbf{1\text{–}10\ ms}$ | $\mathbf{0.1\text{–}1\ ms}$ |

---

## 3.5 Selection Algorithm (Pseudo-Logic Flow for Exam)

The KTU 2024 module often asks "justify your choice". Use this 4-step decision chain:

$$
\text{If target material} = \text{metal} \rightarrow \text{Inductive sensor}
$$

$$
\text{If target material} = \text{non-metal (plastic/liquid)} \rightarrow \text{Capacitive or Ultrasonic}
$$

$$
\text{If range} > 1\text{ m} \rightarrow \text{Photoelectric through-beam or Ultrasonic}
$$

$$
\text{If force/pressure feedback} \rightarrow \text{Piezoresistive Tactile/Strain Gauge}
$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between contact and non-contact sensors in a robotic system. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Contact Sensor | Non-Contact Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| Touch | Physical touch is mandatory | No physical touch |
| Wear & Tear | Yes, mechanical contact causes wear | Almost zero wear |
| Examples | **Micro-switch / Limit switch, Tactile sensor, Strain gauge, Potentiometer** | **Inductive proximity, Capacitive, Photoelectric, Ultrasonic, Machine Vision** |

*(1 mark each for clear differentiation along any one row + 1 mark for examples = 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any four non-contact sensors used in robotics. State the principle of operation of an inductive proximity sensor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Four non-contact sensors:
1. Inductive proximity sensor
2. Capacitive proximity sensor
3. Photoelectric sensor
4. Ultrasonic sensor

**Principle of an Inductive Sensor (2 marks):**
An inductive proximity sensor contains an **LC oscillator** that generates a high-frequency alternating electromagnetic field at the active face. When a **metallic target** enters this field, **eddy currents** are induced in the target, which absorb energy from the oscillator and cause a measurable drop in the oscillation amplitude. A trigger circuit detects this amplitude drop and switches the output transistor. *(Statement of the principle + mention of eddy currents + output switching = full marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> The KTU ESE pattern (2024 Scheme) requires **internal choice**. Both options are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Classify sensors used in industrial robotics. With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **photoelectric sensor**. Discuss its three modes of operation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ultrasonic sensor measures a time-of-flight of **$\Delta t = 12.4 \text{ ms}$** to a target. The ambient air temperature is **$35^{\circ}\text{C}$**. Calculate the distance to the target. Given that $v_0 = 331 \text{ m/s}$ at $0^{\circ}\text{C}$ and the temperature coefficient is **$0.6 \text{ m/s per } ^{\circ}\text{C}$**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Photoelectric Sensor (7 marks):**

**Classification (1 mark):**
$$
\text{Sensors} \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Internal} \\ \text{External} \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Contact} \\ \text{Non-Contact} \rightarrow \text{Photo, Inductive, Capacitive, Ultrasonic} \end{cases} \end{cases}
$$

**Construction (2 marks):**
A photoelectric sensor consists of an **emitter** (LED or laser diode), a **receiver** (photodiode or phototransistor), a **signal conditioning circuit**, and an **output switching stage**. The emitter generates modulated light (typically infrared) to reject ambient interference.

**Working (2 marks):**
The emitted light travels to the receiver either directly, after reflection from a target, or after reflection from a retro-reflector. When the light beam is interrupted or reflected (depending on mode), the receiver output changes state, triggering the switching transistor.

**Three Modes (2 marks):**
1. **Diffuse Reflective** — Object reflects light back to co-located receiver.
2. **Retro-Reflective** — Reflector is needed; object breaks the beam.
3. **Through-Beam** — Separate emitter and receiver; object breaks the beam. Longest range.

---

**(b) Ultrasonic Distance Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute speed of sound at $35^{\circ}\text{C}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= v_0 + 0.6 \cdot T \\
&= 331 + 0.6 \times 35 \\
&= 331 + 21 \\
&= 352 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the time-of-flight equation:**

$$
D = \frac{v \cdot \Delta t}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values (note $\Delta t = 12.4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \frac{352 \times 12.4 \times 10^{-3}}{2} \\
&= \frac{4.3648}{2} \\
&= 2.1824 \text{ m} \\
&\approx 2182 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**
The target is approximately **$\mathbf{2.18\ m}$** away from the sensor.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **tactile sensor** and a **strain gauge-based force sensor**. With a suitable diagram, describe a **robot wrist force/torque sensor**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A strain gauge with **$G_F = 2.1$** and nominal resistance **$R = 120 \ \Omega$** is bonded to a robot gripper link. When the gripper lifts a load, the resistance increases to **$R' = 120.50 \ \Omega$**. If the link has a cross-sectional area **$A = 25 \text{ mm}^2$** and Young's modulus **$E = 210 \text{ GPa}$**, determine:
(i) the strain produced,
(ii) the stress in the link,
(iii) the lifting force in Newtons. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Tactile + Strain Gauge Force Sensor (7 marks):**

**Tactile Sensor (2 marks):**
A tactile sensor is a contact sensor that detects the magnitude and distribution of pressure applied to its surface. The most common type uses a **conductive elastomer** whose resistance changes with applied pressure. Electrodes are arranged in a row-column matrix, and a scanning circuit reads each cell's resistance to build a **pressure map** of the contact area.

**Strain Gauge (2 marks):**
A strain gauge is a thin metallic foil pattern bonded to a flexible element. When the element deforms under load, the foil stretches or compresses, causing a fractional change in resistance $\Delta R / R$ proportional to strain $\varepsilon$. The gauge factor $G_F$ relates these quantities:
$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_F \cdot \varepsilon
$$

**Wrist Force/Torque Sensor Diagram (3 marks):**
A typical wrist sensor is a **cylindrical cross** with four strain gauges mounted on its beams. Forces $F_x, F_y, F_z$ and torques $T_x, T_y, T_z$ produce measurable strains, which a microprocessor resolves into the six force/torque components. (Block diagram from SECTION 4 can be redrawn here.)

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $G_F = 2.1$, $R = 120 \ \Omega$, $R' = 120.50 \ \Omega$, $A = 25 \text{ mm}^2 = 25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$, $E = 210 \text{ GPa}$.

**Step 1 — Compute $\Delta R$:**

$$
\Delta R = R' - R = 120.50 - 120.00 = 0.50 \ \Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Compute strain $\varepsilon$:**

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta R / R}{G_F}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= \frac{0.50 / 120}{2.1} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{0.004167}{2.1} \\[6pt]
&= 1.984 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute stress $\sigma$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= E \cdot \varepsilon \\
&= 210 \times 10^{9} \times 1.984 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 416.7 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute force $F$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \sigma \cdot A \\
&= 416.7 \times 10^{6} \times 25 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 10416.75 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The lifting force is approximately **$\mathbf{10.42\ kN}$** (or **$\mathbf{1062\ kgf}$**).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Contact vs Non-Contact Sensor Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in ultrasonic distance formula — costs **1–2 marks** every KTU exam cycle.
> 2. **Unit conversion mistakes:** Students often leave $\Delta t$ in ms without converting to seconds. Always write the unit conversion step explicitly.
> 3. **Confusing gauge factor $G_F$ with Poisson's ratio:** They are completely different quantities; do not interchange them.
> 4. **Mixing sensor types:** Writing "ultrasonic" when the question describes an *eddy current* principle. Read the working principle carefully.
> 5. **Skipping diagrams:** A KTU 14-mark answer without a diagram loses **2–3 marks**. Always redraw the block diagram from memory.
> 6. **Wrong hysteresis sign convention:** Use absolute value $\vert x_{rel} - x_{op} \vert$ when reporting $H$ in $\%FS$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Contact sensors **require physical touch**; non-contact sensors **do not**.
- **Limit switch:** Spring-loaded mechanical contact; key metric is **hysteresis**.
- **Tactile sensor:** Pressure map built from **row-column electrode matrix**.
- **Strain gauge:** $G_F = (\Delta R/R) / \varepsilon$; $G_F \approx \mathbf{2.0}$ for constantan.
- **Potentiometer:** Output is voltage divider ratio $V_{out}/V_{in} = R_x / R_{total}$.
- **Inductive sensor:** Works only on **metals**; uses **eddy currents** to dampen an LC oscillator.
- **Capacitive sensor:** Detects **metals + non-metals** (plastic, liquid, wood); uses $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A/d$.
- **Photoelectric modes:** Diffuse, Retro-reflective, Through-beam. Through-beam = longest range.
- **Ultrasonic range formula:** $D = v \cdot \Delta t / 2$, with $v = 331 + 0.6T$ in m/s.
- **Standard speed of sound at $20^{\circ}\text{C}$:** $v = 343 \text{ m/s}$ (memorize this value).
- **Machine Vision:** CCD/CMOS cameras + image processing for object identification.
- **Selection rule of thumb:** **Metal + short range → Inductive**; **Long range → Photo or Ultrasonic**; **Force → Strain gauge**; **Pressure map → Tactile**.
- **Lifetime order:** Non-contact (~$10^9$ cycles) **$\gg$** Contact (~$10^6$ cycles).
- **Always include units** in the final numerical answer (mm for distance, N for force, MPa for stress).
- **Always redraw the Mermaid block diagrams** from memory in the 14-mark answer to secure full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Position, Velocity, Acceleration, Proximity, and Force Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT865 - INDUSTRIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Position, Velocity, Acceleration, Proximity, and Force Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Robotic Sensors

In industrial robotics, sensors function as the **nervous system** of a robotic manipulator. They convert physical phenomena — such as displacement, motion, nearness, or applied load — into measurable electrical signals that the robot controller interprets. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEEIT865 (Module 2)**, sensors are broadly classified as **internal (proprioceptive)** and **external (exteroceptive)** sensors. The topic at hand covers a mix of both, primarily proprioceptive in nature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> A **robot sensor** is a device that detects a physical quantity (position, velocity, force, etc.) and converts it into a signal that can be read by the robot's controller. Sensors provide the feedback necessary for **closed-loop control**, **trajectory tracking**, and **safe human-robot interaction**.

---

## 1.2 Position Sensors — Intuitive Analogy

**Formal Definition:** Position sensors measure the linear or angular displacement of a robot joint or end-effector relative to a reference frame, outputting a signal proportional to the measured position.

**Real-World Analogy:**
Imagine you are blindfolded and asked to find a wall. You extend your hand and **touch** the wall to know *where* it is. The brain then converts that touch into a distance estimate. A position sensor works similarly — it **"touches"** a physical mark, scale, or magnetic field and tells the controller *where the robot joint currently is*. The most common examples are **potentiometers**, **encoders**, **LVDTs**, and **resolvers**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Categories of Position Sensors:**
> 1. **Contact type** — Potentiometers, microswitches
> 2. **Non-contact type** — Optical encoders, magnetic encoders, LVDT, resolvers

---

## 1.3 Velocity Sensors — Intuitive Analogy

**Formal Definition:** Velocity sensors measure the rate of change of position with respect to time (linear or angular), typically expressed in **m/s** or **rad/s**.

**Real-World Analogy:**
The **speedometer** in your car is a velocity sensor. It doesn't tell you *where* you are (that's GPS), nor *how fast you are accelerating* (that's an accelerometer) — it directly tells you *how fast* you are moving. In robots, **tachogenerators** and **pulse-counting encoders** perform this exact function, providing velocity feedback to the controller.

---

## 1.4 Acceleration Sensors — Intuitive Analogy

**Formal Definition:** Acceleration sensors (accelerometers) measure the rate of change of velocity with respect to time. They output a signal proportional to the **proper acceleration** experienced by the sensor (excluding gravitational acceleration).

**Real-World Analogy:**
When you sit in an accelerating car, you feel pushed back into your seat. Your inner ear and body's pressure receptors act as biological accelerometers, detecting *changes in motion*. A MEMS accelerometer in a robot works on the same principle — a tiny suspended mass shifts position when accelerated, and this shift is measured capacitively or piezoresistively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constant:** Standard gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface is **g ≈ 9.81 m/s²**. Accelerometer readings are often expressed in units of "g" for convenience.

---

## 1.5 Proximity Sensors — Intuitive Analogy

**Formal Definition:** Proximity sensors detect the presence or absence of an object within a defined sensing range *without physical contact*, using electromagnetic fields, light, sound, or capacitance.

**Real-World Analogy:**
Think of a bat using **echolocation** — it sends out sound waves and detects when they bounce back, knowing there's something nearby without touching it. Similarly, **ultrasonic** and **optical proximity sensors** emit waves/light and detect the reflection. **Inductive** and **capacitive** sensors emit electromagnetic fields and detect disturbances caused by nearby metallic or dielectric objects.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Proximity Sensors Matter in Robotics:**
> They enable **obstacle avoidance**, **object detection for pick-and-place**, **safety curtains** around robotic work cells, and **end-effector approach** during grasping tasks.

---

## 1.6 Force Sensors — Intuitive Analogy

**Formal Definition:** Force sensors measure the magnitude and direction of an applied force or torque, typically using the deformation principle of a strain element (strain gauge, piezoelectric crystal, or elastomer).

**Real-World Analogy:**
When you hold an egg, your fingers apply just the right amount of force — not too much (to crush it) and not too little (to drop it). Force sensors in a robot gripper do exactly the same thing. The **strain gauge-based force/torque sensor** at the robot wrist allows the controller to "feel" how hard the gripper is squeezing and adjust in real time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Metric:** Force is measured in **Newtons (N)** in SI units, and torque in **N·m**. Strain gauge sensors have a sensitivity typically in the range of **1–5 mV/V** (millivolts per volt of excitation) at full scale.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Position Sensors — Detailed Classification

### 2.1.1 Potentiometric Position Sensor

A **potentiometer** is a resistive element with a sliding wiper. As the joint rotates, the wiper moves along the resistive track, producing an output voltage proportional to the angular position.

**Operating Principle:**
- A reference voltage **V_ref** is applied across the resistive track.
- The output voltage at the wiper is divided in proportion to the wiper's position.

### 2.1.2 Optical Encoders

Optical encoders are the **most widely used position sensors in modern industrial robots**. They convert angular position into a digital pulse train.

**Types:**
1. **Incremental Encoder** — Outputs pulses as the disc rotates. Position is determined by *counting pulses* from a reference (home) position.
2. **Absolute Encoder** — Each angular position has a unique binary code (e.g., Gray code) on the disc, so position is known immediately upon power-up.

### 2.1.3 Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)

An **LVDT** is a passive inductive transducer used for linear displacement measurement. It consists of a primary coil and two secondary coils wound on a cylindrical former, with a movable ferromagnetic core.

**Operating Principle:**
- The primary is excited by an AC voltage.
- The differential output voltage **V_out = V_S1 − V_S2** varies linearly with the core's displacement from the null position.

### 2.1.4 Resolvers

A **resolver** is a robust electromagnetic position sensor used in harsh industrial environments. It is essentially a rotating transformer with two stator windings positioned 90° apart and one rotor winding.

- **Sine winding:** $V_s = V_0 \sin(\omega t) \sin(\theta)$
- **Cosine winding:** $V_c = V_0 \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta)$

The shaft angle **θ** is decoded from the ratio of these two outputs.

---

## 2.2 Velocity Sensors — Operating Principle

### 2.2.1 DC Tachogenerator

A DC tachogenerator is a small DC generator whose output voltage is directly proportional to the rotational speed of its shaft.

$$V_{out} = K_t \cdot \omega$$

where **K_t** is the tachogenerator constant (V·s/rad).

### 2.2.2 AC Tachogenerator

An AC (or induction) tachogenerator produces an AC voltage whose frequency is constant (equal to the excitation frequency) but whose **amplitude** is proportional to shaft speed. The rectified DC output is used as the feedback signal.

### 2.2.3 Encoder-based Velocity Measurement

Since encoders provide discrete pulses, velocity can be computed via two methods:
- **Pulse-counting method:** Count pulses over a fixed time interval → average velocity.
- **Pulse-timing method:** Measure the time between two consecutive pulses → instantaneous velocity.

---

## 2.3 Accelerometers — Operating Principle

### 2.3.1 Piezoelectric Accelerometer

When a piezoelectric crystal (e.g., quartz, PZT) is subjected to acceleration, the inertial mass exerts a force on it, generating a charge proportional to the applied force:

$$Q = d \cdot F = d \cdot m \cdot a$$

where:
- **Q** = generated charge (Coulombs)
- **d** = piezoelectric charge constant (C/N)
- **m** = seismic mass (kg)
- **a** = acceleration (m/s²)

### 2.3.2 MEMS Capacitive Accelerometer

A tiny proof mass suspended by flexure beams moves under acceleration, changing the gap between it and fixed capacitor plates. The resulting capacitance change is measured.

$$\Delta C = C_0 \cdot \frac{\Delta x}{d}$$

where **d** is the nominal gap and **Δx** is the mass displacement.

---

## 2.4 Proximity Sensors — Operating Principle

### 2.4.1 Inductive Proximity Sensor

An inductive proximity sensor consists of an **LC oscillator**, a **detection coil**, and a **trigger circuit**. When a metallic object enters the electromagnetic field, eddy currents are induced, damping the oscillation. This triggers the output.

**Sensing Range:** Typically **1–50 mm**, depending on target material and coil size.

### 2.4.2 Capacitive Proximity Sensor

A capacitive proximity sensor detects both metallic and non-metallic objects by measuring changes in capacitance between the sensor electrode and the target (which acts as the second plate of a capacitor, with air as the dielectric).

### 2.4.3 Optical Proximity Sensor

Two main types:
- **Through-beam** — Separate emitter and receiver; object breaks the beam.
- **Retro-reflective / Diffuse reflective** — Emitter and receiver in same housing; object reflects the beam.

### 2.4.4 Ultrasonic Proximity Sensor

Emits high-frequency sound waves (~40 kHz) and measures the **time of flight** of the echo:

$$d = \frac{v_{sound} \cdot t}{2}$$

where **v_sound ≈ 343 m/s** in air at 20°C.

---

## 2.5 Force Sensors — Operating Principle

### 2.5.1 Strain Gauge Load Cell

A strain gauge is a thin metallic foil pattern whose electrical resistance changes with applied strain:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$$

where:
- **G** = gauge factor (typically **2.0 for constantan**, ~2.1 for Karma alloy)
- **ε** = strain (ΔL/L)

Strain gauges are usually arranged in a **Wheatstone bridge** to convert small resistance changes into a measurable voltage output:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{4(1 + \Delta R/2R)}$$

For small strain, this simplifies to:

$$V_{out} \approx V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R}$$

### 2.5.2 Piezoelectric Force Sensor

Similar to the piezoelectric accelerometer, but optimized for quasi-static force measurement. Used in dynamic applications (impact, vibration) where strain gauges are too slow.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Variables / Units** |
|---|---|---|
| Position (Encoder) | $N = \theta / \theta_{res}$ | $N$ = pulses, $\theta$ = angle, $\theta_{res}$ = resolution |
| Resolution | $\theta_{res} = 360° / L$ | $L$ = lines per revolution |
| Encoder Velocity | $\omega = \frac{N_p}{L \cdot \Delta t}$ | $N_p$ = pulses counted |
| LVDT Output | $V_{out} = K_{LVDT} \cdot x$ | $x$ = core displacement |
| Tachogenerator | $V_{out} = K_t \cdot \omega$ | $K_t$ in V·s/rad |
| Accelerometer (Piezo) | $Q = d \cdot m \cdot a$ | $Q$ = charge, $d$ = piezo constant |
| Strain Gauge | $\Delta R/R = G \cdot \varepsilon$ | $G$ = gauge factor, $\varepsilon$ = strain |
| Wheatstone Bridge | $V_{out} \approx V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R / 4R$ | $V_{ex}$ = excitation |
| Ultrasonic Range | $d = v_{sound} \cdot t / 2$ | $v_{sound}$ = speed of sound |
| Resolver Output | $V_s, V_c = V_0 \sin(\omega t) \cdot \sin(\theta), \cos(\theta)$ | $\theta$ = shaft angle |
| Force from Strain | $F = \varepsilon \cdot E \cdot A$ | $E$ = Young's modulus, $A$ = area |
| Stress-Strain | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | $\sigma$ = stress (Pa) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Application:** In automotive welding robots, **resolver-based position feedback** is preferred because of its immunity to oil, dirt, and vibration. In precision CNC and cleanroom robotics, **optical encoders** dominate due to their high resolution.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Position Resolution of an Incremental Optical Encoder

### Problem Setup
An incremental optical encoder has a disc with **L = 2000 lines/revolution**. The encoder is mounted on a robot joint that rotates through **θ_total = 90°**. Determine:
1. The angular resolution per pulse.
2. The total number of pulses generated during the 90° rotation.
3. The linear displacement at a pinion of radius **r = 25 mm**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Angular Resolution per Pulse**

The encoder generates one pulse for each line on the disc that crosses the photodetector. The angular resolution is:

$$\theta_{res} = \frac{360°}{L} = \frac{360°}{2000} = 0.18°$$

This means the encoder can distinguish a minimum angular displacement of **0.18°** per pulse.

**Step 2 — Total Pulses for 90° Rotation**

$$N = \frac{\theta_{total}}{\theta_{res}} = \frac{90°}{0.18°} = 500 \text{ pulses}$$

**Step 3 — Linear Displacement at the Pinion**

$$s = r \cdot \theta_{total} \text{ (in radians)}$$

Converting 90° to radians:

$$\theta_{total} = 90° \times \frac{\pi}{180°} = \frac{\pi}{2} \approx 1.5708 \text{ rad}$$

$$s = 0.025 \text{ m} \times 1.5708 = 0.03927 \text{ m} = 39.27 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answer:**
- Angular resolution = **0.18°/pulse**
- Total pulses = **500**
- Linear displacement = **39.27 mm**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Strain Gauge Force Sensor Calculation

### Problem Setup
A robot gripper uses a cantilever beam with a strain gauge bonded to its surface. The beam has:
- Length **L = 100 mm**
- Cross-section: **b × h = 10 mm × 2 mm**
- Young's modulus **E = 200 GPa** (steel)
- Gauge factor **G = 2.0**
- Bridge excitation **V_ex = 5 V**
- Force applied at free end: **F = 50 N**

Find:
1. The bending stress at the gauge location.
2. The strain ε.
3. The fractional resistance change ΔR/R.
4. The bridge output voltage V_out.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Bending Moment and Stress**

The bending moment at the fixed end of a cantilever beam with a point load at the free end is:

$$M = F \cdot L = 50 \text{ N} \times 0.1 \text{ m} = 5 \text{ N·m}$$

The section modulus for a rectangular cross-section is:

$$Z = \frac{b \cdot h^2}{6} = \frac{0.010 \times (0.002)^2}{6} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-8}}{6} = 6.667 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^3$$

The maximum bending stress at the surface is:

$$\sigma = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{5}{6.667 \times 10^{-9}} = 7.5 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} = 750 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Strain Calculation**

Using Hooke's Law:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{7.5 \times 10^{8}}{200 \times 10^{9}} = 3.75 \times 10^{-3}$$

So the strain is **3750 microstrain (µε)**.

**Step 3 — Fractional Resistance Change**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon = 2.0 \times 3.75 \times 10^{-3} = 7.5 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Bridge Output Voltage**

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R} = 5 \times \frac{7.5 \times 10^{-3}}{4} = 9.375 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 9.375 \text{ mV}$$

**Final Answer:**
- Stress σ = **750 MPa**
- Strain ε = **3.75 × 10⁻³**
- ΔR/R = **7.5 × 10⁻³**
- V_out = **9.375 mV**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Ultrasonic Proximity Sensor Distance

### Problem Setup
An ultrasonic proximity sensor emits a 40 kHz pulse and detects the echo after **t = 8 ms**. Speed of sound in air at 20°C: **v = 343 m/s**. Find the distance to the object.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the time-of-flight formula**

$$d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$$

The factor of **2** accounts for the round-trip travel of the sound wave (to the object and back).

**Step 2 — Substitute values**

$$d = \frac{343 \times 0.008}{2} = \frac{2.744}{2} = 1.372 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:** Distance to object = **1.372 m**

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Piezoelectric Accelerometer Sensitivity

### Problem Setup
A piezoelectric accelerometer has:
- Seismic mass **m = 5 g = 0.005 kg**
- Piezoelectric constant **d = 2.3 × 10⁻¹² C/N** (PZT-5A)
- Capacitance of crystal **C = 1000 pF**

Determine:
1. Charge generated at acceleration **a = 10 g**.
2. Output voltage across the crystal.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the force on the mass**

The force experienced by the seismic mass is:

$$F = m \cdot a = 0.005 \times 10 \times 9.81 = 0.4905 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the charge**

$$Q = d \cdot F = 2.3 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.4905 = 1.128 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C} = 1.128 \text{ pC}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the output voltage**

Using the relation $Q = C \cdot V$:

$$V_{out} = \frac{Q}{C} = \frac{1.128 \times 10^{-12}}{1000 \times 10^{-12}} = 1.128 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 1.128 \text{ mV}$$

**Final Answer:**
- Q = **1.128 pC**
- V_out = **1.128 mV**

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Selection for Robotic Applications

| **Robot Application** | **Recommended Sensor** | **Justification** |
|---|---|---|
| Joint position feedback (slow, high torque) | Resolver | Rugged, immune to oil/dust |
| Joint position feedback (precision) | Optical encoder | High resolution, digital output |
| Wheel speed in mobile robot | DC tachogenerator / Encoder | Real-time velocity |
| Gripper contact detection | Force sensor (strain gauge) | Direct force feedback |
| Obstacle avoidance | Ultrasonic / IR proximity | Non-contact, range sensing |
| Wrist compliance in assembly | 6-DOF F/T sensor | Measures full wrench |
| Collision detection in cobots | Capacitive / current sensing | Detects unexpected contact |
| Vibration monitoring in machining robot | Piezoelectric accelerometer | High-frequency response |
| Linear slide position | LVDT | High accuracy, frictionless |
| Metal part detection on conveyor | Inductive proximity | Works only on metals, no false triggers |
| Picking varied materials (plastic, glass) | Capacitive proximity | Detects all dielectrics |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Robot Sensor Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS["ROBOTIC SENSORS"] --> nodeA["PROPRIOCEPTIVE SENSORS"]
    nodeS --> nodeB["EXTEROCEPTIVE SENSORS"]

    nodeA --> nodeC["POSITION SENSORS"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["VELOCITY SENSORS"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["ACCELERATION SENSORS"]
    nodeA --> nodeF["FORCE TORQUE SENSORS"]

    nodeC --> nodeC1["POTENTIOMETER"]
    nodeC --> nodeC2["OPTICAL ENCODER"]
    nodeC --> nodeC3["LVDT"]
    nodeC --> nodeC4["RESOLVER"]

    nodeD --> nodeD1["DC TACHOMETER"]
    nodeD --> nodeD2["AC TACHOMETER"]
    nodeD --> nodeD3["ENCODER DERIVED"]

    nodeE --> nodeE1["PIEZOELECTRIC ACCEL"]
    nodeE --> nodeE2["MEMS CAPACITIVE ACCEL"]
    nodeE --> nodeE3["STRAIN GAUGE ACCEL"]

    nodeF --> nodeF1["STRAIN GAUGE LOAD CELL"]
    nodeF --> nodeF2["PIEZOELECTRIC FORCE"]
    nodeF --> nodeF3["6DOF FORCE TORQUE"]

    nodeB --> nodeG["PROXIMITY SENSORS"]
    nodeG --> nodeG1["INDUCTIVE"]
    nodeG --> nodeG2["CAPACITIVE"]
    nodeG --> nodeG3["OPTICAL"]
    nodeG --> nodeG4["ULTRASONIC"]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Strain Gauge Wheatstone Bridge

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeV["Vex DC Source"] --> nodeR1["R1"]
    nodeV --> nodeR2["R2"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeN1["Node A"]
    nodeR2 --> nodeN2["Node B"]
    nodeN1 --> nodeRG["RG Strain Gauge"]
    nodeN2 --> nodeR3["R3"]
    nodeRG --> nodeGND["Ground"]
    nodeR3 --> nodeGND
    nodeN1 --> nodeOP["Vo Output"]
    nodeN2 --> nodeOP
    nodeOP --> nodeAMP["Amplifier"]
    nodeAMP --> nodeADC["ADC"]
    nodeADC --> nodeMC["Microcontroller"]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Proximity Sensor Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeT["TARGET OBJECT"] --> nodeEM["EMITTER BLOCK"]
    nodeEM --> nodeOSC["OSCILLATOR or PULSE GENERATOR"]
    nodeOSC --> nodeCOIL["COIL or TRANSDUCER"]
    nodeCOIL --> nodeMED["TRANSMISSION MEDIUM"]
    nodeMED --> nodeREF["REFLECTED or DISTURBED SIGNAL"]
    nodeREF --> nodeDET["DETECTOR"]
    nodeDET --> nodeCMP["COMPARATOR and THRESHOLD"]
    nodeCMP --> nodeOUT["DIGITAL or ANALOG OUTPUT"]
    nodeOUT --> nodeCON["ROBOT CONTROLLER INPUT"]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Force Sensor Signal Conditioning Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeF["APPLIED FORCE F"] --> nodeBS["BEAM / ELASTOMER STRUCTURE"]
    nodeBS --> nodeSG["STRAIN GAUGES WHEATSTONE BRIDGE"]
    nodeSG --> nodeEXC["EXCITATION Vex"]
    nodeSG --> nodeSIG["mV LEVEL OUTPUT SIGNAL"]
    nodeSIG --> nodeIA["INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER"]
    nodeIA --> nodeFL["LOW PASS FILTER"]
    nodeFL --> nodeADC["ADC"]
    nodeADC --> nodeDSP["DSP OR MCU"]
    nodeDSP --> nodeFB["FEEDBACK TO ROBOT CONTROLLER"]
```

---

## 4.5 Functional Architecture — Closed-Loop Robot Joint Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeREF["REFERENCE POSITION q_d"] --> nodeSUM["SUMMING JUNCTION"]
    nodeFB["FEEDBACK POSITION q_actual"] --> nodeSUM
    nodeSUM --> nodeERR["ERROR SIGNAL e"]
    nodeERR --> nodeCTRL["CONTROLLER PID"]
    nodeCTRL --> nodeDRV["MOTOR DRIVER"]
    nodeDRV --> nodeMOT["DC / SERVO MOTOR"]
    nodeMOT --> nodeJNT["ROBOT JOINT"]
    nodeJNT --> nodeENC["ENCODER POSITION SENSOR"]
    nodeENC --> nodeFB
    nodeJNT --> nodeTACH["TACHOMETER VELOCITY SENSOR"]
    nodeTACH --> nodeVFB["VELOCITY FEEDBACK"]
    nodeVFB --> nodeCTRL
```

---

## 4.6 Functional Architecture — 6-DOF Force/Torque Sensor at Wrist

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeWR["ROBOT WRIST FLANGE"] --> nodeFS["6DOF FORCE TORQUE SENSOR"]
    nodeFS --> nodeFS1["STRAIN GAUGES Fx Fy Fz"]
    nodeFS --> nodeFS2["STRAIN GAUGES Mx My Mz"]
    nodeFS1 --> nodeAMP1["MULTI-CHANNEL AMPLIFIER"]
    nodeFS2 --> nodeAMP1
    nodeAMP1 --> nodePROC["SIGNAL PROCESSOR MATRIX INVERSION"]
    nodePROC --> nodeOUT["6 COMPONENT WRIST Wrench OUTPUT"]
    nodeOUT --> nodeCC["COMPLIANCE CONTROLLER"]
    nodeCC --> nodeACT["ROBOT JOINT ACTUATORS"]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q:** Differentiate between incremental and absolute optical encoders used in robots.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Incremental Encoder** | **Absolute Encoder** |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Pulses (relative to start) | Unique binary code per position |
| Reference on power-up | Required (homing routine) | Not required (instant position) |
| Complexity | Simple disc with single track | Multi-track disc with Gray/binary code |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Use case | Speed measurement, light-duty joints | Heavy-duty robotic joints needing absolute position |

**Awarding marks:**
- 1 mark for correct definition of each type
- 1 mark for 2–3 differentiating points

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Q:** Explain the working principle of an LVDT used as a position sensor in robotics.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** consists of a primary coil and two secondary coils (S1 and S2) wound on a hollow cylindrical former. A ferromagnetic core slides inside the former.

1. The primary is excited by a sinusoidal AC voltage.
2. The two secondaries are connected in **series opposition** (differential connection).
3. When the core is at the **null (central) position**, the induced EMFs in S1 and S2 are equal and opposite, so the differential output V_out = 0.
4. As the core moves toward one secondary, the EMF in that secondary increases while the other decreases, producing a **linear output voltage proportional to displacement**.
5. The **direction** of motion is indicated by the **phase** of V_out with respect to the excitation.

**Awarding marks:** 1 mark each for construction, working, and direction-detection feature.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — 14 Marks

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **piezoelectric accelerometer**. Derive the expression for charge sensitivity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot manipulator joint uses a **resolver** for position feedback. Explain its construction, working, and how the shaft angle is decoded from the output signals. Show the relevant mathematical expressions. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**Construction and Working (4 Marks):**

A piezoelectric accelerometer consists of:
- A **piezoelectric crystal** (e.g., PZT, quartz) sandwiched between electrodes
- A **seismic mass (m)** on top of the crystal
- A **base** fixed to the vibrating structure whose acceleration is to be measured
- A **preamplifier (charge amplifier)** to convert the high-impedance charge output into a usable voltage

**Working Principle (3 Marks):**

When the accelerometer is subjected to acceleration **a**, the seismic mass experiences an inertial force:

$$F = m \cdot a$$

This force compresses the piezoelectric crystal (according to Newton's second law). Due to the piezoelectric effect, the crystal generates a charge proportional to the applied force:

$$Q = d \cdot F = d \cdot m \cdot a$$

The **charge sensitivity** is defined as charge per unit acceleration:

$$S_Q = \frac{Q}{a} = d \cdot m \quad (\text{C·s}^2/\text{m})$$

For voltage sensitivity, dividing by the crystal's capacitance **C** and amplifier gain **A**:

$$S_V = \frac{V_{out}}{a} = \frac{d \cdot m \cdot A}{C}$$

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Construction (2 Marks):**

A resolver is essentially a **rotating transformer** with:
- A **rotor winding** on the shaft, excited by an AC reference voltage
- Two **stator windings** oriented mechanically at 90° to each other (sine and cosine windings)

**Working Principle (3 Marks):**

The rotor is excited with:

$$V_r = V_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

The flux linking the two stator windings depends on the shaft angle θ. The induced EMFs are:

$$V_s = V_0 \sin(\omega t) \sin(\theta) \quad \text{(sine winding)}$$

$$V_c = V_0 \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta) \quad \text{(cosine winding)}$$

**Decoding the Angle (2 Marks):**

Dividing the two outputs after demodulation removes the carrier:

$$\tan(\theta) = \frac{V_s}{V_c}$$

$$\theta = \arctan\left(\frac{V_s}{V_c}\right)$$

This gives the absolute shaft angle in the range [0, 2π].

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – June 2024] — 14 Marks

**(a)** With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the working of a **Wheatstone bridge-based strain gauge force sensor**. Derive the bridge output voltage expression. A strain gauge with gauge factor **G = 2.1** is bonded to a steel cantilever. The strain experienced is **ε = 1000 µε**. Find the fractional change in resistance if the unstrained resistance is **R = 350 Ω**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain in detail the working of **inductive, capacitive, optical, and ultrasonic proximity sensors** with neat sketches. Compare their sensing ranges and typical applications in robotics. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Wheatstone Bridge Circuit and Working (3 Marks):**

A Wheatstone bridge consists of four resistors R1, R2, R3, R4 in a diamond configuration, excited by a DC voltage V_ex across opposite nodes. The output is taken across the other two nodes. A strain gauge typically replaces one or more arms (active gauges in tension/compression, dummy gauges for temperature compensation).

**Derivation of Output Voltage (2 Marks):**

When the active gauge changes by ΔR:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left[ \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4} - \frac{R_2}{R_1 + \Delta R + R_2} \right]$$

For a balanced bridge with all R equal, and assuming ΔR << R:

$$V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

**Numerical Calculation (2 Marks):**

Given: G = 2.1, ε = 1000 µε = 1000 × 10⁻⁶ = 10⁻³, R = 350 Ω

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon = 2.1 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\Delta R = 2.1 \times 10^{-3} \times 350 = 0.735 \text{ Ω}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta R = 0.735 \text{ Ω}}$$

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Inductive Proximity Sensor (2 Marks):**
- Uses an LC oscillator and detection coil.
- Eddy current losses in approaching metallic target damp the oscillation.
- Trigger circuit flips the output state.
- Range: 1–50 mm; used for metal part detection, end-of-travel limits.

**Capacitive Proximity Sensor (2 Marks):**
- The target and sensor electrode form a capacitor with air as dielectric.
- Distance change alters capacitance, detected by an oscillator circuit.
- Detects both metallic and non-metallic (plastic, wood, liquid) targets.
- Used for level sensing and material identification.

**Optical Proximity Sensor (1.5 Marks):**
- Emitter (LED/laser) and photodetector.
- Through-beam: object breaks beam; reflective: object returns beam.
- Used for precise object detection over longer ranges (mm to several m).

**Ultrasonic Proximity Sensor (1.5 Marks):**
- Piezoelectric transducer emits 40 kHz sound bursts.
- Measures time-of-flight of echo.
- Works on any material (sound-reflecting); immune to color/transparency.
- Used in mobile robots for obstacle avoidance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Students often **confuse LVDT with resolver** — remember LVDT is for **linear** displacement while resolver is for **rotary** angle.
> - In strain gauge problems, students forget to **convert microstrain (µε) to strain** before multiplying by gauge factor (1 µε = 10⁻⁶ strain). This is the most common 1-mark loss.
> - In ultrasonic sensor formulas, students **forget to divide by 2** for round-trip travel. Always include the factor of 2.
