> **Physical Interpretation:** At points inside the dexterous workspace, the robot has the full 6 degrees of freedom available (3 for position + 3 for orientation). At points in the reachable workspace but outside the dexterous workspace, the robot can reach the point but cannot arbitrarily orient the tool.

### 1.4 Solvability — When Does a Solution Exist?

A kinematic equation is **solvable** if and only if the desired end-effector pose $X_d$ lies within the manipulator's reachable workspace. The solvability check has three necessary conditions:

1. **Existence Condition:** $X_d \in \mathcal{W}_R$
2. **Reachability in Planar Manipulators:** For a planar 2R manipulator with link lengths $l_1, l_2$, the point $(x, y)$ is reachable iff:
$$\sqrt{x^2 + y^2} \leq l_1 + l_2 \quad \text{(outer boundary)}$$
$$|l_1 - l_2| \leq \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} \quad \text{(inner boundary)}$$
3. **Orientation Constraint:** Even if a point is reachable, the desired orientation may not be achievable — the full pose $X_d$ must be checked against the dexterous workspace if orientation is critical.

### 1.5 Solution Techniques — Taxonomy Overview

IK solutions are classified into two broad families:

| Method | Type | Speed | Accuracy | KTU Relevance |
|--------|------|-------|----------|---------------|
| **Closed-Form (Analytical)** | Algebraic / Geometric | Very fast (closed-form computation) | Exact | **Highest** — primary focus of Module 2 |
| **Numerical (Iterative)** | e.g., Newton-Raphson, Jacobian Transpose, CCD, FABRIK | Slower, iterative | Approximate, convergence issues | Secondary focus |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Focus:** The syllabus explicitly prioritizes **closed-form solutions** for the analytical method. Numerical techniques are typically deferred to advanced robotics or motion-planning modules.

### 1.6 Closed-Form Solution — Definition

A **closed-form solution** is an analytical expression for the joint variables in terms of the end-effector pose, obtained by directly manipulating the kinematic equations using algebraic substitution or geometric reasoning. It yields the **exact** solution in a fixed number of arithmetic operations — no iteration required.

> [!NOTE]
> **Existence Theorem (Pieper's Criterion):** A 6-DOF manipulator has a closed-form solution if three adjacent joint axes intersect at a common point (as in spherical wrists). This is why most industrial robots (PUMA, ABB, KUKA) are designed with **spherical wrist architectures** — to guarantee closed-form solvability.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Sub-problems of Inverse Kinematics

To systematically solve the IK problem, it is decomposed into **two sub-problems** by exploiting the kinematic structure:

**Sub-problem 1: Position Inverse Kinematics**
Determine the joint variables of the positioning sub-chain (typically the first 3 joints) that place the wrist center $P_c$ at the desired location.

$$P_c = X_d - d_6 \cdot R_d \cdot \hat{z}_6$$

Where $d_6$ is the wrist offset and $\hat{z}_6$ is the approach direction of the end-effector.

**Sub-problem 2: Orientation Inverse Kinematics**
Determine the joint variables of the spherical wrist (last 3 joints) that produce the desired orientation $R_d$.

$$R_{3}^{0}(q_1, q_2, q_3) \cdot R_{3}^{6}(q_4, q_5, q_6) = R_d$$

Solving for $q_4, q_5, q_6$ becomes trivial once $R_3^0$ is known, by premultiplying with its inverse.

### 2.2 The Pieper Approach — Systematic Closed-Form Solution

For a 6-DOF manipulator with the last three axes intersecting:

**Step 1:** Compute wrist center position from desired pose.
**Step 2:** Solve position IK for $(q_1, q_2, q_3)$ using geometric/algebraic methods.
**Step 3:** Compute $R_3^0$ from the found joint angles.
**Step 4:** Compute $R_3^6 = (R_3^0)^{-1} \cdot R_d$.
**Step 5:** Solve orientation IK for $(q_4, q_5, q_6)$ using Euler-angle-style extraction.

### 2.3 Solution Existence and Multiplicity

**Number of Solutions for 6-DOF Manipulators:**
- General 6-DOF: Up to **16 solutions** (algebraic degree is 16 in the half-angle tangent substitution).
- With spherical wrist: Up to **8 solutions**.
- Typical industrial 6R: 4 to 8 solutions (multiple elbow-up/elbow-down, shoulder-left/right, wrist-flip combinations).

**Why Multiple Solutions Exist:**

| Joint Configuration Choice | Number of Variants |
|---------------------------|-------------------|
| Elbow up / Elbow down | 2 |
| Shoulder left / Shoulder right | 2 |
| Wrist flip / No flip | 2 |
| **Total** | **8** |

### 2.4 The Two Closed-Form Methods

**Method A: Algebraic (Inverse Transform) Method**
- Premultiply the kinematic equation $T_6^0 = T_n^{n-1} \cdots T_1^0$ with successive inverses.
- Equate corresponding matrix elements to isolate one joint variable at a time.
- Use $\sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1$ to extract closed-form values via $\text{atan2}(y, x)$.

**Method B: Geometric Method**
- Decompose the manipulator into planar triangles.
- Apply the law of cosines to find joint angles.
- Highly intuitive; often yields the same result as Method A.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forward Kinematics | $X = K(q)$ | Given $q$, find $X$ |
| 2 | Inverse Kinematics | $q = K^{-1}(X)$ | Given $X$, find $q$ — generally non-unique |
| 3 | 2R Reachability | $\vert l_1 - l_2 \vert \leq \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} \leq l_1 + l_2$ | Outer = max reach; Inner = min reach |
| 4 | Workspace Volume (3R Spherical) | $V = \frac{4}{3} \pi r_{max}^3$ | For fully articulated spherical |
| 5 | Atan2 function | $\theta = \text{atan2}(y, x) = \arctan(y/x)$ with quadrant | Used to recover joint angle uniquely |
| 6 | Law of Cosines (2-link planar) | $c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab\cos C$ | Core of geometric method |
| 7 | Half-angle substitution | $t = \tan(\theta/2)$ | Converts $\sin, \cos$ to rational functions |
| 8 | Wrist-center equation | $P_c = P_{ee} - d_6 R \hat{a}$ | For spherical wrist architectures |
| 9 | Number of solutions (6R with spherical wrist) | $\leq 8$ | From algebraic-degree analysis |
| 10 | Existence of closed-form (Pieper) | Three adjacent axes intersect | Sufficient condition for 6-DOF |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Note:** All angles are in **radians** internally; degrees are used only for human display. Link lengths are in consistent length units (**meters** in SI; **millimeters** in KTU robotics lab practice).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Inverse kinematics is the **computational heart** of every industrial robot controller. Real-world applications include:

- **Welding Robots:** IK computed at every waypoint along a curved weld seam at rates of **100–250 Hz**.
- **Surgical Robots (e.g., Da Vinci):** Sub-millimeter precision IK with redundant solutions chosen to avoid collisions with patient anatomy.
- **CNC Machining:** Multi-axis IK for 5-axis tool paths to maintain tool orientation normal to curved surfaces.
- **Autonomous Manipulation (Humanoids):** Whole-body IK where multiple end-effector goals (e.g., holding a tray while walking) must be simultaneously satisfied.
- **Animatronics & Film:** Pre-computed IK keyframes drive character motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why It Matters in KTU Exams:** The closed-form IK solution is what determines whether a robot can be programmed *offline* (using software like RoboDK or ROS MoveIt) versus *online* (requiring teach pendants). Closed-form solvability makes offline programming feasible and is a critical industrial requirement.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Complete Derivation: 2-Link Planar Manipulator (Geometric Method)

Consider a 2R planar manipulator with link lengths $l_1$ and $l_2$. The end-effector position is given in the base frame by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= l_1 \cos\theta_1 + l_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\
y &= l_1 \sin\theta_1 + l_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Goal:** Given $(x, y)$, find $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$.

**Step 1: Apply the Law of Cosines to the triangle formed by the two links and the line from base to end-effector.**

Let $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$ be the distance from the base to the end-effector. The angle at the base (between link 1 and the line of length $r$) is denoted $\alpha$, and the interior angle at the elbow is $\beta$. By the Law of Cosines:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r^2 &= l_1^2 + l_2^2 - 2 l_1 l_2 \cos(180° - \theta_2) \\
r^2 &= l_1^2 + l_2^2 + 2 l_1 l_2 \cos\theta_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Solve for $\cos\theta_2$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos\theta_2 &= \frac{x^2 + y^2 - l_1^2 - l_2^2}{2 l_1 l_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Solve for $\sin\theta_2$ using the Pythagorean identity, accounting for both elbow-up and elbow-down branches.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin\theta_2 &= \pm \sqrt{1 - \cos^2\theta_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Recover $\theta_2$ using the two-argument arctangent.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_2 &= \text{atan2}\left(\pm\sqrt{1 - \cos^2\theta_2},\ \cos\theta_2\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

The $\pm$ sign gives the **two solution branches** (elbow-up and elbow-down).

**Step 5: Compute $\theta_1$ using the projection of $r$ onto link 1.**

Let $\phi = \text{atan2}(y, x)$ be the angle from the base x-axis to the target point. Let $\psi$ be the angle between link 1 and the line $r$ (computed using the Law of Sines):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin\psi &= \frac{l_2 \sin\theta_2}{r} \\
\psi &= \text{atan2}\left(l_2 \sin\theta_2,\ l_1 + l_2 \cos\theta_2\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Combine to obtain $\theta_1$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_1 &= \text{atan2}(y, x) - \text{atan2}\left(l_2 \sin\theta_2,\ l_1 + l_2 \cos\theta_2\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This gives **two valid solutions** for each elbow configuration, completing the closed-form solution for the 2R planar manipulator.

### 3.2 Complete Derivation: 3-Link Manipulator with Spherical Wrist (Pieper's Method)

Consider a manipulator with the first three joints forming a positioning chain and the last three forming a spherical wrist. The position of the wrist center is $P_c = (p_x, p_y, p_z)$ in the base frame, and the end-effector orientation is given by the rotation matrix $R_d$.

**Step 1: Compute the wrist center position.**

For a typical configuration where the end-effector has a final link offset $d_6$ along the tool z-axis:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_c &= P_{ee} - d_6 \cdot R_d \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Solve for $q_1$ (revolute joint about the base z-axis).**

Project the wrist center onto the xy-plane:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \sqrt{p_x^2 + p_y^2} \\
q_1 &= \text{atan2}(p_y, p_x) - \text{atan2}(d_2, \pm\sqrt{r^2 - d_2^2})
\end{aligned}
$$

The $\pm$ corresponds to the **shoulder-left/shoulder-right** configurations, giving **2 solutions** for $q_1$.

**Step 3: Solve for $q_3$ (elbow joint) using the projection onto the $q_1$-rotated plane.**

Define the projected coordinates:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= p_x \cos q_1 + p_y \sin q_1 - d_2 \\
h &= p_z - d_1
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply the Law of Cosines to find $\cos q_3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos q_3 &= \frac{s^2 + h^2 - a_2^2 - a_3^2}{2 a_2 a_3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $a_2$ and $a_3$ are the lengths of links 2 and 3 respectively. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_3 &= \text{atan2}\left(\pm\sqrt{1 - \cos^2 q_3},\ \cos q_3\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This adds another factor of **2 solutions**.

**Step 4: Solve for $q_2$ (shoulder joint) using both branches.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_2 &= \text{atan2}(s, h) - \text{atan2}\left(a_3 \sin q_3,\ a_2 + a_3 \cos q_3\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the orientation matrix $R_3^0(q_1, q_2, q_3)$ from the known values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^0 &= R_1^0(q_1) \cdot R_2^1(q_2) \cdot R_3^2(q_3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Extract the wrist orientation $R_3^6$ from the desired end-effector orientation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^6 &= (R_3^0)^T \cdot R_d
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Solve the spherical wrist for $(q_4, q_5, q_6)$.**

For a Z-Y-Z Euler angle wrist, the kinematic equation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^6 &= \begin{bmatrix} c_4 c_5 c_6 - s_4 s_6 & -c_4 c_5 s_6 - s_4 c_6 & c_4 s_5 \\ s_4 c_5 c_6 + c_4 s_6 & -s_4 c_5 s_6 + c_4 c_6 & s_4 s_5 \\ -s_5 c_6 & s_5 s_6 & c_5 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Equating elements:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_5 &= \text{atan2}\left(\pm\sqrt{r_{13}^2 + r_{23}^2},\ r_{33}\right) \\
q_4 &= \text{atan2}(r_{23}/s_5,\ r_{13}/s_5) \\
q_6 &= \text{atan2}(r_{32}/s_5,\ -r_{31}/s_5)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $r_{ij}$ denotes the $(i,j)$ element of $R_3^6$ and $s_5 = \sin q_5$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The complete 6-DOF closed-form IK yields **8 solutions** in general: 2 (shoulder) × 2 (elbow) × 2 (wrist) = 8. This matches the **algebraic-degree theorem** for 6R manipulators with a spherical wrist.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Solving Strategy for Common KTU Problems

| Manipulator Type | Method Used | Solutions | Difficulty |
|-----------------|-------------|-----------|------------|
| 2R planar | Geometric (cosine rule) | 2 | Easy |
| 3R planar | Algebraic + atan2 | 2 | Easy |
| RP planar | Direct algebraic | 1 | Trivial |
| 3R with spherical wrist (positioning) | Geometric (Pieper) | 2 | Medium |
| 6R with spherical wrist | Pieper decomposition | 8 | Hard |
| 6R general | No closed form in general | Up to 16 | Very Hard |
| SCARA (RRP) | Algebraic (2R + 1P) | 2 + 1 | Medium |
| Spherical (RRP) | Geometric | 2 | Medium |
| Cylindrical (RPP) | Direct | 1 | Trivial |
| Cartesian (PPP) | Direct | 1 | Trivial |

### 3.4 Worked Example: 2R Manipulator Numerical Solution

**Problem:** A 2R planar manipulator has $l_1 = l_2 = 1$ m. Find all joint angles to reach the point $(x, y) = (0.866, 1.5)$ m.

**Step 1: Compute $r$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \sqrt{0.866^2 + 1.5^2} = \sqrt{0.75 + 2.25} = \sqrt{3.0} \approx 1.732 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $\cos\theta_2$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos\theta_2 &= \frac{r^2 - l_1^2 - l_2^2}{2 l_1 l_2} = \frac{3.0 - 1 - 1}{2 \cdot 1 \cdot 1} = \frac{1.0}{2} = 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute $\sin\theta_2$ (both branches).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin\theta_2 &= \pm\sqrt{1 - 0.25} = \pm\sqrt{0.75} = \pm 0.866
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Recover $\theta_2$ using atan2.**

- Elbow-down: $\theta_2 = \text{atan2}(0.866, 0.5) = 60°$
- Elbow-up: $\theta_2 = \text{atan2}(-0.866, 0.5) = -60°$

**Step 5: Compute $\phi = \text{atan2}(y, x)$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi &= \text{atan2}(1.5, 0.866) = 60°
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Compute $\psi$ for each branch.**

For $\theta_2 = 60°$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\psi &= \text{atan2}\left(l_2 \sin\theta_2,\ l_1 + l_2 \cos\theta_2\right) \\
&= \text{atan2}(0.866, 1.5) = 30°
\end{aligned}
$$

For $\theta_2 = -60°$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\psi &= \text{atan2}(-0.866, 1.5) = -30°
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Compute $\theta_1$ for each branch.**

- Branch 1 (elbow-down): $\theta_1 = 60° - 30° = 30°$
- Branch 2 (elbow-up): $\theta_1 = 60° - (-30°) = 90°$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** Two valid solutions: $(\theta_1, \theta_2) = (30°, 60°)$ and $(90°, -60°)$. This illustrates the **two-fold multiplicity** inherent in 2R manipulators.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Define the inverse kinematic problem. Why is it considered more difficult than the forward kinematic problem?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The inverse kinematic problem is defined as the determination of the joint variables $(q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n)$ of a robotic manipulator given a desired end-effector pose (position and orientation) in Cartesian space.

It is more difficult than forward kinematics because:

1. **Non-linearity:** The kinematic equations $X = K(q)$ are transcendental (involving sines and cosines), so they cannot simply be inverted by matrix inversion.
2. **Non-uniqueness (Multiple Solutions):** Multiple distinct sets of joint angles can produce the same end-effector pose (e.g., elbow-up vs elbow-down). The IK solver must select one of up to 8 (for 6-DOF) solutions.
3. **No General Closed-Form Solution:** Closed-form solutions exist only when specific structural conditions are met (e.g., Pieper's criterion of three intersecting wrist axes).
4. **Singularities:** At singular configurations, the Jacobian loses rank and the IK problem may have infinite solutions (or no solution despite being inside the workspace).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*Differentiate between the reachable workspace and the dexterous workspace of a manipulator. Why is the dexterous workspace always a subset of the reachable workspace?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Reachable Workspace ($\mathcal{W}_R$) | Dexterous Workspace ($\mathcal{W}_D$) |
|--------|----------------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| Definition | All points reachable in **at least one** orientation | All points reachable in **all possible** orientations |
| Size | Larger | Smaller |
| Subset Property | $\mathcal{W}_D \subseteq \mathcal{W}_R$ | Strictly contained |

The dexterous workspace is a subset of the reachable workspace because **requiring all orientations is a stronger condition than requiring at least one orientation**. Any point that satisfies the stronger condition automatically satisfies the weaker one, but not vice versa. Mathematically, $\mathcal{W}_D = \mathcal{W}_R$ holds only for isotropic manipulators (e.g., 3R with orthogonal axes and equal link lengths), which is a special case.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A: Inverse Kinematics of 2R Planar Manipulator (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

A 2R planar manipulator has link lengths $l_1 = 4$ units and $l_2 = 3$ units.

**(a)** Derive the closed-form inverse kinematic solution for $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ given the end-effector position $(x, y)$ using the geometric method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the target position $(x, y) = (5, 2)$ units, compute all valid joint solutions and identify them as either *elbow-up* or *elbow-down*. Verify your solution using forward kinematics. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q-A (a) — Geometric Derivation (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Set up the geometry.** (1 mark)

The end-effector position is given by the forward kinematics:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= l_1 \cos\theta_1 + l_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\
y &= l_1 \sin\theta_1 + l_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

The distance $r$ from the base to the end-effector and the polar angle $\phi$ are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} \\
\phi &= \text{atan2}(y, x)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the Law of Cosines.** (2 marks)

In the triangle formed by link 1, link 2, and the line of length $r$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r^2 &= l_1^2 + l_2^2 - 2 l_1 l_2 \cos(180° - \theta_2) \\
\cos\theta_2 &= \frac{x^2 + y^2 - l_1^2 - l_2^2}{2 l_1 l_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Recover $\theta_2$ with atan2.** (2 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_2 &= \text{atan2}\left(\pm\sqrt{1 - \cos^2\theta_2},\ \cos\theta_2\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

The $\pm$ yields two solutions: **elbow-up** ($\theta_2 > 0$) and **elbow-down** ($\theta_2 < 0$).

**Step 4: Compute $\theta_1$.** (2 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_1 &= \text{atan2}(y, x) - \text{atan2}\left(l_2 \sin\theta_2,\ l_1 + l_2 \cos\theta_2\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This yields the closed-form solution with exactly **2 solutions** for the 2R planar manipulator.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q-A (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Compute $r$ and check reachability.** (1 mark)

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \sqrt{5^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{29} \approx 5.385 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

Check: $|l_1 - l_2| = 1 \leq 5.385 \leq 7 = l_1 + l_2$ ✓ Reachable.

**Step 2: Compute $\cos\theta_2$ and $\sin\theta_2$.** (2 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos\theta_2 &= \frac{29 - 16 - 9}{2 \cdot 4 \cdot 3} = \frac{4}{24} = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.1667 \\
\sin\theta_2 &= \pm\sqrt{1 - 1/36} = \pm\sqrt{35/36} = \pm\frac{\sqrt{35}}{6} \approx \pm 0.9860
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Find the two values of $\theta_2$.** (1 mark)

- Elbow-down: $\theta_2 = \text{atan2}(0.9860, 0.1667) \approx 80.41°$
- Elbow-up: $\theta_2 = \text{atan2}(-0.9860, 0.1667) \approx -80.41°$

**Step 4: Compute $\phi$ and the offset angles for each branch.** (2 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi &= \text{atan2}(2, 5) \approx 21.80°
\end{aligned}
$$

For $\theta_2 = 80.41°$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\psi &= \text{atan2}(3 \cdot 0.9860,\ 4 + 3 \cdot 0.1667) = \text{atan2}(2.958,\ 4.500) \approx 33.33° \\
\theta_1 &= 21.80° - 33.33° = -11.53°
\end{aligned}
$$

For $\theta_2 = -80.41°$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\psi &= \text{atan2}(-2.958,\ 4.500) \approx -33.33° \\
\theta_1 &= 21.80° - (-33.33°) = 55.13°
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Verify by forward kinematics.** (1 mark)

For $(\theta_1, \theta_2) = (-11.53°, 80.41°)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= 4\cos(-11.53°) + 3\cos(68.88°) = 4(0.980) + 3(0.359) = 3.919 + 1.078 = 4.997 \approx 5 ✓ \\
y &= 4\sin(-11.53°) + 3\sin(68.88°) = -0.799 + 2.798 = 1.999 \approx 2 ✓
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Solutions:** (i) $\theta_1 \approx -11.53°,\ \theta_2 \approx 80.41°$ (elbow-down); (ii) $\theta_1 \approx 55.13°,\ \theta_2 \approx -80.41°$ (elbow-up).

---

#### **Question B: Inverse Kinematics of 3R Spherical Wrist Manipulator (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

A 3R positioning manipulator (like a PUMA shoulder assembly) has DH parameters:
- $d_1 = 0.5$ m (vertical offset of shoulder)
- $a_2 = 0.4$ m (upper arm length)
- $a_3 = 0.3$ m (forearm length)
- $d_2 = 0$ (no shoulder offset)

**(a)** Set up the inverse kinematic equations to find $q_1$ from the wrist center position $(p_x, p_y, p_z)$. Show that **two solutions** exist for $q_1$ and identify the geometric meaning of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** After finding $q_1$, derive the closed-form expression for $q_3$ (elbow joint). Discuss the solvability criterion (when the solution exists) in terms of the workspace boundary. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q-B (a) — Solving for $q_1$ (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Express wrist center coordinates in base frame.** (1 mark)

The wrist center is the origin of frame 3 in the base frame. By forward kinematics of the first three joints:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_x &= (a_2 \cos q_2 + a_3 \cos(q_2 + q_3)) \cos q_1 \\
p_y &= (a_2 \cos q_2 + a_3 \cos(q_2 + q_3)) \sin q_1 \\
p_z &= d_1 + a_2 \sin q_2 + a_3 \sin(q_2 + q_3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Project onto xy-plane to isolate $q_1$.** (2 marks)

Let $r = \sqrt{p_x^2 + p_y^2}$ be the radial distance of the wrist center in the horizontal plane. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_1 &= \text{atan2}(p_y, p_x) - \text{atan2}\left(d_2, \pm\sqrt{r^2 - d_2^2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

For $d_2 = 0$, this simplifies to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_1 &= \text{atan2}(p_y, p_x) \quad \text{or} \quad q_1 = \text{atan2}(p_y, p_x) + 180°
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Identify the two solutions geometrically.** (2 marks)

- **Solution 1 ($q_1 = \text{atan2}(p_y, p_x)$):** The arm extends to the **front** of the base — this is the **shoulder-left/forward** configuration.
- **Solution 2 ($q_1 = \text{atan2}(p_y, p_x) + 180°$):** The arm extends to the **back** of the base — this is the **shoulder-right/backward** configuration.

**Step 4: Solvability condition for $q_1$.** (2 marks)

The solution exists iff $r^2 \geq d_2^2$ (i.e., $\sqrt{p_x^2 + p_y^2} \geq |d_2|$). If $d_2 = 0$, the solution exists for all $r \geq 0$, which corresponds to the geometric fact that the shoulder joint can always be oriented to face any point in the horizontal plane.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q-B (b) — Solving for $q_3$ (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Express the projection in the plane perpendicular to $q_1$.** (1 mark)

After $q_1$ is known, define:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= p_x \cos q_1 + p_y \sin q_1 \\
h &= p_z - d_1
\end{aligned}
$$

These represent the horizontal and vertical distances from the shoulder joint to the wrist center, measured in the plane containing link 2.

**Step 2: Apply Law of Cosines.** (2 marks)

The triangle in this plane has sides $a_2, a_3$, and $\sqrt{s^2 + h^2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s^2 + h^2 &= a_2^2 + a_3^2 + 2 a_2 a_3 \cos q_3
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $\cos q_3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos q_3 &= \frac{s^2 + h^2 - a_2^2 - a_3^2}{2 a_2 a_3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Recover $q_3$ with both branches.** (2 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_3 &= \text{atan2}\left(\pm\sqrt{1 - \cos^2 q_3},\ \cos q_3\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

The two solutions correspond to **elbow-up** and **elbow-down** configurations.

**Step 4: Solvability criterion.** (2 marks)

A real solution for $q_3$ exists iff $|\cos q_3| \leq 1$, i.e.:

$$
\begin{aligned}
|a_2^2 + a_3^2 - (s^2 + h^2)| &\leq 2 a_2 a_3 \\
|a_2 - a_3| &\leq \sqrt{s^2 + h^2} \leq a_2 + a_3
\end{aligned}
$$

This defines the **annular workspace** of the manipulator in the shoulder plane. The inner radius is $|a_2 - a_3|$ and the outer radius is $a_2 + a_3$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Note:** Combining the 2 solutions for $q_1$ and the 2 for $q_3$ gives 4 position solutions. $q_2$ is then determined uniquely from $q_1$ and $q_3$ for each pair, yielding a complete closed-form position solution.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\pm$ branch in $\sin\theta_2$:** Students often compute $\cos\theta_2$ and recover $\theta_2$ using only $\arccos$, which loses the **elbow-up/elbow-down** multiplicity and gives only ONE solution instead of TWO. (Lose 2-3 marks.)
>
> 2. **Confusing atan2 with atan:** Always use $\text{atan2}(y, x)$ (not just $\arctan(y/x)$) to recover joint angles. The 2-argument version preserves the correct **quadrant information**. (Lose 1-2 marks per occurrence.)
>
> 3. **Not verifying reachability:** Before attempting IK, you must check if the target is in the workspace. If you compute a solution with $|\cos\theta_2| > 1$, the point is **unreachable** and no real solution exists.
>
> 4. **Mixing up reachable vs dexterous workspace:** In KTU exams, the question often asks for the **volume** or **area** of one specifically. Read the wording carefully.
>
> 5. **Skipping the law of cosines derivation:** For 2R/3R IK, the law of cosines step is mandatory — don't jump directly to the final answer. (Lose 2 marks.)
>
> 6. **Pieper's criterion miss:** For 6-DOF IK, students often forget to mention **Pieper's criterion** (three adjacent axes intersect) as the enabling condition for closed-form solutions.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**
>
> - [ ] **IK Definition:** Given end-effector pose $X$, find joint variables $q = K^{-1}(X)$.
> - [ ] **Workspace Hierarchy:** $\mathcal{W}_D \subseteq \mathcal{W}_R$ — always, with equality only for special isotropic manipulators.
> - [ ] **2R Reachability:** $|l_1 - l_2| \leq \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} \leq l_1 + l_2$ — **must memorize.**
> - [ ] **Two closed-form methods:** Geometric (Law of Cosines) and Algebraic (Inverse Transform).
> - [ ] **Pieper's Criterion:** Three adjacent joint axes intersecting ⇒ closed-form solution exists for 6-DOF.
> - [ ] **Solution count:** 2R → 2 solutions; 6R with spherical wrist → up to 8 solutions.
> - [ ] **atan2 is mandatory:** Never use plain arctan in IK; it loses quadrant.
> - [ ] **Wrist center formula:** $P_c = P_{ee} - d_6 R_d \hat{z}_6$ for spherical-wrist architectures.
> - [ ] **Three solution sub-categories:** Shoulder left/right × Elbow up/down × Wrist flip/no-flip = 8.
> - [ ] **Numerical vs Closed-Form:** Numerical methods (Newton-Raphson, CCD) are slower, approximate, used when no closed-form exists.
> - [ ] **Singularities:** Make Jacobian rank-deficient; IK may have infinite or zero solutions.
> - [ ] **Use of $\pm$:** Always consider both branches for sin/cos to capture multiple configurations.
> - [ ] **Verification step:** Always plug solutions back into forward kinematics to confirm.
> - [ ] **Half-angle substitution** $t = \tan(\theta/2)$ converts IK to polynomial form, revealing max algebraic degree 16 for 6R.
> - [ ] **Real-world link:** Every industrial robot (ABB, KUKA, FANUC, Yaskawa) uses closed-form IK with spherical wrist design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Tip for KTU Exam Day:**
> When solving a 2R or 3R IK problem on the exam board, **always draw the geometry first** — label the link lengths, the polar angle $\phi$, the offset angle $\psi$, and the interior angle $\beta$. This visual scaffolding is worth 1-2 marks on its own in KTU's step-marking scheme, and it prevents algebraic errors. Then apply the Law of Cosines systematically. Most lost marks in IK come from skipping this geometric setup.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industrial Robot Installation and Commissioning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT865 - INDUSTRIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Industrial Robot Installation and Commissioning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Robot Installation and Commissioning** is the systematic, multi-stage engineering process of physically positioning, mechanically anchoring, electrically integrating, programmatically configuring, and functionally validating an industrial robot cell within a manufacturing environment, ensuring it meets specified performance, safety, and productivity benchmarks before being released for production.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this topic covers the **end-to-end deployment lifecycle** of a robotic manipulator — from the moment the robot crate arrives at the factory floor to the moment it performs its first certified production cycle. The term comprises two distinct yet interconnected phases:

- **Installation** — The *physical* and *electrical* integration of the robot into its operational environment (mounting, cabling, controller hookup, end-effector coupling).
- **Commissioning** — The *functional* and *programmatic* verification of the installed system (teach pendant calibration, I/O mapping, path tuning, safety zone testing, performance sign-off).

According to ISO 10218-1 and ISO 9283 standards (which KTU references under NEP 2020 outcome-based modules), commissioning is not merely "powering on" — it is a documented engineering procedure producing measurable **performance indices** such as pose accuracy, pose repeatability, and path accuracy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Bringing a New Employee to Your Factory Floor**

Think of robot installation and commissioning as **hiring and onboarding a highly skilled but very literal-minded new employee**.

- **Unpacking & Mounting** = Setting up their desk and chair in the right cubicle. If you place a precision worker in a vibrating, unlevel workstation, even the best worker makes mistakes.
- **Wiring & Power** = Plugging in their computer and giving them login credentials. The robot needs clean power, compressed air, network access, and signals from upstream machines (PLCs, conveyors).
- **Tool Calibration** = Teaching them how to hold a pen properly. The end-effector (gripper, welding torch) must be precisely aligned to the robot's tool center point (TCP).
- **Teach Pendant Programming** = Verbally walking them through their first task: "Pick here, move here, place there, with this much force."
- **Test Run & Validation** = Watching them do a dry run while you hold a clipboard and check their work. Only when they pass your checklist do you hand them the production keys.

Just as you would **not** throw a new employee directly into a critical production line on Day 1, you **must not** put a freshly installed robot into production without proper commissioning. The cost of skipping commissioning — a crashed spindle, a damaged gearbox, a workplace injury — far exceeds the investment of doing it right.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Highlighted

The following **key performance indicators** are evaluated during commissioning per **ISO 9283**:

> [!NOTE]
> - **Pose Accuracy (AP)** — Deviation between a commanded pose and the mean of attained poses. Measured in **mm**.
> - **Pose Repeatability (RP)** — Closeness of agreement between attained poses after repeated movements to the same command. Measured in **mm**.
> - **Path Accuracy (PA)** — Maximum deviation between the commanded path and the actual path at a specified velocity.
> - **Pose Stabilization Time** — Time required for the robot to settle within a specified tolerance band around the target pose.

These metrics are usually reported on a **manufacturer's test certificate** and must be **re-verified** post-installation to confirm no damage occurred during shipping or mounting.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Sequential Phases of Installation & Commissioning

The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to articulate installation and commissioning as a **sequential, gated engineering workflow**. Each phase has deliverables and approval gates.

### Phase 1 — Pre-Installation Site Survey

This phase occurs **before** the robot arrives and is the most commonly skipped phase in real industries (and the most commonly asked question in KTU exams).

**Activities:**
- Verify floor flatness (typically ≤ **3 mm/m** for heavy robots).
- Confirm foundation rigidity (concrete cured ≥ **28 days** for full strength).
- Check power supply (voltage stability, phase balance, harmonics).
- Map cable trays, air supply lines, and coolant routing.
- Calculate reach envelope clearance against ceiling beams, columns, and overhead cranes.

**Floor Flatness Check Equation:**

$$F = \frac{\Delta h}{L} \leq F_{max}$$

Where:
- $F$ = Measured floor flatness ratio
- $\Delta h$ = Height deviation across the measurement span
- $L$ = Measurement span (typically **1 m**)
- $F_{max}$ = Manufacturer-specified maximum (typically **3 mm/m** or **1/8 inch per foot**)

### Phase 2 — Mechanical Installation

**Activities:**
- Crane-lifting using the manufacturer's designated lift points.
- Bolt-down to foundation plate or pedestal.
- Torque application per manufacturer specification (critical for vibration damping).
- Mounting of end-effector and end-effector cabling.

**Bolt Torque Formula (Pre-load Calculation):**

$$T = k \cdot d \cdot F_p$$

Where:
- $T$ = Applied torque (**N·m**)
- $k$ = Nut factor (typically **0.20** for plain steel, **0.15** for plated)
- $d$ = Nominal bolt diameter (**mm**)
- $F_p$ = Desired pre-load force (**N**)

The number and grade of anchor bolts are typically specified by the robot manufacturer — a **6-axis 20 kg payload robot** often requires **4 × M16 grade 8.8 anchors** torqued to approximately **150 N·m** per bolt.

### Phase 3 — Electrical Integration

**Activities:**
- Mains power connection (typically **400V AC, 3-phase, 50 Hz** in India/Europe; **480V AC, 3-phase, 60 Hz** in North America).
- Controller cabinet placement (respecting the manufacturer's minimum clearance, usually **500 mm** rear and sides for cooling airflow).
- Connection of motor encoder cables, servo drive bus, and emergency stop (E-Stop) circuits.
- Fieldbus networking (PROFINET, EtherCAT, DeviceNet) to upstream PLCs.

**Cable Bend Radius Rule:**

$$R_{min} = k_c \cdot D_{cable}$$

Where:
- $R_{min}$ = Minimum static bend radius (**mm**)
- $k_c$ = Cable type factor (typically **6× for continuous-flex**, **4× for fixed**)
- $D_{cable}$ = Cable outer diameter (**mm**)

### Phase 4 — Software & Controller Commissioning

**Activities:**
- Controller boot-up and license activation.
- Motor phasing test (each axis is jogged individually to verify encoder polarity).
- Mastering (calibrating each axis to its mechanical zero using the manufacturer's home position marks).
- Tool Center Point (TCP) definition.
- Work object / base coordinate system definition.

**TCP Definition Math (4-Point Method):**

The TCP is computed by approaching a fixed reference point from four different robot orientations. The transformation matrix from the flange to the TCP is solved as:

$$T_{flange \to TCP} = \begin{bmatrix} R_{4x4} & p_{x,y,z} \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Where the orientation $R_{4x4}$ is derived from quaternion interpolation across the four calibration samples, and the position $p_{x,y,z}$ is the unique common point in flange coordinates.

### Phase 5 — Functional Verification & Performance Testing

**Activities:**
- Run all programmed paths at **10%, 25%, 50%, 75%, and 100%** of rated speed.
- Measure **pose accuracy and repeatability** using a laser tracker or coordinate measuring machine (CMM).
- Verify safety zones (light curtains, safety scanners, hard-guarded envelope).
- Confirm emergency stop response time (typically **≤ 250 ms** per ISO 13850).

**Repeatability Calculation (Statistical):**

$$RP = \bar{X} + 3\sigma$$

Where:
- $\bar{X}$ = Mean of attained pose deviations
- $\sigma$ = Standard deviation
- $3\sigma$ = The **3-sigma confidence interval** covering **99.73%** of measurements

### Phase 6 — Documentation & Handover

**Activities:**
- Compile the **Site Acceptance Test (SAT)** report.
- Record serial numbers, firmware versions, calibration certificates.
- Train operators and maintenance staff.
- Issue the **handover certificate** signed by both the vendor commissioning engineer and the customer's plant engineer.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Floor Flatness | $F = \Delta h / L$ | mm/m | ≤ 3 mm/m typical |
| Bolt Pre-load Torque | $T = k \cdot d \cdot F_p$ | N·m | k = 0.20 plain steel |
| Cable Min Bend Radius | $R_{min} = k_c \cdot D_{cable}$ | mm | 4× to 6× cable OD |
| Repeatability (3-sigma) | $RP = \bar{X} + 3\sigma$ | mm | Per ISO 9283 |
| Payload Margin (Safety Factor) | $SF = M_{max} / M_{work}$ | dimensionless | Recommended ≥ 1.5 |
| Workspace Reach Verification | $R_{req} \leq R_{max} \cdot \eta_{mount}$ | mm | η_mount ≈ 0.95 |
| Compressed Air Drop | $\Delta P = \frac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi d^4}$ | Pa | Hagen-Poiseuille |
| Servo Loop Bandwidth | $f_{BW} = \frac{v_{max}}{2 \pi \cdot e_{max}}$ | Hz | v_max = path velocity |
| Stopping Distance | $d_{stop} = v_0^2 / (2 a_{max})$ | m | a_max = decel |
| E-Stop Response | $t_{ES} \leq 0.25$ s | s | Per ISO 13850 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exams**: Memorize the four **ISO** standards that govern this topic:
> 1. **ISO 10218** — Safety requirements for industrial robots.
> 2. **ISO 9283** — Performance criteria and test methods.
> 3. **ISO 13850** — Emergency stop function.
> 4. **ISO 4413 / 4414** — Pneumatic / hydraulic safety.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In real manufacturing setups, poor installation and commissioning are responsible for approximately **30–40% of all robot downtime in the first year** (industry survey estimates from automotive body shops). Common failure modes include:

- **Foundation settling** → drift in TCP → weld seam misalignment.
- **Loose anchor bolts** → vibration → encoder fault trips.
- **Improper grounding** → electrical noise → I/O false triggers.
- **Skipped TCP calibration** → crash during first part load.

Proper commissioning is therefore not a bureaucratic checkbox — it is a **profitability safeguard** that pays for itself many times over in the first year of operation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bolt Pre-load Torque for Robot Foundation

A KTU examiner's favourite numerical problem asks: *Given a robot mass, base plate dimensions, and bolt count, compute the required pre-load torque.*

**Problem Statement:**
A 6-axis articulated robot with a total mass of **M = 850 kg** is to be bolted to a concrete pedestal using **n = 4** anchor bolts of diameter **d = 16 mm**. The bolt grade is **8.8** (yield strength = **640 N/mm²**). Compute the required tightening torque, given that a safety factor of **SF = 1.5** is to be maintained against bolt yield, and the nut factor is **k = 0.20**.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the dominant load on the foundation.**

The robot's own weight acts vertically downward at the center of the base plate. During dynamic operation, accelerations generate additional overturning moments. For the *static* pre-load check, we consider the worst-case vertical pull-out per bolt.

Total downward force from the robot:

$$F_{total} = M \cdot g = 850 \times 9.81$$

$$F_{total} = 8338.5 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 2 — Distribute the load across n anchor bolts.**

Assuming a uniform load distribution (static case, no overturning moment applied yet):

$$F_{bolt, static} = \frac{F_{total}}{n} = \frac{8338.5}{4}$$

$$F_{bolt, static} = 2084.6 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the safety factor to obtain design pre-load force.**

The pre-load force per bolt must be sufficient to clamp the base plate firmly. The recommended pre-load is typically **75% of the bolt's proof load** for permanent joints. However, using the safety factor approach requested in the problem:

$$F_{p} = SF \cdot F_{bolt, static} = 1.5 \times 2084.6$$

$$F_{p} = 3126.9 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the proof load of a single M16 grade 8.8 bolt.**

The proof stress for grade 8.8 is approximately **83% of ultimate tensile strength**. For an M16 bolt:

- Tensile stress area: $A_s \approx 157 \, \text{mm}^2$
- Proof load: $F_{proof} = 0.83 \times 800 \times 157 = 104,228 \, \text{N}$

This is well above the pre-load of **3126.9 N**, confirming the bolt is heavily over-designed for static load. The 8.8 grade selection is driven instead by **fatigue** and **seismic** considerations.

**Step 5 — Compute the tightening torque.**

Applying the torque-pre-load relationship:

$$T = k \cdot d \cdot F_p$$

$$T = 0.20 \times 16 \times 3126.9$$

$$T = 10005.9 \, \text{N·mm} = 10.0 \, \text{N·m}$$

**Step 6 — Sanity check against manufacturer recommendation.**

Most robot manufacturers specify torque values between **40 N·m and 80 N·m** for M16 grade 8.8 foundation bolts on a pedestal-mounted manipulator. Our computed **10.0 N·m** is far below this, indicating that **bolt pre-load is not the design driver** for this robot. The controlling factor is the **stiffness of the foundation** to prevent resonance and the **bending resistance** of the base plate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** Always state in your KTU answer whether your computed torque exceeds or falls below the manufacturer-recommended value. The two often differ by an order of magnitude because the formulas compute different quantities (pre-load vs. installation torque).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Common Commissioning Checks

| # | Check Item | Tool / Method | Acceptance Criterion | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Floor flatness | Precision level, laser | ≤ 3 mm per 1 m span | High |
| 2 | Anchor bolt torque | Calibrated torque wrench | Per OEM spec (40–80 N·m typical) | Very High |
| 3 | Mains voltage at cabinet | True RMS multimeter | ± 10% of nominal | High |
| 4 | Earth resistance | Earth tester | ≤ 1 Ω (industrial) | High |
| 5 | E-Stop response | Stop-time analyzer | ≤ 250 ms (ISO 13850) | Very High |
| 6 | Axis mastering | Teach pendant + dial gauge | Within encoder counts | Very High |
| 7 | TCP accuracy | 4-point or 6-D method | ≤ 0.5 mm deviation | Very High |
| 8 | Payload test | Rated load + 10% | No fault trip, no drift | High |
| 9 | Path accuracy | Laser tracker / ballbar | Per ISO 9283 table | Medium |
| 10 | Safety zone breach | Light curtain / scanner test | Triggers category-1 stop | High |
| 11 | Cooling airflow | Anemometer at vents | ≥ 0.5 m/s at controller intake | Low |
| 12 | Cable bend radius | Visual + template | Per cable spec | Low |

## 3.3 Derivation: Emergency Stop Stopping Distance

**Problem Statement:**
A robot axis is moving at **v₀ = 1.5 m/s** when an emergency stop is triggered. The maximum deceleration of the drive is **a_max = 8 m/s²**. Compute the stopping distance and verify it lies within the safeguarded space.

**Step 1 — Apply kinematic equation for constant deceleration.**

The robot's stopping distance from initial velocity to zero under constant deceleration is:

$$d_{stop} = \frac{v_0^2}{2 \cdot a_{max}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$d_{stop} = \frac{(1.5)^2}{2 \times 8}$$

$$d_{stop} = \frac{2.25}{16}$$

$$d_{stop} = 0.1406 \, \text{m} = 140.6 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Add the E-Stop circuit response delay contribution.**

The total stopping distance is the kinematic distance plus the distance traveled during the controller's response delay $t_{r}$ (typically 50 ms for servo drives):

$$d_{total} = v_0 \cdot t_r + \frac{v_0^2}{2 \cdot a_{max}}$$

$$d_{total} = 1.5 \times 0.05 + 0.1406$$

$$d_{total} = 0.075 + 0.1406 = 0.2156 \, \text{m} = 215.6 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 4 — Verify against safety standard.**

Per ISO 13850, the total stopping distance must be accounted for in the safety layout. The safeguarded space must be at least **d_total** away from the operator station or be guarded by devices that detect intrusion before the robot reaches the operator.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world adjustment**: For collaborative robots (cobots), the safety threshold is dramatically lower because the cobot must reduce its speed or stop upon contact. Power and Force Limiting (PFL) is verified using a **biomechanical gauge**, not a stop-time analyzer.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Installation & Commissioning Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram renders the full sequential workflow as a gated process, with parallel documentation activities flowing alongside the main path.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Kickoff and Scope Review] --> B[Pre-Installation Site Survey]
    B --> B1[Verify Floor Flatness]
    B --> B2[Confirm Power and Air Supply]
    B --> B3[Map Reach Envelope]
    B1 --> C{Site Approved?}
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C -- No --> C1[Issue Non-Conformance Report]
    C1 --> B
    C -- Yes --> D[Mechanical Installation]
    D --> D1[Crane Lift to Pedestal]
    D --> D2[Anchor Bolt Torquing]
    D --> D3[End Effector Mounting]
    D1 --> E[Electrical Integration]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> E1[Mains Power Connection]
    E --> E2[Controller Cabinet Setup]
    E --> E3[Fieldbus and I/O Wiring]
    E1 --> F[Software Commissioning]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> F1[Axis Mastering]
    F --> F2[TCP Calibration]
    F --> F3[Base Coordinate Setup]
    F1 --> G[Functional Verification]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> G1[Speed Sweep Test]
    G --> G2[Pose Accuracy Test]
    G --> G3[Safety Zone Test]
    G1 --> H{All Tests Pass?}
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H -- No --> H1[Loop Back to Commissioning]
    H1 --> F
    H -- Yes --> I[Documentation and Handover]
    I --> I1[Compile SAT Report]
    I --> I2[Train Operators]
    I --> I3[Sign Handover Certificate]
    I3 --> J[Robot Released to Production]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Commissioned Robot Cell

This diagram shows the **physical and logical interconnections** of every component that must be installed and validated during commissioning.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MECHANICAL_BLOCK
        P1[Pedestal and Foundation]
        P2[Robot Manipulator]
        P3[End Effector Tool]
    end
    subgraph POWER_BLOCK
        Q1[Three Phase Mains 400V AC]
        Q2[Isolation Transformer]
        Q3[Uninterruptible Power Supply UPS]
        Q4[Servo Drive Cabinet]
    end
    subgraph CONTROL_BLOCK
        R1[Robot Controller CPU]
        R2[Teach Pendant HMI]
        R3[Safety PLC]
    end
    subgraph COMM_BLOCK
        S1[PROFINET Fieldbus]
        S2[Cell PLC]
        S3[Vision System Camera]
        S4[Conveyor Encoder]
    end
    subgraph SAFETY_BLOCK
        T1[Light Curtain]
        T2[Safety Laser Scanner]
        T3[E-Stop Pushbutton]
        T4[Safety Relay]
    end
    P1 --- P2 --- P3
    Q1 --> Q2 --> Q3 --> Q4
    Q4 --> R1
    R1 <--> R2
    R1 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S1 --> S3
    S1 --> S4
    T1 --> T4
    T2 --> T4
    T3 --> T4
    T4 --> R3
    R3 --> R1
    S2 --> R1
```

## 4.3 TCP Calibration Geometry (4-Point Method)

The Mermaid diagram below illustrates the geometric principle of the four-point TCP calibration. The same TCP is approached from four distinct flange orientations, and the controller solves for the common point in flange coordinates.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Reference Point Fixed in Workspace] --> A1[Approach 1 Flange Orientation 0 deg]
    A0 --> A2[Approach 2 Flange Orientation 90 deg]
    A0 --> A3[Approach 3 Flange Orientation 180 deg]
    A0 --> A4[Approach 4 Flange Orientation 270 deg]
    A1 --> B1[Record Flange Pose P1]
    A2 --> B2[Record Flange Pose P2]
    A3 --> B3[Record Flange Pose P3]
    A4 --> B4[Record Flange Pose P4]
    B1 --> C[Controller Solves Linear System]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> D[Output TCP Translation and Orientation]
    D --> E[Save as TOOL DATA in Controller]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Differentiate between Installation and Commissioning of an industrial robot. Why are both considered mandatory under ISO 10218?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Installation** is the physical placement and electrical connection of the robot, its controller, cabling, and end-effector into the manufacturing cell. It is essentially *mechanical and electrical* in nature.
- **Commissioning** is the subsequent functional validation, including mastering, TCP calibration, I/O mapping, safety zone testing, and performance verification. It is *software, functional, and measurement-based*.
- Both are mandatory under **ISO 10218** because the standard requires a **documented risk assessment** and **performance verification** before a robot is released for production. Installation without commissioning leaves the safety and performance claims unvalidated.

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q2. List any four pre-installation site survey checks with their acceptance criteria.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Floor flatness** — measured with precision level; acceptance ≤ **3 mm per 1 m span**.
2. **Power supply voltage** — measured with true-RMS multimeter; acceptance within **±10% of nominal**.
3. **Foundation rigidity** — concrete cured ≥ **28 days**; compressive strength ≥ **25 N/mm²**.
4. **Reach envelope clearance** — verified against floor plan; minimum **300 mm** clearance to any structural obstruction.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the complete step-by-step procedure for **mechanical installation of an articulated robot** onto a factory floor, including foundation preparation, lifting, mounting, and bolt tightening. Highlight safety precautions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **TCP calibration procedure** using the four-point method. Include a labeled diagram, the mathematical formulation, and the typical accuracy achievable. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-Step Mechanical Installation (7 marks):**

1. **Foundation Preparation (1 mark):**
   - Pour concrete pedestal per OEM drawing, typically **300 mm above floor level**.
   - Allow **≥ 28 days** cure time.
   - Verify flatness with precision level (≤ **3 mm/m**).
   - Embed anchor bolts using template during pour, or drill-and-epoxy on existing floor (Hilti HIT-HY 200 or equivalent).

2. **Pre-Lift Inspection (1 mark):**
   - Inspect crate for transit damage; photograph any dents before unpacking.
   - Verify robot serial number on the crate matches the shipping documents.
   - Check that the robot is in **transport position** (joints locked or supported per OEM).
   - Identify OEM-marked lift points (usually **4 lift lugs** on the base or upper arm).

3. **Lifting and Placement (2 marks):**
   - Use **rated sling/chain** with minimum SWL **2× robot mass**.
   - Lift vertically; avoid swinging.
   - **Never** lift by the wrist or end-effector — risk of encoder damage.
   - Lower onto pedestal, align dowel pins, and insert **temporary support blocks** before bolt tightening.

4. **Anchor Bolt Torquing (2 marks):**
   - Use a **calibrated torque wrench**.
   - Apply torque in a **star (criss-cross) pattern** in 3 stages: **30%, 60%, 100%** of final value.
   - This prevents base plate distortion and uneven pre-load.
   - Final torque per OEM spec — typically **40–80 N·m** for M16 grade 8.8 bolts.

5. **Safety Precautions (1 mark):**
   - Barricade the lift zone; only rigger and signalman in the area.
   - Use **lockout-tagout (LOTO)** on all power sources before mechanical work.
   - Wear hard hat, steel-toe boots, gloves.
   - Keep crane operator in line of sight with the signalman at all times.

**(b) TCP Calibration — Four-Point Method (7 marks):**

1. **Concept (2 marks):**
   - The **Tool Center Point (TCP)** is the point on the end-effector that is to be positioned in space (e.g., the tip of a welding torch or the center of a gripper jaw).
   - In the four-point method, the same physical reference point is touched by the TCP from **four different flange orientations**.

2. **Procedure (2 marks):**
   - Mount a sharp reference pin in a fixed, rigid location in the workspace.
   - Using the teach pendant, jog the robot so that the TCP touches the pin in orientation 1 (e.g., flange vertical). Confirm and record pose.
   - Rotate the flange (e.g., by 90° about the wrist) and re-touch the same pin. Record pose 2.
   - Repeat for orientations 3 and 4 (typically **180°** and **270°**).
   - The controller solves the four equations for the unknown TCP position in flange coordinates.

3. **Mathematical Formulation (2 marks):**
   - Let $P_i$ be the position of the reference pin expressed in flange coordinates for orientation $i$. The TCP is the unique point $T$ such that $P_i = T$ for all $i$ in the absence of measurement noise.
   - In practice, the controller minimizes:
   $$\min_{T} \sum_{i=1}^{4} \vert P_i - T \vert^2$$
   - This yields the **least-squares TCP estimate**:
   $$T = \frac{1}{4} \sum_{i=1}^{4} P_i$$

4. **Achievable Accuracy (1 mark):**
   - With careful procedure, **4-point TCP** gives a TCP repeatability of approximately **±0.25 mm to ±0.5 mm**.
   - For higher accuracy, a **6-point or extended method** is used that also captures the tool orientation quaternion.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Discuss the **safety considerations** to be addressed during installation and commissioning as per **ISO 10218 and ISO 13850**. Include E-Stop circuit design, safeguarded space calculation, and risk assessment steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot manipulator has the following specifications: Reach **R = 1.45 m**, Payload **M = 12 kg**, Mass **W = 150 kg**. Compute (i) the required floor mounting torque for **n = 6** anchor bolts of **M12 grade 8.8**, given a safety factor of **2.0** and nut factor **k = 0.18**, and (ii) the stopping distance if the TCP is moving at **1.2 m/s** and the maximum deceleration is **6 m/s²**, with a controller response delay of **40 ms**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Safety Considerations (7 marks):**

1. **E-Stop Circuit Design (2 marks):**
   - Must be **hardwired**, not software-only, per ISO 13850.
   - Dual-channel architecture with **positively-driven contacts**.
   - **Category 0 or Category 1 stop** per IEC 60204-1; category 0 is uncontrolled, category 1 is controlled stop then power removal.
   - E-Stop pushbuttons must be **red mushroom-head on yellow background**, located at all operator stations.

2. **Safeguarded Space Calculation (2 marks):**
   - Defined as the volume enclosing the **maximum space** the robot can reach plus the **stopping distance** of all moving parts.
   - $V_{safe} = V_{reach} + V_{stop}$
   - All floor openings, walls, fences must lie outside this envelope.

3. **Risk Assessment (2 marks):**
   - Follow **ISO 12100** — three-step process: determine limits, identify hazards, estimate and evaluate risk.
   - Apply **ISO 10218-2** for the cell-level integration.
   - Document residual risk; apply hierarchy of controls: **eliminate → substitute → engineering control → administrative → PPE**.

4. **Other Considerations (1 mark):**
   - Reduced speed zones for maintenance.
   - Pendant-only programming in teach mode.
   - Three-position enabling device on teach pendant.

**(b) Numerical Computations (7 marks):**

**(i) Floor Mounting Torque (3.5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute total downward force:**

$$F_{total} = W \cdot g = 150 \times 9.81 = 1471.5 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 2 — Distribute across 6 bolts:**

$$F_{bolt} = \frac{1471.5}{6} = 245.25 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Apply safety factor:**

$$F_p = SF \cdot F_{bolt} = 2.0 \times 245.25 = 490.5 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 4 — Compute torque:**

$$T = k \cdot d \cdot F_p = 0.18 \times 12 \times 490.5 = 1059.5 \, \text{N·mm} = 1.06 \, \text{N·m}$$

**Step 5 — Reality check:** This computed value is far below the OEM-recommended torque (typically **25–35 N·m** for M12). The reason is that the **static weight is not the design driver** — dynamic forces, vibration, and overturning moments during operation are. Always defer to OEM torque specifications for actual installation.

**(ii) Stopping Distance (3.5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Kinematic stopping distance:**

$$d_{kin} = \frac{v_0^2}{2 \cdot a_{max}} = \frac{(1.2)^2}{2 \times 6} = \frac{1.44}{12} = 0.12 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Distance during controller response:**

$$d_{ctrl} = v_0 \cdot t_r = 1.2 \times 0.04 = 0.048 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Total stopping distance:**

$$d_{total} = d_{kin} + d_{ctrl} = 0.12 + 0.048 = 0.168 \, \text{m} = 168 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** The safeguarded space must extend at least **168 mm** beyond the maximum reach envelope to ensure operator safety during emergency stops.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "static weight torque" with "OEM installation torque."** A common mistake is to compute one and present it as the other. The OEM torque is typically an order of magnitude higher because it is derived from fatigue and vibration analysis, not from static weight.
> 2. **Do NOT forget the controller response delay** $t_r$ in the stopping distance calculation. Skipping it costs you 2 marks.
> 3. **Do NOT describe mastering and TCP calibration as the same step.** Mastering is calibrating each *axis* to its mechanical zero; TCP calibration is defining the *tool* in flange coordinates. Examiners specifically look for this distinction.
> 4. **Always cite the ISO standard number** (e.g., ISO 10218, ISO 9283, ISO 13850) when answering safety or performance questions. Marks are explicitly allocated for standard references under KTU 2024 OBE rubrics.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Industrial Robot Installation and Commissioning**

- **Installation ≠ Commissioning.** Installation is physical/electrical; commissioning is functional/programmatic.
- The process has **six sequential phases** — site survey, mechanical, electrical, software, verification, handover. Each has a **gate** before proceeding.
- **Floor flatness ≤ 3 mm/m** is the most common pre-installation check; non-flat floors cause TCP drift over time.
- **Anchor bolts** are torqued in a **star pattern** in **three stages** (30%, 60%, 100%) to prevent plate distortion.
- **Bolt torque formula:** $T = k \cdot d \cdot F_p$ with $k \approx 0.20$ for plain steel.
- **Cable bend radius** must respect $R_{min} = k_c \cdot D_{cable}$ — typically **4× to 6×** the cable diameter.
- **Mastering** aligns each axis encoder to its mechanical zero; it is **mandatory** after any joint disturbance.
- **TCP calibration** is done by the **4-point** (position only) or **6-point** (position + orientation) method.
- **Pose accuracy (AP)** and **pose repeatability (RP)** are defined in **ISO 9283**; repeatability is reported as $\bar{X} + 3\sigma$.
- **E-Stop response time** must be **≤ 250 ms** per ISO 13850.
- **Stopping distance** $d_{total} = v_0 \cdot t_r + \frac{v_0^2}{2 a_{max}}$ — remember both terms.
- **Safety circuit must be hardwired**, not software-based; **dual-channel** with positively-driven contacts.
- **Commissioning documentation** includes the **Site Acceptance Test (SAT) report**, calibration certificates, and the signed **handover certificate**.
- The **three ISO standards** to memorize cold: **ISO 10218** (safety), **ISO 9283** (performance), **ISO 13850** (E-stop).
- **Skipping commissioning costs 30–40% of first-year robot downtime** — use this statistic in essay questions to demonstrate industry awareness.
- **Power margin** — never run a robot at 100% of rated payload continuously; maintain a **safety factor ≥ 1.5**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mastering a Robot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT865 - INDUSTRIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mastering a Robot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robot Mastering** is the precise procedure by which the mechanical zero reference position of each individual joint (axis) of an industrial robot is aligned and synchronized with the logical (software-defined) zero position stored in the robot's motion controller. It establishes a known, repeatable datum point so that all subsequent motion commands produce the intended Cartesian trajectory within the robot's working envelope.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, mastering falls under the broader category of **Robot Calibration, Commissioning, and Accuracy Enhancement**, and is treated as a foundational step that must be completed before any trajectory planning, path execution, or kinematic transformation can be trusted for production use.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Calibrating a Weighing Scale"**
> Imagine a traditional analog weighing scale that has been moved across a room. Before you can trust its readings, you must first remove all weight and rotate the dial until the needle points to **zero**. That "zeroing" action is precisely what mastering does for a robot.
> 
> Without mastering:
> - The robot **thinks** its arm is at 45°, but the **actual** mechanical arm is at 47°.
> - It will reach the wrong physical point, miss the part, and fail the quality check.
> 
> With mastering:
> - The controller's internal counter and the real joint angle agree exactly.
> - Every subsequent "move to point P" command is geometrically true.

## 1.3 Why Is Mastering Necessary? — Physical Justification

Robotic joints use **gear reduction** (harmonic drives, RV reducers, planetary gears) with ratios ranging from **50:1** to **200:1**. This creates three unavoidable problems:

1. **Backlash** — The minute clearance between gear teeth creates a dead zone where motor rotation does not produce joint motion. Typical backlash: **0.05° to 0.20°**.
2. **Mechanical Tolerance Stack-up** — Manufacturing imperfections in links, housings, and bearing seats accumulate across all six joints.
3. **Loss of Encoder Reference on Power-Down** — When the controller powers off, the relative position of motor encoders to the link is unknown until re-mastered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Industry Metric:** Industrial robots typically achieve **repeatability of ±0.02 mm to ±0.10 mm**, but **absolute accuracy of ±1.0 mm to ±3.0 mm**. Mastering is the bridge that pushes accuracy closer to repeatability.

## 1.4 Standards and Reference Metrics

- **ISO 9283** — Specifies performance criteria including accuracy, repeatability, and mastering-related tests.
- **Repeatability (RP)** — Closeness of agreement of repeated position measurements under identical conditions.
- **Accuracy (AP)** — Closeness of the attained position to the commanded position.
- **Resolution** — Smallest incremental motion the controller can command (often 0.001° per joint).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Categories of Mastering

Robot mastering is broadly classified into two main operational categories:

### A. **Mastering (Single-Axis Datum Setting)**
- Aligns each joint's encoder counter to a known mechanical reference.
- Performed joint-by-joint.
- Required after every power cycle, battery replacement, or mechanical disturbance.

### B. **Master-and-Calibrate (Full Kinematic Calibration)**
- A more advanced procedure that corrects for **all kinematic parameter errors** simultaneously.
- Includes:
  - Link length errors
  - Joint offset errors
  - Axis misalignment errors
  - Gear ratio errors
  - Tool center point (TCP) errors
- Uses external measurement systems (laser tracker, coordinate measuring machine, theodolites).

## 2.2 Methods of Mastering (Detailed)

### 1. **Hard-Stop Mastering**
- Each joint is driven against a **mechanical stop** that physically constrains the link at a known angle.
- A torque-limit or current-limit in the motor detects contact.
- The controller records this position as the new zero.
- *Limitation:* Induces mechanical stress; cannot be done at high frequency.

### 2. **Micro-Switch / Limit-Switch Mastering**
- A spring-loaded micro-switch is mounted at a known joint angle.
- The joint is jogged until the switch trips; this event is the reference pulse.
- Cost-effective but contact-based and prone to wear.

### 3. **Optical Mastering**
- A slotted disk is mounted on the motor shaft (before the gearbox).
- An **optical interrupter** (LED + phototransistor) detects the slot edge.
- The slot edge corresponds to a precise mechanical reference.
- *Non-contact*, so it has the longest service life.

### 4. **Hall-Effect / Magnetic Mastering**
- A permanent magnet and Hall-effect sensor detect a magnetic field peak at the reference position.
- Common in collaborative robots (cobots) for compact design.

### 5. **Absolute Encoder Mastering**
- Uses multi-turn absolute encoders that retain position even when powered off.
- Mastering is needed only once during commissioning.
- Provides the highest positional integrity for premium applications.

### 6. **Software / Preset Mastering**
- Master values are typed manually into the controller based on a known reference measurement.
- Used for partial corrections or for axes already at known positions.

## 2.3 The Mastering Process — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Power Up** the controller in mastering mode (safety-reduced speed, typically **≤ 250 mm/s**).
2. **Select Axis** to be mastered (J1, J2, J3, J4, J5, or J6).
3. **Drive Axis** toward its mechanical reference using jog controls.
4. **Detect Reference** via sensor signal (optical pulse, switch closure, hard-stop current spike).
5. **Latch Encoder** — controller stores the reference count value (e.g., `Mastering_Count[J2] = 124583`).
6. **Verify** by commanding small angular increments and reading the encoder back.
7. **Repeat** for all six joints in sequence.
8. **Save** the mastering values to non-volatile memory.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Table for Robot Mastering & Calibration**

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Variable Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|--------------------|------------------|-------|
| 1 | Position Error | $e = q_{commanded} - q_{actual}$ | Joint position error | rad or deg |
| 2 | Encoder Resolution | $\theta_{res} = \dfrac{360°}{N \cdot G}$ | $N$ = encoder lines, $G$ = gear ratio | degrees |
| 3 | Repeatability | $RP = \pm 3\sigma$ | Standard deviation of positions | mm |
| 4 | Accuracy | $AP = \vert P_{commanded} - P_{attained} \vert$ | Vector difference | mm |
| 5 | Kinematic Error (DH) | $\Delta x = J(\theta) \cdot \Delta \theta$ | Jacobian-based error | mm |
| 6 | Tool Center Point (TCP) | $P_{TCP} = P_{flange} + R \cdot P_{tool}$ | TCP in world frame | mm |
| 7 | Master Offset | $q_{true} = q_{raw} + \delta_{master}$ | $\delta_{master}$ = master correction | rad |
| 8 | Backlash Compensation | $q_{corrected} = q_{cmd} + b \cdot \text{sgn}(\dot{q})$ | $b$ = backlash half-width | rad |
| 9 | Absolute Accuracy Improvement | $A_{calib} \approx \dfrac{A_{uncalib}}{k}$ | $k$ = calibration gain (typically 5–20) | — |
| 10 | Thermal Drift | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L_0 \cdot \Delta T$ | $\alpha$ = thermal expansion coeff. | mm |

**Boundary Conditions / Constraints for Mastering:**
- Ambient temperature: **15 °C to 35 °C**
- Joint speed during mastering: **≤ 5% of rated speed**
- Robot must be in **non-singular configuration** (avoid wrist lock and shoulder alignment)
- Payload must be **zero** (no tool or part attached)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive welding lines** — Mastering every 8 hours ensures weld seam accuracy on car bodies.
- **Aerospace drilling** — Master-and-calibrate on a 7-axis drilling robot enables hole accuracy of ±0.1 mm on aluminum panels.
- **Semiconductor wafer handling** — Absolute encoders with one-time mastering maintain micron-level accuracy in cleanrooms.
- **Collaborative robots** — Auto-mastering on power-up enables rapid redeployment between tasks.

## 2.6 Why and How of the Master-and-Calibrate Process

- **Why:** Mechanical manufacturing tolerances mean that the robot's *actual* kinematic parameters differ from the *nominal* parameters programmed into the controller. This produces Cartesian errors of 2–5 mm even when individual joints are accurate to 0.01°.
- **How:** A laser tracker measures the TCP at 30–50 poses distributed throughout the workspace. An optimization algorithm (Levenberg–Marquardt) minimizes the position residuals by adjusting the 24–48 kinematic parameters of the modified Denavit–Hartenberg model.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Joint Error Propagation to Cartesian Space

The relationship between a small joint error $\Delta \theta$ and the resulting Cartesian error $\Delta x$ is fundamental to mastering.

### Given:
- $J(\theta)$ is the **geometric Jacobian** of the robot, a 6×n matrix for an n-axis robot.
- $\Delta \theta$ is the vector of small joint errors (radians).
- $\Delta x$ is the resulting Cartesian error (mm or m).

### Step 1 — Start with the basic differential motion equation

$$
\Delta x = J(\theta) \cdot \Delta \theta
$$

This equation states that a small change in joint space, when multiplied by the Jacobian, gives the resulting change in Cartesian space.

### Step 2 — Decompose for a single joint

For joint $i$ only, with the remaining joints at fixed configuration:

$$
\Delta x_i = J_i(\theta) \cdot \Delta \theta_i
$$

where $J_i$ is the $i$-th column of the Jacobian.

### Step 3 — Calculate the magnitude of Cartesian error

Taking the Euclidean norm of both sides:

$$
\vert \Delta x_i \vert = \vert J_i(\theta) \vert \cdot \vert \Delta \theta_i \vert
$$

For a six-axis robot, the columns of the Jacobian have units of length (m) for the linear part and dimensionless for the angular part.

### Step 4 — Apply to the entire error vector

For simultaneous errors in all six joints:

$$
\Delta x_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{6} J_i(\theta) \cdot \Delta \theta_i
$$

The total error magnitude is bounded by:

$$
\vert \Delta x_{total} \vert \leq \sum_{i=1}^{6} \vert J_i(\theta) \vert \cdot \vert \Delta \theta_i \vert
$$

### Engineering Interpretation
> [!NOTE]
> This is why mastering the **base joints (J1, J2, J3)** — which have long Jacobian columns — is **more critical** than mastering the **wrist joints (J4, J5, J6)**. A 0.05° error in J1 can shift the TCP by several millimeters, while the same error in J6 may shift it by less than 0.5 mm.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Encoder Resolution After Gear Reduction

The angular resolution of the joint (as seen by the world) depends on the encoder resolution and the gear ratio.

### Given:
- $N$ = number of encoder lines per revolution (lines/rev)
- $G$ = gear reduction ratio (dimensionless)
- $\theta_{motor}$ = motor shaft angle

### Step 1 — Write the motor-side resolution

The motor can resolve its own angle to:

$$
\theta_{motor,res} = \frac{360°}{N}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the gear reduction

Since the joint angle is the motor angle divided by the gear ratio:

$$
\theta_{joint} = \frac{\theta_{motor}}{G}
$$

The joint resolution becomes:

$$
\theta_{joint,res} = \frac{360°}{N \cdot G}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to radians

$$
\theta_{joint,res} = \frac{2\pi}{N \cdot G} \quad \text{(radians)}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute typical values

For an encoder with $N = 10{,}000$ lines and gear ratio $G = 100$:

$$
\theta_{joint,res} = \frac{2\pi}{10{,}000 \times 100} = 6.2832 \times 10^{-6} \text{ rad}
$$

Converting to arcseconds:

$$
\theta_{joint,res} = 6.2832 \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{180°}{\pi} \times 3600 = 1.296 \text{ arcsec}
$$

This is the smallest commanded motion at the joint.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Repeatability vs. Accuracy Relationship

Repeatability is statistically tied to the standard deviation of measured positions.

### Given:
- $n$ repeated measurements of a commanded point $P_c$
- $P_i$ = $i$-th measured position
- $\bar{P}$ = mean position

### Step 1 — Compute the mean position

$$
\bar{P} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the standard deviation (per ISO 9283 convention)

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_i - \bar{P})^2}
$$

### Step 3 — Define repeatability

ISO 9283 defines repeatability as three standard deviations:

$$
RP = \pm 3\sigma
$$

### Step 4 — Define accuracy

$$
AP = \vert P_c - \bar{P} \vert
$$

### Step 5 — Geometric relationship

The three quantities form a triangle:

$$
d_{max} = AP + RP
$$

where $d_{max}$ is the radius of the smallest sphere containing all measurements.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why mastering improves accuracy but not repeatability:** Mastering reduces the systematic error (accuracy offset), but it does not change the random scatter (repeatability) caused by backlash and friction.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Backlash Compensation Model

### Given:
- $b$ = half-width of the backlash dead zone
- $\dot{q}$ = joint velocity
- $\text{sgn}(\dot{q})$ = sign function (+1 forward, -1 reverse)

### Compensation law:

$$
q_{actual} = 
\begin{cases}
q_{cmd} & \text{if } \dot{q} > 0 \text{ (forward motion)} \\
q_{cmd} - 2b & \text{if } \dot{q} < 0 \text{ (reverse motion)} \\
q_{last} & \text{if } \dot{q} = 0 \text{ (stationary)}
\end{cases}
$$

### Unified form:

$$
q_{actual} = q_{cmd} - b \cdot [1 - \text{sgn}(\dot{q})]
$$

This ensures that on direction reversal, the controller accounts for the lost motion in the gear teeth.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mastering Trade-offs

| Method | Accuracy | Repeatability | Wear | Cost | Maintenance Interval |
|--------|----------|---------------|------|------|----------------------|
| Hard-stop | Medium | High | High | Low | Every shift |
| Micro-switch | Medium | Medium | High | Low | Weekly |
| Optical | High | Very High | None | Medium | Monthly (cleaning) |
| Hall-effect | Medium | High | None | Low | Quarterly |
| Absolute encoder | Very High | Very High | None | High | Annual |
| Master-and-calibrate | Highest | Highest | None | Highest | Weekly (drift) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Mastering Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Power On Controller] --> nodeB[Enter Mastering Mode]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Select Axis Jn}
    nodeC --> nodeD[Enable Reduced Speed Mode]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Jog Axis Toward Reference]
    nodeE --> nodeF{Reference Signal Detected}
    nodeF -- No --> nodeE
    nodeF -- Yes --> nodeG[Latch Encoder Counter Value]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Store Master Offset in NVM]
    nodeH --> nodeI{All Six Axes Mastered?}
    nodeI -- No --> nodeC
    nodeI -- Yes --> nodeJ[Exit Mastering Mode]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Run Verification Routine]
    nodeK --> nodeL{Verification Pass?}
    nodeL -- No --> nodeB
    nodeL -- Yes --> nodeM[Robot Ready for Production]
```

## 4.2 Mastering Methods Comparison Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeRoot[Robot Mastering Methods] --> nodeB1[Contact Based]
    nodeRoot --> nodeB2[Non Contact Based]
    nodeRoot --> nodeB3[Software Based]
    nodeB1 --> nodeC1[Hard Stop]
    nodeB1 --> nodeC2[Micro Switch]
    nodeB2 --> nodeC3[Optical Encoder]
    nodeB2 --> nodeC4[Hall Effect Sensor]
    nodeB2 --> nodeC5[Absolute Encoder]
    nodeB3 --> nodeC6[Preset Master Values]
    nodeB3 --> nodeC7[Master and Calibrate]
```

## 4.3 Master-and-Calibrate Procedure Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Mount Laser Tracker] --> nodeB[Define Measurement Poses]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Command Robot to Pose 1]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Record TCP Position]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Repeat for 30 to 50 Poses]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Compute Residual Errors]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Apply Levenberg Marquardt Optimization]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Update Kinematic Parameters]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Verify Improved Accuracy]
    nodeI -- Pass --> nodeJ[Save Calibration to Controller]
    nodeI -- Fail --> nodeF
```

## 4.4 Error Source Hierarchy in an Industrial Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeRoot[Total Cartesian Error] --> nodeA1[Geometric Errors 80 percent]
    nodeRoot --> nodeA2[Non Geometric Errors 20 percent]
    nodeA1 --> nodeB1[Link Length Error]
    nodeA1 --> nodeB2[Joint Offset Error]
    nodeA1 --> nodeB3[Axis Misalignment]
    nodeA2 --> nodeC1[Backlash]
    nodeA2 --> nodeC2[Thermal Expansion]
    nodeA2 --> nodeC3[Encoder Quantization]
    nodeA2 --> nodeC4[Mechanical Compliance]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The above Mermaid diagrams render a hierarchical, functional view of the mastering process. The percentages shown for error source hierarchy are typical values for general-purpose six-axis robots; the exact distribution varies by manufacturer and payload class.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q1]**
> **Q1. Define robot mastering. Why is it necessary before any production run?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Robot mastering is the process of aligning each robot joint's mechanical reference position with the logical zero position stored in the motion controller. It is necessary because **(i)** gear reduction introduces backlash, **(ii)** mechanical tolerances cause the actual joint angle to differ from the encoder reading, and **(iii)** the controller loses absolute position reference on every power cycle. Without mastering, commanded Cartesian positions can be off by several millimeters, causing failed pick-and-place, missed welds, or collision events.

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q2]**
