$$\mathbf{u}_e \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2\sin\theta_e}\begin{bmatrix} R_{32} - R_{23} \\ R_{13} - R_{31} \\ R_{21} - R_{12} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Reconstruct via Rodrigues' formula (for verification).**

$$R_e^{\text{recon}} \;=\; I_{3} + \sin\theta_e\,[\mathbf{u}_e]_{\times} + (1 - \cos\theta_e)\,[\mathbf{u}_e]_{\times}^{2}$$

If $R_e^{\text{recon}} \approx R_e$ (within numerical tolerance), the axis-angle extraction is correct.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Linearized Identification of Kinematic Errors

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the kinematic parameter error vector.**

Let the robot be modeled by a set of $k$ geometric parameters stacked in a vector:

$$\boldsymbol{\phi} \;=\; [\,d_1,\,a_2,\,\alpha_2,\,d_3,\,\dots\,]^T \;\in\; \mathbb{R}^{k}$$

The true parameters are $\boldsymbol{\phi}_{\text{true}} = \boldsymbol{\phi}_{\text{nom}} + \Delta\boldsymbol{\phi}$, where $\Delta\boldsymbol{\phi}$ is what we want to identify.

**Step 2 — Linearize the orientation error with respect to parameter errors.**

For each commanded pose $i$ (with joint vector $\boldsymbol{q}_i$), the orientation error in the world frame is approximated to first order:

$$\delta\boldsymbol{\theta}_i \;\approx\; \frac{\partial R_{W}^{T}}{\partial \boldsymbol{\phi}}\bigg|_{\boldsymbol{\phi}_{\text{nom}}, \boldsymbol{q}_i} \cdot \Delta\boldsymbol{\phi}$$

Define the **identification Jacobian row** for pose $i$:

$$J_i \;\triangleq\; \left.\frac{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}_i}{\partial \boldsymbol{\phi}}\right|_{\text{nom}}$$

Then:

$$\delta\boldsymbol{\theta}_i \;=\; J_i \cdot \Delta\boldsymbol{\phi}$$

**Step 3 — Stack measurements for $N$ poses.**

For $N$ measurement poses, we obtain an overdetermined linear system:

$$\underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} J_1 \\ J_2 \\ \vdots \\ J_N \end{bmatrix}}_{J \in \mathbb{R}^{3N \times k}} \cdot \Delta\boldsymbol{\phi} \;=\; \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} \delta\boldsymbol{\theta}_1 \\ \delta\boldsymbol{\theta}_2 \\ \vdots \\ \delta\boldsymbol{\theta}_N \end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^{3N}}$$

**Step 4 — Solve via least-squares (pseudo-inverse).**

$$\boxed{\;\Delta\boldsymbol{\phi} \;=\; \left(J^{T}J\right)^{-1} J^{T}\,\mathbf{b} \;=\; J^{\dagger}\,\mathbf{b}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Iterative refinement (Levenberg–Marquardt extension, if needed).**

For nonlinear residuals, the linear estimate is refined by iterating:

$$\Delta\boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k+1)} \;=\; \Delta\boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)} + \left(J_{k}^{T}J_{k} + \lambda I\right)^{-1} J_{k}^{T}\,\mathbf{r}_k$$

where $\mathbf{r}_k$ is the residual and $\lambda$ is the damping factor.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Expectation:** For Module 3, the linearized form $\Delta\boldsymbol{\phi} = J^{\dagger}\mathbf{b}$ is the **expected level of derivation**. The Levenberg–Marquardt extension is bonus credit.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Given:**

$$R_c \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad R_m \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0.9999 & -0.0099 & 0 \\ 0.0099 & 0.9999 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 1 — Compute the error rotation.**

$$R_e \;=\; R_m \, R_c^{T} \;=\; R_m$$

**Step 2 — Apply the small-angle extraction formula.**

$$\delta\theta_x \;=\; \frac{1}{2}(R_{32} - R_{23}) \;=\; \frac{1}{2}(0 - 0) \;=\; 0$$

$$\delta\theta_y \;=\; \frac{1}{2}(R_{13} - R_{31}) \;=\; \frac{1}{2}(0 - 0) \;=\; 0$$

$$\delta\theta_z \;=\; \frac{1}{2}(R_{21} - R_{12}) \;=\; \frac{1}{2}(0.0099 - (-0.0099)) \;=\; 0.0099 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to degrees.**

$$\delta\theta_z \;=\; 0.0099 \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} \;\approx\; 0.567°$$

**Interpretation:** The robot is **0.567° off** about the world-frame $z$-axis. This is the **orientation error** that the world frame method has identified and that the controller will now **compensate**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — World Frame Method Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1[START World Frame Calibration] --> n2[Step 1 Place External Sensor in Fixed Location]
    n2 --> n3[Step 2 Define World Coordinate Frame from Sensor Origin]
    n3 --> n4[Step 3 Mount Calibration Artifact on End Effector]
    n4 --> n5[Step 4 Command Robot to N Distinct Orientations]
    n5 --> n6[Step 5 Sensor Captures Measured Orientation in World Frame]
    n6 --> n7[Step 6 Compute Error Rotation R_e equals R_m times R_c Transpose]
    n7 --> n8{Step 7 Orientation Error Within Tolerance}
    n8 -- NO --> n9[Step 8 Compute Identification Jacobian J]
    n9 --> n10[Step 9 Solve Least Squares Delta Phi equals J dagger b]
    n10 --> n11[Step 10 Update Robot Kinematic Parameters]
    n11 --> n12[Step 11 Re-measure and Validate]
    n12 --> n8
    n8 -- YES --> n13[END Robot is Calibrated to World Frame]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The process is **iterative** — calibration does not terminate after one pass. The loop between **Step 7** and **Step 12** continues until the orientation error falls below the prescribed tolerance. This is the hallmark of the **closed-loop iterative world frame calibration procedure**.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph External_World_Domain
        A1[World Frame Sensor] --> A2[World Coordinates Definition]
    end
    subgraph Robot_Internal_Domain
        B1[Robot Controller] --> B2[Forward Kinematic Model]
        B2 --> B3[Commanded Orientation R_c]
        B4[End Effector Tool Frame] --> B5[Measured Orientation R_m]
    end
    subgraph Calibration_Core
        C1[Error Computation R_e] --> C2[Parameter Identification]
        C2 --> C3[Parameter Update Vector Delta Phi]
    end
    A2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B5 --> C1
    C3 --> B1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Architecture Reveals the Key Idea:** The **World Frame (sensor domain)** and the **Robot Internal Domain (controller domain)** are *separate coordinate spaces*. The **Calibration Core** is the *bridge* that translates measured world-frame errors into controller-internal parameter updates.

---

## 4.3 Coordinate Frame Relationship Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[World Frame W] -->|Transformation R_W_B| B[Base Frame B]
    B -->|Joint Rotations R_0_1, R_1_2, ..., R_n_minus1_n| C[Tool Frame T]
    C -->|Offset R_T_E| D[End Effector Pose in World]
    A -.->|Direct Measurement via External Sensor| D
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Insight for KTU Exam:** The *dashed* arrow from **World Frame $\{W\}$** directly to **End-Effector Pose** represents the **external measurement** path. The *solid* chain of transformations represents the **kinematic chain** predicted by the model. The **calibration** is precisely the act of making these two paths *agree* in the world frame.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define orientation calibration. Differentiate between the World Frame Method and the Tool Frame Method of robot calibration.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

**Orientation calibration** is the process of identifying and compensating for systematic errors in a robot's end-effector orientation by comparing the **kinematically predicted orientation** with the **measured orientation** in a known reference frame.

| Aspect | World Frame Method | Tool Frame Method |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Reference Origin** | External, fixed sensor | Moving end-effector |
| **Sensor Location** | Stationary in world | Mounted on/relative to tool |
| **Typical Instrument** | Laser tracker, CMM | Touch probe, sphere-on-plate |
| **Error Detection** | Absolute (vs. world) | Relative (between poses) |
| **Complexity** | Higher | Lower |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the significance of the small-angle approximation in orientation calibration? Write the linearized error extraction formula.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

**Significance (1.5 marks):** The small-angle approximation **linearizes** the otherwise nonlinear rotation manifold $SO(3)$ around the identity. This allows us to treat the 3-DOF orientation error as a simple **3-vector** $\delta\boldsymbol{\theta}$ rather than a $3\times 3$ matrix, which is essential for using **linear least-squares identification** of kinematic parameters.

**Formula (1.5 marks):**

$$\delta\boldsymbol{\theta} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} R_{32} - R_{23} \\ R_{13} - R_{31} \\ R_{21} - R_{12} \end{bmatrix}$$

valid for $\vert \delta\boldsymbol{\theta} \vert < 5°$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module 3 Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 End Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern**, the student answers **ONE** of the two choices. Each is split into two **7-mark sub-parts** that *must* both be attempted.

---

### **🔷 QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

#### **Q.A. (a) [7 Marks] — Derive the orientation error model for the World Frame Method, starting from the commanded orientation $R_c$ and measured orientation $R_m$, and show how the small-angle orientation error vector $\delta\boldsymbol{\theta}$ is extracted.**

#### Model Answer:

**Step 1 — Define the error rotation (1 mark):**

$$R_e \;=\; R_m \, R_c^{T}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the small-angle hypothesis (2 marks):**

For a near-calibrated robot, $R_e \approx I_{3}$, hence:

$$R_e \;\approx\; I_{3} + [\delta\boldsymbol{\theta}]_{\times} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -\delta\theta_z & \delta\theta_y \\ \delta\theta_z & 1 & -\delta\theta_x \\ -\delta\theta_y & \delta\theta_x & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Equate and extract components (3 marks):**

By inspection of the off-diagonal entries:

$$\delta\theta_x \;=\; \frac{1}{2}(R_{32} - R_{23}), \quad \delta\theta_y \;=\; \frac{1}{2}(R_{13} - R_{31}), \quad \delta\theta_z \;=\; \frac{1}{2}(R_{21} - R_{12})$$

**Step 4 — Validity bound (1 mark):**

This linearization is valid for $\vert \delta\boldsymbol{\theta} \vert < 5°$ ($\approx 0.087$ rad).

#### **Q.A. (b) [7 Marks] — With a neat block diagram, explain the step-by-step procedure of the World Frame Method of orientation calibration. List the equipment used.**

#### Model Answer:

**Procedure (5 marks):**

1. **Sensor Placement** — A high-precision measurement device (laser tracker / photogrammetry system) is placed at a fixed location in the workspace.
2. **World Frame Definition** — The sensor's intrinsic frame is designated as the world frame $\{W\}$.
3. **Artifact Mounting** — A calibration artifact (precision cube / sphere cluster) is rigidly attached to the robot's end-effector.
4. **Pose Set Generation** — The robot is commanded to $N$ distinct orientations covering the operational workspace.
5. **Measurement** — For each pose, the sensor captures the actual orientation $R_m^i$ in the world frame.
6. **Error Computation** — $R_e^i = R_m^i \, (R_c^i)^{T}$ is computed; orientation error vector $\delta\boldsymbol{\theta}_i$ is extracted.
7. **Identification** — A least-squares algorithm identifies the kinematic parameter error $\Delta\boldsymbol{\phi}$ using $\Delta\boldsymbol{\phi} = J^{\dagger}\mathbf{b}$.
8. **Update & Validate** — The controller is updated, and the procedure is iterated until tolerance is met.

**Equipment (2 marks):**
- Laser tracker (e.g., Leica, API)
- Photogrammetry system (e.g., GOM)
- Precision calibration artifact (cube, sphere bar)
- Reference marker / nestling point
- Calibration software (e.g., RoboDK, MATLAB Robotics Toolbox)

---

### **🔷 QUESTION B (14 Marks)**

#### **Q.B. (a) [7 Marks] — Explain the World Frame Method in detail. Discuss the role of the identification Jacobian and the least-squares parameter update rule.**

#### Model Answer:

**World Frame Method Overview (3 marks):**
The world frame method uses a **stationary external sensor** as the absolute reference. The end-effector's pose is measured in this fixed frame, and the difference between the **kinematic model prediction** and the **measured reality** drives a **kinematic parameter identification** step.

**Identification Jacobian (2 marks):**
For each commanded pose $i$, the **identification Jacobian** $J_i$ maps small changes in kinematic parameters $\Delta\boldsymbol{\phi}$ to the resulting orientation residual $\delta\boldsymbol{\theta}_i$:

$$\delta\boldsymbol{\theta}_i \;=\; J_i(\boldsymbol{q}_i,\boldsymbol{\phi}_{\text{nom}}) \cdot \Delta\boldsymbol{\phi}$$

For $N$ poses, the Jacobians are stacked vertically to form a tall matrix $J \in \mathbb{R}^{3N \times k}$.

**Least-Squares Update (2 marks):**
The overdetermined system $J\,\Delta\boldsymbol{\phi} = \mathbf{b}$ is solved using the Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse:

$$\Delta\boldsymbol{\phi} \;=\; \left(J^{T}J\right)^{-1} J^{T}\,\mathbf{b}$$

This is the **closed-form update** of the robot's kinematic parameters, and it minimizes the sum of squared orientation residuals across all $N$ poses.

#### **Q.B. (b) [7 Marks] — A 6-DOF robot is commanded to reach an orientation $R_c = I_{3 \times 3}$ but the laser tracker measures an actual orientation:**

$$R_m \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0.9998 & 0.0199 & 0 \\ -0.0199 & 0.9998 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Compute the orientation error vector $\delta\boldsymbol{\theta}$ in both radians and degrees, and identify the dominant axis of error.**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Compute $R_e$ (1 mark):**

$$R_e \;=\; R_m \, R_c^{T} \;=\; R_m \cdot I \;=\; R_m$$

**Step 2 — Apply the small-angle formula (3 marks):**

$$\delta\theta_x \;=\; \frac{1}{2}(R_{32} - R_{23}) \;=\; \frac{1}{2}(0 - 0) \;=\; 0$$

$$\delta\theta_y \;=\; \frac{1}{2}(R_{13} - R_{31}) \;=\; \frac{1}{2}(0 - 0) \;=\; 0$$

$$\delta\theta_z \;=\; \frac{1}{2}(R_{21} - R_{12}) \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\bigl(-0.0199 - 0.0199\bigr) \;=\; -0.0199 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to degrees (2 marks):**

$$\delta\theta_z \;=\; -0.0199 \times \frac{180}{\pi} \;\approx\; -1.140°$$

**Step 4 — Dominant axis identification (1 mark):**

Since $\delta\theta_x = \delta\theta_y = 0$ and $\delta\theta_z \neq 0$, the **dominant axis of error is the world-frame $z$-axis**, with a magnitude of $\approx 1.14°$. The controller should apply a **$-1.14°$ correction** about $z_W$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Read This Carefully):**
> - **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{2}$ factor** in the small-angle formula — common 1-mark loss.
> - **Mixing up $R_c^{T}$ vs. $R_c^{-1}$** — they are equal for rotation matrices, but be careful with the *order* of multiplication.
> - **Failing to convert to degrees** when the problem explicitly asks for it — 1-mark deduction.
> - **Confusing the world-frame $z$-axis with the tool-frame $z$-axis** — calibration is performed *in the world frame*; do not project the error back into the tool frame unless asked.
> - **Linearization breakdown:** If the error exceeds $5°$, the small-angle formula gives **biased** results. Use Rodrigues' formula in such cases.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ⚡

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Pin This to Your Wall!**

- ✅ **World Frame Method** = measurement in a *fixed, external* reference frame, NOT in the robot's own frame.
- ✅ **Error rotation** is $R_e = R_m \, R_c^{T}$ — memorize this *order*.
- ✅ **Small-angle extraction** uses the off-diagonal anti-symmetric entries of $R_e$, scaled by $\frac{1}{2}$.
- ✅ **Validity bound** for linearization: $\vert \delta\boldsymbol{\theta} \vert < 5°$. Beyond this, use **Rodrigues' formula**.
- ✅ **Rodrigues' formula** reconstructs $R$ from axis-angle: $R = I + \sin\theta\,[\mathbf{u}]_{\times} + (1-\cos\theta)\,[\mathbf{u}]_{\times}^{2}$.
- ✅ **Identification** uses the **Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse**: $\Delta\boldsymbol{\phi} = (J^{T}J)^{-1}J^{T}\mathbf{b}$.
- ✅ **Equipment** for world frame calibration: **laser tracker**, **photogrammetry system**, or **CMM** + calibration artifact.
- ✅ **Repeatability ≠ Accuracy** — calibration closes the gap.
- ✅ **Iterative procedure** — calibration does not terminate in one pass; tolerance convergence is required.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Hot Topics** for Module 3: error matrix, small-angle extraction formula, axis-angle representation, Jacobian identification, and numerical example solving.
- ✅ **Distinguish World Frame vs. Tool Frame methods** — the examiner will *definitely* test this comparison.
- ✅ **Trace relation** $\mathrm{tr}(R) = 1 + 2\cos\theta$ is a *favourite* of KTU question setters.
- ✅ **Skew-symmetric matrix** $[\mathbf{v}]_{\times}$ such that $[\mathbf{v}]_{\times}\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{v} \times \mathbf{u}$ — draw this if confused.
- ✅ **Real-world applications**: aerospace drilling, automotive welding, robotic machining, surgical robotics.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT865 - INDUSTRIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Two-Point Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two-Point Method (Bounded-Degree Polynomial Trajectory Generation):**
> The Two-Point Method is a fundamental trajectory planning technique in industrial robotics used to generate a **smooth, continuous motion profile** for a robot joint between two specified points in space. Unlike the Three-Point Method which controls only positions, the Two-Point Method constrains **both the position and the velocity** at the start and end points, resulting in a bounded-velocity, jerk-limited motion governed by a **cubic polynomial** (third-order polynomial) function of time.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework (Module 3: Trajectory Planning), the Two-Point Method is classified under **Joint-Space Trajectory Planning Schemes**, where the trajectory is expressed as a time-dependent function of joint variables $\theta(t)$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Elevator Ride" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Driving a Car with Specified Entry and Exit Speeds**

Imagine you are driving a car in a parking garage:

- **Point 1 (Start):** You enter the ramp at exactly **0 km/h** (a smooth start from rest).
- **Point 2 (End):** You reach the next level and stop at exactly **0 km/h** (a smooth stop).
- Between these two points, your car accelerates, cruises, and decelerates **smoothly** — no jerks, no sudden direction changes.

Now consider a **robotic arm** moving from Point A to Point B to pick up a component:
- The engineer specifies the **exact starting velocity** (often zero for a clean start).
- The engineer specifies the **exact ending velocity** (often zero for a precise placement).
- The Two-Point Method mathematically **solves for the smoothest possible path** connecting these two constraints.

Just as a smooth driving profile prevents passenger discomfort, the Two-Point Method prevents **mechanical vibration, stress on actuators, and overshoot** in the robot — critical in precision manufacturing like **PCB assembly** or **welding**.

## 1.3 Key Parameters at a Glance

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Industrial Value | Physical Meaning |
|-----------|--------|------------------------|------------------|
| Initial Position | $\theta_0$ | Specified in **radians** or **degrees** | Starting joint angle |
| Final Position | $\theta_f$ | Specified in **radians** or **degrees** | Target joint angle |
| Initial Velocity | $\dot{\theta}_0$ | Often **0 rad/s** for pick-and-place | Starting joint velocity |
| Final Velocity | $\dot{\theta}_f$ | Often **0 rad/s** for pick-and-place | Ending joint velocity |
| Total Time | $T = t_f - t_0$ | **1 to 5 seconds** for typical motion | Motion duration |

## 1.4 Why Cubic (3rd Order) and Not Lower?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mathematical Necessity of the Cubic Polynomial:**
> A cubic polynomial has **4 coefficients** ($a_0, a_1, a_2, a_3$). The Two-Point Method imposes exactly **4 boundary conditions** (two positions + two velocities). A lower-order polynomial (linear or quadratic) has too few coefficients; a higher-order polynomial has too many (leading to under-determined systems).

$$\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$

This **4-coefficient ↔ 4-constraint** matching is the cornerstone reason the cubic polynomial is universally adopted for the Two-Point Method in KTU-validated textbooks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

> [!NOTE]
> The Two-Point Method operates on **4 boundary conditions** and produces a **unique cubic polynomial**. Below is the structured logic flow:

### Step 1: Define the Trajectory Function

We assume a **time-parameterized joint trajectory** in joint space:

$$\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$

where $a_0, a_1, a_2, a_3 \in \mathbb{R}$ are the unknown polynomial coefficients.

### Step 2: Compute the Velocity Profile (First Derivative)

$$\dot{\theta}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2$$

> Velocity is the **time rate of change of position** — it tells the motor controller how fast the joint should rotate at any instant.

### Step 3: Apply the Four Boundary Conditions

For convenience, let $t_0 = 0$ (without loss of generality). Then the four constraints are:

| # | Constraint | Equation |
|---|-----------|----------|
| 1 | Position at $t = 0$ | $\theta(0) = \theta_0 \Rightarrow a_0 = \theta_0$ |
| 2 | Velocity at $t = 0$ | $\dot{\theta}(0) = \dot{\theta}_0 \Rightarrow a_1 = \dot{\theta}_0$ |
| 3 | Position at $t = t_f$ | $\theta(t_f) = \theta_f$ |
| 4 | Velocity at $t = t_f$ | $\dot{\theta}(t_f) = \dot{\theta}_f$ |

### Step 4: Solve the Linear System for $a_2$ and $a_3$

The **Why**: We already have $a_0$ and $a_1$ from Steps 1 and 2 of constraints. Now we must solve two simultaneous equations for two unknowns ($a_2$ and $a_3$).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER CHEAT SHEET — Two-Point Method (Memorize for KTU ESE)**

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ | Position trajectory (cubic) |
| 2 | $\dot{\theta}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2$ | Velocity trajectory |
| 3 | $a_0 = \theta_0$ | Initial position coefficient |
| 4 | $a_1 = \dot{\theta}_0$ | Initial velocity coefficient |
| 5 | $a_2 = \dfrac{3 h}{T^2} - \dfrac{2 \dot{\theta}_0}{T} - \dfrac{\dot{\theta}_f}{T}$ | Acceleration coefficient |
| 6 | $a_3 = -\dfrac{2 h}{T^3} + \dfrac{\dot{\theta}_0}{T^2} + \dfrac{\dot{\theta}_f}{T^2}$ | Jerk-related coefficient |
| 7 | $h = \theta_f - \theta_0$ | Displacement |
| 8 | $T = t_f - t_0$ | Time interval |
| 9 | $\ddot{\theta}(t) = 2 a_2 + 6 a_3 t$ | Acceleration profile |

where the **boundary velocity conditions** yield:

$$\dot{\theta}_0 = a_1, \quad \dot{\theta}_f = a_1 + 2 a_2 T + 3 a_3 T^2$$

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Application Spotlight:**
>
> 1. **Pick-and-Place Robots (SCARA, Cartesian):** Used in SMT (Surface Mount Technology) lines to ensure zero overshoot when placing microchips.
> 2. **Welding Robots (Articulated 6-DOF):** Ensures smooth arc ignition and termination by specifying zero velocity at weld start/end points.
> 3. **CNC Machining:** Generates smooth tool-path segments between waypoints.
> 4. **Collaborative Robots (Cobots):** Critical for safe human-robot interaction since abrupt velocity changes could injure nearby workers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Four Coefficients

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Mandate:** Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No intermediate steps are skipped — this matches the KTU ESE board examiner's expectation for full-mark solutions.

### Starting Point — Position Equation

$$\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$

### Velocity Equation (First Derivative w.r.t. time)

$$\dot{\theta}(t) = \frac{d\theta}{dt} = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2$$

### Boundary Condition 1: Position at $t = 0$ equals $\theta_0$

$$\theta(0) = a_0 + a_1(0) + a_2(0)^2 + a_3(0)^3 = \theta_0$$

$$\therefore a_0 = \theta_0$$

> **Interpretation:** The constant term equals the starting position — geometrically obvious but mathematically essential.

### Boundary Condition 2: Velocity at $t = 0$ equals $\dot{\theta}_0$

$$\dot{\theta}(0) = a_1 + 2 a_2(0) + 3 a_3(0)^2 = \dot{\theta}_0$$

$$\therefore a_1 = \dot{\theta}_0$$

> **Interpretation:** The linear coefficient directly encodes the starting velocity.

### Boundary Condition 3: Position at $t = T$ equals $\theta_f$

Let $T = t_f - t_0$. For simplicity, assume $t_0 = 0$, so $t_f = T$.

$$\theta(T) = a_0 + a_1 T + a_2 T^2 + a_3 T^3 = \theta_f$$

Substitute $a_0 = \theta_0$ and $a_1 = \dot{\theta}_0$:

$$\theta_0 + \dot{\theta}_0 T + a_2 T^2 + a_3 T^3 = \theta_f$$

Rearrange by isolating the unknowns on the left:

$$a_2 T^2 + a_3 T^3 = \theta_f - \theta_0$$

Define the **displacement** $h = \theta_f - \theta_0$:

$$a_2 T^2 + a_3 T^3 = h \quad \text{...(Equation A)}$$

### Boundary Condition 4: Velocity at $t = T$ equals $\dot{\theta}_f$

$$\dot{\theta}(T) = a_1 + 2 a_2 T + 3 a_3 T^2 = \dot{\theta}_f$$

Substitute $a_1 = \dot{\theta}_0$:

$$\dot{\theta}_0 + 2 a_2 T + 3 a_3 T^2 = \dot{\theta}_f$$

Rearrange:

$$2 a_2 T + 3 a_3 T^2 = \dot{\theta}_f - \dot{\theta}_0 \quad \text{...(Equation B)}$$

### Solving the 2×2 Linear System

We have two equations:

$$T^2 a_2 + T^3 a_3 = h$$

$$2T a_2 + 3T^2 a_3 = \dot{\theta}_f - \dot{\theta}_0$$

> **Method:** Use elimination. Multiply Equation B by $T/2$ to match the $a_2$ coefficient:

$$\frac{T}{2} \cdot (2T a_2 + 3T^2 a_3) = \frac{T}{2}(\dot{\theta}_f - \dot{\theta}_0)$$

$$T^2 a_2 + \frac{3T^3}{2} a_3 = \frac{T(\dot{\theta}_f - \dot{\theta}_0)}{2}$$

Subtract Equation A:

$$\left(T^2 a_2 + \frac{3T^3}{2} a_3\right) - (T^2 a_2 + T^3 a_3) = \frac{T(\dot{\theta}_f - \dot{\theta}_0)}{2} - h$$

$$\frac{3T^3}{2} a_3 - T^3 a_3 = \frac{T(\dot{\theta}_f - \dot{\theta}_0) - 2h}{2}$$

$$\frac{T^3}{2} a_3 = \frac{T(\dot{\theta}_f - \dot{\theta}_0) - 2h}{2}$$

$$a_3 = \frac{T(\dot{\theta}_f - \dot{\theta}_0) - 2h}{T^3}$$

> **Conversion logic:** The right-hand side numerator was multiplied by $2/2$ to enable subtraction with a common denominator.

$$\therefore a_3 = -\frac{2h}{T^3} + \frac{\dot{\theta}_f - \dot{\theta}_0}{T^2}$$

$$\boxed{a_3 = -\frac{2h}{T^3} + \frac{\dot{\theta}_0}{T^2} + \frac{\dot{\theta}_f}{T^2}}$$

### Solving for $a_2$

From Equation A:

$$a_2 T^2 = h - a_3 T^3$$

$$a_2 = \frac{h - a_3 T^3}{T^2}$$

Substitute $a_3$:

$$a_2 = \frac{h - T^3 \left(-\frac{2h}{T^3} + \frac{\dot{\theta}_0 + \dot{\theta}_f}{T^2}\right)}{T^2}$$

$$a_2 = \frac{h + 2h - T(\dot{\theta}_0 + \dot{\theta}_f)}{T^2}$$

$$a_2 = \frac{3h - T(\dot{\theta}_0 + \dot{\theta}_f)}{T^2}$$

$$\boxed{a_2 = \frac{3h}{T^2} - \frac{2\dot{\theta}_0}{T} - \frac{\dot{\theta}_f}{T}}$$

> **Conversion logic:** The $T^3$ multiplied by $-2h/T^3$ gives $-2h$, and $T^3$ multiplied by $(\dot{\theta}_0 + \dot{\theta}_f)/T^2$ gives $T(\dot{\theta}_0 + \dot{\theta}_f)$.

## 3.2 The Final Compact Equation Form

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry-Standard Form (Spong, Hutchinson, Vidyasagar — Robotics Textbook Convention):**
> Using the normalized time variable $\tau = (t - t_0)/T \in [0,1]$, the Two-Point trajectory simplifies elegantly to:

$$\theta(\tau) = \theta_0 + \dot{\theta}_0 T \tau + \left(\frac{3h - (2\dot{\theta}_0 + \dot{\theta}_f)T}{1}\right) \tau^2 + \left(\frac{-2h + (\dot{\theta}_0 + \dot{\theta}_f)T}{1}\right) \tau^3$$

> **Note:** The above is the direct expansion. The coefficient form already derived is the most common KTU-board expected form.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Solution Verification

For sanity-checking, the boundary conditions should yield:

| Check | Evaluation | Result |
|-------|-----------|--------|
| $\theta(0)$ | $a_0$ | $\theta_0$ ✓ |
| $\theta(T)$ | $\theta_0 + \dot{\theta}_0 T + a_2 T^2 + a_3 T^3$ | $\theta_f$ ✓ |
| $\dot{\theta}(0)$ | $a_1$ | $\dot{\theta}_0$ ✓ |
| $\dot{\theta}(T)$ | $\dot{\theta}_0 + 2 a_2 T + 3 a_3 T^2$ | $\dot{\theta}_f$ ✓ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Trajectory Planning Hierarchy — Block Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 4.1: Position of Two-Point Method in the Robotics Trajectory Planning Hierarchy**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Trajectory Planning"] --> nodeB["Joint Space Schemes"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Cartesian Space Schemes"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Point-to-Point Motion"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["Continuous Path Motion"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Two-Point Method"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["Three-Point Method"]
    nodeD --> nodeH["Four-Point Method"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["Cubic Polynomial"]
    nodeF --> nodeJ["Boundary Constraints: Position + Velocity"]
    nodeI --> nodeK["Outputs theta t, dot theta t, ddot theta t"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL["Robot Joint Actuator Commands"]
```

## 4.2 Two-Point Method — Functional Block Architecture Flow

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 4.2: Detailed Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Two-Point Method**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp1["Initial Position theta0"] --> m1
    inp2["Final Position thetaf"] --> m1
    inp3["Initial Velocity dot theta0"] --> m1
    inp4["Final Velocity dot thetaf"] --> m1
    inp5["Total Time T"] --> m1
    m1["Boundary Condition Block: 4 constraints"] --> m2
    m2["Coefficient Solver: Linear 4x4 System"] --> m3
    m3["Outputs a0, a1, a2, a3"] --> m4
    m4["Cubic Polynomial Engine: theta t equals a0 plus a1 t plus a2 t squared plus a3 t cubed"] --> m5
    m5["Joint Command Stream to Servo Drive"] --> m6
    m6["Robot Joint Motion Execution"]
    m4 --> m7["Velocity Profile: dot theta t"]
    m4 --> m8["Acceleration Profile: ddot theta t"]
```

## 4.3 Boundary Condition Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TIMEAXIS["Time Axis: 0 to T"]
        T0["t equals 0: Start Point"]
        TMID["t equals T by 2: Mid Point"]
        TF["t equals T: End Point"]
    end
    T0 -->|"theta equals theta0"| BC1["Constraint 1: Position Start"]
    T0 -->|"dot theta equals dot theta0"| BC2["Constraint 2: Velocity Start"]
    TF -->|"theta equals thetaf"| BC3["Constraint 3: Position End"]
    TF -->|"dot theta equals dot thetaf"| BC4["Constraint 4: Velocity End"]
    BC1 --> SOLVE["Cubic Solver"]
    BC2 --> SOLVE
    BC3 --> SOLVE
    BC4 --> SOLVE
    SOLVE --> RESULT["Cubic Polynomial Trajectory"]
```

## 4.4 Signal Flow Comparison — Position, Velocity, Acceleration

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 4.4: Conceptual Plot of the Three Profiles Generated by the Two-Point Method**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    POS["Position Profile theta t: Smooth S-Curve from theta0 to thetaf"] --> VEL
    VEL["Velocity Profile dot theta t: Parabolic from dot theta0 to dot thetaf"] --> ACC
    ACC["Acceleration Profile ddot theta t: Linear from 2a2 to 2a2 plus 6a3 T"]
    POS -.->|"Derivative operator d by dt"| VEL
    VEL -.->|"Derivative operator d by dt"| ACC
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Each successive differentiation **lowers the polynomial degree by 1**:
> - Position → **Degree 3** (cubic)
> - Velocity → **Degree 2** (quadratic / parabolic)
> - Acceleration → **Degree 1** (linear)
> - Jerk → **Degree 0** (constant) — this means jerk is bounded, ensuring smooth motion.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part A question is calibrated to KTU Bloom Level 2 (Understand) / Level 3 (Apply).

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` 
**Define the Two-Point Method of trajectory planning. Why is a cubic polynomial chosen for this method?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Two-Point Method** is a joint-space trajectory planning technique that generates a smooth motion profile between **two specified waypoints**, with constraints imposed on both the **positions and velocities** at the start and end points.

A **cubic polynomial** of the form $\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ is chosen because:
1. **(1 mark)** It has exactly **four coefficients** ($a_0, a_1, a_2, a_3$) which perfectly matches the **four boundary conditions** (2 positions + 2 velocities).
2. **(1 mark)** Lower-order polynomials (linear/quadratic) cannot satisfy all four constraints.
3. **(1 mark)** Higher-order polynomials introduce unnecessary oscillations and require additional constraints.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the four boundary conditions used in the Two-Point Method for trajectory generation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four boundary conditions are:

1. **(0.75 marks)** $\theta(0) = \theta_0$ — Initial position
2. **(0.75 marks)** $\dot{\theta}(0) = \dot{\theta}_0$ — Initial velocity
3. **(0.75 marks)** $\theta(T) = \theta_f$ — Final position
4. **(0.75 marks)** $\dot{\theta}(T) = \dot{\theta}_f$ — Final velocity

where $T$ is the total time of motion.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part B question carries sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks, following the **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice** pattern.

---

### **QUESTION A** (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A robotic joint needs to move from an initial angle of $20°$ to a final angle of $80°$ in $4$ seconds. The initial velocity is $0°/\text{s}$ and the final velocity is $0°/\text{s}$. Using the Two-Point Method:**

**(a)** Derive the cubic polynomial trajectory equation $\theta(t)$.

**(b)** Compute the joint position, velocity, and acceleration at $t = 2$ seconds.

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

**Given Data:**
- $\theta_0 = 20°$, $\theta_f = 80°$
- $\dot{\theta}_0 = 0°/\text{s}$, $\dot{\theta}_f = 0°/\text{s}$
- $T = 4$ s

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Compute displacement.**
$$h = \theta_f - \theta_0 = 80° - 20° = 60°$$

**Step 2: Apply boundary conditions to find coefficients.**

$$\therefore a_0 = \theta_0 = 20°$$

$$\therefore a_1 = \dot{\theta}_0 = 0°/\text{s}$$

**Step 3: Compute $a_2$.** Since $\dot{\theta}_0 = \dot{\theta}_f = 0$:

$$a_2 = \frac{3h}{T^2} - \frac{2\dot{\theta}_0}{T} - \frac{\dot{\theta}_f}{T} = \frac{3 \times 60}{4^2} - 0 - 0 = \frac{180}{16}$$

$$a_2 = 11.25 °/\text{s}^2$$

**Step 4: Compute $a_3$.**

$$a_3 = -\frac{2h}{T^3} + \frac{\dot{\theta}_0}{T^2} + \frac{\dot{\theta}_f}{T^2} = -\frac{2 \times 60}{64} + 0 + 0$$

$$a_3 = -1.875 °/\text{s}^3$$

**Step 5: Write the trajectory equation.**

$$\boxed{\theta(t) = 20 + 11.25 t^2 - 1.875 t^3 \quad \text{(degrees)}}$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 6: Velocity profile.**

$$\dot{\theta}(t) = 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2 = 22.5 t - 5.625 t^2 \quad °/\text{s}$$

At $t = 2$ s:
$$\dot{\theta}(2) = 22.5(2) - 5.625(4) = 45 - 22.5 = 22.5 °/\text{s}$$

**Step 7: Acceleration profile.**

$$\ddot{\theta}(t) = 2 a_2 + 6 a_3 t = 22.5 - 11.25 t \quad °/\text{s}^2$$

At $t = 2$ s:
$$\ddot{\theta}(2) = 22.5 - 11.25(2) = 22.5 - 22.5 = 0 °/\text{s}^2$$

**Step 8: Position at $t = 2$ s.**

$$\theta(2) = 20 + 11.25(4) - 1.875(8) = 20 + 45 - 15 = 50°$$

**Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Value at $t = 2$ s |
|----------|-------------------|
| Position $\theta(2)$ | $50°$ |
| Velocity $\dot{\theta}(2)$ | $22.5 °/\text{s}$ |
| Acceleration $\ddot{\theta}(2)$ | $0 °/\text{s}^2$ |

---

### **QUESTION B** (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A single-link manipulator has initial position $\theta_0 = 10°$ and final position $\theta_f = 70°$. It is required to move such that the initial velocity is $5°/\text{s}$, the final velocity is $10°/\text{s}$, and the total time is $T = 3$ seconds.**

**(a)** Derive the coefficients of the cubic trajectory.

**(b)** Determine whether the motion has uniform or non-uniform acceleration. Justify your answer.

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**Given Data:**
- $\theta_0 = 10°$, $\theta_f = 70°$
- $\dot{\theta}_0 = 5°/\text{s}$, $\dot{\theta}_f = 10°/\text{s}$
- $T = 3$ s

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Displacement.**
$$h = 70 - 10 = 60°$$

**Step 2: Coefficient $a_0$ and $a_1$.**
$$a_0 = 10°, \quad a_1 = 5 °/\text{s}$$

**Step 3: Coefficient $a_2$.**

$$a_2 = \frac{3h}{T^2} - \frac{2\dot{\theta}_0}{T} - \frac{\dot{\theta}_f}{T}$$

$$a_2 = \frac{3 \times 60}{9} - \frac{2 \times 5}{3} - \frac{10}{3}$$

$$a_2 = 20 - \frac{10}{3} - \frac{10}{3} = 20 - \frac{20}{3} = \frac{40}{3}$$

$$a_2 \approx 13.33 °/\text{s}^2$$

**Step 4: Coefficient $a_3$.**

$$a_3 = -\frac{2h}{T^3} + \frac{\dot{\theta}_0}{T^2} + \frac{\dot{\theta}_f}{T^2}$$

$$a_3 = -\frac{2 \times 60}{27} + \frac{5}{9} + \frac{10}{9}$$

$$a_3 = -\frac{120}{27} + \frac{15}{9} = -\frac{40}{9} + \frac{15}{9} = -\frac{25}{9}$$

$$a_3 \approx -2.778 °/\text{s}^3$$

**Step 5: Final trajectory.**

$$\boxed{\theta(t) = 10 + 5t + 13.33 t^2 - 2.778 t^3 \quad \text{(degrees)}}$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 6: Acceleration profile.**

$$\ddot{\theta}(t) = 2 a_2 + 6 a_3 t = \frac{80}{3} - \frac{50}{3} t \quad °/\text{s}^2$$

**Step 7: Evaluate at $t = 0$ and $t = T$.**

$$\ddot{\theta}(0) = \frac{80}{3} \approx 26.67 °/\text{s}^2$$

$$\ddot{\theta}(3) = \frac{80}{3} - \frac{50}{3}(3) = \frac{80 - 150}{3} = -\frac{70}{3} \approx -23.33 °/\text{s}^2$$

**Step 8: Conclusion.**

> **(3 marks for justification)** Since $\ddot{\theta}(0) \neq \ddot{\theta}(T)$, the acceleration is **NOT uniform**. The motion exhibits **non-uniform acceleration** — specifically, **linear deceleration** since $a_3 \neq 0$. The negative value of $a_3$ indicates the joint decelerates toward the end of the motion, allowing it to reach the final velocity of $10°/\text{s}$ smoothly.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Sign error in $a_3$ derivation:** Many students forget the negative sign. Always re-derive: $a_3 = -\frac{2h}{T^3} + \frac{\dot{\theta}_0 + \dot{\theta}_f}{T^2}$.
>
> 2. **Unit confusion:** Mixing degrees and radians. KTU accepts both, but you MUST be consistent throughout the problem.
>
> 3. **Forgetting $a_0$ and $a_1$:** Examiners frequently give 1 mark each for stating $a_0 = \theta_0$ and $a_1 = \dot{\theta}_0$. Skipping these explicit statements costs easy marks.
>
> 4. **No verification step:** Always substitute $t = 0$ and $t = T$ back into your final polynomial to verify boundary conditions. **(2 marks reserved for verification in 14-mark problems)**
>
> 5. **Misinterpreting $h$:** Students sometimes compute $h = \theta_0 - \theta_f$ (wrong sign). Always: $h = \theta_f - \theta_0$.
>
> 6. **Failing to state physical units** in the final answer — e.g., writing "11.25" without "$°/\text{s}^2$".

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Two-Point Method**
>
> ☐ **Core definition:** Smooth joint motion between 2 points with position + velocity constraints.
>
> ☐ **Trajectory form:** $\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ (cubic polynomial).
>
> ☐ **Four boundary conditions:** $\theta_0, \dot{\theta}_0, \theta_f, \dot{\theta}_f$.
>
> ☐ **Direct coefficients:** $a_0 = \theta_0$ and $a_1 = \dot{\theta}_0$ (no computation needed).
>
> ☐ **Key formulas:**
> - $a_2 = \dfrac{3h}{T^2} - \dfrac{2\dot{\theta}_0}{T} - \dfrac{\dot{\theta}_f}{T}$
> - $a_3 = -\dfrac{2h}{T^3} + \dfrac{\dot{\theta}_0}{T^2} + \dfrac{\dot{\theta}_f}{T^2}$
> - where $h = \theta_f - \theta_0$, $T = t_f - t_0$
>
> ☐ **Pick-and-place applications:** When $\dot{\theta}_0 = \dot{\theta}_f = 0$, the trajectory simplifies significantly.
>
> ☐ **Degree cascade:** Position (cubic) → Velocity (quadratic) → Acceleration (linear) → Jerk (constant).
>
> ☐ **Bounded jerk:** The constant jerk means smooth motion without abrupt acceleration changes — critical for vibration-sensitive operations like precision assembly.
>
> ☐ **Limitation:** Cannot constrain acceleration; for acceleration constraints, use the **Three-Point Method** (quintic polynomial).
>
> ☐ **Always verify** by substituting boundary times back into the polynomial.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calibration of Industrial Robot System: External Fixed Tool
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT865 - INDUSTRIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Calibration of Industrial Robot System: External Fixed Tool

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robot Calibration** is the process of identifying and compensating for the geometric and non-geometric errors of an industrial robot to improve its absolute positioning accuracy, where the **measured** pose of the end-effector is matched against the **commanded** pose through a defined mathematical model.

In the **External Fixed Tool (EFT)** configuration, the robot is required to manipulate a workpiece and present it precisely to a **stationary tool** mounted in the workspace. Unlike a robot-mounted tool, the tool here is **rigidly fixed in the environment** (e.g., a stationary welding gun, a fixed drill press, a gluing nozzle, or a laser cutting head), while the robot carries a **workpiece** or a **probe** to interact with it.

The calibration objective is therefore to identify the **homogeneous transformation matrix** that precisely locates the external fixed tool frame $\{T\}$ with respect to the robot's **world (base) frame** $\{B\}$:

$$
{}^{B}_{T}T = \begin{bmatrix} {}^{B}_{T}R & {}^{B}p_{T,org} \\ 0\ 0\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

where ${}^{B}_{T}R \in SO(3)$ is the rotational orientation and ${}^{B}p_{T,org} \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$ is the position vector of the tool's origin expressed in the base frame.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Blindfolded Handshake"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:** Imagine a blindfolded person (the **robot**) holding a coffee cup (the **workpiece**). There is a stationary water tap (the **fixed tool**) in the room. To pour water into the cup, the blindfolded person must know **exactly** where the tap is located in the room and how it is oriented. The "calibration" is essentially the blindfolded person being guided to touch the tap with their cup at multiple angles — by observing where the tap is, the person builds a mental map. Once the map is created, they can reliably bring the cup to the tap every time, even blindfolded.

## 1.3 Why External Fixed Tool Calibration Matters

- **Static tool = fixed reference**: Because the tool never moves, any pose of the robot that "touches" the tool can be used to compute its coordinates.
- **No mechanical changeover**: Unlike a robot-mounted tool, swapping between different tools does not require a new tool transformation on the robot side.
- **Critical for high-precision tasks**: Tolerances in aerospace riveting, micro-machining, and adhesive dispensing are often below **±0.05 mm**, which is impossible to achieve with the robot's nominal accuracy (typically **±1–5 mm**).

## 1.4 Key Terminology Box

> [!NOTE]
> - **TCP (Tool Center Point):** The point on the workpiece/probe that must coincide with the fixed tool's reference point.
> - **World Frame $\{B\}$:** The fixed reference frame attached to the robot base.
> - **Flange Frame $\{F\}$:** The mechanical interface at the robot's wrist.
> - **Tool Frame $\{T\}$:** The frame attached to the stationary tool.
> - **Workpiece Frame $\{W\}$:** The frame on the object held by the robot.
> - **Forward Kinematics (FK):** Mapping joint angles to Cartesian pose.
> - **Inverse Kinematics (IK):** Mapping a Cartesian pose to joint angles.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Kinematic Chain for External Fixed Tool

The complete kinematic chain for the robot–tool interaction in the EFT case is:

$$
{}^{B}_{T}T \;=\; {}^{B}_{B}T \cdot {}^{B}_{F}T(q) \cdot {}^{F}_{W}T \cdot {}^{W}_{T}T
$$

where:
- ${}^{B}_{B}T = I$ (identity, since the base frame is the world frame).
- ${}^{B}_{F}T(q)$ is the **forward kinematics** of the robot, a function of joint vector $q = (q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n)^T$.
- ${}^{F}_{W}T$ is the **probe/workpiece-to-flange** transformation (often identity if the probe is mounted coaxially).
- ${}^{W}_{T}T$ is the transformation from the workpiece/probe to the **external fixed tool's reference point**.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Calibration Logic (Six-Point Method)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **six-point method** (also called the **ABC World / 6-Point method** by ABB) is the gold-standard technique taught in KTU 2024 Scheme. It uses **six distinct robot poses** to decouple the **3 translational** and **3 rotational** DOFs of the tool.

### Step A — Establish the X-axis of the Tool
1. Move the robot so that the probe touches the tool reference point from **pose 1**.
2. Rotate the robot about the tool reference point (using IK) to **pose 2** along a known direction (e.g., a calibrated reference edge).
3. The vector from pose 1 to pose 2 defines the tool's **+X-axis direction**.

### Step B — Establish the Y-axis of the Tool
1. From pose 1, move to **pose 3** at a known offset along the tool's **+Y direction** (perpendicular to X).
2. The vector from pose 1 to pose 3 defines the **+Y-axis**.

### Step C — Establish the Z-axis (Cross Product)
1. The Z-axis is computed as:
$$
\hat{z}_T = \hat{x}_T \times \hat{y}_T
$$
2. This ensures orthonormality (right-hand rule).

### Step D — Establish the Origin
1. Use **pose 4, 5, 6** (three non-collinear points) and solve the **sphere-intersection problem** to find the tool's TCP origin. The TCP is equidistant from all three points.

### Step E — Compute the Transformation
1. Construct the rotation matrix ${}^{B}_{T}R = [\hat{x}_T \; \hat{y}_T \; \hat{z}_T]$.
2. Build the homogeneous matrix and store it in the robot controller.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Description | Unit / Constraint |
|---|--------------------|-------------|--------------------|
| 1 | ${}^{B}_{T}T = {}^{B}_{F}T(q) \cdot {}^{F}_{W}T \cdot {}^{W}_{T}T$ | Full kinematic chain (EFT) | $4\times 4$ homogeneous matrix |
| 2 | $\hat{z}_T = \hat{x}_T \times \hat{y}_T$ | Z-axis from cross product | Unit vector, $\vert \hat{z}_T\vert = 1$ |
| 3 | $p_{TCP} = \frac{1}{3}\sum_{i=1}^{3} p_i$ | TCP origin via sphere centroid (if equal radii) | meters (m) |
| 4 | $\Delta p = p_{commanded} - p_{measured}$ | Positional error vector | meters (m) |
| 5 | $e_{pos} = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2 + \Delta z^2}$ | Scalar position error | meters (m) |
| 6 | $e_{ori} = \arccos\left(\frac{\text{tr}(R_{cmd}^T R_{meas}) - 1}{2}\right)$ | Orientation error (angle-axis) | radians (rad) |
| 7 | $\text{Cond}(J) = \sigma_{max} / \sigma_{min}$ | Jacobian conditioning (singularity measure) | $\text{Cond}(J) \geq 1$ |
| 8 | $R_{ortho} \Rightarrow R^T R = I$ | Rotation matrix orthonormality check | $3\times 3$ identity |
| 9 | $\dot{X} = J(q)\dot{q}$ | Differential velocity mapping | m/s, rad/s |
| 10 | $\epsilon_{repeat} \approx 0.02\text{–}0.05$ | Typical robot repeatability | mm |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe symbol alert:** In the table above, $\vert \hat{z}_T\vert$ and $\text{Cond}(J) \geq 1$ use the LaTeX `\vert` macro — **never** the literal pipe character `|` — to avoid breaking the markdown parser.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace assembly:** Drilling and riveting fuselage panels where the tool is a stationary drill jig.
- **Automotive welding:** Stationary spot-welding tips that the robot brings body panels to.
- **Electronics:** PCB depaneling saws that remain fixed while the robot positions the board.
- **Adhesive dispensing:** Fixed nozzles for consistent bead geometry on moving parts.

The key engineering payoff is a **6× to 10× improvement** in absolute accuracy, reducing cycle time spent on teach-pendant correction and minimizing scrap rates.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — TCP Origin from Sphere-Intersection (3-Point Method)

Suppose the robot TCP touches the tool at three non-collinear points $P_1, P_2, P_3$ in the world frame. The TCP center $C$ is equidistant from all three:

$$
\vert C - P_1 \vert^2 = \vert C - P_2 \vert^2 = \vert C - P_3 \vert^2
$$

### Step-by-Step Expansion

Let $C = (x, y, z)^T$ and $P_i = (x_i, y_i, z_i)^T$.

**Equation 1 (between $P_1$ and $P_2$):**

$$
(x-x_1)^2 + (y-y_1)^2 + (z-z_1)^2 = (x-x_2)^2 + (y-y_2)^2 + (z-z_2)^2
$$

**Expanding and cancelling $x^2, y^2, z^2$:**

$$
-2xx_1 + x_1^2 - 2yy_1 + y_1^2 - 2zz_1 + z_1^2 = -2xx_2 + x_2^2 - 2yy_2 + y_2^2 - 2zz_2 + z_2^2
$$

**Rearranging (linear in $x, y, z$):**

$$
2x(x_2 - x_1) + 2y(y_2 - y_1) + 2z(z_2 - z_1) = x_2^2 + y_2^2 + z_2^2 - x_1^2 - y_1^2 - z_1^2
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \; 2(P_2 - P_1)^T C = \vert P_2 \vert^2 - \vert P_1 \vert^2
$$

**Equation 2 (between $P_1$ and $P_3$):**

$$
\Rightarrow \; 2(P_3 - P_1)^T C = \vert P_3 \vert^2 - \vert P_1 \vert^2
$$

**Stacking into matrix form $A C = b$:**

$$
A = 2 \begin{bmatrix} (P_2 - P_1)^T \\ (P_3 - P_1)^T \\ (P_4 - P_1)^T \end{bmatrix}, \quad
b = \begin{bmatrix} \vert P_2 \vert^2 - \vert P_1 \vert^2 \\ \vert P_3 \vert^2 - \vert P_1 \vert^2 \\ \vert P_4 \vert^2 - \vert P_1^2 \vert \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Solution (using 4th redundant point for least-squares):**

$$
C = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T b
$$

This is the **closed-form origin** of the fixed tool.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Orientation Error Metric (Angle-Axis Form)

Given the commanded rotation $R_{cmd}$ and the measured rotation $R_{meas}$:

$$
R_{err} = R_{cmd}^T R_{meas}
$$

**Step 1 — Extract trace:**

$$
\text{tr}(R_{err}) = 1 + 2\cos\theta
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for angle $\theta$:**

$$
\theta = \arccos\left(\frac{\text{tr}(R_{err}) - 1}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Extract axis (skew-symmetric part):**

$$
\hat{n} = \frac{1}{2\sin\theta}\begin{bmatrix} R_{err}(3,2) - R_{err}(2,3) \\ R_{err}(1,3) - R_{err}(3,1) \\ R_{err}(2,1) - R_{err}(1,2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Final error quaternion:**

$$
q_{err} = \left(\cos\frac{\theta}{2},\ \hat{n}\sin\frac{\theta}{2}\right)
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Error Compensation Update Law

The calibrated tool frame is updated iteratively to minimize the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)**:

$$
J(\theta_T) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert p_i^{cmd} - p_i^{meas}(\theta_T) \vert^2
$$

**Gradient descent update:**

$$
\theta_T^{(k+1)} = \theta_T^{(k)} - \alpha \nabla_{\theta_T} J
$$

where $\alpha$ is the **learning rate** (typically $0.001$–$0.1$).

**Closed-form linearized solution (Gauss-Newton):**

$$
\Delta\theta_T = (J_{jac}^T J_{jac})^{-1} J_{jac}^T \epsilon
$$

where $J_{jac}$ is the **Jacobian of measured positions w.r.t. tool parameters** and $\epsilon$ is the residual error vector.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix Summary

| Derivation | Input | Output | Use Case |
|------------|-------|--------|----------|
| TCP origin (3-pt sphere) | 3+ non-collinear touch points | TCP position $C$ | Position calibration |
| Orientation error | $R_{cmd}, R_{meas}$ | $\theta, \hat{n}$ | Orientation accuracy check |
| Iterative update | $N$ measured points | Refined $\theta_T$ | Full 6-DOF calibration |
| Conditioning number | Jacobian $J(q)$ | $\sigma_{max}/\sigma_{min}$ | Singularity avoidance |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

The Mermaid diagram below illustrates the **frame-propagation chain** from the robot base to the external fixed tool, including the calibration data flow.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**

**Q1. Differentiate between Robot-Mounted Tool and External Fixed Tool calibration. Why is the calibration procedure different in both cases? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

In **Robot-Mounted Tool** calibration, the tool is rigidly attached to the robot's end-effector, and the calibration goal is to find the transformation from the **flange frame $\{F\}$** to the **tool frame $\{T\}$**, denoted ${}^{F}_{T}T$. The tool moves with the robot, so calibration depends on the joint angles $q$.

In **External Fixed Tool (EFT)** calibration, the tool is **stationary in the workspace** and the robot carries a workpiece or probe. The calibration goal is to find the transformation from the **robot base frame $\{B\}$** to the **fixed tool frame $\{T\}$**, denoted ${}^{B}_{T}T$. Since the tool is fixed, multiple robot poses are recorded to compute its position via sphere-intersection and orientation via the cross-product method.

**Key Difference (1 mark):** The reference frame in the transformation matrix changes — flange-to-tool in the first case, base-to-tool in the second.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**Q2. State the formula for computing orientation error between the commanded and measured rotation matrices. What is the unit of the result? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The orientation error in **angle-axis form** is computed as:

$$
\theta = \arccos\left(\frac{\text{tr}(R_{cmd}^T R_{meas}) - 1}{2}\right)
$$

The **unit of the result is radians (rad)**, which can be converted to degrees using $1\ \text{rad} = 57.2958°$.

For a **6-DOF** robot, an acceptable orientation error after calibration is typically **$\theta < 0.5°$** ($\approx 8.7$ mrad).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Question Paper Pattern]**

### Question A — 14 Marks

**Q(a). With a neat block diagram, explain the six-point (ABC World) calibration procedure for an external fixed tool. List the assumptions made. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The six-point method is a systematic KTU-endorsed procedure:

1. **Mount a sharp probe** on the robot flange; the probe tip becomes the **TCP**.
2. **Pose 1:** Move the robot so the probe tip touches the tool's reference point.
3. **Pose 2:** Rotate the robot about the tool reference so the probe tip touches a point offset along the tool's **+X direction** (defined by a calibrated reference edge on the tool). This defines the X-axis.
4. **Pose 3:** Similarly, touch a point offset along the tool's **+Y direction**, defining the Y-axis.
5. **Pose 4, 5, 6:** Touch three more points at varied orientations to compute the **TCP origin** using the sphere-intersection method.
6. **Cross product:** Compute $\hat{z}_T = \hat{x}_T \times \hat{y}_T$.
7. **Build the homogeneous matrix** and upload to the controller.

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
- The tool is rigidly fixed during calibration.
- The robot's repeatability is significantly better than its absolute accuracy.
- The probe tip is sharp and well-defined.
- The reference edge/feature on the tool is precisely machined.

**Block diagram (4 marks):** As shown in SECTION_4.3.

**Why six points (1 mark):** Three points for X & Y axis direction (poses 1, 2, 3), and three more for the TCP origin (poses 4, 5, 6) — providing both **orientation** and **position** decoupling.

**Q(b). A robot touches an external fixed tool at three non-collinear points with the following world-frame coordinates:**
- $P_1 = (300, 100, 50)\ \text{mm}$
- $P_2 = (305, 102, 51)\ \text{mm}$
- $P_3 = (302, 105, 49)\ \text{mm}$

**Compute the TCP origin using the sphere-intersection method. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Using the derivation in SECTION 3.1, the matrix equation is $A C = b$ where:

$$
P_2 - P_1 = (5, 2, 1), \quad P_3 - P_1 = (2, 5, -1)
$$

$$
\vert P_1 \vert^2 = 300^2 + 100^2 + 50^2 = 90000 + 10000 + 2500 = 102500\ \text{mm}^2
$$

$$
\vert P_2 \vert^2 = 305^2 + 102^2 + 51^2 = 93025 + 10404 + 2601 = 106030\ \text{mm}^2
$$

$$
\vert P_3 \vert^2 = 302^2 + 105^2 + 49^2 = 91204 + 11025 + 2401 = 104630\ \text{mm}^2
$$

$$
A = 2 \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 & -1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
b = \begin{bmatrix} 106030 - 102500 \\ 104630 - 102500 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3530 \\ 2130 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
A C = b \;\Rightarrow\; C = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T b
$$

Computing $A^T A$:

$$
A^T A = 4 \begin{bmatrix} 25+4 & 10+10 & 5-2 \\ 10+10 & 4+25 & 2-5 \\ 5-2 & 2-5 & 1+1 \end{bmatrix} = 4 \begin{bmatrix} 29 & 20 & 3 \\ 20 & 29 & -3 \\ 3 & -3 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Using least-squares solution (or the analytical formula for 2 equations in 3 unknowns — underdetermined, requires a third equation or a constraint):

For a **physically meaningful** solution, we add a **third non-collinear point** $P_4 = (301, 103, 52)$ mm and repeat:

$$
P_4 - P_1 = (1, 3, 2), \quad \vert P_4 \vert^2 = 90601 + 10609 + 2704 = 103914\ \text{mm}^2
$$

$$
b = \begin{bmatrix} 3530 \\ 2130 \\ 1414 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
A = 2 \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 & -1 \\ 1 & 3 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Solving $A C = b$ (using Cramer's rule / matrix inversion):

$$
C \approx \begin{bmatrix} 301.5 \\ 102.8 \\ 50.3 \end{bmatrix}\ \text{mm}
$$

> **Final TCP Origin:** $C = (301.5,\ 102.8,\ 50.3)\ \text{mm}$ in the world frame.

**Note to student:** With only 3 points, the sphere center is at the **circumcenter of the triangle** $P_1 P_2 P_3$ — for full 3D sphere, **4 non-coplanar points** are needed.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative)

**Q(a). Explain the sources of error in industrial robot calibration. How does external fixed tool calibration mitigate these errors? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Sources of Error (4 marks):**

1. **Geometric errors:** Link length tolerances, joint offset errors, encoder quantization (typically $\pm 0.05°$), gear backlash.
2. **Non-geometric errors:** Link deflection under payload, joint compliance, thermal expansion (steel: $\approx 12\ \mu\text{m/m/°C}$).
3. **Kinematic model errors:** Simplified DH parameters vs. real geometry.
4. **Measurement noise:** Probe contact repeatability, fixture alignment.

**How EFT Calibration Mitigates (3 marks):**

- By **directly measuring** the tool position with the robot's own probe, the calibration **bypasses the cumulative DH-parameter error chain** — the tool position is solved from observed poses, not from nominal forward kinematics.
- **Iterative refinement** (least-squares / Gauss-Newton) reduces the residual error to within sensor noise floor.
- **Repeatability is leveraged:** Robot repeatability ($\pm 0.02$ mm) is 10× better than absolute accuracy, so measured positions cluster tightly around the true mean.

**Q(b). A robot's commanded tool position is $p_{cmd} = (500, 200, 300)\ \text{mm}$ and the measured position is $p_{meas} = (500.8, 199.4, 300.3)\ \text{mm}$. The commanded orientation is given by $R_{cmd}$ and measured $R_{meas}$ such that $\text{tr}(R_{cmd}^T R_{meas}) = 2.985$. Compute the position error magnitude and orientation error angle. Comment on whether the robot meets a typical industrial accuracy threshold of 0.5 mm and 0.5°. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Position Error Vector (3 marks):**

$$
\Delta p = p_{cmd} - p_{meas} = (-0.8,\ 0.6,\ -0.3)\ \text{mm}
$$

**Position Error Magnitude:**

$$
e_{pos} = \sqrt{(-0.8)^2 + (0.6)^2 + (-0.3)^2} = \sqrt{0.64 + 0.36 + 0.09} = \sqrt{1.09} \approx 1.044\ \text{mm}
$$

**Orientation Error Angle (3 marks):**

$$
\theta = \arccos\left(\frac{2.985 - 1}{2}\right) = \arccos(0.9925) \approx 0.1225\ \text{rad} \approx 7.02°
$$

**Commentary (1 mark):**

- **Position:** $e_{pos} = 1.044\ \text{mm} > 0.5\ \text{mm}$ — **FAILS** the accuracy threshold.
- **Orientation:** $\theta = 7.02° >> 0.5°$ — **FAILS** catastrophically.
- **Conclusion:** The robot **must be recalibrated** using the six-point EFT procedure before deployment in precision tasks. Without calibration, the position error exceeds the threshold by 2× and the orientation error by 14×.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing frames:** Writing ${}^{F}_{T}T$ instead of ${}^{B}_{T}T$ for EFT — this is the #1 error and costs 2–3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Skipping units:** Forgetting to write mm or rad in numerical answers — examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 3. **Insufficient points:** Using only 2 or 3 points for sphere-intersection when **4 non-coplanar points** are mathematically required.
> 4. **Wrong cross product order:** Writing $\hat{z} = \hat{y} \times \hat{x}$ instead of $\hat{z} = \hat{x} \times \hat{y}$ — flips the right-hand rule.
> 5. **Ignoring the conditioning number:** A poorly conditioned Jacobian (Cond$(J) > 100$) makes the calibration numerically unstable — students must check.
> 6. **No mention of repeatability:** Examiners expect students to state that the robot's **repeatability is exploited** in calibration.
> 7. **Missing block diagram:** For 7-mark questions, the diagram is mandatory — without it, maximum score is 5/7.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> - **Definition:** External Fixed Tool calibration determines ${}^{B}_{T}T$ — the homogeneous transform from robot base to the stationary tool.
> - **Six-Point Method:** 3 points for orientation (X, Y axes) + 3 points for origin (sphere intersection).
> - **Z-axis formula:** $\hat{z}_T = \hat{x}_T \times \hat{y}_T$ (right-hand rule).
> - **TCP Origin:** Solved via $A C = b$ where $A$ contains pairwise coordinate differences.
> - **Orientation Error:** $\theta = \arccos\left(\frac{\text{tr}(R^T R_{err}) - 1}{2}\right)$.
> - **Position Error:** $e_{pos} = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2 + \Delta z^2}$.
> - **Kinematic Chain:** ${}^{B}_{T}T = {}^{B}_{F}T(q) \cdot {}^{F}_{W}T \cdot {}^{W}_{T}T$.
> - **Accuracy vs. Repeatability:** Calibration improves **accuracy** (absolute) by exploiting **repeatability** (relative).
> - **Typical industrial threshold:** Position $\le \mathbf{0.5\ mm}$, orientation $\le \mathbf{0.5°}$.
> - **Avoid singularities:** Ensure Cond$(J)$ is finite during the calibration poses.
> - **Thermal drift:** Re-calibrate if ambient temperature changes by more than $\mathbf{5°C}$ (for precision tasks).
> - **Frame naming convention:** Always use superscript-source-subscript-target notation ${}^{source}_{target}T$.
> - **Tool is fixed:** The workpiece moves to the tool — opposite of robot-mounted configuration.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Workpiece Calibration: Direct and Indirect
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT865 - INDUSTRIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Workpiece Calibration: Direct and Indirect

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Workpiece Calibration: Direct and Indirect

## 1. Core Technical Definition

In the context of industrial robotics, **Workpiece Calibration** is the procedure of establishing the spatial relationship between the **Robot Base Coordinate Frame** $\lbrace B \rbrace$ and the **Workpiece Coordinate Frame** $\lbrace W \rbrace$. This is achieved by computing a 4×4 homogeneous transformation matrix $T_W^B$ that precisely defines the position and orientation of the workpiece relative to the robot's base.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Workpiece calibration is the process of identifying the workpiece frame position and orientation with respect to the robot base frame to enable accurate tool path generation in world coordinates.

A robot's accuracy in performing programmed tasks (welding, drilling, deburring, painting, etc.) is fundamentally dependent on knowing *where the workpiece actually is* in the robot's working envelope. Even if the workpiece is placed at the "correct" nominal position, manufacturing tolerances in fixtures, clamping errors, and assembly stack-ups cause positional deviations that **must be quantified and compensated for** before motion commands are issued.

### Two Principal Categories

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The two mandatory calibration methodologies mandated by the KTU 2024 Scheme curriculum are:
> 1. **Direct Calibration** – Performed by physically moving the robot tool (TCP) to known geometric features of the workpiece.
> 2. **Indirect Calibration** – Performed using external metrology devices (laser tracker, CMM, optical CMM) to measure workpiece feature coordinates.

---

## 2. Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are blindfolded and asked to draw a straight line on a sheet of paper resting on a table. The paper might have shifted from its expected position due to a draft of wind. Before drawing, you would *touch* three corners of the paper with your fingertip to "register" its actual position and orientation. The mental map you now have of where the paper is — that's exactly what **workpiece calibration** does for the robot.

- **Direct Calibration** = Touching the paper corners with your finger (TCP).
- **Indirect Calibration** = Asking someone standing nearby with a measuring tape to tell you where the corners are.

Both approaches yield the same result: knowing exactly *where* the workpiece sits relative to you (or the robot). The choice depends on accessibility, accuracy demands, and physical constraints.

### Why is this Critical?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A typical 6-axis articulated robot has a **repeatability** of $\pm 0.05$ mm but an **absolute accuracy** of $\pm 1$ to $\pm 3$ mm. Without workpiece calibration, the robot will repeat its motions precisely but on the *wrong location*. Calibration bridges this repeatability-to-accuracy gap.

The standard metrics used in calibration literature include:

- **RMSE (Root Mean Square Error):** typically **$\sigma_{RMS} < 0.5$ mm** for industrial-grade calibration
- **Residual transformation error:** typically below **$\Delta T < 0.1$ mm**
- **Repeatability (ISO 9283):** quoted in **mm** with a confidence interval of **95%**

---

## 3. Coordinate Frames Involved

A minimum of **three coordinate frames** are involved in any workpiece calibration:

| Frame Symbol | Frame Name | Reference Point |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| $\lbrace B \rbrace$ | Base Frame | Fixed at the robot pedestal |
| $\lbrace T \rbrace$ | Tool Frame (TCP) | At the robot's end-effector tip |
| $\lbrace W \rbrace$ | Workpiece Frame | Defined on a feature of the workpiece |

The fundamental kinematic chain is:

$$\lbrace B \rbrace \;\longrightarrow\; \lbrace T \rbrace \;\longrightarrow\; \lbrace W \rbrace$$

The calibration objective is to compute $T_W^B$ (or its inverse $T_B^W$) with maximum geometric fidelity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1. The Homogeneous Transformation Backbone

Every calibration methodology ultimately reduces to solving for a 4×4 homogeneous transformation matrix of the form:

$$
T_W^B = \begin{bmatrix} R_W^B & \vec{p}_W^B \\ 0\;0\;0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Where:
- $R_W^B$ is a 3×3 rotation matrix (orthonormal, $\det = +1$) defining orientation.
- $\vec{p}_W^B$ is a 3×1 translation vector defining the workpiece origin in base coordinates.

The composition law for serial kinematic chains yields the fundamental calibration equation:

$$T_W^B \;=\; T_T^B \cdot T_W^T$$

where $T_T^B$ is obtained directly from the robot's forward kinematics solver at the current joint configuration, and $T_W^T$ is the tool-to-workpiece relationship at the instant of measurement.

---

## 2. Direct Calibration — Theory of Operation

Direct calibration requires that the robot's TCP be physically moved by the operator (in jog mode) to **three or more pre-selected reference points** on the workpiece. The robot's controller samples its internal joint encoders, computes $T_T^B$ via forward kinematics at each pose, and stores these as transformation snapshots.

### Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1:** Define at least **three non-collinear reference points** $P_1, P_2, P_3$ on the workpiece. A fourth point $P_4$ is often added to verify orientation (non-coplanar check).
- **Step 2:** Jog the TCP (or run a search routine) until it physically contacts $P_1$. Record the joint angles $\vec{q}_1$ and compute $T_{T,1}^B$.
- **Step 3:** Repeat for $P_2, P_3$ (and optionally $P_4$) recording $T_{T,2}^B$, $T_{T,3}^B$, $T_{T,4}^B$.
- **Step 4:** Assuming the TCP position vector coincides with the workpiece point at each contact:

$$\vec{p}_i^B \;=\; \vec{p}_{T,i}^B \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, 3, 4$$

- **Step 5:** Solve for $T_W^B$ using least-squares minimization of the residuals.
- **Step 6:** Validate by re-touching the points and checking that the re-projection error falls within tolerance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandate:** A minimum of **three non-collinear points** are required. A **fourth point** is mandatory for redundancy-based error verification. The points must not all lie on the same line, and (preferably) not all on the same plane.

### Mathematical Formulation

Let $P_i^W$ be the **known coordinates** of the $i$-th reference point expressed in the workpiece frame. The observed coordinates in the base frame are:

$$\hat{P}_i^B \;=\; T_W^B \cdot P_i^W$$

The objective is to minimize the sum of squared residuals:

$$J(T_W^B) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left\| \hat{P}_i^B - P_{T,i}^B \right\|^2$$

subject to the orthonormality constraint $R^T R = I$ and $\det(R) = +1$.

This is the classical **Wahba's Problem** in attitude estimation, solvable via the SVD (Singular Value Decomposition) method or the **Davenport's q-method** (quaternion-based).

---

## 3. Indirect Calibration — Theory of Operation

When the workpiece features are **inaccessible** to the robot's TCP (e.g., a deep cavity, an internal bore, an underwater structure, or a hazardous environment), direct calibration is impossible. In such cases, **indirect calibration** is deployed using external metrology.

### Working Principle

A **metrology device** (laser tracker, total station, photogrammetry rig, or CMM) measures the 3D coordinates of the workpiece's reference features in a **global or device frame** $\lbrace M \rbrace$. A separate measurement captures the position of the robot's TCP in the same metrology frame, allowing the system to mathematically compose the chain:

$$T_W^B \;=\; T_M^B \cdot T_W^M$$

Where:
- $T_M^B$ = transformation from metrology frame to robot base (calibrated once per session).
- $T_W^M$ = transformation from workpiece frame to metrology frame (measured).

### Categories of Indirect Calibration

> [!NOTE]
> **Three sub-categories** recognized in KTU syllabus:
> 1. **Constraint-Based Indirect Calibration:** The workpiece features impose geometric constraints (parallelism, perpendicularity) that the calibration algorithm exploits. Reduces the number of free parameters from 6 to 3 or 4.
> 2. **External Sensor Indirect Calibration:** A laser tracker or stereo-camera rig measures TCP and workpiece feature positions simultaneously.
> 3. **Master-Slave Indirect Calibration:** A master robot (CMM-like) measures the TCP of a slave robot while both are referenced to a common workpiece.

---

## 4. KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Use | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $T_W^B = T_T^B \cdot T_W^T$ | Master calibration composition law | dimensionless matrix |
| 2 | $\vec{p}_W^B = \vec{p}_{T}^B - R_T^B \cdot \vec{p}_W^T$ | Translation component extraction | mm |
| 3 | $R_W^B = R_T^B \cdot R_W^T$ | Rotation composition rule | dimensionless |
| 4 | $J = \sum \Vert \hat{P}_i^B - P_{T,i}^B \Vert^2$ | Least-squares cost function | $\text{mm}^2$ |
| 5 | $\sigma_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \Vert \hat{P}_i^B - P_{T,i}^B \Vert^2}$ | Root-mean-square calibration error | mm |
| 6 | $\vec{p}_i^B = R_W^B \cdot \vec{p}_i^W + \vec{p}_W^B$ | Forward projection of workpiece point $i$ | mm |
| 7 | $\hat{q} = \arg\min_q \; \sum w_i \Vert \hat{P}_i^B - P_i^B \Vert^2$ | Wahba's problem / Davenport's q-method | quaternion |
| 8 | $\Delta T = \Vert \hat{T}_W^B - T_{W,\text{ref}}^B \Vert_F$ | Frobenius-norm deviation from reference | dimensionless |
| 9 | $T_M^B = T_T^B \cdot T_M^T$ | Frame chaining for indirect calibration | dimensionless |
| 10 | $H = \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i^W (P_{T,i}^B)^T$ | Cross-covariance matrix (Arun's method) | $\text{mm}^2$ |

### Notation Conventions

- $T_A^B$ = 4×4 homogeneous transformation matrix from frame $\lbrace A \rbrace$ to frame $\lbrace B \rbrace$.
- $R_A^B$ = 3×3 rotation sub-matrix.
- $\vec{p}_A^B$ = 3×1 translation vector.
- $w_i$ = scalar weighting coefficient (often $w_i = 1$).
- $N$ = number of calibration points (typically $N \geq 3$).

---

## 5. Engineering Real-World Utility

| Application Domain | Typical Calibration Method | Achievable Accuracy |
|:---|:---|:---:|
| Automotive body welding | Direct (3-point + verification) | $\pm 0.3$ mm |
| Aerospace fuselage drilling | Indirect (laser tracker) | $\pm 0.1$ mm |
| Large ship-hull panel cutting | Indirect (photogrammetry) | $\pm 1.0$ mm |
| PCB assembly pick-and-place | Direct (vision-assisted) | $\pm 0.05$ mm |
| Nuclear decommissioning | Indirect (constrained master-slave) | $\pm 0.5$ mm |
| Additive manufacturing (large-scale 3D printing) | Indirect (CMM-based) | $\pm 0.2$ mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry 4.0 Context:** Modern smart factories integrate workpiece calibration with digital twins. The calibrated $T_W^B$ is uploaded to the digital twin, allowing real-time virtual commissioning and OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) optimization.

---

## 6. Comparison Matrix — Direct vs Indirect

| Parameter | Direct Calibration | Indirect Calibration |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Measurement device | Robot's own joint encoders | External metrology (laser tracker, CMM) |
| Accessibility requirement | TCP must reach all features | Features may be inaccessible |
| Accuracy | $\pm 0.1$ to $\pm 0.5$ mm | $\pm 0.05$ to $\pm 0.2$ mm |
| Setup time | Low (5–15 min) | High (30–90 min) |
| Capital cost | Included in robot | Requires external device |
| Best for | Small/medium workpieces | Large/complex workpieces |
| Susceptible to | Joint backlash, encoder noise | Sensor noise, line-of-sight |
| KTU sub-topic weightage | High (≈ 60%) | High (≈ 40%) |
