# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 1. Derivation 1 — Composition Law for $T_W^B$

We begin from first principles. Consider a point $P$ described by its position vector in the workpiece frame as $\vec{p}^W \in \mathbb{R}^3$. We seek its representation in the base frame $\vec{p}^B$.

### Step 1 — Transformation from Workpiece Frame to Tool Frame

The relationship between $\vec{p}^W$ and $\vec{p}^T$ involves a rotation $R_W^T$ and a translation $\vec{p}_W^T$:

$$
\vec{p}^T \;=\; R_W^T \cdot \vec{p}^W \;+\; \vec{p}_W^T
$$

### Step 2 — Transformation from Tool Frame to Base Frame

Similarly, the relationship between $\vec{p}^T$ and $\vec{p}^B$ is:

$$
\vec{p}^B \;=\; R_T^B \cdot \vec{p}^T \;+\; \vec{p}_T^B
$$

### Step 3 — Substituting Step 1 into Step 2

$$
\vec{p}^B \;=\; R_T^B \cdot \bigl( R_W^T \cdot \vec{p}^W + \vec{p}_W^T \bigr) \;+\; \vec{p}_T^B
$$

### Step 4 — Distribution of Matrix Multiplication

$$
\vec{p}^B \;=\; \underbrace{\bigl( R_T^B \cdot R_W^T \bigr)}_{R_W^B} \cdot \vec{p}^W \;+\; \underbrace{\bigl( R_T^B \cdot \vec{p}_W^T + \vec{p}_T^B \bigr)}_{\vec{p}_W^B}
$$

### Step 5 — Final Compact Form

$$
\vec{p}^B \;=\; R_W^B \cdot \vec{p}^W \;+\; \vec{p}_W^B
$$

> This is the **classical 6-DOF rigid-body transformation**. When written in homogeneous form, it becomes:

$$
T_W^B \;=\; T_T^B \cdot T_W^T \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} R_T^B \cdot R_W^T & R_T^B \cdot \vec{p}_W^T + \vec{p}_T^B \\ 0\;0\;0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

## 2. Derivation 2 — Three-Point Direct Calibration (Closed Form)

Suppose we touch three non-collinear points $P_1, P_2, P_3$ on the workpiece. In the workpiece frame, these are known a priori:

$$
P_1^W = (x_1, y_1, z_1)^T, \quad P_2^W = (x_2, y_2, z_2)^T, \quad P_3^W = (x_3, y_3, z_3)^T
$$

The robot reports the corresponding TCP positions in the base frame:

$$
P_1^B = (X_1, Y_1, Z_1)^T, \quad P_2^B = (X_2, Y_2, Z_2)^T, \quad P_3^B = (X_3, Y_3, Z_3)^T
$$

### Step 1 — Define Workpiece Frame Vectors in Local Coordinates

Select $P_1$ as the workpiece origin. Define the workpiece-frame basis vectors from the known geometry:

$$
\vec{u}_W = \frac{P_2^W - P_1^W}{\Vert P_2^W - P_1^W \Vert}, \quad \vec{v}_W = \frac{P_3^W - P_1^W}{\Vert P_3^W - P_1^W \Vert}, \quad \vec{w}_W = \vec{u}_W \times \vec{v}_W
$$

### Step 2 — Define Base Frame Vectors from Observations

Apply the same construction using the measured TCP positions:

$$
\vec{u}_B = \frac{P_2^B - P_1^B}{\Vert P_2^B - P_1^B \Vert}, \quad \vec{v}_B = \frac{P_3^B - P_1^B}{\Vert P_3^B - P_1^B \Vert}, \quad \vec{w}_B = \vec{u}_B \times \vec{v}_B
$$

### Step 3 — Construct the Rotation Matrix

The rotation matrix that maps the workpiece basis to the base basis is:

$$
R_W^B \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \vec{u}_B & \vec{v}_B & \vec{w}_B \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} \vec{u}_W & \vec{v}_W & \vec{w}_W \end{bmatrix}^{-1}
$$

Because the basis vectors form an orthonormal right-handed triad, the inverse equals the transpose:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \vec{u}_W & \vec{v}_W & \vec{w}_W \end{bmatrix}^{-1} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \vec{u}_W & \vec{v}_W & \vec{w}_W \end{bmatrix}^{T}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Translation Vector

The translation $\vec{p}_W^B$ is the position of the workpiece origin $P_1^W$ in the base frame:

$$
\vec{p}_W^B \;=\; P_1^B
$$

### Step 5 — Assemble the Homogeneous Transformation

$$
T_W^B \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} R_W^B & \vec{p}_W^B \\ 0\;0\;0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} u_{Bx} & v_{Bx} & w_{Bx} & X_1 \\ u_{By} & v_{By} & w_{By} & Y_1 \\ u_{Bz} & v_{Bz} & w_{Bz} & Z_1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a 4th Point?** With three points, the rotation matrix has 9 elements constrained by 6 orthonormality conditions ($\det = +1$, columns unit), leaving only **3 independent parameters** (the 3 DOF of rotation). Using 4 points gives an **over-determined system** solvable by least squares for noise robustness.

---

## 3. Derivation 3 — Least-Squares Solution (Arun's Method for N Points)

For $N \geq 4$ calibration points, we use **Arun's Point-Set Registration Method** to obtain the optimal $T_W^B$.

### Step 1 — Compute Centroids

$$
\bar{P}^W = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i^W, \quad \bar{P}^B = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i^B
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the 3×3 Cross-Covariance Matrix

$$
H \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \bigl( P_i^W - \bar{P}^W \bigr) \cdot \bigl( P_i^B - \bar{P}^B \bigr)^T
$$

### Step 3 — Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)

Decompose $H$:

$$
H \;=\; U \cdot S \cdot V^T
$$

Where $U, V \in \mathbb{R}^{3\times 3}$ are orthonormal and $S = \text{diag}(s_1, s_2, s_3)$ with $s_1 \geq s_2 \geq s_3 \geq 0$.

### Step 4 — Compute the Optimal Rotation Matrix

$$
R_W^B \;=\; V \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \det(V U^T) \end{bmatrix} \cdot U^T
$$

The middle diagonal matrix (with determinant correction) ensures $\det(R_W^B) = +1$, preventing reflections.

### Step 5 — Compute the Optimal Translation Vector

$$
\vec{p}_W^B \;=\; \bar{P}^B - R_W^B \cdot \bar{P}^W
$$

### Step 6 — Compute the Residual Error Metric

$$
\sigma_{RMS} \;=\; \sqrt{ \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \bigl\| P_i^B - \bigl( R_W^B \cdot P_i^W + \vec{p}_W^B \bigr) \bigr\|^2 }
$$

---

## 4. Derivation 4 — Indirect Calibration via Frame Chaining

Suppose a laser tracker measures the workpiece origin and three feature points in its own frame $\lbrace L \rbrace$. Let these be $P_i^L$ for $i = 1, 2, 3$.

### Step 1 — Compute Workpiece-to-Laser Transformation

Apply the three-point algorithm above to obtain:

$$
T_W^L
$$

### Step 2 — Measure Robot-TCP-to-Laser Transformation

Place the robot TCP at a known configuration; the laser tracker measures the TCP position in $\lbrace L \rbrace$, giving $\vec{p}_{T}^L$. The robot's forward kinematics provides $T_T^B$. Therefore:

$$
T_B^L \;=\; T_T^L \cdot T_B^T \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} I & \vec{p}_T^L \\ 0\;0\;0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \bigl( T_T^B \bigr)^{-1}
$$

(Note: $T_B^T = (T_T^B)^{-1}$ and we assume the TCP rotation aligns with the base frame for simplicity, which is the standard case when the laser tracker measures only the TCP sphere center.)

### Step 3 — Compose to Get Workpiece-to-Base Transformation

$$
T_W^B \;=\; T_L^B \cdot T_W^L \;=\; \bigl( T_B^L \bigr)^{-1} \cdot T_W^L
$$

### Step 4 — Verification Step

Re-measure at least one feature point with the laser tracker and verify that the predicted and measured positions agree within $\sigma_{RMS} < 0.5$ mm.

---

## 5. Analytical Matrix — Computational Workflow Comparison

| Stage | Direct Calibration | Indirect Calibration |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1. Preparation | Define 3 reference features on workpiece CAD | Mount laser tracker / CMM; warm up sensors |
| 2. Measurement | Jog robot TCP to each feature; sample encoders | Laser tracker captures 3D coordinates of features |
| 3. Data collection | $N \geq 3$ joint-angle sets | $N \geq 3$ point clouds in sensor frame |
| 4. Frame composition | Forward kinematics to get $T_T^B$ | Sensor internal processing to get $P_i^L$ |
| 5. Core computation | Arun's SVD method | Frame chaining via $T_W^B = T_L^B \cdot T_W^L$ |
| 6. Verification | Re-touch points and check $\sigma_{RMS}$ | Re-measure points and check $\sigma_{RMS}$ |
| 7. Update world model | Overwrite $T_W^B$ in robot controller | Overwrite $T_W^B$ in robot controller |
| 8. Cycle time | 5–10 minutes | 30–60 minutes |

```mermaid
    nodeREG["Three Point Registration Algorithm"]:::algoNode
    nodeSVD["SVD Decomposition Module"]:::algoNode
    nodeSOL["Solution T_W_B R and p"]:::dataNode
    nodeCTRL["Robot Controller Workpiece Frame Register"]:::ctrlNode
    nodePATH["Motion Path Generator"]:::ctrlNode
    nodeROBOT["6 Axis Robot Arm"]:::actNode

    nodeJK --> nodeFK --> nodeTTB --> nodeREG --> nodeSVD --> nodeSOL --> nodeCTRL --> nodePATH --> nodeROBOT
    nodeROBOT -. "Feedback TCP position" .-> nodeJK
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define workpiece calibration. Why is it necessary in an industrial robotic cell?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Workpiece calibration is the procedure of computing the homogeneous transformation matrix $T_W^B$ that defines the position and orientation of the workpiece coordinate frame $\lbrace W \rbrace$ relative to the robot base frame $\lbrace B \rbrace$.

**(1 mark)** Formal definition with equation $T_W^B = T_T^B \cdot T_W^T$.

**(1 mark)** Necessity — Robots have high **repeatability** ($\pm 0.05$ mm) but low **absolute accuracy** ($\pm 1$ to $\pm 3$ mm) due to kinematic parameter errors, gear backlash, link deflection, and thermal drift. Without calibration, programmed paths would be systematically offset from their intended locations.

**(1 mark)** Practical significance — Calibration corrects fixture errors, thermal expansion, and re-fixturing deviations, enabling the robot to perform precision tasks like drilling, welding, and inspection with sub-millimeter accuracy in production environments.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between direct and indirect workpiece calibration with two key parameters.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Direct Calibration | Indirect Calibration |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Measurement principle | Uses robot's own TCP to touch features | Uses external sensor (laser tracker, CMM) |
| Accessibility | TCP must reach features | Features may be inaccessible |
| Accuracy range | $\pm 0.1$ to $\pm 0.5$ mm | $\pm 0.05$ to $\pm 0.2$ mm |
| Setup cost | Low (uses existing robot) | High (external metrology required) |

**(1 mark)** Each distinction well-explained (any two distinguishing parameters).

**(2 marks)** Each supported with a real-world example (e.g., direct for body-in-white welding; indirect for aerospace fuselage).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Module 3 long-answer questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice between two questions. Each question typically has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the three-point direct calibration method in detail. Derive the rotation matrix $R_W^B$ and translation vector $\vec{p}_W^B$ using the geometric construction approach.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the workpiece frame. Select three non-collinear reference points $P_1, P_2, P_3$ on the workpiece. Choose $P_1$ as the origin. The vectors $\vec{P_1 P_2}$ and $\vec{P_1 P_3}$ define the workpiece's local X- and Y-axes respectively (per CAD model).

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Express points in workpiece coordinates. From the CAD model:

$$
\vec{u}_W = \frac{P_2^W - P_1^W}{\Vert P_2^W - P_1^W \Vert}, \quad \vec{v}_W = \frac{P_3^W - P_1^W}{\Vert P_3^W - P_1^W \Vert}, \quad \vec{w}_W = \vec{u}_W \times \vec{v}_W
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Express the same points in base coordinates by touching them with the TCP and reading joint encoders:

$$
\vec{u}_B = \frac{P_2^B - P_1^B}{\Vert P_2^B - P_1^B \Vert}, \quad \vec{v}_B = \frac{P_3^B - P_1^B}{\Vert P_3^B - P_1^B \Vert}, \quad \vec{w}_B = \vec{u}_B \times \vec{v}_B
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Construct the rotation matrix. The rotation that maps $\vec{u}_W, \vec{v}_W, \vec{w}_W$ to $\vec{u}_B, \vec{v}_B, \vec{w}_B$ is:

$$
R_W^B = \begin{bmatrix} \vec{u}_B & \vec{v}_B & \vec{w}_B \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \vec{u}_W & \vec{v}_W & \vec{w}_W \end{bmatrix}^{T}
$$

$$
R_W^B = \begin{bmatrix} u_B & v_B & w_B \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} u_W^T \\ v_W^T \\ w_W^T \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Translation vector:

$$
\vec{p}_W^B = P_1^B
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Assemble the homogeneous matrix:

$$
T_W^B = \begin{bmatrix} R_W^B & \vec{p}_W^B \\ 0\;0\;0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A robot TCP touches three points of a workpiece. The base-frame coordinates are:

- $P_1^B = (500, 200, 300)^T$ mm
- $P_2^B = (600, 200, 300)^T$ mm
- $P_3^B = (500, 300, 300)^T$ mm

The CAD-defined workpiece coordinates are:

- $P_1^W = (0, 0, 0)^T$, $P_2^W = (100, 0, 0)^T$, $P_3^W = (0, 100, 0)^T$

Compute $T_W^B$ and verify by computing the calibration residual $\sigma_{RMS}$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute workpiece-frame basis vectors:

$$
\vec{u}_W = (1, 0, 0)^T, \quad \vec{v}_W = (0, 1, 0)^T, \quad \vec{w}_W = (0, 0, 1)^T
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute base-frame basis vectors:

$$
\vec{u}_B = \frac{(600, 200, 300) - (500, 200, 300)}{100} = (1, 0, 0)^T
$$

$$
\vec{v}_B = \frac{(500, 300, 300) - (500, 200, 300)}{100} = (0, 1, 0)^T
$$

$$
\vec{w}_B = \vec{u}_B \times \vec{v}_B = (0, 0, 1)^T
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Rotation matrix:

$$
R_W^B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = I_3
$$

(No rotation; the workpiece axes are aligned with the base frame.)

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Translation vector:

$$
\vec{p}_W^B = P_1^B = (500, 200, 300)^T \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Final homogeneous matrix:

$$
T_W^B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & 500 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 200 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 300 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 (verification, included in marks):** Since the basis vectors match exactly and the points are noise-free, the residual $\sigma_{RMS} = 0$ mm. In practice, $\sigma_{RMS} < 0.1$ mm indicates an excellent calibration.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the indirect calibration method using a laser tracker. Derive the frame-chaining equation for computing $T_W^B$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Working principle. A laser tracker emits a laser beam that reflects off a retroreflective target (spherically mounted retroreflector, SMR). The tracker measures the beam's azimuth, elevation, and radial distance, computing the target's 3D position in its own frame $\lbrace L \rbrace$ at **sub-millimeter accuracy** (typically $\pm 50 \, \mu\text{m} + 6 \, \mu\text{m/m}$).

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Measurement procedure:
- Mount the laser tracker at a stable, vibration-isolated location.
- Three (or more) reference features on the workpiece are sequentially targeted by the laser beam. Their coordinates $P_i^L$ are recorded.
- The robot's TCP (holding the SMR) is also measured, giving $\vec{p}_T^L$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Frame composition. Define:
- $T_B^L$ = transformation from base to laser frame (one-time calibration per session).
- $T_W^L$ = transformation from workpiece to laser frame (from three-point registration).

The sought calibration result is:

$$
T_W^B = T_L^B \cdot T_W^L = (T_B^L)^{-1} \cdot T_W^L
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Practical computation of $T_B^L$. Place the robot TCP at the SMR. The robot reports $T_T^B$ via forward kinematics. The laser tracker reports $T_T^L$. Therefore:

$$
T_B^L = T_T^L \cdot T_B^T = T_T^L \cdot (T_T^B)^{-1}
$$

**Step 5 (extra for completeness):** Substituting back:

$$
T_W^B = \bigl( T_T^L \cdot (T_T^B)^{-1} \bigr)^{-1} \cdot T_W^L = T_T^B \cdot (T_T^L)^{-1} \cdot T_W^L
$$

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A four-point Arun's least-squares calibration is performed. The cross-covariance matrix $H$ and SVD $H = U S V^T$ yield:

$$
U = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 & -0.6 & 0 \\ 0.6 & 0.8 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad V = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad S = \text{diag}(50, 30, 20)
$$

The centroids are $\bar{P}^W = (10, 20, 5)^T$ mm and $\bar{P}^B = (510, 220, 305)^T$ mm. Compute $R_W^B$ and $\vec{p}_W^B$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute the determinant correction:

$$
\det(V U^T) = \det(V) \cdot \det(U^T) = (+1) \cdot (+1) = +1
$$

Therefore, the correction matrix is the identity:

$$
D = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & +1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the rotation matrix:

$$
R_W^B = V \cdot D \cdot U^T = I \cdot I \cdot U^T = U^T
$$

$$
R_W^B = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 & 0.6 & 0 \\ -0.6 & 0.8 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Verify orthonormality:
- $R \cdot R^T = I_3$ ✓
- $\det(R_W^B) = (0.8)(0.8)(1) - (0.6)(-0.6)(1) = 0.64 + 0.36 = +1$ ✓

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Compute the translation vector:

$$
\vec{p}_W^B = \bar{P}^B - R_W^B \cdot \bar{P}^W
$$

$$
R_W^B \cdot \bar{P}^W = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 & 0.6 & 0 \\ -0.6 & 0.8 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 10 \\ 20 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 8 + 12 + 0 \\ -6 + 16 + 0 \\ 0 + 0 + 5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 20 \\ 10 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\vec{p}_W^B = \begin{bmatrix} 510 \\ 220 \\ 305 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 20 \\ 10 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 490 \\ 210 \\ 300 \end{bmatrix} \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 (extra for clarity):** Final homogeneous matrix:

$$
T_W^B = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 & 0.6 & 0 & 490 \\ -0.6 & 0.8 & 0 & 210 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 300 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The rotation represents a **counter-clockwise rotation about the Z-axis** by $\theta = \arctan(0.6 / 0.8) \approx 36.87°$.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the homogeneous row** — Students frequently write the 3×3 rotation and 3×1 translation but forget the bottom row `[0 0 0 1]`. KTU examiners deduct **1 full mark** for this omission.
>
> 2. **Confusing $T_W^B$ with $T_B^W$** — Many students interchange these in their derivations. Always verify: $T_B^W = (T_W^B)^{-1}$.
>
> 3. **Ignoring orthonormality** — When solving numerical problems, students often forget to verify $\det(R) = +1$. KTU explicitly checks this for 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Three collinear points** — Using three collinear points makes $R_W^B$ singular (determinant = 0). Always emphasize "**non-collinear**" — a favorite trick question in KTU papers.
>
> 5. **Skipping the 4th verification point** — In 14-mark questions, students often stop at 3 points. The 4th point's role in residual computation is worth **at least 2 marks**.
>
> 6. **Unit mismatch** — Mixing mm and m in calibration residuals. The standard unit is **mm**.
>
> 7. **Wrong cross-product direction** — Using $\vec{w} = \vec{v} \times \vec{u}$ instead of $\vec{w} = \vec{u} \times \vec{v}$ yields a left-handed frame and fails the $\det = +1$ test.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Workpiece Calibration**

- **Core Objective:** Compute $T_W^B$ relating workpiece frame to robot base frame.

- **Master Equation:** $T_W^B = T_T^B \cdot T_W^T$ — the composition law for serial kinematic chains.

- **Direct Calibration:**
  - Uses robot's own TCP.
  - Requires **minimum 3 non-collinear points**.
  - **4th point** recommended for residual computation.
  - Cost function: $J = \sum \Vert \hat{P}_i^B - P_{T,i}^B \Vert^2$.
  - Solve via **Arun's SVD method** or Davenport's q-method.
  - Best for small-to-medium workpieces, accessible features.

- **Indirect Calibration:**
  - Uses external metrology (laser tracker, CMM, photogrammetry).
  - Frame chain: $T_W^B = T_L^B \cdot T_W^L$.
  - Sub-categories: constraint-based, external-sensor, master-slave.
  - Best for large workpieces, inaccessible features, hazardous environments.

- **Performance Metric:** $\sigma_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum \Vert \hat{P}_i^B - P_{T,i}^B \Vert^2}$; target **$\sigma_{RMS} < 0.5$ mm** for industrial-grade work.

- **Rotation Matrix Validity Checks:**
  - $R \cdot R^T = I_3$
  - $\det(R) = +1$
  - Columns (and rows) form a right-handed orthonormal triad.

- **Cross-Covariance Matrix (Arun's Method):**

$$
H = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (P_i^W - \bar{P}^W)(P_i^B - \bar{P}^B)^T
$$

followed by SVD: $H = U S V^T$, then $R = V \cdot \text{diag}(1, 1, \det(V U^T)) \cdot U^T$.

- **Typical Accuracy Bands:**
  - Direct: $\pm 0.1$ to $\pm 0.5$ mm.
  - Indirect: $\pm 0.05$ to $\pm 0.2$ mm.

- **Industrial Applications:**
  - Automotive: body-in-white welding (direct).
  - Aerospace: fuselage drilling (indirect).
  - Marine: hull panel cutting (indirect + photogrammetry).
  - Nuclear: decommissioning (indirect + remote sensors).

- **Industry 4.0 Hook:** Calibrated $T_W^B$ is uploaded to the **digital twin** for virtual commissioning and real-time path verification.

- **Common Pitfall (KTU):** Forgetting to verify $\det(R) = +1$ costs marks; three collinear points make $R$ singular — always say **"non-collinear"** explicitly.

- **Mnemonic for Direct Calibration Steps:** **D-R-T-F-V** → **D**efine points, **R**each with TCP, **T**ouch & record, **F**orward kinematics, **V**erify with $\sigma_{RMS}$.

- **Mnemonic for Indirect Calibration:** **L-C-C-V** → **L**aser mount, **C**apture features, **C**ompose frames, **V**erify.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 — Workpiece Calibration Notes.** These notes align with KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcomes (CO3 — Robot Programming & Calibration) and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels: **L2 (Understand)**, **L3 (Apply)**, **L4 (Analyze)**, and **L5 (Evaluate)**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Imaging Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT865 - INDUSTRIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Imaging Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Imaging Components in Industrial Robotics

**Imaging Components** in industrial robotics refer to the integrated set of electro-optical hardware elements that capture, condition, and digitize visual information from the robot's operational workspace. These components collectively form the **Machine Vision System (MVS)** — the "eyes" of an industrial robot. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme, imaging components encompass the sensors, optics, illumination sources, frame grabbers, and supporting electronics that enable a robot to perceive its environment non-destructively and in real time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Aligned):**
> *Imaging Components are the hardware subsystems of a robotic vision system responsible for converting optical information from a scene into a structured digital representation (image matrix) that can be processed by algorithms for object recognition, localization, inspection, and guidance.*

The formal classification of imaging components under KTU's Industrial Robotics framework includes:

| # | Component Category | Role |
|---|--------------------|------|
| 1 | **Image Sensors** (CCD, CMOS) | Photon-to-electron conversion |
| 2 | **Optics / Lenses** | Focus and project light onto sensor |
| 3 | **Illumination Subsystem** | Control lighting conditions |
| 4 | **Camera Electronics** | Timing, gain, exposure control |
| 5 | **Frame Grabber / Interface** | Digitization and data transfer |
| 6 | **Housing & Filters** | Environmental protection, spectral control |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric:** The standard resolution of an industrial camera is measured in **pixels (Picture Elements)**, with typical values ranging from **640 × 480 (VGA)** up to **5000 × 5000 (high-resolution inspection cameras)**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Human Eye vs. Robotic Vision"

Imagine walking into a **completely darkroom** and trying to identify a friend's face. Even with perfect memory, you cannot see because there is no light. Now imagine shining a torch, looking through spectacles, and your brain instantly recognizing the face — that is precisely what an imaging system does for a robot.

| Human Vision | Robotic Imaging System | Analogy |
|--------------|------------------------|---------|
| Retina (rods & cones) | **CCD / CMOS Sensor** | Light-capturing surface |
| Pupil & Iris | **Lens + Aperture** | Controls light entering |
| Eyelid / Eyebrow | **Illumination Source** | Adjusts scene brightness |
| Optic Nerve | **Frame Grabber / Data Cable** | Carries signal to brain |
| Brain (Visual Cortex) | **Image Processor / CPU** | Interprets the data |

So when a robotic arm picks a defective chip from a conveyor belt, the **imaging components** work exactly like a human inspector's eyes — but with **zero fatigue, micron-level accuracy, and operation in pitch darkness using IR illumination**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why are imaging components critical in KTU robotics?**
> Without imaging, a robot is essentially "blind" — capable only of repetitive, pre-programmed motion. Imaging components unlock **flexible automation** where the robot adapts to part variation, position changes, and defects.

## 1.3 The Role of Imaging in the Robot Perception Pipeline

In a typical KTU 2024-aligned industrial robotics application, the flow is:

**Scene → Light → Lens → Sensor → Signal → Digitization → Processing → Robot Action**

This is a **5-stage hardware chain** (Light, Lens, Sensor, Electronics, Interface) before any algorithm even runs. Each stage is an *imaging component* and is graded in KTU exams for its individual function.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Component-by-Component Theoretical Breakdown

### A. Image Sensors — The Core Imaging Component

The **image sensor** is a semiconductor integrated circuit that converts incident photons (light) into electrical charge, which is then read out as a voltage proportional to light intensity.

#### A.1 CCD (Charge-Coupled Device)

- **Working Principle:** Photons strike the silicon substrate, generating electron-hole pairs via the **photoelectric effect**. These electrons are collected in **potential wells** (pixels) and then sequentially transferred row-by-row to a common output amplifier using clocked voltage pulses.
- **Key Property:** Charge is **transported**, not copied — hence "Charge-Coupled".
- **Strengths:** Low noise, high uniformity, excellent for **scientific and metrology-grade imaging**.
- **Weakness:** Higher power consumption; requires complex driving circuitry.

#### A.2 CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor)

- **Working Principle:** Each pixel has its **own amplifier and readout transistor** (active pixel sensor architecture).
- **Key Property:** Charge is **converted to voltage in-pixel** and read out via row/column addressing like a memory array.
- **Strengths:** Lower power, on-chip integration of ADC, faster readout, cheaper to manufacture.
- **Weakness:** Historically noisier than CCD; modern CMOS has largely closed this gap.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Comparison:**
> CCD is preferred in **high-precision inspection**; CMOS dominates in **high-speed robotic applications** (e.g., conveyor tracking, bin picking) due to **global shutter** capability.

### B. Optics / Lenses

The lens system performs two critical functions:
1. **Collects light** from the scene.
2. **Projects a focused image** onto the sensor plane.

#### B.1 The Fundamental Lens Equation

$$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{u} + \frac{1}{v}$$

Where:
- $f$ = focal length (mm)
- $u$ = object distance (mm)
- $v$ = image distance (mm)

#### B.2 Field of View (FOV)

The FOV defines the visible area of the scene that the sensor captures:

$$\text{FOV} = 2 \cdot d \cdot \tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$$

Where $d$ is the working distance and $\theta$ is the lens's **angular field of view** (determined by sensor size and focal length):

$$\theta = 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\frac{h}{2f}\right)$$

Here $h$ is the sensor height (or width for horizontal FOV).

#### B.3 Magnification (M)

$$M = \frac{v}{u} = \frac{\text{Image Size}}{\text{Object Size}}$$

### C. Illumination Subsystem

Controlled lighting is **often more important than the camera itself** in machine vision. KTU emphasizes that **70% of vision system failures** are due to improper lighting.

Common illumination techniques:

| Technique | Application | Lighting Type |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|
| **Backlighting** | Silhouette, edge detection | LED panel behind object |
| **Diffuse Dome Lighting** | Highly reflective surfaces | Ring of LEDs in dome |
| **Structured Light** | 3D profiling | Laser line projector + camera |
| **Dark Field Illumination** | Detecting surface defects | Low-angle LED |
| **Bright Field** | General inspection | Standard ring light |
| **Strobe / Pulsed** | High-speed moving parts | Xenon or high-power LED |

### D. Camera Electronics & Frame Grabber

- **Camera Electronics** control:
  - **Exposure time** (shutter speed)
  - **Gain** (analog/digital amplification)
  - **Triggering** (synchronization with robot/conveyor)
  - **White balance**
- **Frame Grabber** is a dedicated hardware card that:
  - Receives analog video signal (in older systems) **or** digital stream
  - Performs **A/D conversion** (in analog systems)
  - Buffers frames and transfers to host via **PCIe, GigE Vision, USB3 Vision, Camera Link**

### E. Spectral Filtering

- **Optical Filters** restrict the wavelengths reaching the sensor.
- Common types: **band-pass, long-pass, short-pass, polarizer, IR-cut filter**.
- Useful for suppressing ambient light, isolating fluorescent markers, or working in IR spectrum.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{u} + \frac{1}{v}$ | Thin lens equation | $f, u, v$ in mm |
| 2 | $M = \frac{v}{u}$ | Linear magnification | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{FOV} = 2d \cdot \tan(\theta/2)$ | Field of view | mm |
| 4 | $\theta = 2\arctan\left(\frac{h}{2f}\right)$ | Angular FOV | Radians / degrees |
| 5 | $\text{Resolution} = \frac{\text{FOV}}{\text{Pixel Count}}$ | Spatial resolution (mm/pixel) | mm/pixel |
| 6 | $D = \frac{f}{\#} = \frac{f}{N}$ | Aperture diameter (f-number $N$) | mm |
| 7 | $E_v = \frac{L \cdot \pi \cdot \tau \cdot \cos^4\omega}{4 \cdot N^2 \cdot (1+M)^2}$ | Image irradiance (vignetting included) | W/m² |
| 8 | $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | dB |
| 9 | $t_{\text{exp}} = \frac{\text{Flux} \cdot \text{ISO}}{L \cdot A}$ | Exposure time approximation | seconds |
| 10 | $\text{Frame Rate} = \frac{1}{t_{\text{line}} \cdot N_{\text{lines}}}$ | Camera frame rate | fps |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used in KTU problems to compute lens selection, working distance, required sensor resolution, and camera placement for given inspection tasks (e.g., "find the focal length needed to image a 100 mm part with 0.1 mm/pixel resolution on a 2/3" sensor").

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Field of View (FOV) from Lens Geometry

We derive the FOV of a camera-lens system from first principles, as this is a high-frequency derivation in KTU 2024 exams.

### Starting Point — Geometric Ray Diagram

A thin lens forms an image according to the paraxial approximation. The half-angle of the cone of light accepted by the lens is determined by the sensor's linear dimension and the focal length.

**Step 1: Identify the geometry**

Consider a sensor of height $h$ placed at the image plane, distance $v = f$ from the lens (object at infinity gives $v = f$). Rays from a point on the optical axis at infinity strike the lens parallel to the axis and converge to the focal point.

For a finite object, the half-angle subtended at the lens is:

$$\tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) = \frac{h/2}{f}$$

**Step 2: Cross-multiply**

Multiplying both sides by 2 to get the full angle:

$$\tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) = \frac{h}{2f}$$

**Step 3: Solve for $\theta$**

$$\theta = 2 \arctan\left(\frac{h}{2f}\right)$$

**Step 4: Relate $\theta$ to FOV at a working distance $d$**

The FOV at distance $d$ (where the lens is focused) is:

$$\text{FOV} = 2 d \tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$$

Substituting $\theta$:

$$\text{FOV} = 2 d \tan\left(\arctan\left(\frac{h}{2f}\right)\right) = 2 d \cdot \frac{h}{2f}$$

$$\boxed{\text{FOV} = \frac{d \cdot h}{f}}$$

> **Conversion Logic Note:** This is the working **FOV formula** used universally in machine vision engineering. The term $h/f$ is intrinsic to the lens-sensor combination, and multiplying by $d$ scales it to the actual scene distance.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A robotic inspection cell uses a lens of focal length $f = 25$ mm mounted on a sensor of height $h = 9.6$ mm (2/3" format). If the working distance is $d = 500$ mm, find the field of view.

**Solution:**

$$\text{FOV} = \frac{d \cdot h}{f} = \frac{500 \times 9.6}{25} = \frac{4800}{25} = 192 \text{ mm}$$

**Interpretation:** The camera sees a 192 mm wide region at 500 mm working distance.

**Resolution check:** If the sensor has 1024 horizontal pixels, spatial resolution is:

$$\text{Resolution} = \frac{\text{FOV}}{\text{Pixels}} = \frac{192}{1024} = 0.1875 \text{ mm/pixel}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Spatial Resolution Constraint for Inspection

A common KTU problem: *Find the minimum number of pixels required to detect a defect of size $d_{\min}$.*

**Step 1: Required Nyquist sampling**

Per the **Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem**, to resolve a feature, you need at least **2 pixels per feature dimension**:

$$N_{\text{pixels}} = \frac{2 \cdot \text{FOV}}{d_{\min}}$$

**Step 2: Substitute FOV formula**

$$N_{\text{pixels}} = \frac{2 \cdot d \cdot h}{f \cdot d_{\min}}$$

**Step 3: Example calculation**

Given: $d = 300$ mm, $h = 7.2$ mm (1/2" sensor), $f = 16$ mm, $d_{\min} = 0.2$ mm.

$$N_{\text{pixels}} = \frac{2 \times 300 \times 7.2}{16 \times 0.2} = \frac{4320}{3.2} = 1350 \text{ pixels}$$

**Result:** A camera with at least **1350 pixels** is required along the critical dimension. A standard 2 MP (1600 × 1200) camera suffices.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Sensor Selection for Common Robotic Tasks

| Robotic Task | Sensor Type | Shutter | Frame Rate | Lighting |
|--------------|-------------|---------|------------|----------|
| Pick & Place (static) | CMOS, Area scan | Global | 30 fps | Diffuse dome |
| Conveyor tracking | CMOS, Line scan | Global | >1 kHz | LED bar |
| Weld seam inspection | CMOS, Area scan | Global | 60 fps | Laser line |
| PCB defect detection | CCD, Area scan | Global | 15 fps | Ring light |
| 3D bin picking | CMOS + Structured light | Global + Modulated | 30 fps | Laser projector |
| Pharma label reading | CMOS | Global | 30 fps | IR backlight |

## 3.4 Derivation: Exposure Time for Moving Objects

For a moving object with velocity $v$ on a conveyor, the maximum allowable exposure to avoid **motion blur** exceeding 1 pixel is:

$$t_{\text{exp}} = \frac{\text{Pixel Size on Object}}{v}$$

The pixel size on object is:

$$p_{\text{obj}} = \frac{\text{FOV}}{N_{\text{pixels}}} = \frac{d \cdot h}{f \cdot N_{\text{pixels}}}$$

Therefore:

$$t_{\text{exp}} = \frac{d \cdot h}{f \cdot N_{\text{pixels}} \cdot v}$$

**Example:** $d = 400$ mm, $h = 7.2$ mm, $f = 12$ mm, $N_{\text{pixels}} = 1280$, $v = 0.5$ m/s.

$$t_{\text{exp}} = \frac{400 \times 7.2}{12 \times 1280 \times 500} = \frac{2880}{7{,}680{,}000} \approx 3.75 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 375 \text{ μs}$$

This is a typical **high-speed machine vision** exposure, requiring a powerful illumination source (often **strobed LED**).

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Imaging Component Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Scene Object] --> B[Illumination Source]
    B --> C[Optics Lens Assembly]
    C --> D[Image Sensor CCD or CMOS]
    D --> E[Analog Signal Processing]
    E --> F[ADC and Frame Grabber]
    F --> G[Host PC with Image Processor]
    G --> H[Robot Controller]
    H --> I[Robot Manipulator Action]

    subgraph Sensor1[Image Acquisition Subsystem]
        D
        E
        F
    end

    subgraph Optics1[Optical Front End]
        B
        C
    end

    subgraph Decision1[Processing and Control]
        G
        H
        I
    end

    A -. Light .-> B
    G -. Image Matrix .-> H
    H -. Joint Commands .-> I
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow — Pixel Formation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Photon Hits Pixel] --> P2[Photoelectric Effect Electron Hole Pair]
    P2 --> P3[Charge Collected in Potential Well]
    P3 --> P4{Readout Type}
    P4 -- CCD --> P5[Charge Shifted to Output Node]
    P4 -- CMOS --> P6[In Pixel Amplifier Activated]
    P5 --> P7[Voltage Quantization via ADC]
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8[Digital Pixel Value 0 to 255 for 8 bit]
    P8 --> P9[Stored in Frame Buffer]
```

## 4.3 Vision System Integration with Robot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Cam1[Camera over Conveyor] --> Trig1[Trigger Sensor Photoeye]
    Trig1 --> Cam1
    Cam1 --> Proc1[Vision Processor]
    Proc1 --> Net1[Ethernet or Fieldbus]
    Net1 --> RC1[Robot Controller]
    RC1 --> R1[6 Axis Robot Arm]
    Proc1 --> Disp1[HMI Display for Operator]
    RC1 --> Disp1
    Proc1 --> Reject1[Reject Mechanism Air Jet]
```

## 4.4 CCD vs. CMOS Readout Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CCD1[CCD Architecture]
        CCDrow1[Row 1 Pixels] --> CCDrow2[Row 2 Pixels]
        CCDrow2 --> CCDrow3[Row 3 Pixels]
        CCDrow3 --> CCDOut[Single Output Amplifier]
        CCDOut --> CCDADC[External ADC]
    end

    subgraph CMOS1[CMOS Architecture]
        CMOScol1[Column 1 with Per Pixel Amplifier] --> CMOSADC1[Column ADC]
        CMOScol2[Column 2 with Per Pixel Amplifier] --> CMOSADC2[Column ADC]
        CMOScol3[Column 3 with Per Pixel Amplifier] --> CMOSADC3[Column ADC]
        CMOSADC1 --> CMOSMUX[Multiplexer]
        CMOSADC2 --> CMOSMUX
        CMOSADC3 --> CMOSMUX
        CMOSMUX --> CMOSOut[High Speed Digital Output]
    end
```

## 4.5 KTU Robot Vision Inspection Cell — Complete Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputStage[Input Stage]
        Conv[Conveyor Belt]
        Part[Workpart arrives]
        Photoeye[Photoelectric Sensor Trigger]
    end

    subgraph VisionStage[Vision Stage]

    end

    subgraph DecisionStage[Decision Stage]

    end

    Conv --> Part
    Part --> Photoeye
    Photoeye -- Trigger Signal --> Cam1

    Cam1 -- Image Stream --> FrameGrabber1
```
