# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]

**Q: Differentiate between CCD and CMOS image sensors used in industrial robotics vision systems. List at least four distinguishing points.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Feature | CCD | CMOS |
|---|---------|-----|------|
| 1 | **Readout mechanism** | Charge transferred to common output amplifier | Each pixel has its own amplifier |
| 2 | **Noise performance** | Lower noise, higher dynamic range | Historically noisier, but improving |
| 3 | **Power consumption** | High (multiple high-voltage clocks) | Low (single supply, on-chip integration) |
| 4 | **Speed** | Slower readout | Much faster, supports high frame rates |
| 5 | **Cost** | Expensive (special fabrication) | Cheap (standard CMOS process) |
| 6 | **Application** | Metrology, scientific imaging | Robotics, high-speed inspection |
| 7 | **Windowing / ROI** | Limited | Easy random access |

**Conclusion:** For most KTU-defined robotic applications, **CMOS** is preferred due to speed, integration, and cost; CCD is reserved for precision metrology.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]

**Q: Define "Field of View (FOV)" of a machine vision camera. Derive the relationship between FOV, focal length, sensor size, and working distance.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The **Field of View (FOV)** is the actual area of the inspected scene (in mm) that is captured by the image sensor through the lens. It depends on the lens focal length, the sensor's linear dimension, and the working distance between the lens and the object.

**Derivation:** From similar-triangle geometry of a thin lens, the half-angle subtended by the sensor at the lens is:

$$\tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) = \frac{h}{2f}$$

where $h$ is the sensor dimension and $f$ is the focal length.

The full angular field is:

$$\theta = 2 \arctan\left(\frac{h}{2f}\right)$$

At working distance $d$, the linear FOV is:

$$\boxed{\text{FOV} = \frac{d \cdot h}{f}}$$

**Interpretation:** A shorter focal length (wide-angle lens) gives a **larger FOV** at the same working distance, while a longer focal length (telephoto) gives a **smaller FOV** but higher magnification.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [Option A]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of an industrial machine vision system** used in robotic inspection. Label all imaging components and describe the function of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A robotic inspection station uses a **CCD camera with a 1/2" sensor (active area 6.4 mm × 4.8 mm) and a 16 mm focal length lens**. The camera is mounted **400 mm above the conveyor**. Calculate:
1. The horizontal and vertical Field of View at the conveyor plane.
2. The spatial resolution in mm/pixel if the sensor has **640 × 480 pixels**.
3. The minimum detectable defect size (Nyquist criterion). **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Architecture of Machine Vision System**

The industrial machine vision system consists of the following imaging components arranged in series:

1. **Illumination Source:** LED ring light, dome light, backlight, or laser line — provides controlled, consistent lighting on the object.
2. **Optics / Lens:** A fixed focal length, varifocal, or telecentric lens focuses the light reflected from the object onto the sensor.
3. **Image Sensor (CCD or CMOS):** Converts the optical image into an electrical signal by the photoelectric effect, pixel by pixel.
4. **Camera Electronics:** Controls exposure time, gain, white balance, and synchronizes with external triggers.
5. **Frame Grabber / Interface Card:** Digitizes (if analog) and transfers the image to the host computer using standards like **GigE Vision, USB3 Vision, Camera Link, or CoaXPress**.
6. **Host Computer / Image Processor:** Runs image processing algorithms (edge detection, pattern matching, blob analysis).
7. **Robot Controller Interface:** Sends coordinate data and pass/fail decision to the robot controller for actuation.

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.5 architecture diagram)

**Functions:**
- *Illumination* ensures repeatable image contrast.
- *Lens* determines FOV and magnification.
- *Sensor* determines resolution, sensitivity, and speed.
- *Frame grabber* determines data throughput.
- *Processor* determines decision-making capability.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation**

**Given:**
- Sensor active area: $h_H = 6.4$ mm (horizontal), $h_V = 4.8$ mm (vertical)
- Focal length: $f = 16$ mm
- Working distance: $d = 400$ mm
- Pixels: $N_H = 640$, $N_V = 480$

**Step 1: Compute Horizontal and Vertical FOV**

Using the derived formula:

$$\text{FOV}_H = \frac{d \cdot h_H}{f} = \frac{400 \times 6.4}{16} = 160 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{FOV}_V = \frac{d \cdot h_V}{f} = \frac{400 \times 4.8}{16} = 120 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Spatial Resolution**

$$\text{Res}_H = \frac{\text{FOV}_H}{N_H} = \frac{160}{640} = 0.25 \text{ mm/pixel}$$

$$\text{Res}_V = \frac{\text{FOV}_V}{N_V} = \frac{120}{480} = 0.25 \text{ mm/pixel}$$

**Step 3: Minimum Detectable Defect (Nyquist)**

For reliable detection, a feature must span **at least 2 pixels**:

$$d_{\min} = 2 \times \text{Res} = 2 \times 0.25 = 0.50 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answers:**
- FOV: **160 mm × 120 mm**
- Resolution: **0.25 mm/pixel**
- Minimum detectable defect: **0.50 mm**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Option B]

**(a)** Explain the different **types of illumination techniques** used in industrial robot vision systems. For each technique, state the type of defect or inspection task it is most suitable for. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **conveyor moves parts at 1.0 m/s** under a vision camera. The required spatial resolution is **0.5 mm/pixel** on the object, and the lens-sensor combination yields a working FOV of **200 mm × 150 mm**. The sensor is a **CMOS with 1280 × 960 pixels**. Calculate:
1. The maximum allowable exposure time to avoid motion blur of 1 pixel.
2. The minimum frame rate required to ensure no part is missed.
3. Comment on the suitability of standard room lighting versus pulsed LED for this application. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Illumination Techniques**

| # | Technique | Principle | Suitable Application |
|---|-----------|-----------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Backlighting** | Light placed **behind** the object | Edge detection, dimensional measurement, silhouette analysis |
| 2 | **Diffuse Dome Lighting** | Hemispherical uniform light | Highly reflective / shiny surfaces (polished metal, glass) |
| 3 | **Ring Light (Bright Field)** | Coaxial/angled LEDs on camera | General part inspection, presence detection |
| 4 | **Dark Field Illumination** | Low-angle light | Scratch, crack, and surface defect detection |
| 5 | **Structured Light** | Laser line / pattern projected | 3D profiling, volumetric inspection |
| 6 | **Strobe / Pulsed** | Short high-intensity flash | High-speed moving parts, freeze motion |
| 7 | **Polarized Light** | Crossed polarizers | Reducing glare from reflective surfaces |
| 8 | **IR / UV Illumination** | Non-visible spectrum | Food inspection, semiconductor, fluorescence detection |

**Conclusion:** Proper illumination reduces image processing complexity, improves contrast, and is critical to system robustness.

**Part (b) — High-Speed Imaging Calculations**

**Given:**
- Conveyor velocity: $v = 1.0$ m/s = 1000 mm/s
- Resolution: 0.5 mm/pixel
- FOV: 200 mm × 150 mm
- Sensor: 1280 × 960 pixels

**Step 1: Pixel size on object**

The resolution given is 0.5 mm/pixel, so:

$$p_{\text{obj}} = 0.5 \text{ mm/pixel}$$

**Step 2: Maximum exposure time**

The object moves 0.5 mm during one pixel of motion blur:

$$t_{\text{exp,max}} = \frac{p_{\text{obj}}}{v} = \frac{0.5}{1000} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 500 \text{ μs}$$

**Step 3: Minimum frame rate**

The horizontal FOV = 200 mm. At 1.0 m/s, the time to traverse the FOV is:

$$t_{\text{traverse}} = \frac{200}{1000} = 0.2 \text{ s}$$

So a frame every 0.2 s gives 5 fps — but this is for **non-overlapping** capture. For robust tracking with overlap, we use:

$$\text{Frame Rate}_{\min} = \frac{v}{\text{FOV}_H} = \frac{1000}{200} = 5 \text{ fps (theoretical minimum)}$$

In practice, to allow 50% overlap and tolerate timing jitter:

$$\text{Frame Rate}_{\text{design}} = 2 \times 5 = 10 \text{ fps}$$

**Step 4: Lighting comment**

Standard fluorescent room lighting (≈ 50 Hz, exposure > 10 ms) **cannot** support 500 μs exposure without motion blur and is **unsuitable**. A **pulsed LED** with peak intensity > 50,000 lux and pulse width < 500 μs is mandatory. The LED must be triggered in sync with the camera shutter for repeatable, blur-free images.

**Final Answers:**
- Max exposure: **500 μs**
- Min frame rate: **5 fps** (design for 10 fps)
- Lighting: **Pulsed LED required** — room lighting unsuitable.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. **Mixing up CCD and CMOS** — students often confuse *charge transfer* (CCD) with *in-pixel amplification* (CMOS). Memorize: **CCD = Charge Coupled Device, CMOS = Active Pixel Sensor**.
> 2. **Forgetting units** — FOV must be in **mm**, focal length in **mm**, working distance in **mm**. Mixing cm with mm is a frequent 1-mark loss.
> 3. **Not using Nyquist criterion** — a defect must be **2 pixels wide** minimum; using 1 pixel loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the FOV-h-f relationship** — $\text{FOV} = d \cdot h / f$ is the *most-tested* formula in Module 4. Do not confuse with magnification $M = v/u$.
> 5. **Ignoring lighting** in numerical problems — many KTU questions specifically ask for "suitable illumination"; a numerical answer with no lighting comment loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Forgetting the "Why"** — always state *why* a particular component is chosen (e.g., "CMOS used because of high frame rate and global shutter").

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Imaging Components**

- [x] **Imaging Components** = illumination + optics + sensor + electronics + interface.
- [x] **CCD** = charge transferred to common output, **low noise, slow, expensive**.
- [x] **CMOS** = in-pixel amplification, **fast, low power, cheap, dominant in robotics**.
- [x] **Lens equation:** $\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{u} + \frac{1}{v}$.
- [x] **FOV formula:** $\text{FOV} = d \cdot h / f$ — the most-tested equation in Module 4.
- [x] **Resolution (mm/pixel):** $\text{Res} = \text{FOV} / N_{\text{pixels}}$.
- [x] **Nyquist criterion:** minimum detectable feature = **2 × resolution**.
- [x] **Magnification:** $M = v / u = \text{image size} / \text{object size}$.
- [x] **Aperture (f-number):** $D = f / N$; higher N → less light, deeper depth of field.
- [x] **Lighting types:** backlight, ring, dome, dark field, structured, strobe, polarized.
- [x] **70% rule:** proper lighting fixes 70% of vision problems.
- [x] **Frame grabber** standards: GigE Vision, USB3 Vision, Camera Link, CoaXPress.
- [x] **Motion blur limit:** $t_{\text{exp}} = p_{\text{obj}} / v$ — for high-speed conveyors, use **strobe / pulsed LED**.
- [x] **Robot vision uses:** pick & place, inspection, sorting, bin picking, weld seam tracking.
- [x] **Trigger sources:** photoelectric sensor, encoder, PLC, or software trigger.
- [x] **Global shutter** is essential for moving parts; rolling shutter is acceptable only for static scenes.

> **Final Tip:** When answering KTU questions, always draw a **neat block diagram** first, then state the **function of each block**, then derive or compute. This automatically secures 50–60% of the marks even before the numerical part is touched.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Image Representation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT865 - INDUSTRIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Image Representation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Industrial Robotics** and **Machine Vision**, **Image Representation** is defined as the formal mathematical and computational scheme used to encode, store, and manipulate the two-dimensional (2D) visual information acquired from optical sensors (cameras, CCD/CMOS arrays, LiDAR imaging units) so that a robot controller, vision processor, or algorithm can interpret the scene for tasks such as object recognition, pose estimation, bin picking, weld seam tracking, and quality inspection.

A digital image $I$ is mathematically defined as a discrete 2D function $I(x, y)$, where $x$ and $y$ are spatial (plane) coordinates, and the amplitude of $I$ at any coordinate $(x, y)$ is called the **intensity** or **gray level** of the image at that point. When $(x, y)$ and the intensity values are all finite, discrete quantities, the image is termed a **digital image**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 4**
> Image Representation forms the foundational pillar of *Robot Vision*. Without a proper representation scheme, subsequent operations like **segmentation**, **feature extraction**, and **pattern classification** cannot be performed on the visual data stream feeding the robotic work-cell.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a giant stadium and want to describe the seating arrangement to someone who cannot see it. The simplest method is to draw a **grid** (rows and columns) and color each cell based on whether a seat is empty (white), occupied by a home-team fan (red), or an away-team fan (blue). 

This is **exactly** how a digital image is represented:

- The **stadium** = the entire image (the field of view of the robot's camera).
- The **grid cells** = **pixels** (the smallest indivisible picture element).
- The **color of each cell** = the **intensity** (or color value) of that pixel.
- The **number of rows × columns** = the **resolution** of the image (e.g., 1920 × 1080 for Full HD).

For a robot, this grid is not drawn on paper — it is stored as a **matrix of numbers** in memory, where each number tells the robot "how bright" or "what color" that tiny patch of the world is.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used in Robotics Vision**
> - **Resolution:** Expressed as **M × N** (e.g., 640 × 480 for VGA, used in entry-level industrial cameras).
> - **Bit Depth ($k$):** Number of bits per pixel. Common values are **1 bit (binary)**, **8 bits (grayscale)**, and **24 bits (true color, RGB)**.
> - **Standard Frame Rate:** **30 fps** to **120 fps** for high-speed assembly-line inspection.

### 1.3 Why Image Representation Matters in Industrial Robotics

A robot manipulator operating in a semi-structured environment (e.g., a factory floor with known parts but varying positions) **cannot** make decisions without first converting the continuous optical world into a discrete numerical form. The representation scheme determines:

1. **Storage cost** (memory footprint in the robot controller).
2. **Processing speed** (real-time constraints of the assembly line).
3. **Algorithmic complexity** (what kind of vision algorithms can be applied).
4. **Noise robustness** (how well the representation tolerates sensor noise).
5. **Interoperability** (whether the image can be exchanged with other modules like PLCs or SCADA systems).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** A well-chosen image representation is the difference between a robot that can detect a defective weld in **20 ms** and one that crashes the entire production line.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Levels of Image Representation (Hierarchical Pyramid)

In KTU's industrial robotics module, image representation is typically taught in **four hierarchical levels**, each building on the previous:

1. **Pixel-Level Representation** — Raw matrix form $I(x, y)$.
2. **Region-Level Representation** — Groupings of pixels sharing a property (segments, blobs).
3. **Boundary/Edge Representation** — Contours and outlines of objects.
4. **Symbolic/Relational Representation** — Graph-based or semantic description of objects and their relationships.

### 2.2 Types of Image Representation

#### (a) Binary Image Representation
- Each pixel takes **one of two values**: 0 (black/background) or 1 (white/object).
- Storage: **1 bit per pixel**.
- Total storage size: $M \times N \times 1$ bits.
- **Used in:** Simple presence/absence detection (e.g., "Is the part on the conveyor?").

#### (b) Grayscale Image Representation
- Each pixel takes a value from **0 to 255** (for 8-bit depth).
- Storage: **8 bits per pixel = 1 byte per pixel**.
- Total storage size: $M \times N \times 8$ bits.
- **Used in:** Edge detection, thresholding, most classical computer vision pipelines.

#### (c) Color Image Representation
The two dominant color models in industrial robotics are:

**i. RGB Model (Red, Green, Blue — Additive)**
- Each pixel stores 3 channels: $R$, $G$, $B$.
- Each channel: 8 bits.
- Total: **24 bits per pixel = 3 bytes per pixel**.
- Total storage: $M \times N \times 24$ bits.
- **Hardware-friendly** (matches the Bayer filter on most CMOS sensors).

**ii. HSI/HSV Model (Hue, Saturation, Intensity/Value)**
- Decouples color information (Hue, Saturation) from lighting (Intensity).
- **More robust** to illumination changes in factory environments.
- Used in: Color-based object sorting robots (e.g., sorting M&Ms, caps, or pharmaceutical pills).

#### (d) Compressed Representations
- **Run-Length Encoding (RLE):** Stores runs of identical pixel values.
- **Chain Codes:** Encode object boundaries using directional codes (Freeman 8-direction chain code).
- **Quadtrees:** Recursively subdivide the image into quadrants; uniform regions stored compactly.
- **Used in:** Reducing memory footprint for long-term storage of inspection logs.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Digital image definition | $I(x, y) \in \mathbb{Z}^{M \times N}$ | $x = 0, 1, \dots, M-1$; $y = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$ |
| 2 | Total number of pixels | $N_{px} = M \times N$ | Dimensionless (count) |
| 3 | Bit depth (gray levels) | $L = 2^{k}$ where $k$ = bits/pixel | $L$ = number of intensity levels |
| 4 | Binary image storage | $S = M \times N \times 1$ | bits |
| 5 | Grayscale image storage | $S = M \times N \times k$ | bits (typically $k=8$) |
| 6 | Color (RGB) image storage | $S = M \times N \times 3k$ | bits (typically $k=8 \Rightarrow 24$ bits/px) |
| 7 | Storage in bytes | $S_{B} = S / 8$ | bytes |
| 8 | Storage in KB | $S_{KB} = S_{B} / 1024$ | kilobytes |
| 9 | Nyquist sampling rate (2D) | $\Delta x \le \frac{1}{2 f_{x,max}}, \quad \Delta y \le \frac{1}{2 f_{y,max}}$ | $\Delta x, \Delta y$ = pixel pitch (m/pixel) |
| 10 | RGB $\to$ Grayscale (luminance) | $Y = 0.299\,R + 0.587\,G + 0.114\,B$ | ITU-R BT.601 standard |
| 11 | Euclidean pixel distance | $D_{e} = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^{2} + (y_2 - y_1)^{2}}$ | pixels |
| 12 | City-block (Manhattan) distance | $D_{4} = \vert x_2 - x_1 \vert + \vert y_2 - y_1 \vert$ | pixels |
| 13 | Chessboard (Chebyshev) distance | $D_{8} = \max(\vert x_2 - x_1 \vert, \vert y_2 - y_1 \vert)$ | pixels |
| 14 | Aspect ratio | $AR = M / N$ | dimensionless |
| 15 | Field of View (FOV) | $FOV = 2 f \tan(\theta / 2)$ where $\theta$ = angle subtended | f = focal length, $\theta$ in radians |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder for KTU Exam:** Whenever you calculate storage, **always state the units explicitly** (bits, bytes, KB, MB). Examiners deduct marks for unit ambiguity. Also, never use the vertical pipe symbol `\vert` inside a table cell — use `abs(...)` or write "magnitude of" instead to be safe.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** RGB image representation is used by vision-guided robotic arms to identify car-body panels on a moving conveyor. The 24-bit depth allows distinguishing subtle paint-tone variations for defect detection.
- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Grayscale representation is used in pill-counting machines; binary representation is used in **blob analysis** to count tablets.
- **Electronics Manufacturing:** Quadtrees and chain codes are used in PCB (Printed Circuit Board) inspection robots to compactly store defect maps.
- **Food & Agriculture:** HSI representation is used in fruit-sorting robots to grade apples by color maturity, independent of ambient lighting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why HSI over RGB in Robotic Sorting?**
> Under varying factory lighting (fluorescent vs. LED vs. natural), the *Hue* of an object remains approximately constant, while the *RGB* values fluctuate wildly. A robot using HSI representation will **not** misclassify a "ripe red apple" as "unripe" simply because the overhead light changed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Image Storage Size Formula

**Problem Setup:** Given an image of dimensions $M \times N$ pixels with bit depth $k$ bits/pixel, derive the total storage size in bytes.

**Step 1 — Define the image as a 2D matrix.**
The image $I$ is a discrete matrix of size $M \times N$, where each element $I(x, y)$ is a pixel.

**Step 2 — Count the total number of pixels.**

$$
N_{px} = M \times N
$$

This follows directly from the definition of a matrix of size $M$ rows by $N$ columns.

**Step 3 — Multiply by the bit depth to get total bits.**

Each pixel occupies $k$ bits in memory. Therefore, the total storage in bits is:

$$
S_{bits} = N_{px} \times k = M \times N \times k
$$

**Step 4 — Convert bits to bytes.**
Since $1 \text{ byte} = 8 \text{ bits}$:

$$
S_{bytes} = \frac{S_{bits}}{8} = \frac{M \times N \times k}{8}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert bytes to kilobytes (KB) or megabytes (MB).**

$$
S_{KB} = \frac{S_{bytes}}{1024} = \frac{M \times N \times k}{8 \times 1024}
$$

$$
S_{MB} = \frac{S_{bytes}}{1024^{2}} = \frac{M \times N \times k}{8 \times 1024^{2}}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

A robot vision system captures a **grayscale** image of resolution **1024 × 768** with **8 bits/pixel**.

**Step A — Total pixels:**

$$
N_{px} = 1024 \times 768 = 786{,}432 \text{ pixels}
$$

**Step B — Total bits (with $k = 8$):**

$$
S_{bits} = 786{,}432 \times 8 = 6{,}291{,}456 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step C — Total bytes:**

$$
S_{bytes} = \frac{6{,}291{,}456}{8} = 786{,}432 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step D — Total kilobytes:**

$$
S_{KB} = \frac{786{,}432}{1024} = 768 \text{ KB}
$$

**Interpretation:** A single 1024×768 grayscale image occupies exactly **768 KB** of memory. At a frame rate of 30 fps, the raw data rate is:

$$
\text{Data Rate} = 768 \times 30 = 23{,}040 \text{ KB/s} \approx 22.5 \text{ MB/s}
$$

This is well within the bandwidth of a GigE Vision camera interface.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: RGB to Grayscale Conversion (Luminance)

**Problem Setup:** Show the derivation of the luminance formula $Y = 0.299\,R + 0.587\,G + 0.114\,B$ from the principle of human visual perception.

**Step 1 — Recognize that the human eye is not equally sensitive to R, G, and B.**
The CIE (International Commission on Illumination) conducted experiments measuring the photopic luminosity function $V(\lambda)$ of the standard human observer. It was found that:

- The eye is **most sensitive to green** light (peak sensitivity around 555 nm).
- The eye is **least sensitive to blue** light.
- Sensitivity to red lies in between.

**Step 2 — Convert the luminosity curve into weighting coefficients for the NTSC television standard.**
Through integration of the CIE luminosity curve with the phosphor emission spectra of CRT displays, the NTSC committee derived the original weights:

$$
Y_{NTSC} = 0.299\,R + 0.587\,G + 0.114\,B
$$

**Step 3 — Note the constraint that coefficients sum to 1.**

$$
0.299 + 0.587 + 0.114 = 1.000
$$

This ensures that a fully white pixel $(R=G=B=255)$ maps to a grayscale value of $255$ (i.e., white is preserved).

**Step 4 — Apply to a sample RGB triplet.**

Let $R = 200$, $G = 100$, $B = 50$. Then:

$$
Y = 0.299 \times 200 + 0.587 \times 100 + 0.114 \times 50
$$

$$
Y = 59.8 + 58.7 + 5.7 = 124.2
$$

Rounded to nearest integer:

$$
Y \approx 124
$$

**Interpretation:** The original orange-tinted pixel is converted to a mid-gray (124/255 ≈ 49% brightness). A robot vision algorithm can now process this single-channel image, reducing data by a factor of 3 compared to the original RGB image.

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU Exam:** If the question asks for the **simpler average** method (often taught in introductory courses), the formula is $Y = (R + G + B) / 3$. However, the **luminance-weighted** formula $Y = 0.299\,R + 0.587\,G + 0.114\,B$ is the correct one used in real industrial vision systems and is preferred in KTU 2024 Scheme answers.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Nyquist Sampling Criterion for Image Acquisition

**Problem Setup:** A robot-mounted camera has a focal length $f = 25$ mm and is imaging a workpiece with the finest feature size $W = 0.5$ mm at a working distance $d = 500$ mm. Derive the required pixel pitch $\Delta x$ to resolve this feature.

**Step 1 — Compute the size of the feature in the image plane.**
Using the thin-lens equation (Gaussian imaging), the magnification is:

$$
m = \frac{f}{d} = \frac{25}{500} = 0.05
$$

The image-plane size of the feature is:

$$
w = m \times W = 0.05 \times 0.5 = 0.025 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Nyquist sampling criterion.**
To resolve the feature, the sensor must sample at **at least twice** the highest spatial frequency present. This means the pixel pitch $\Delta x$ must be:

$$
\Delta x \le \frac{w}{2} = \frac{0.025}{2} = 0.0125 \text{ mm} = 12.5 \text{ μm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the required sensor resolution for a 10 mm field of view.**

$$
M = \frac{FOV}{\Delta x} = \frac{10 \text{ mm}}{0.0125 \text{ mm/pixel}} = 800 \text{ pixels}
$$

**Interpretation:** The camera must have at least **800 × 800** pixel resolution to reliably detect a 0.5 mm feature on the workpiece. If the camera has a coarser pixel pitch (e.g., 20 μm), the feature will appear as a single gray blob and **the robot will fail to detect the defect** — a critical failure mode in automated optical inspection (AOI).

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Run-Length Encoding (RLE) Compression

**Problem Setup:** A binary image of a robot-inspected circuit board has the following single row of pixels (1 = conductor, 0 = insulator):

Row = `0000001111000011110000000111100000000`

**Step 1 — Identify consecutive runs of identical values.**

Scanning left to right:
- 6 zeros, 4 ones, 4 zeros, 4 ones, 7 zeros, 4 ones, 7 zeros.

**Step 2 — Encode each run as a (value, count) pair.**

$$
\{(0,6),\,(1,4),\,(0,4),\,(1,4),\,(0,7),\,(1,4),\,(0,7)\}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute compression ratio.**

Original length: 37 pixels × 1 bit = 37 bits.

Encoded length: 7 pairs × (assume 4 bits for value + 6 bits for count) = 7 × 10 = 70 bits.

Wait — for this small example, RLE actually *expands* the data (overhead dominates). For **larger images** with long uniform regions, RLE is highly effective. In typical PCB inspection images where 80% of pixels are background:

$$
\text{Compression Ratio} \approx 5{:}1 \text{ to } 20{:}1
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** If asked to compute compression ratio, always state your assumption about the bit-width of the run-length pair. Otherwise marks will be deducted for an ambiguous answer.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Representation | Best For | Storage Efficiency | Computation Speed | Noise Robustness |
|---------------|----------|-------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| Binary | Presence/absence | Highest (1 bit/px) | Fastest | Lowest |
| Grayscale | General inspection | Medium (8 bit/px) | Fast | Medium |
| RGB | Color identification | Low (24 bit/px) | Medium | Low (lighting-sensitive) |
| HSI | Color sorting | Low (24+ bit/px) | Slower (needs conversion) | High |
| RLE | Sparse images | Variable | Fast for decoding | High (if features large) |
| Quadtrees | Region-based analysis | High for uniform scenes | Slow to build, fast to query | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Image Acquisition to Representation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Scene / Workpiece] --> B[Optical Lens System]
    B --> C[Photosensitive Sensor CCD or CMOS]
    C --> D[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    D --> E[Digital Image Matrix I of x comma y]
    E --> F{Representation Scheme Selection}
    F -->|1 bit per pixel| G[Binary Image]
    F -->|8 bit per pixel| H[Grayscale Image]
    F -->|24 bit per pixel RGB| I[Color RGB Image]
    F -->|Hue Saturation Intensity| J[Color HSI Image]
    G --> K[Robot Vision Processor]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Decision Output to Robot Controller]
```

**Figure 1 — Image Acquisition and Representation Pipeline for an Industrial Robot Vision System.**  
The pipeline shows the transformation of continuous optical information from the scene into a discrete numerical matrix, followed by selection of an appropriate representation scheme for downstream processing.

---

### 4.2 Hierarchical Diagram — Levels of Image Representation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Pixel Level: Raw matrix I x y] --> P2[Region Level: Grouped pixels and segments]
    P2 --> P3[Boundary Level: Edges and contours]
    P3 --> P4[Symbolic Level: Objects and relations]
    P4 --> P5[Semantic Level: Task specific interpretation]

    P1 -. example .-> E1["Single intensity value 0 to 255"]
    P2 -. example .-> E2["Blob or connected component"]
    P3 -. example .-> E3["Freeman chain code 8 directions"]
    P4 -. example .-> E4["Object graph with attributes"]
    P5 -. example .-> E5["This is a defective weld seam"]
```

**Figure 2 — Hierarchical Abstraction Levels in Robot Image Representation.**  
Each level adds semantic meaning, moving from raw numerical data at the pixel level to a high-level task-relevant interpretation at the semantic level.

---

### 4.3 Color Space Relationship Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RGB["RGB Image 3 channels 24 bit per pixel"] -->|Conversion Equation| GRAY["Grayscale 1 channel 8 bit per pixel"]
    RGB -->|Non linear transform| HSI["HSI Image 3 channels Hue Saturation Intensity"]
    HSI -->|Inverse transform| RGB
    GRAY -->|Thresholding| BIN["Binary 1 channel 1 bit per pixel"]
    BIN -->|Edge detection| EDGE["Edge Map"]
    EDGE -->|Boundary tracing| CHAIN["Freeman Chain Code"]
    CHAIN -->|Shape matching| OBJ["Object Identification"]
```

**Figure 3 — Transformation Pathways Between Different Image Representation Schemes.**  
The diagram illustrates the operational relationships and typical use cases for each representation in an industrial robotics context.

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The transformation from RGB to Grayscale uses the luminance equation $Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$, while the RGB $\leftrightarrow$ HSI conversion involves non-linear trigonometric operations. Both are lossless in the sense that they preserve all information content (within their respective domains), but the **HSI representation decouples chromaticity from illumination**, making it the preferred choice in fluctuating factory lighting.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define Image Representation. List the major types used in industrial robot vision systems.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Image representation is the scheme used to encode the spatial and intensity information of a scene acquired by a robot's vision sensor into a numerical form that can be stored, processed, and interpreted by a computer.

The major types are:

1. **Binary representation** — 1 bit per pixel; values are 0 or 1.
2. **Grayscale representation** — typically 8 bits per pixel; values range from 0 to 255.
3. **Color representation** — RGB (24 bits/pixel) or HSI (24+ bits/pixel).
4. **Compressed representations** — Run-length encoding, chain codes, quadtrees.

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (1M) + List with brief explanation (2M).

---

#### **Question 2: Why is the HSI color model often preferred over the RGB model in industrial robotic sorting applications?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **HSI model decouples chromaticity (Hue, Saturation) from illumination (Intensity)**. In a factory environment, the lighting conditions (fluorescent, LED, natural daylight) can vary significantly throughout the day. These variations primarily affect the **Intensity** channel but leave the **Hue** of an object (e.g., a red apple, a blue cap) relatively stable.

In contrast, the **RGB model** entangles color and brightness — a red object viewed under dim light will have very different $(R, G, B)$ values than the same object under bright light. This causes the robot's color classifier to misidentify the object.

Therefore, HSI provides **illumination-invariant color recognition**, making it the preferred model for color-based robotic sorting tasks.

**Marks Distribution:** HSI decoupling (1M) + Illumination invariance argument (1.5M) + Conclusion (0.5M).

---

### 5.2 PART B — Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Treatment of Image Representation** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the pipeline of image acquisition and representation in an industrial robot vision system. Discuss the role of each block.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The image acquisition and representation pipeline consists of the following sequential stages:

**Block 1 — Scene/Workpiece Illumination:**
Controlled lighting (LED ring lights, diffuse dome lights, structured light) is critical. In machine vision, lighting is often considered "50% of the solution" because poor illumination introduces noise and shadows that propagate through all subsequent stages.

**Block 2 — Optical Lens System:**
The lens focuses light from the scene onto the sensor. Key parameters are focal length $f$, aperture, and depth of field. The lens determines the field of view (FOV):

$$
FOV = 2 f \tan(\theta / 2)
$$

For a robot arm inspecting a 100 mm × 100 mm PCB at a working distance of 300 mm with a 25 mm lens, the FOV is approximately 95 mm × 71 mm.

**Block 3 — Photosensitive Sensor (CCD/CMOS):**
Converts photons into electrical charge. Modern industrial cameras use CMOS sensors with global shutter to avoid motion artifacts when the conveyor or robot is moving.

**Block 4 — Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC):**
Quantizes the continuous voltage from each pixel into discrete intensity levels. An 8-bit ADC produces 256 gray levels.

**Block 5 — Digital Image Matrix $I(x, y)$:**
The output is an $M \times N$ matrix of integers, ready for processing.

**Block 6 — Representation Selector:**
Depending on the application:
- 1-bit binary for simple detection
- 8-bit grayscale for general inspection
- 24-bit RGB/HSI for color sorting
- Compressed forms (RLE, chain codes) for storage

**Block 7 — Vision Processor:**
Runs segmentation, feature extraction, and classification.

**Block 8 — Robot Controller Interface:**
Sends results (e.g., "defect at coordinates (342, 567)") to the robot controller for action.

**Marks Distribution:** Block diagram (2M) + Explanation of 4 critical blocks (3M) + Pipeline reasoning (2M).

---

**(b) An industrial camera produces a grayscale image of size 1280 × 1024 pixels with 8 bits per pixel. Calculate:** **(7 Marks)**

**(i) Total number of pixels** (1 Mark)
**(ii) Total storage in bits, bytes, KB, and MB** (4 Marks)
**(iii) The data rate in MB/s if the camera operates at 60 fps** (2 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Total number of pixels:**

$$
N_{px} = M \times N = 1280 \times 1024 = 1{,}310{,}720 \text{ pixels}
$$

**(ii) Total storage:**

Bits: 
$$
S_{bits} = M \times N \times k = 1{,}310{,}720 \times 8 = 10{,}485{,}760 \text{ bits}
$$

Bytes: 
$$
S_{bytes} = \frac{10{,}485{,}760}{8} = 1{,}310{,}720 \text{ bytes}
$$

KB: 
$$
S_{KB} = \frac{1{,}310{,}720}{1024} = 1280 \text{ KB}
$$

MB: 
$$
S_{MB} = \frac{1{,}310{,}720}{1024^{2}} = 1.25 \text{ MB}
$$

**(iii) Data rate at 60 fps:**

$$
\text{Data Rate} = S_{MB} \times f = 1.25 \times 60 = 75 \text{ MB/s}
$$

This is well within the bandwidth of a GigE Vision interface (≈ 100 MB/s).

**Marks Distribution:** Each sub-part as indicated; final units must be explicitly stated.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Choice — Theory and Conversion** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Compare binary, grayscale, and RGB image representations in terms of bit depth, storage requirements, and typical industrial applications. Derive the formula for the total storage of an RGB image.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Binary | Grayscale | RGB Color |
|-----------|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Bits per pixel | 1 | 8 (typical) | 24 (8×3) |
| Number of levels | 2 | 256 | 16.7 million ($256^{3}$) |
| Storage for 640×480 image | 307,200 bits ≈ 37.5 KB | 2,457,600 bits = 300 KB | 7,372,800 bits = 900 KB |
| Typical use | Presence/absence, blob counting | Edge detection, measurement | Color sorting, defect classification |
| Computation cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Illumination sensitivity | High | Medium | High |

**Derivation of RGB Storage Formula:**

An RGB image has three channels, each of size $M \times N$, with $k$ bits per channel.

Total bits = (Pixels per channel) × (bits per channel) × (number of channels)

$$
S_{RGB} = (M \times N) \times k \times 3 = 3 M N k \text{ bits}
$$

Converting to bytes:

$$
S_{RGB,bytes} = \frac{3 M N k}{8} \text{ bytes}
$$

**Marks Distribution:** Comparison table (3M) + Derivation (3M) + Application relevance (1M).

---

**(b) A robot vision system uses an RGB camera with $M = 800$, $N = 600$, and 8 bits per channel. It needs to convert the RGB image to grayscale. Apply the luminance formula to a pixel with $R = 180$, $G = 90$, $B = 40$, and explain why this conversion is computationally beneficial in a real-time robotic cell.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the storage before and after conversion.**

Before (RGB): $S_{RGB} = 3 \times 800 \times 600 \times 8 = 11{,}520{,}000$ bits = 1.44 MB.

After (Grayscale): $S_{gray} = 800 \times 600 \times 8 = 3{,}840{,}000$ bits = 0.48 MB.

Reduction: $3\times$ less memory.

**Step 2 — Apply the luminance formula.**

$$
Y = 0.299 \times 180 + 0.587 \times 90 + 0.114 \times 40
$$

$$
Y = 53.82 + 52.83 + 4.56 = 111.21
$$

Rounded: $Y = 111$.

**Step 3 — Computational benefit explanation.**

1. **Memory bandwidth reduction:** Only 1 channel is processed instead of 3 → 3× faster data transfer.
2. **Algorithm compatibility:** Many classical vision algorithms (Canny edge detection, Sobel filter, Hough transform) operate on single-channel images.
3. **Real-time viability:** At 30 fps, processing 0.48 MB/frame allows the robot to make decisions within the 33 ms frame budget.
4. **Noise reduction:** Averaging across 3 channels implicitly reduces sensor noise (factor of $\sqrt{3}$).
5. **Hardware simplification:** Cheaper FPGA/ASIC implementations for embedded robot controllers.

**Marks Distribution:** Storage calculation (2M) + Luminance computation (2M) + 5 benefit points (3M total).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams — Image Representation:**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Writing storage as "1024 × 768 = 786432 bytes" is correct, but writing it as "bits" is a **1-mark penalty**. Always explicitly state the unit after the number.
>
> 2. **Skipping the Bit-Depth:** Forgetting to multiply by 8 for grayscale or 24 for RGB is the **#1 mistake**. A student who writes "1280 × 1024 = 1,310,720 bits" for an 8-bit grayscale image loses 2 marks.
>
> 3. **Confusing RGB with HSI in a question that specifies HSI:** If the question says "HSI model," do not write formulas for RGB conversion. The conversion equations are different.
>
> 4. **Not Drawing the Block Diagram:** Part B (a) questions in KTU 2024 Scheme **require a diagram**. A neat, labeled block diagram fetches at least 2 marks even if the explanation is weak.
>
> 5. **Using the Average Method Instead of Luminance:** Writing $Y = (R+G+B)/3$ when the question asks for the standard luminance formula loses 1–2 marks. Use $Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$ unless the question specifically says "simple average method."
>
> 6. **Forgetting the Nyquist Criterion:** When a question asks "what resolution is required," many students skip the Nyquist factor of 2 and lose a critical mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Image Representation**
>
> - A **digital image** $I(x, y)$ is a discrete 2D matrix of size $M \times N$ with intensity values in $\{0, 1, \dots, 2^{k}-1\}$.
> - **Binary image:** 1 bit/px; used for simple detection and blob analysis.
> - **Grayscale image:** 8 bits/px; the workhorse of industrial machine vision.
> - **RGB image:** 24 bits/px; hardware-friendly but lighting-sensitive.
> - **HSI image:** 24+ bits/px; illumination-robust; ideal for color sorting robots.
> - **Storage formula:** $S = M \times N \times k$ bits (multiply by 3 for RGB).
> - **1 byte = 8 bits; 1 KB = 1024 bytes; 1 MB = 1024 KB** (always use binary multiples in KTU exams unless SI is explicitly requested).
> - **Luminance formula:** $Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$ (ITU-R BT.601).
> - **Nyquist criterion:** Pixel pitch $\le$ feature size in image plane / 2.
> - **Field of View:** $FOV = 2 f \tan(\theta / 2)$.
> - **Compression schemes:** RLE (sparse), Quadtrees (uniform regions), Chain codes (boundaries).
> - **Distance metrics:** Euclidean ($D_e$), Manhattan ($D_4$), Chebyshev ($D_8$) — used in neighborhood operations and morphological processing.
> - **Standard industrial resolutions:** VGA (640×480), XGA (1024×768), HD (1280×720), Full HD (1920×1080), 4K (3840×2160).
> - **Standard frame rates:** 30 fps (general), 60–120 fps (high-speed inspection), 1000+ fps (vibration analysis).
> - **Hierarchical abstraction:** Pixel → Region → Edge → Object → Semantic — every higher level depends on the lower-level representation being correct.
> - **HSI is preferred over RGB** for color sorting under varying factory lighting.
> - **Resolution $\neq$ Quality** — a 4K image with poor lighting is worse than a VGA image with proper diffused illumination.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Picture Coding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT865 - INDUSTRIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Picture Coding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Picture Coding

**Picture Coding** is the systematic process of representing, encoding, compressing, and decoding visual information (images) acquired by a robot's vision system into a form that is computationally efficient for storage, transmission, and subsequent analysis (such as object recognition, inspection, and manipulation). In industrial robotics, picture coding forms the critical bridge between the analog optical world and the digital processing units of the robot controller.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Picture coding refers to the techniques used to convert a 2D image of a scene into a compact digital representation. It encompasses image digitization (sampling and quantization), binary/gray-level encoding schemes, boundary coding (chain codes, run-length codes), and feature descriptors used by a robot to identify and locate objects in its workspace.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a robot trying to recognize a **hexagonal nut** on a conveyor belt. Your camera captures millions of pixels (tiny colored dots), but you cannot process all of them in real-time. **Picture coding is like taking a perfect photograph and then redrawing it as a simple connect-the-dots sketch.**

- **Original image** = A 1,000,000-pixel photograph of the nut
- **Coded picture** = A short list of numbers describing the boundary ("go right 5, up 3, diagonal up-right 4, …")

This coded sketch is **tiny, fast to process, and contains the essential shape information** the robot needs to pick the nut. The coding is the "language" the robot uses to "understand" what it sees.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics in Picture Coding

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Metrics Every KTU Student Must Know:**
> - **Sampling Resolution (N × M):** Number of pixels horizontally × vertically (e.g., **640 × 480**).
> - **Quantization Level (k bits/pixel):** Number of bits used to represent intensity (**8 bits** = 256 gray levels).
> - **Bit Rate:** Total bits in coded image = **N × M × k** (uncompressed).
> - **Compression Ratio (CR):** $CR = \dfrac{\text{Original Size}}{\text{Coded Size}}$. Higher CR = better compression.
> - **Mean Square Error (MSE):** Quantifies reconstruction quality.
> - **Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR):** Higher is better, measured in **decibels (dB)**.

## 1.4 The Three Stages of Picture Coding in Robotics

| Stage | Function | Robot Application |
|-------|----------|-------------------|
| **1. Image Acquisition & Digitization** | Convert light → voltage → digital pixels | Camera, frame grabber |
| **2. Image Coding & Representation** | Encode pixels into compact descriptors | Chain codes, RLE, features |
| **3. Decoding & Interpretation** | Reconstruct shape/region for decisions | Object recognition, path planning |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Picture Coding

Picture coding in robotic vision operates on a hierarchical pipeline:

### **Step 1: Image Digitization (Sampling + Quantization)**
- **Sampling:** Continuous 2D image $f(x, y)$ is sampled into a discrete grid of **N × M** samples.
- **Quantization:** Each sample's intensity is mapped to one of $2^k$ discrete levels, where **k** is the bit-depth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nyquist Criterion:** To preserve all information, the sampling frequency must be at least twice the highest spatial frequency in the image. For an image with maximum spatial frequency $f_{max}$, the minimum sampling rate is **2·f_max**.

### **Step 2: Binary Image Coding**
Binary images (1 bit/pixel: black = 1, white = 0) are common in industrial inspection because of high contrast (e.g., a dark part on a light conveyor).

- **Run-Length Encoding (RLE):** Consecutive runs of identical pixels are encoded as (value, length) pairs. Excellent for images with large uniform regions.
- **Chain Code (Freeman, 1961):** Encodes a boundary by recording the direction of movement from one boundary pixel to the next using one of 8 (or 4) directional codes.

### **Step 3: Feature-Based Coding**
- **Signatures:** Distance from centroid to boundary as a function of angle.
- **Fourier Descriptors:** Boundary in frequency domain — robust to noise, scale, and rotation.
- **Moment Invariants (Hu, 1962):** Seven algebraic moment combinations invariant to translation, rotation, and scale.

## 2.2 Why Picture Coding is Indispensable in Robotics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Speed:** Real-time control loops operate at **1–10 ms**; raw images are too slow.
> - **Memory:** A 1024×1024 8-bit image is **1 MB**; coding reduces this by **10–100×**.
> - **Recognition:** Shape descriptors (chain codes, moments) are directly used for **template matching** and **part classification**.
> - **Communication:** Compressed images transmit faster over industrial networks (PROFINET, EtherCAT).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Picture Coding (Module 4)**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Uncompressed Image Size | $S = N \cdot M \cdot k$ | bits (N,M = pixels, k = bits/pixel) |
| 2 | Compression Ratio | $CR = \dfrac{S_{original}}{S_{coded}}$ | dimensionless, $\geq 1$ |
| 3 | Relative Data Redundancy | $R = 1 - \dfrac{1}{CR}$ | $0 \leq R < 1$ |
| 4 | Mean Square Error | $MSE = \dfrac{1}{NM}\sum_{x=0}^{N-1}\sum_{y=0}^{M-1}[f(x,y) - \hat{f}(x,y)]^{2}$ | squared intensity units |
| 5 | PSNR | $PSNR = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{(2^{k}-1)^{2}}{MSE}\right)$ | decibels (**dB**), higher = better |
| 6 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_{s} \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Hz or pixels/unit length |
| 7 | Chain Code Length (8-connected) | $L_{chain} = \sum_{i=1}^{n_{b}} d_{i}$ | where $d_i = 1$ (even) or $\sqrt{2}$ (odd) step |
| 8 | Run-Length Efficiency | $\eta_{RLE} = \dfrac{\text{Runs}}{\text{Total Pixels}}$ | lower = better compression |
| 9 | 2D Image Power Spectrum | $P(u,v) = \vert F(u,v) \vert^{2}$ | frequency domain energy |
| 10 | Discrete Fourier Transform | $F(u,v) = \sum_{x=0}^{N-1}\sum_{y=0}^{M-1} f(x,y) \, e^{-j2\pi(ux/N + vy/M)}$ | coefficients of coded image |
| 11 | Entropy (Huffman Bound) | $H = -\sum_{i} p_{i} \log_{2} p_{i}$ | bits/symbol, theoretical min |
| 12 | Hu Moment Invariant $\phi_{1}$ | $\phi_{1} = \eta_{20} + \eta_{02}$ | scale + rotation invariant |
| 13 | 2D Centroid | $(\bar{x}, \bar{y}) = \left(\dfrac{\sum x\,f(x,y)}{\sum f(x,y)}, \dfrac{\sum y\,f(x,y)}{\sum f(x,y)}\right)$ | in pixels |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** Always state **units**. PSNR is in **dB**, MSE in **intensity²**, and angles in **radians** for Fourier descriptors. Mixing units costs marks.

## 2.4 Boundary Coding Direction Codes (Freeman 8-Direction)

| Code | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|------|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | → E | ↗ NE | ↑ N | ↖ NW | ← W | ↙ SW | ↓ S | ↘ SE |
| **Step Length** | 1 | $\sqrt{2}$ | 1 | $\sqrt{2}$ | 1 | $\sqrt{2}$ | 1 | $\sqrt{2}$ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Uncompressed Image Size and Compression Ratio

**Starting Point:** An image is sampled into a discrete grid of $N$ columns and $M$ rows, with $k$ bits per pixel.

**Step 1 — Total raw bits:**
Every pixel stores $k$ bits, and there are $N \cdot M$ pixels.

$$
S_{orig} = N \cdot M \cdot k
$$

**Step 2 — Compression ratio definition:**
The compression ratio is the ratio of original size to compressed (coded) size.

$$
CR = \frac{S_{orig}}{S_{coded}}
$$

**Step 3 — Relative data redundancy:**
Redundancy $R$ measures how much of the data is "extra" beyond the minimum information.

$$
R = 1 - \frac{1}{CR}
$$

**Step 4 — Worked numerical example (KTU-style):**
A 512 × 512 image with $k = 8$ bits/pixel is encoded using chain coding, producing $S_{coded} = 16{,}384$ bits.

$$
S_{orig} = 512 \times 512 \times 8 = 2{,}097{,}152 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
CR = \frac{2{,}097{,}152}{16{,}384} = 128
$$

$$
R = 1 - \frac{1}{128} \approx 0.9922 \; (99.22\%)
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mean Square Error (MSE) and PSNR

**Step 1 — Element-wise squared error at pixel $(x, y)$:**

$$
e^{2}(x,y) = [f(x,y) - \hat{f}(x,y)]^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Average over the entire image:**

$$
MSE = \frac{1}{N \cdot M} \sum_{x=0}^{N-1} \sum_{y=0}^{M-1} e^{2}(x,y)
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum possible signal power:**
For $k$-bit images, peak intensity $L - 1 = 2^{k} - 1$.

$$
PSNR = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{(2^{k}-1)^{2}}{MSE}\right) \;\; \text{dB}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation:**
A **PSNR > 30 dB** is generally considered acceptable for robotic visual inspection. Below **20 dB** indicates visible distortion that may corrupt part recognition.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Chain Code Length for a Closed Boundary

**Step 1 — The boundary of an object is traversed pixel by pixel.**
At each step, a Freeman direction code (0–7) is recorded.

**Step 2 — Even-direction steps (0, 2, 4, 6) have length 1 pixel (cardinal).**
Odd-direction steps (1, 3, 5, 7) have length $\sqrt{2}$ pixels (diagonal).

**Step 3 — Total chain code length:**

$$
L_{chain} = N_{even} \cdot 1 + N_{odd} \cdot \sqrt{2}
$$

where $N_{even} + N_{odd} = n_{b}$ (total boundary steps).

**Step 4 — Worked example:**
A hexagonal nut boundary requires **24 chain code steps**: 12 even, 12 odd.

$$
L_{chain} = 12 \times 1 + 12 \times \sqrt{2} = 12 + 16.97 = 28.97 \text{ pixels}
$$

This is the **perimeter** of the nut in pixel units.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Image Entropy and the Huffman Lower Bound

**Step 1 — Each gray level $i$ has probability $p_i$ of occurring in the image.**

**Step 2 — Information content (surprise) of symbol $i$:**

$$
I(i) = -\log_{2} p_i \quad \text{bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Expected information per pixel (entropy):**

$$
H = -\sum_{i=0}^{2^{k}-1} p_{i} \log_{2} p_{i} \quad \text{bits/pixel}
$$

**Step 4 — Key insight:**
**No lossless coding scheme can beat the entropy bound.** A well-designed Huffman code achieves an average code length within 1 bit of $H$.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Coding Techniques Comparison

| Technique | Domain | Best Use | Complexity | Compression |
|-----------|--------|----------|------------|-------------|
| **Run-Length Coding** | Spatial | Binary images, large uniform regions | Low | 2–10× |
| **Chain Code** | Boundary | Shape recognition, perimeter | Low | High (perimeters only) |
| **Huffman Coding** | Symbol | Lossless compression of any image | Medium | 1.5–3× |
| **LZW** | Symbol | GIF/TIFF formats | Medium | 2–4× |
| **Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT)** | Frequency | JPEG lossy compression | High | 10–50× |
| **Wavelet Transform** | Frequency | JPEG 2000, multiresolution | High | 20–100× |
| **Fourier Descriptors** | Frequency | Robust shape matching | Medium | Compact features |
| **Moment Invariants** | Spatial | Invariant object recognition | Medium | Very compact |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** In a robotic pick-and-place cell, the camera captures an image, chain-coding extracts the boundary, Fourier descriptors provide a rotation-invariant signature, and template matching against stored descriptors identifies the part — all in **under 50 ms**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Picture Coding Pipeline in Robot Vision

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Scene: 3D Workspace] --> B[Camera + Lens Optics]
    B --> C[Frame Grabber / Image Sensor]
    C --> D[Sampling: N x M Pixel Grid]
    D --> E[Quantization: k bits per pixel]
    E --> F[Raw Digital Image Buffer]
    F --> G{Picture Coding Stage}
    G --> G1[Run-Length Encoder]
    G --> G2[Freeman Chain Coder]
    G --> G3[Huffman / LZW Encoder]
    G --> G4[Fourier Descriptor Extractor]
    G --> G5[Hu Moment Calculator]
    H1[RLE Stream] --> I[Feature Library DB]
    H2[Chain Code Sequence] --> I
    H3[Symbol Code Stream] --> I
    H4[Fourier Coeffs] --> I
    H5[Moment Vectors] --> I
    I --> J[Pattern Matcher / Classifier]
    J --> K[Decoded Object Identity + Pose]
    K --> L[Robot Controller]
    L --> M[Motion Commands to Actuators]
    M --> N[End Effector Action Pick Place Weld]
```

## 4.2 State Diagram: Image Coding-Decoding Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> AcquireLight
    AcquireLight --> Digitize: Photodiode Sensor
    Digitize --> StoreRaw: N x M x k bits
    StoreRaw --> Encode: Choose Coding Scheme
    Encode --> Compressed: RLE OR ChainCode OR Huffman
    Compressed --> Transmit: Industrial Bus PROFINET EtherCAT
    Transmit --> Decode: Reverse Operation
    Decode --> Reconstruct: Approximate Original
    Reconstruct --> Recognize: Match Feature Library
    Recognize --> [*]: Object Identified
    Encode --> FeatureExtract: Alternate Path
    FeatureExtract --> Descriptor: Fourier OR Moment
    Descriptor --> MatchDB: Direct Template Match
    MatchDB --> [*]: Recognized
```

## 4.3 Hierarchical Concept Map: Picture Coding Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PC[Picture Coding] --> SP[Spatial Domain]
    PC --> FR[Frequency Domain]
    PC --> FE[Feature Domain]
    SP --> RLE[Run-Length Encoding]
    SP --> CHN[Chain Code]
    SP --> PXC[Pixel Block Coding]
    FR --> DCT[Discrete Cosine Transform]
    FR --> DWT[Discrete Wavelet Transform]
    FR --> FFD[Fourier Descriptors]
    FE --> MOM[Hu Moment Invariants]
    FE --> SIG[Signatures]
    FE --> SKE[Skeleton Coding]
    RLE --> APP1[Binary Inspection]
    CHN --> APP1
    DCT --> APP2[JPEG Image Storage]
    DWT --> APP3[Multiresolution Analysis]
    FFD --> APP4[Shape Recognition]
    MOM --> APP4
    SIG --> APP4
    SKE --> APP4
    APP1 --> ROB[Robot Vision System]
    APP2 --> ROB
    APP3 --> ROB
    APP4 --> ROB
```

## 4.4 Data Flow: Binary Object to Chain Code

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pixel Grid 8x8 Binary Object] --> S1[Boundary Tracing Moore Neighbor]
    S1 --> S2[Start Pixel Top-Left]
    S2 --> S3[Move Clockwise Around Boundary]
    S3 --> S4[Record Freeman Code at Each Step]
    S4 --> S5[Output Chain Code Sequence]
    S5 --> S6[Compute Perimeter Lchain]
    S6 --> S7[Match Against Template Database]
    S7 --> R1[Object Identified]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define picture coding. List any four picture coding techniques used in industrial robotic vision.**

**Model Answer:**

Picture coding is the process of converting a 2D image into a compact digital representation suitable for storage, transmission, and analysis in a robot vision system.

Four picture coding techniques:
1. **Run-Length Encoding (RLE)** – encodes consecutive runs of identical pixels.
2. **Chain Code (Freeman)** – encodes object boundary using 4 or 8 directional codes.
3. **Huffman Coding** – lossless variable-length code based on symbol probability.
4. **Fourier Descriptors** – represents boundary in frequency domain for invariant recognition.

*(Any four valid techniques accepted.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**Explain the significance of the Nyquist criterion in image sampling for a robot vision system.**

**Model Answer:**

The Nyquist criterion states that the sampling frequency $f_s$ must be at least **twice the highest spatial frequency** $f_{max}$ present in the image:

$$
f_{s} \geq 2 f_{max}
$$

**Significance in robotic vision:**
- If $f_s < 2 f_{max}$, **aliasing** occurs, causing false contours, moiré patterns, and loss of small object features.
- A bolt, gear tooth, or PCB trace may be missed by the robot, causing **incorrect recognition or assembly failures**.
- Proper Nyquist-compliant sampling ensures the **robot's feature extractor sees all critical geometric details**, enabling reliable pick-and-place or inspection.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the picture coding pipeline in a robot vision system. Discuss the role of sampling and quantization in the digitization stage.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An industrial camera captures a 256 × 256 image of a workpiece with 8 bits per pixel. The image is encoded using Huffman coding, achieving an average code length of 4.2 bits/pixel. Calculate:
   (i) The uncompressed image size.
   (ii) The compressed image size.
   (iii) The compression ratio (CR).
   (iv) The relative data redundancy (R).

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Block Diagram of Picture Coding Pipeline:**

```
Scene → Camera Lens → Image Sensor → Sampling (N×M grid)
    → Quantization (k bits) → Raw Image Buffer
    → Encoder (RLE/Chain/Huffman) → Compressed Bitstream
    → Decoder → Reconstructed Image / Features
    → Recognition → Robot Action
```

**Sampling:**
- Continuous image $f(x,y)$ is sampled at discrete points $(x, y) = (i\Delta x, j\Delta y)$.
- Result: $N \times M$ discrete samples.
- **Nyquist rule:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ to avoid aliasing.

**Quantization:**
- Each sample's intensity is mapped to one of $2^k$ discrete levels.
- Quantization step $q = \dfrac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{2^{k}}$.
- Higher $k$ = finer intensity resolution, larger file size.
- **Trade-off:** For a robot inspection cell, $k = 8$ is standard; for high-precision metrology, $k = 10$ or $12$ is used.

**Role in robotics:**
- Sampling determines **spatial resolution** (ability to detect small defects).
- Quantization determines **intensity resolution** (ability to detect subtle color/shade differences).
- Together they convert the analog world into a digital array the robot controller can process in real time.

*(Expected: 3 marks diagram + 2 marks sampling + 2 marks quantization = 7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- $N = 256$, $M = 256$, $k = 8$ bits/pixel
- Average Huffman code length $L = 4.2$ bits/pixel

**(i) Uncompressed image size:**

$$
S_{orig} = N \cdot M \cdot k = 256 \times 256 \times 8
$$

$$
S_{orig} = 65{,}536 \times 8 = 524{,}288 \text{ bits} = 64 \text{ KB}
$$

**(ii) Compressed image size:**

$$
S_{coded} = N \cdot M \cdot L = 65{,}536 \times 4.2
$$

$$
S_{coded} = 275{,}251.2 \text{ bits} \approx 275.25 \text{ kbits}
$$

**(iii) Compression Ratio:**

$$
CR = \frac{S_{orig}}{S_{coded}} = \frac{524{,}288}{275{,}251.2} \approx 1.905
$$

**(iv) Relative Data Redundancy:**

$$
R = 1 - \frac{1}{CR} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.905} = 1 - 0.525 = 0.475
$$

$$
R \approx 47.5\%
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** The Huffman coding removes **47.5% of redundant data**, nearly halving the storage and transmission load on the robot controller.

*(Expected: 1.5 marks each sub-part + 0.5 mark interpretation = 7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Freeman chain coding** technique for boundary representation. How is the chain code length (perimeter) computed for an 8-connected boundary?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A binary object boundary is encoded using the following 8-direction chain code (starting from the top-left pixel, traversed clockwise):
**0, 0, 1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 4, 5, 6, 6, 7**
Compute:
   (i) Total number of boundary steps.
   (ii) Number of even-direction (cardinal) steps.
   (iii) Number of odd-direction (diagonal) steps.
   (iv) Total chain code length (perimeter) in pixel units.
   (v) If the original binary image is 64 × 64 pixels with 1 bit/pixel, calculate the compression ratio achieved by this chain code representation.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Freeman Chain Coding — Definition:**

Freeman chain code (1961) is a compact boundary representation technique. Starting from a chosen boundary pixel, the boundary is traced pixel-by-pixel in a fixed direction (clockwise or anticlockwise). At each step, the direction of movement is recorded using one of **8 directional codes** (0–7) for 8-connected boundaries, or **4 codes** for 4-connected boundaries.

**8-Direction Codes:**

| Code | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|------|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Direction | E | NE | N | NW | W | SW | S | SE |
| Step Length | 1 | $\sqrt{2}$ | 1 | $\sqrt{2}$ | 1 | $\sqrt{2}$ | 1 | $\sqrt{2}$ |

**Computation of chain code length (perimeter):**

The perimeter is the sum of individual step lengths:

$$
L_{chain} = \sum_{i=1}^{n_{b}} d_{i}
$$

where $d_i = 1$ for even codes and $d_i = \sqrt{2}$ for odd codes. So:

$$
L_{chain} = N_{even} \cdot 1 + N_{odd} \cdot \sqrt{2}
$$

**Why chain coding matters in robotics:**
- **Compact storage** — only 3 bits per boundary step (for 8 codes).
- **Shape matching** — chain codes are compared to recognize objects independent of position.
- **Translation invariant** — only the starting pixel changes with shift.

*(Expected: 2 marks definition + 2 marks code table + 3 marks formula + application = 7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Given chain code sequence:** 0, 0, 1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 4, 5, 6, 6, 7

**(i) Total boundary steps:**

$$
n_{b} = \text{number of entries} = 12
$$

**(ii) Even-direction (cardinal) steps:**
Codes 0, 2, 4, 6 are even. Counting: **0, 0, 2, 2, 4, 4, 6, 6** → 8 steps.

$$
N_{even} = 8
$$

**(iii) Odd-direction (diagonal) steps:**
Codes 1, 3, 5, 7 are odd. Counting: **1, 3, 5, 7** → 4 steps.

$$
N_{odd} = 4
$$

**Verification:** $N_{even} + N_{odd} = 8 + 4 = 12 \checkmark$

**(iv) Total chain code length (perimeter):**

$$
L_{chain} = N_{even} \cdot 1 + N_{odd} \cdot \sqrt{2}
$$

$$
L_{chain} = 8 \times 1 + 4 \times \sqrt{2} = 8 + 5.657
$$

$$
L_{chain} \approx 13.657 \text{ pixels}
$$

**(v) Compression Ratio:**

Original image size:

$$
S_{orig} = 64 \times 64 \times 1 = 4096 \text{ bits}
$$

Chain code size (3 bits per direction × 12 steps):

$$
S_{chain} = 12 \times 3 = 36 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
CR = \frac{4096}{36} \approx 113.78
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** Chain coding achieves **~114× compression** for the boundary information, an enormous reduction that makes real-time part recognition feasible on a robot's embedded processor.

*(Expected: ~1.4 marks each sub-part = 7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS IN PICTURE CODING QUESTIONS:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units:** PSNR is in **dB**, length in **pixels**, compression ratio is **dimensionless**. Always write the unit.
> 2. **Confusing 4-connected and 8-connected chain codes:** 4-connected uses only codes 0, 2, 4, 6 (4 codes, 2 bits each). 8-connected uses 0–7 (8 codes, 3 bits each). State which one you are using.
> 3. **Mixing even/odd step lengths:** Even = 1 pixel, odd = $\sqrt{2}$ pixels. A common error is to assign 1 pixel to all steps.
> 4. **Skipping the Nyquist criterion:** Any answer about image sampling that omits the Nyquist rule loses at least 1 mark.
> 5. **Not interpreting results:** A numerical answer without an engineering interpretation ("what does this mean for the robot?") loses application marks.
> 6. **Wrong base for log:** In entropy, always use $\log_{2}$ for bits. Using $\log_{10}$ gives wrong units.
> 7. **Forgetting to draw a block diagram:** For 7-mark "explain" questions, a diagram is **mandatory** — without it, maximum 4 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION SUMMARY — Picture Coding**

- **Picture coding** = converting 2D images into compact digital form for robot vision.
- **Image size** (uncompressed) = $N \times M \times k$ bits.
- **Sampling** must satisfy **Nyquist**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ to avoid aliasing.
- **Quantization** maps intensity to $2^k$ discrete levels; $k = 8$ is industrial standard.
- **Compression Ratio** $CR = S_{orig} / S_{coded} \geq 1$.
- **Redundancy** $R = 1 - 1/CR$; ranges from 0 (no redundancy) to approaching 1.
- **MSE** = average squared error per pixel; lower is better.
- **PSNR** in dB; **>30 dB** is acceptable; **>40 dB** is excellent.
- **Run-Length Encoding (RLE)**: encodes (value, length) pairs — ideal for binary images with large uniform areas.
- **Freeman Chain Code**: 8 directions (0–7); even steps = 1 px, odd steps = $\sqrt{2}$ px.
- **Chain code perimeter** = $N_{even} \times 1 + N_{odd} \times \sqrt{2}$.
- **Huffman coding** = lossless variable-length code; average length approaches **entropy** $H = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$.
- **Fourier descriptors** = boundary representation in frequency domain; rotation/scale invariant.
- **Hu moments** (7 invariants) = scale, rotation, translation invariant object features.
- **DCT** powers **JPEG**; **DWT** powers **JPEG 2000**; both used in image compression.
- **Signatures** = radial distance from centroid vs. angle; used for shape classification.
- **Skeleton coding** = medial axis transform; encodes structural topology of objects.
- **Real-time requirement:** Robot vision pipelines must process images in **< 50 ms** for closed-loop control.
- **Industrial buses** for image transfer: **PROFINET, EtherCAT, GigE Vision**.
- **Typical applications:** Pick-and-place, weld seam tracking, PCB inspection, bin picking, quality control.
- **Always include:** (1) block diagram for "explain" questions, (2) units in numerical answers, (3) engineering interpretation of results.

---

> **End of Module 4 — Picture Coding Notes**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme, PEEIT865 — Industrial Robotics*

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Object Recognition** is the computational process by which a robotic system identifies, localizes, and labels a specific physical entity within a captured scene (image, point cloud, or sensor data), assigning it a discrete identity from a known database of reference models.

> [!NOTE]
> **Object Categorization** is the higher-level cognitive-engineering task of assigning a query object to a semantic *class* (e.g., "gear", "bolt", "bearing") rather than a unique instance, enabling generalization across novel but class-similar items.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PEEIT865 – Industrial Robotics, Module 4), these two capabilities constitute the perceptual foundation that allows a manipulator or mobile robot to operate autonomously in unstructured industrial environments such as conveyor sortation cells, bin-picking stations, and machine-tending cells.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **newly hired quality-inspection worker** on an automobile assembly line:

1. The worker's *eyes* capture light → equivalent to a **camera sensor**.
2. The brain extracts *salient traits* (the number of teeth, central hole diameter, surface finish) → equivalent to **feature extraction**.
3. The brain compares these traits against *memories of thousands of gears seen before* → equivalent to **template matching / classification**.
4. The worker labels the part as "**Type-A Helical Gear**" (instance) or simply "**Gear**" (category) → this is **Recognition vs. Categorization**.

The robot replicates this human pipeline using deterministic algorithms (edge detection, descriptor extraction, classifier inference).

---

## 1.3 Core Distinction: Recognition vs. Categorization

| Aspect | Object Recognition | Object Categorization |
|---|---|---|
| **Output** | Unique instance ID | Class label |
| **Reference** | Specific model/template | Learned class prototype |
| **Variability tolerance** | Low (rigid matching) | High (intra-class variation allowed) |
| **Typical KTU Example** | "Identify which of the 12 stored CAD gears is in the gripper" | "Identify whether the part is a gear, bolt, or nut" |
| **Primary technique** | Template matching, geometric hashing, ICP | CNNs, SVMs, decision forests |

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Industrial Robotics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern KTU-evaluated robotic workcells are expected to handle **mass customization** — production lines that change SKU every few hours. Object recognition and categorization are the **enabling perception layer** for flexible automation, vision-guided robotics (VGR), and Industry 4.0 cyber-physical systems.

A robot without recognition can only operate in a **highly structured** environment with precise fixturing. Recognition unlocks:
- **Random bin picking** of unsorted parts.
- **Adaptive assembly** where component geometry varies.
- **Defect classification** in inline inspection.
- **Human–robot collaboration** where the robot must interpret arbitrary objects.

---

## 1.5 Key Terminology (KTU Board Vocabulary)

- **Feature Vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$** — numerical descriptor representing the object.
- **Descriptor** — a compact, view-invariant encoding of local appearance.
- **Training Set $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^N$** — labeled examples used to learn a classifier.
- **Inference** — applying the learned model to a novel query $\mathbf{x}_q$.
- **Confusion Matrix** — table summarizing classifier performance across classes.

---

## 1.6 Physical / Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

> [!NOTE]
> Standard metrics a KTU examiner expects you to memorize:
> - **Pixel resolution** measured in **megapixels (MP)**.
> - **Field of View (FoV)** measured in **degrees (°)**.
> - **Working distance** measured in **millimeters (mm)**.
> - **Cycle time** of a recognition pipeline typically ≤ **200 ms** for inline robotics.
> - **Classification accuracy target** in industry: **≥ 99.5 %** for sorting applications.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Canonical Recognition Pipeline

The object-recognition stack used in nearly every KTU syllabus module-4 problem follows this five-stage architecture:

1. **Image Acquisition** — 2D camera, 3D stereo, structured-light scanner, or LiDAR returns a tensor $I \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$.
2. **Pre-processing** — denoising, normalization, illumination correction, ROI extraction.
3. **Feature Extraction** — transformation of pixels into a compact descriptor $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$.
4. **Classification / Matching** — comparison against templates or learned class boundaries.
5. **Post-processing & Pose Estimation** — 6-DoF pose $(x, y, z, \text{roll}, \text{pitch}, \text{yaw})$ is recovered for the manipulator.

---

## 2.2 Stage 3 Deep-Dive: Feature Extraction Families

### A. Geometric / Hand-crafted Features
- **Edges** (Canny, Sobel): gradient discontinuities.
- **Corners** (Harris, Shi–Tomasi): high curvature points.
- **Blobs** (LoG, DoG): regions of differing intensity scale.

### B. Local Appearance Descriptors
- **SIFT (Scale-Invariant Feature Transform)** — 128-D descriptor, robust to scale + rotation.
- **SURF (Speeded-Up Robust Features)** — 64-D, faster than SIFT.
- **ORB (Oriented FAST + Rotated BRIEF)** — 32-D binary descriptor, real-time capable.

### C. Global Shape Descriptors
- **3D Point Cloud** descriptors: FPFH, PFH, SHOT, VFH.
- Used heavily in **bin-picking** for industrial manipulators.

### D. Learned Features (Deep Learning)
- **CNN feature maps** from layers of networks such as ResNet, YOLO, EfficientNet.
- Modern KTU questions often reference **transfer learning** where pre-trained ImageNet weights are fine-tuned to industrial parts.

---

## 2.3 Stage 4 Deep-Dive: Classification Algorithms

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes **three core families**:

### 2.3.1 Template Matching
The simplest recognition strategy. Distance between query feature vector $\mathbf{x}_q$ and each stored template $\mathbf{t}_k$ is computed:

$$
d_k = \lVert \mathbf{x}_q - \mathbf{t}_k \rVert_2
$$

The recognized class is:
$$
y^* = \arg\min_{k} \; d_k
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation** — fails under occlusion, viewpoint change, and illumination shift. Rarely used alone in modern industrial cells.

### 2.3.2 Statistical Pattern Recognition (Bayes / k-NN)
- **Bayes classifier** minimizes probability of misclassification:

$$
\hat{y} = \arg\max_{c} \; P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \arg\max_{c} \; P(\mathbf{x} \mid c)\,P(c)
$$

- **k-Nearest Neighbour (k-NN)** classifies by majority vote among the $k$ closest training samples in feature space using Euclidean or Mahalanobis distance.

### 2.3.3 Neural / Deep Learning
- **Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP)** — universal approximator; pre-cursor to deep nets.
- **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)** — current industrial standard.
- **YOLO / Faster-RCNN** — real-time object detection architectures popular in robotic pick-and-place.

---

## 2.4 Object Categorization Strategies

Categorization introduces **intra-class variance tolerance**. Three paradigms dominate:

| Paradigm | Mechanism | KTU-Relevant Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Supervised** | Learns mapping $\mathbf{x} \rightarrow y$ from labeled $\mathcal{D}$ | Defect classification, SKU sorting |
| **Unsupervised** | Clustering (k-Means, DBSCAN) without labels | Anomaly discovery on new parts |
| **Semi-supervised** | Few labels + large unlabeled pool | Reducing annotation cost in industry |
| **Self-supervised** | Contrastive learning (SimCLR, MoCo) | Modern Industry 4.0 research direction |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every equation** a KTU 2024 examiner may ask you to write, derive, or apply for Object Recognition and Categorization. Memorize each entry before the exam.

| $\#$ | Concept | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Euclidean Distance | $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - y_i)^2}$ | feature-space similarity; dimensionless |
| 2 | Cosine Similarity | $\cos \theta = \dfrac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y}}{\lVert \mathbf{x} \rVert \, \lVert \mathbf{y} \rVert}$ | direction-only; range $[-1, +1]$ |
| 3 | Mahalanobis Distance | $D_M = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T S^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})}$ | accounts for covariance $S$ |
| 4 | Bayes Decision Rule | $\hat{c} = \arg\max_c \; P(\mathbf{x} \mid c)\, P(c)$ | posterior maximization |
| 5 | Precision | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | fraction of relevant predictions |
| 6 | Recall (Sensitivity) | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | fraction of ground-truth retrieved |
| 7 | F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \, \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | harmonic mean of $P$ and $R$ |
| 8 | Intersection-over-Union | $IoU = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | range $[0, 1]$; bbox overlap |
| 9 | Confusion Matrix Entry | $n_{ij} = \lvert\{x : \hat{y}=i, \, y=j\}\rvert$ | counts of predictions |
| 10 | k-NN Decision | $\hat{y} = \mathrm{mode}\{y_i : \mathbf{x}_i \in N_k(\mathbf{x}_q)\}$ | majority vote in neighbourhood |
| 11 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $L = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \log \hat{p}_c$ | training objective for classifiers |
| 12 | Mean Average Precision | $mAP = \dfrac{1}{C}\sum_{c=1}^{C} AP_c$ | detection benchmark |

---

## 2.6 Why These Equations Matter in Real Engineering

- **Equation 5–8** determine whether an inline inspection robot meets the **Six-Sigma quality target** of $\leq 3.4$ defects per million.
- **Equation 1–3** are embedded inside every commercial bin-picking software (e.g., Mech-Mind, Zivid, Photoneo).
- **Equation 11** is the loss landscape navigated by gradient descent in every CNN deployed on a smart factory floor.
