## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{u} + \dfrac{1}{v}$ | Thin lens imaging equation | mm |
| 2 | $M = \dfrac{v}{u} = \dfrac{h_i}{h_o}$ | Lateral magnification | dimensionless |
| 3 | $FOV_x = 2 \cdot u \cdot \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta_h}{2}\right)$ | Horizontal field of view | mm |
| 4 | $\Delta_{obj} = \dfrac{p_x \cdot u}{f}$ | Object-space pixel resolution | mm/pixel |
| 5 | $DOF \approx 2 N_c \dfrac{u^2}{f} \cdot c$ | Depth of field | mm |
| 6 | $T_c = \dfrac{T_{travel}}{n} + n \cdot t_a$ | Cycle time per part | seconds |
| 7 | $V_{insp} = \dfrac{L}{T_c}$ | Inspection throughput | parts/hour |
| 8 | $S/N = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Vision signal-to-noise ratio | dB |
| 9 | $D_{min} = \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \cdot NA}$ | Optical diffraction-limited defect size | mm |
| 10 | $A = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | CMM standard deviation (ISO 10360) | µm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse **resolution** with **accuracy**. **Resolution** is the smallest detectable increment; **accuracy** is how close a measurement is to the true value. Robotic CMMs typically have **accuracy ≈ 3× to 10× worse than their resolution**.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Body Panels:** 100% inline inspection of weld seams using laser triangulation.
- **Semiconductor Wafer Inspection:** Sub-micron defect detection using bright-field microscopy.
- **Aerospace Turbine Blades:** CT-scanned by articulated robots for internal porosity.
- **Pharmaceutical Vial Inspection:** Vision robots detect particulate contamination.
- **Food Industry:** Hyperspectral + NIR vision robots check ripeness, bruises, foreign bodies.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pixel Resolution from Camera Geometry

> **Problem:** A 6-DOF robotic arm carries a CCD camera (sensor: $1/3$ inch, $1024 \times 768$ pixels, pixel pitch $p_x = 4.65\ \mu m$) to inspect a flat workpiece. The lens focal length is $f = 16\ mm$ and the working distance (object distance) is $u = 200\ mm$. Compute (a) image distance $v$, (b) lateral magnification $M$, (c) field of view $FOV_x$, and (d) object-space pixel resolution $\Delta_{obj}$.

### Step 1 — Apply the thin-lens equation to find $v$

$$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{u} + \frac{1}{v}$$

Substituting $f = 16$ mm and $u = 200$ mm:

$$\frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{16} - \frac{1}{200} = \frac{200 - 16}{16 \times 200} = \frac{184}{3200} = 0.0575$$

$$\boxed{v = \frac{1}{0.0575} \approx 17.39\ \text{mm}}$$

**Interpretation:** The lens forms a sharp image **17.39 mm** behind the lens — physically inside the camera body.

### Step 2 — Compute the lateral magnification $M$

$$M = \frac{v}{u} = \frac{17.39}{200} = 0.0869$$

**Interpretation:** The image is reduced to roughly **8.69%** of the actual object size — typical for inspection cameras.

### Step 3 — Compute the field of view $FOV_x$

Sensor active width:
$$W_{sensor} = N_x \cdot p_x = 1024 \times 4.65\ \mu m = 4.7616\ mm$$

Using the relation $FOV_x = \dfrac{W_{sensor}}{M}$:

$$FOV_x = \frac{4.7616}{0.0869} = \boxed{54.79\ \text{mm}}$$

**Interpretation:** The camera "sees" a **54.79 mm wide** strip of the workpiece.

### Step 4 — Compute the object-space pixel resolution $\Delta_{obj}$

$$\Delta_{obj} = \frac{FOV_x}{N_x} = \frac{54.79\ \text{mm}}{1024\ \text{pixels}} = \boxed{0.0535\ \text{mm/pixel}}$$

**Interpretation:** Each pixel corresponds to a **53.5 µm** square on the workpiece. This is the **smallest detectable feature size** — perfect for detecting hairline cracks > 60 µm.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Robot Inspection Cycle Time Optimization

> **Problem:** A robot must inspect $n = 20$ points on a circular flange of diameter $D = 300\ mm$. The maximum joint speed yields a TCP linear velocity of $v_{tcp} = 500\ mm/s$. Each point requires a vision acquisition + analysis time of $t_a = 0.4\ s$. Estimate the cycle time.

### Step 1 — Total travel distance

Assuming uniform distribution on the circumference:

$$L = n \cdot \pi \cdot D = 20 \times \pi \times 300 = 18{,}849.56\ \text{mm}$$

### Step 2 — Pure travel time (lower bound)

$$T_{travel} = \frac{L}{v_{tcp}} = \frac{18{,}849.56}{500} = 37.70\ \text{s}$$

### Step 3 — Total acquisition time

$$T_{acq} = n \cdot t_a = 20 \times 0.4 = 8.0\ \text{s}$$

### Step 4 — Total cycle time (travel may overlap with acquisition, but we take worst case)

$$T_c = T_{travel} + T_{acq} = 37.70 + 8.0 = \boxed{45.7\ \text{s}}$$

### Step 5 — Throughput

$$V_{insp} = \frac{3600}{T_c} = \frac{3600}{45.7} \approx \boxed{78.8\ \text{parts/hour}}$$

**Interpretation:** To increase throughput, one can reduce $n$ (cluster sampling), increase $v_{tcp}$ (heavier robot), or parallelize acquisition using multiple cameras.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Inspection Method Selection (KTU Decision Framework)

| Defect Type | Material | Recommended Sensor | Robot Type | Typical Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Surface Scratch | Steel, Aluminium | 2D Diffused LED Vision | SCARA / Articulated | 0.05 mm |
| Weld Bead Profile | Steel | Laser Line Triangulation | 6-DOF Articulated | 0.02 mm |
| Internal Crack | Cast Iron | Ultrasonic Probe | Heavy 6-DOF + Compliance | 0.1 mm depth |
| Subsurface Porosity | Turbine Blade | X-ray CT | Precision 6-DOF | 0.05 mm |
| Dimensional Tolerance | Machined Part | Touch Probe (CMM) | 6-DOF with calibration | 5 µm |
| Coating Thickness | Painted Panel | Eddy-Current / Terahertz | Articulated | 1 µm |
| Part Presence | PCB Assembly | 2D Vision + Backlight | SCARA | ±0.1 mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Trend:** Modern exam questions increasingly ask students to **justify the choice of inspection method** for a given scenario. Always discuss *contact vs non-contact*, *cycle time*, and *accuracy class* before naming a sensor.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Signal-to-Noise Ratio for Defect Detectability

A vision system's ability to distinguish a defect from background noise follows the Rose Criterion:

$$S/N_{min} = k \cdot \sqrt{N_{pixels}}$$

where $k \approx 3$ to $5$ for $99.9\%$ detection confidence, and $N_{pixels}$ is the number of pixels spanning the defect.

For a defect of size $D_{defect}$ with object-space resolution $\Delta_{obj}$:

$$N_{pixels} = \left(\frac{D_{defect}}{\Delta_{obj}}\right)^2$$

Solving for the minimum detectable defect size:

$$D_{defect,min} = \Delta_{obj} \cdot \sqrt{\left(\frac{S/N_{min}}{k}\right)^2} = \frac{\Delta_{obj} \cdot S/N_{min}}{k}$$

Substituting $\Delta_{obj} = 0.0535$ mm (from Derivation 1), $S/N_{min} = 5$, $k = 3$:

$$D_{defect,min} = \frac{0.0535 \times 5}{3} = \boxed{0.089\ \text{mm} \approx 89\ \mu m}$$

**Interpretation:** The system can reliably detect defects larger than ~89 µm — sufficient for surface crack inspection per ISO 8785.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram of a Robotic Inspection Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CP[Central Processing Unit]
        CPU[CPU and Vision Processor]
        CAD[CAD Reference Model]
        DB[(Inspection Database)]
    end

    subgraph ROB[Robotic Subsystem]
        CTRL[Robot Controller]
        ARM[6 DOF Articulated Arm]
        TCP[End Effector TCP]
    end

    subgraph SEN[Sensor Subsystem]
        VIS[2D or 3D Vision Camera]
        LAS[Laser Line Projector]
        LED[Illumination Ring LED]
        PROB[Tactile Touch Probe]
    end

    subgraph WP[Workpiece Stage]
        FIX[Rotary Indexing Table]
        PART[Workpiece under Test]
    end

    CPU -->|CAD compare| CTRL
    CTRL -->|Joint commands| ARM
    ARM -->|Moves| TCP
    TCP -->|Holds| VIS
    TCP -->|Optionally holds| PROB
    LAS -->|Projects line| PART
    LED -->|Illuminates| PART
    VIS -->|Image stream| CPU
    PROB -->|Touch coordinates| CPU
    FIX -->|Rotates part| PART
    CPU -->|Pass Fail log| DB
    CPU -->|Accept Reject signal| CTRL
```

**Description:** The CPU loads a CAD reference model and commands the controller to drive the robot arm along inspection trajectories. The vision camera (or touch probe) captures data from the workpiece, which is fed back to the CPU for CAD-comparison. Accept/reject signals are logged to the database for traceability.

---

## 4.2 Flowchart — Inspection Algorithm (Closed-Loop Decision Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Load CAD Model and Tolerance Specs] --> B[Robot moves TCP to Inspection Pose 1]
    B --> C[Capture Image or Probe Reading]
    C --> D[Image Preprocessing: Filter, Threshold, Edge Detect]
    D --> E[Feature Extraction: Edges, Holes, Contours]
    E --> F[Compare Extracted Features with CAD Reference]
    F --> G{Deviation within Tolerance?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Mark Pose as PASS]
    G -->|No| I[Mark Pose as FAIL and Log Defect]
    H --> J{More Poses to Inspect?}
    I --> J
    J -->|Yes| B
    J -->|No| K[Compute Overall Part Decision]
    K --> L{Any Pose Failed?}
    L -->|Yes| M[REJECT Part and Route to Rework]
    L -->|No| N[ACCEPT Part and Route to Next Station]
    M --> O[End]
    N --> O[End]
```

**Description:** This algorithm runs in real-time for each part. The decision logic follows ISO 2859 sampling principles — every pose's deviation is independently evaluated before aggregation.

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Vision vs Tactile vs NDT Inspection

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph A[Vision Based Inspection]
        A1[2D Brightfield]
        A2[2D Darkfield]
        A3[Laser Triangulation]
        A4[Stereo 3D]
        A5[Structured Light]
    end

    subgraph B[Contact Based Inspection]
        B1[Touch Trigger Probe]
        B2[Scanning Probe CMM]
        B3[Force Torque Sensor]
    end

    subgraph C[NDT Based Inspection]
        C1[Ultrasonic Testing]
        C2[Eddy Current Testing]
        C3[X ray CT]
        C4[Thermography]
    end

    OUT[Inspection Decision Pass or Fail] --> A
    OUT --> B
    OUT --> C
```

**Description:** A single inspection cell may combine multiple methods (sensor fusion). For example, a turbine blade inspection uses **vision (A3) for surface profile** + **eddy-current (C2) for crack detection** + **CT scan (C3) for internal porosity**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define robotic inspection. Differentiate between in-line, off-line, and in-process inspection with one industrial example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Robotic Inspection** is the use of industrial robots equipped with sensors to perform automated, non-destructive quality control of manufactured parts.

**Differentiation:**

| Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Off-line inspection** | Part is removed from the production line and inspected in a separate cell | Final dimensional check of a CNC-machined bracket on a robotic CMM |
| **In-line inspection** | Inspection occurs within the production flow, but as a discrete stage | Vision robot inspecting every PCB after reflow soldering |
| **In-process inspection** | Inspection happens *during* the manufacturing operation, providing real-time feedback | Touch probe measuring a bore diameter mid-cycle on a lathe, adjusting feed rate |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**List any four non-contact inspection techniques used in robotic cells. State one advantage and one limitation of machine vision.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Four non-contact techniques:**
1. 2D machine vision (bright-field/dark-field)
2. Laser triangulation / line scanning
3. Eddy-current sensing
4. Ultrasonic testing (with couplant considered contactless to the part)

**Machine Vision — Advantage:** Extremely fast (sub-second acquisition), rich data, full-field coverage.
**Machine Vision — Limitation:** Sensitive to ambient lighting, reflections, and surface finish; cannot detect sub-surface defects.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
**(a)** Explain the **functional architecture of a robotic vision inspection cell** with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of each subsystem. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A robotic vision system uses a CCD camera with sensor size $7.1\ mm \times 5.35\ mm$, $1280 \times 960$ pixels, and a lens of focal length $f = 25\ mm$. The working distance is $u = 300\ mm$. Compute:
- (i) The lateral magnification $M$
- (ii) The field of view $FOV_x$
- (iii) The object-space pixel resolution
- (iv) The minimum detectable defect size using Rose Criterion with $k = 4$, $S/N_{min} = 5$. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Architecture (7 marks):**

A robotic vision inspection cell consists of **five tightly-coupled subsystems**:

1. **Robot Manipulator (1 mark):** A 6-DOF articulated arm positions the camera end-effector with sub-millimeter repeatability at predefined inspection poses.

2. **Vision End-Effector (1 mark):** Includes CCD/CMOS camera, lens, filter, and illumination ring. Mounted on the robot flange via a precision adapter plate.

3. **Workpiece Handling (1 mark):** A rotary indexing table or conveyor orients the part such that the camera's optical axis is normal to the inspection surface.

4. **Illumination & Environment (1 mark):** Diffused LED ring lights, polarizing filters, and light-tight enclosures eliminate ambient interference. Structured light projectors may be added for 3D inspection.

5. **Central Processor (1.5 marks):** A PC with frame grabber captures images, runs preprocessing (noise filtering, thresholding), extracts features (edges, blobs), and compares them against a CAD reference using algorithms like **Normalized Cross-Correlation** or **Iterative Closest Point (ICP)**.

6. **Decision & Output Module (1.5 marks):** The processor outputs Pass/Fail signals to a PLC which routes the part to accept/reject conveyors. All measurements are logged to an SQL database for SPC (Statistical Process Control).

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $W_{sensor,x} = 7.1$ mm, $N_x = 1280$, $f = 25$ mm, $u = 300$ mm, $k = 4$, $S/N_{min} = 5$.

**Step 1 — Image distance using thin-lens equation (1 mark):**

$$\frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{25} - \frac{1}{300} = \frac{12 - 1}{300} = \frac{11}{300} = 0.03667$$

$$v = 27.27\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Magnification (1 mark):**

$$M = \frac{v}{u} = \frac{27.27}{300} = 0.0909$$

**Step 3 — Field of view (1.5 marks):**

$$FOV_x = \frac{W_{sensor,x}}{M} = \frac{7.1}{0.0909} = 78.11\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 4 — Object-space pixel resolution (1.5 marks):**

Pixel pitch:
$$p_x = \frac{W_{sensor,x}}{N_x} = \frac{7.1}{1280} = 0.00555\ \text{mm/pixel}$$

Object resolution:
$$\Delta_{obj} = \frac{FOV_x}{N_x} = \frac{78.11}{1280} = \boxed{0.0610\ \text{mm/pixel}}$$

**Step 5 — Minimum detectable defect (2 marks):**

Applying Rose Criterion:
$$D_{defect,min} = \frac{\Delta_{obj} \cdot S/N_{min}}{k} = \frac{0.0610 \times 5}{4} = \boxed{0.0763\ \text{mm} \approx 76\ \mu m}$$

**Conclusion:** The system can reliably detect surface defects larger than ~76 µm.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper, Module 4]**
**(a)** Describe the **working principle of laser triangulation-based robotic inspection**. With a neat diagram, explain how depth information is extracted. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A robot carries an ultrasonic probe to inspect a steel plate (longitudinal wave velocity $c_L = 5900\ m/s$). The probe emits a pulse of frequency $f_p = 5\ MHz$. If the time-of-flight for a back-wall echo is $t_{bf} = 12\ \mu s$ and a defect echo returns at $t_{def} = 4.5\ \mu s$, compute:
- (i) The plate thickness
- (ii) The depth of the defect from the inspection surface
- (iii) The wavelength of the ultrasonic wave in steel
- (iv) Comment on whether this frequency can resolve a 0.5 mm defect. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Laser Triangulation Principle (7 marks):**

**Principle (3 marks):** A laser line or point is projected onto the workpiece surface at an oblique angle $\alpha$. A CMOS/CCD camera, mounted at a known baseline angle $\beta$, observes the laser spot/line from a different angle. The position of the imaged laser spot on the sensor shifts **proportionally to the height** of the surface point relative to a reference plane.

**Derivation of depth from triangulation (2 marks):**

For a point $P$ at height $h$ above the reference plane, the lateral shift $\Delta x$ on the sensor is given by:

$$h = \frac{B \cdot \Delta x}{f \cdot \sin\beta - \Delta x \cdot \cos\beta}$$

where $B$ is the baseline distance between laser and camera, $f$ is the camera focal length, and $\beta$ is the camera tilt angle. By scanning the laser line across the part (using a rotating mirror or robot motion), a complete **3D point cloud** is built up.

**Applications (2 marks):** Weld seam profiling, turbine blade leading-edge measurement, body-panel gap-and-flush inspection, reverse engineering.

**Part (b) — Ultrasonic NDT Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $c_L = 5900\ m/s$, $f_p = 5\ MHz$, $t_{bf} = 12\ \mu s$, $t_{def} = 4.5\ \mu s$.

**Step 1 — Plate thickness (2 marks):**

Back-wall echo traverses the plate twice (down and up):
$$T = \frac{c_L \cdot t_{bf}}{2} = \frac{5900 \times 12 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \boxed{0.0354\ m = 35.4\ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Defect depth (2 marks):**

Defect echo also traverses twice (down to defect + back up):
$$d_{def} = \frac{c_L \cdot t_{def}}{2} = \frac{5900 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \boxed{0.01328\ m = 13.28\ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Wavelength (1.5 marks):**

$$\lambda = \frac{c_L}{f_p} = \frac{5900}{5 \times 10^6} = \boxed{1.18 \times 10^{-3}\ m = 1.18\ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Defect resolvability comment (1.5 marks):**

A defect is resolvable when its size $D_{def}$ is at least equal to $\lambda$ (Rayleigh criterion). Here $D_{def} = 0.5\ mm < \lambda = 1.18\ mm$.

**Conclusion:** A **5 MHz probe cannot reliably resolve a 0.5 mm defect** in steel. A higher frequency probe (≥ 10 MHz) would be needed, accepting a trade-off in penetration depth.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Units Mistake:** Mixing up mm with m in ultrasonic calculations — KTU strictly deducts 1 mark for unit inconsistency.
> 2. **Confusing $v$ (image distance) with $u$ (object distance):** In the thin-lens equation, always label clearly.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in ultrasonic TOF:** Back-wall and defect echoes traverse the material **twice** — students who forget this get the thickness *half* the correct value.
> 4. **Not justifying sensor choice:** KTU rewards "selecting and justifying" over merely "listing" sensors. Use a comparison table.
> 5. **Confusing accuracy with resolution** — see callout in Section 2.5.
> 6. **Skipping diagrams:** A "neat block diagram" earns at least **2 marks** in long answers — never omit it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Inspection in Robotics**

- ✅ **Definition:** Robotic inspection = robot + sensor + decision algorithm for QC.
- ✅ **Three automation levels:** Off-line → In-line → In-process.
- ✅ **Sensor modalities:** Vision, Laser, Tactile, Ultrasonic, Eddy-Current, X-ray CT.
- ✅ **Thin-lens equation:** $\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{u} + \dfrac{1}{v}$ — *memorize and apply correctly.*
- ✅ **Object-space pixel resolution:** $\Delta_{obj} = \dfrac{p_x \cdot u}{f}$.
- ✅ **Rose Criterion:** $D_{defect,min} = \dfrac{\Delta_{obj} \cdot S/N_{min}}{k}$.
- ✅ **Cycle time formula:** $T_c = T_{travel} + n \cdot t_a$.
- ✅ **Ultrasonic TOF** always multiplied by 2 (round-trip).
- ✅ **Wavelength formula:** $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$.
- ✅ **Resolvable defect rule:** $D_{def} \geq \lambda$.
- ✅ **Accuracy ≠ Resolution** — Accuracy is typically 3–10× worse.
- ✅ **Machine vision = ~70% of all robotic inspection tasks.**
- ✅ **Always draw a block diagram** in long-answer questions — it is *guaranteed marks*.
- ✅ **Justify sensor choice** with contact/non-contact, accuracy, cycle time, and cost.
- ✅ **Laser triangulation angle** $\alpha \neq \beta$ — distinct from stereo vision geometry.
- ✅ **CMM robots** require kinematic calibration (ISO 9283) for micrometer accuracy.
- ✅ **In-process inspection** enables closed-loop manufacturing — the future trend per Industry 4.0.
- ✅ **Defect classification:** Surface (vision), Subsurface (UT, eddy), Internal (CT).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Industrial Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT865 - INDUSTRIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Non-Industrial Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Non-Industrial Robotics** refers to the design, development, and deployment of robotic systems in domains **outside the traditional manufacturing and assembly line environment**. These applications exploit robotic autonomy, precision, and repeatability to perform tasks in environments that are **inaccessible, hazardous, unstructured, or socially oriented**.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEEIT865, non-industrial applications span across the following functional categories:

| Domain Class | Representative Field |
|---|---|
| **Medical & Bio-Robotics** | Surgical robots, rehabilitation exoskeletons, tele-echography |
| **Space Robotics** | Mars rovers, space shuttle remote manipulators, satellite servicing |
| **Underwater Robotics** | ROVs (Remotely Operated Vehicles), AUVs (Autonomous Underwater Vehicles) |
| **Defense & Security** | EOD (Explosive Ordnance Disposal) robots, unmanned combat vehicles |
| **Field & Agriculture** | Autonomous tractors, fruit-picking manipulators, drones |
| **Service & Domestic** | Cleaning robots, assistive personal robots, hospital delivery |
| **Hazardous Environments** | Nuclear decommissioning, fire-fighting, search & rescue |
| **Mining & Excavation** | Underground autonomous loaders, drilling robots |
| **Entertainment & Edutainment** | Humanoid robots, theme-park animatronics |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Specialist Doctor vs. Factory Worker" Analogy**
> Imagine a **factory worker** who does the same task (e.g., tightening a bolt) thousands of times on an assembly line — this represents **industrial robotics**. Now imagine a **specialist surgeon** performing a delicate brain surgery, or a **deep-sea diver** exploring a shipwreck kilometers underwater, or a **Mars rover** navigating alien terrain millions of kilometers away. These specialists operate in **unique, changing, and challenging environments** — this represents **non-industrial robotics**. The robot here is no longer a fixed machine but a **mobile, perceptive, decision-making agent** built for a specific non-factory mission.

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

- It directly maps to **CO4** of PEEIT865 (Application of robotic systems in diverse real-world domains).
- Examiners frequently ask **comparative questions** (e.g., "Compare medical and space robotics") and **application-justification questions** (e.g., "Why is a hydraulic manipulator preferred for underwater applications?").
- It is a **high-yield module** because it tests the *engineering judgment* of the student — not just rote memorization.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics for Non-Industrial Robotics

The following performance metrics are commonly referenced in KTU question papers (especially for 14-mark Part B questions):

- **DOF (Degrees of Freedom)**: $\geq 6$ for medical/space, $3$–$4$ for mobile platforms
- **Payload (kg)**: Surgical: $\mathbf{0.5}$–$\mathbf{10}$ kg, Space arm: $\mathbf{100}$–$\mathbf{500}$ kg
- **Positional Repeatability**: Surgical: $\pm \mathbf{0.05}$ mm, Industrial-grade: $\pm \mathbf{0.02}$ mm
- **Operating Pressure (Underwater)**: Rated up to $\mathbf{600}$ bar for deep-sea ROVs
- **Workspace Reach**: Surgical da Vinci: $\mathbf{0.5}$ m, Space Shuttle arm: $\mathbf{15.2}$ m

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Categorical Breakdown of Non-Industrial Applications

#### A. Medical Robotics

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024):** A medical robot is a **computer-controlled manipulator** with an artificial sensor and feedback system, used for **diagnosis, surgery, therapy, or rehabilitation** in a clinical setting.

**Sub-classifications:**
1. **Surgical Robots** (e.g., *da Vinci Surgical System*)
   - Provide enhanced **dexterity, tremor filtration, and 3D vision** to the surgeon.
   - Operate on a **master–slave teleoperation** principle.
2. **Rehabilitation Robots** (e.g., *Lokomat*, *Armeo*)
   - Used for **gait training** and **upper-limb therapy** post-stroke or spinal injury.
3. **Assistive Robots** (e.g., *PARO* therapeutic seal)
   - Provide **companionship and cognitive stimulation** to elderly patients.
4. **Tele-echography Robots**
   - Allow an expert sonographer to perform ultrasound remotely during emergencies (e.g., space station, rural areas).

#### B. Space Robotics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Space robots must survive **extreme thermal cycling, vacuum, microgravity, and radiation**. Therefore, they use **radiation-hardened electronics, lightweight composites, and often a thermal management system**.

**Key applications:**
- **NASA's Mars Rovers (Sojourner, Spirit, Opportunity, Curiosity, Perseverance)** – Autonomous navigation.
- **Canadarm (Shuttle Remote Manipulator System – SRMS)** – 15.2 m, 6 DOF, used to deploy/retrieve satellites.
- **Dextre (SPDM – Special Purpose Dexterous Manipulator)** – 2-arm, 15 DOF, used for fine tasks on the ISS.
- **ROBOTIC refueling missions** – Servicing of defunct satellites.

#### C. Underwater Robotics

- **ROV (Remotely Operated Vehicle)** – Tethered, human-controlled, no onboard intelligence.
- **AUV (Autonomous Underwater Vehicle)** – Untethered, onboard AI, used for seabed mapping.
- **Hybrid ROV/AUV (HROV)** – Switches between modes.

**Design constraints:**
- Must be **waterproof (IP68 rated)**.
- **Pressure hull** designed for crushing depth.
- **Acoustic communication** (since RF is absorbed by water).

#### D. Defense, Hazardous, and Service Robotics

- **EOD Robots** (e.g., *TALON*, *PackBot*) – Used to disarm IEDs (Improvised Explosive Devices).
- **Fire-fighting Robots** – e.g., *Tauron* (Czech) for tunnel fires.
- **Search & Rescue Robots** – e.g., *Inuktun VGTV*, snake robots for collapsed buildings.
- **Domestic Robots** – e.g., *iRobot Roomba* (vacuum), *AIBO* (Sony pet dog).
- **Agricultural Robots** – e.g., *John Deere autonomous tractors*, *Agrobot strawberry harvester*.
- **Mining Robots** – e.g., *Caterpillar autonomous haul trucks*, *Sandvik auto-drills*.

### 2.2 Critical Engineering Trade-offs

When designing a non-industrial robot, the following trade-offs govern the architecture selection:

| Parameter | Trade-off Direction |
|---|---|
| **Mobility vs. Stability** | More wheels/legs = greater mobility, but reduced static stability |
| **Payload vs. Reach** | Increasing arm length reduces natural frequency and payload capacity |
| **Autonomy vs. Reliability** | Full autonomy requires complex AI; semi-autonomy is more reliable |
| **Power vs. Endurance** | Hydraulic = high power but short battery life; electric = lower power but longer |
| **Speed vs. Precision** | Surgical robots sacrifice speed for sub-millimeter precision |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory formulas and metrics every KTU student must memorize for the Module 4 examination.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydrostatic pressure on underwater robot | $P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | $P$ in Pa, $\rho$ (water) $\approx \mathbf{1025}$ kg/m³, $g = \mathbf{9.81}$ m/s² |
| 2 | Crushing depth rating | $h_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_{allow} \cdot t}{R \cdot \rho \cdot g}$ | $t$ = hull thickness, $R$ = hull radius |
| 3 | Buoyancy force | $F_B = \rho_{fluid} \cdot V_{displaced} \cdot g$ | N — must equal weight for neutral buoyancy |
| 4 | Degrees of freedom needed (spatial) | DOF $= 6$ | 3 translation + 3 rotation |
| 5 | DOF for planar task | DOF $= 3$ | 2 translation + 1 rotation |
| 6 | Resolution of a sensor | $\Delta x = \dfrac{L}{2^n}$ | $L$ = full-scale range, $n$ = bits |
| 7 | Workspace of revolute joint | Full circle ($2\pi$ rad) | — |
| 8 | Workspace of prismatic joint | Limited by link length | — |
| 9 | Teleoperation time delay (space) | $\Delta t = \dfrac{2 \cdot d}{c}$ | $d$ = distance, $c = \mathbf{3 \times 10^8}$ m/s (speed of light) |
| 10 | Round-trip delay to Mars | $\Delta t \approx 6$–$\mathbf{44}$ min | Critical for autonomous decision-making |
| 11 | Acoustic wave attenuation in water | $\alpha(f) \approx \mathbf{0.04} \cdot f^{1.5}$ dB/km | $f$ in kHz |
| 12 | Force feedback gain (master–slave) | $F_{slave} = K \cdot (X_{master} - X_{slave})$ | $K$ = coupling stiffness |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on $\rho_{fluid}$ and $g$:** For **seawater** in KTU numerical problems, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³; for **freshwater**, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³. Always use $g = 9.81$ m/s² unless explicitly told otherwise.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The above formulas are not just academic — they are used by:
- **NASA's JPL** for designing the buoyancy hull of Europa Clipper's submersible components.
- **ISRO's Gaganyaan** project for sizing astronaut-assistive robotic arms.
- **DRDO** for EOD robots operating in the **Himalayan terrain** with low-pressure air.
- **Kerala's own C-DAC** initiatives for medical robotics in post-flood rescue operations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Hydrostatic Pressure on an Underwater ROV

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A spherical ROV is to operate at a depth of 2000 m in seawater. Find the hydrostatic pressure on the hull. The density of seawater is 1025 kg/m³ and $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Step 1: Identify the governing equation.**
For a fluid at rest, pressure at a depth $h$ below the free surface is given by Pascal's law:

$$P = P_{atm} + \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 2: Determine the atmospheric contribution.**
At sea level, the standard atmospheric pressure is $P_{atm} = \mathbf{101325}$ Pa.

**Step 3: Compute the hydrostatic component.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{hydro} &= \rho \cdot g \cdot h \\
&= 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 \times 2000 \text{ m} \\
&= 1025 \times 9.81 \times 2000 \\
&= 1025 \times 19620 \\
&= 20{,}110{,}500 \text{ Pa} \\
&\approx 20.11 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Add atmospheric pressure for the absolute pressure.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{abs} &= 101325 + 20{,}110{,}500 \\
&= 20{,}211{,}825 \text{ Pa} \\
&\approx 20.21 \text{ MPa} \\
&\approx 200.2 \text{ atm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Interpretation for KTU.**
The ROV's pressure hull must be designed to withstand $\mathbf{\sim 200}$ atm, which is why deep-sea ROVs use **titanium or thick-walled steel pressure spheres**, not standard aluminum.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Neutral Buoyancy Condition for a Submersible

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A cylindrical AUV has a dry weight of 5000 N. To achieve neutral buoyancy, what displaced volume is required in seawater?

**Step 1: Apply the buoyancy condition.**
For neutral buoyancy, $F_B = W$ (Buoyancy = Weight).

**Step 2: Substitute the buoyancy formula.**

$$
F_B = \rho_{sw} \cdot V \cdot g = W
$$

**Step 3: Solve for displaced volume $V$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \dfrac{W}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g} \\
&= \dfrac{5000}{1025 \times 9.81} \\
&= \dfrac{5000}{10055.25} \\
&\approx 0.4972 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Practical meaning.**
The AUV's **syntactic foam filling** (a hollow-glass-microsphere composite) must displace at least **0.4972 m³** of seawater to float neutrally. In practice, designers target $V_{displaced} = \mathbf{0.51}$–$\mathbf{0.55}$ m³ to provide a small **positive buoyancy margin** for safety recovery.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Time Delay in Teleoperated Space Robotics

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A teleoperated robot on the Moon's surface is controlled from Earth. The one-way distance is $3.84 \times 10^8$ m. Find the round-trip communication delay.

**Step 1: Apply the delay formula.**

$$\Delta t_{RT} = \dfrac{2 \cdot d}{c}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t_{RT} &= \dfrac{2 \times 3.84 \times 10^8}{3 \times 10^8} \\
&= \dfrac{7.68 \times 10^8}{3 \times 10^8} \\
&= 2.56 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Engineering implication.**
A **2.56-second round-trip delay** means that the operator cannot rely on visual feedback for **real-time control**. Hence, lunar rovers operate in a **supervised autonomy** mode — the operator sends waypoints, and the rover navigates autonomously to them. This is why **NASA's VIPER** rover (2024 mission to the lunar south pole) uses onboard LiDAR and AI.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Robot Type for Various Non-Industrial Tasks

| Application | Environment | Required DOF | Power Source | Communication | Recommended Robot Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Brain surgery | Sterile indoor, cramped | 6 (master–slave) | Electric, servo | Wired | Surgical robot (da Vinci) |
| Mars surface | Vacuum, dust, low gravity | 6 (arm) + 6 (chassis) | Solar + RTG | S/X-band radio | Autonomous rover (Curiosity) |
| Deep-sea pipeline inspection | High pressure, dark | 4–5 (arm) + 5 (vehicle) | Hydraulic (surface via tether) | Acoustic modem | Tethered ROV |
| IED disposal | Urban, mobile | 6 (arm) + tracked base | Battery (Li-ion) | Radio + fiber-optic tether | PackBot / TALON |
| Firefighting | High temperature, smoke | 5–6 | Battery (high-temp) | Radio | Autonomous fire bot |
| Strawberry picking | Outdoor, soft fruit | 4 (arm) + GPS (vehicle) | Diesel/electric | Wi-Fi | Agricultural manipulator |
| Vacuum cleaning | Indoor, flat | 3 (mobility) | Battery | — | Domestic mobile robot |
| Spine rehabilitation | Indoor, patient contact | 2–3 (exoskeleton) | Electric | Wired | Exoskeleton (Lokomat) |
| Satellite servicing | Vacuum, microgravity | 7–15 (dual arm) | Solar + battery | TDRS relay | Free-flying servicer (Restore-L) |
| Underground mining | Dust, confined | 4 (arm) + 6 (vehicle) | Diesel-electric | Wi-Fi / leaky feeder | LHD (Load-Haul-Dump) autonomous |

### 3.5 Decision Algorithm: How to Choose DOF for a Non-Industrial Robot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** "How many DOF does a particular robot need?"

**Step 1:** Identify if the task is **planar (2D)** or **spatial (3D)**.
- Planar → 3 DOF (2 translation + 1 rotation)
- Spatial → 6 DOF (3 translation + 3 rotation)

**Step 2:** Add **redundant DOF** if the workspace has obstacles.
- Each obstacle = +1 redundant DOF for obstacle avoidance.

**Step 3:** If the robot is **mobile**, the base itself contributes 2–3 DOF.
- Wheeled differential drive: 2 DOF ($x$, $y$)
- Omnidirectional (Mecanum): 3 DOF ($x$, $y$, $\theta$)

**Step 4:** Total DOF = Manipulator DOF + Base DOF.

**Example:** A wheeled hospital delivery robot with a 3-DOF arm → Total = 2 (base) + 3 (arm) = **5 DOF**.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Surgical Robot (da Vinci Master–Slave)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **block-level functional architecture** of a teleoperated surgical robot. The surgeon (master) sends motion commands, which are processed and transmitted to the slave robot at the patient side, with force feedback in advanced versions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Surgeon Console Master] --> node2[3D HD Vision System]
    node1 --> node3[Master Manipulator Handle]
    node3 --> node4[Position Sensor Encoder]
    node4 --> node5[Signal Processor FPGA]
    node5 --> node6[Fiber Optic Communication Link]
    node6 --> node7[Patient Side Cart Slave]
    node7 --> node8[Slave Manipulator 4 Arms]
    node8 --> node9[End Effector Instrument]
    node9 --> node10[Force Sensor Tip]
    node10 --> node11[Feedback Loop to Surgeon]
    node2 --> node11
    node11 --> node1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- `node1` is the surgeon's workstation.
- `node5` filters **tremor** (typically 8–12 Hz physiological tremor) using a low-pass Butterworth filter.
- `node10` provides **haptic feedback** (only in newer da Vinci models).

---

### 4.2 Mars Rover Navigation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Earth Mission Control] -->|Command Sequence| nodeB[Deep Space Network DSN]
    nodeB -->|X Band Radio| nodeC[Rover Onboard Computer]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Stereo Vision NavCam]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Hazard Avoidance HazCam]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Autonomous Navigation Planner]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Wheel Actuator Drive]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Robotic Arm 2 m Reach]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Sample Acquisition Drill]
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF -->|Telemetry| nodeA
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **autonomous navigation planner** (`nodeF`) is the heart of the rover.
- For every **10–20 m** of travel, the rover stops, builds a 3D map from `nodeD` and `nodeE`, and re-plans its path. This is called **"Autonomous-Goal-Oriented Mobility."**

---

### 4.3 Underwater ROV System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS[Surface Ship Control Van] --> nodeT[Tether Umbilical Cable]
    nodeT --> nodeR[ROV Pressure Hull]
    nodeR --> nodeP[Thruster Array 5 to 8]
    nodeR --> nodeM[Manipulator Arm]
    nodeR --> nodeL[LED Floodlights]
    nodeR --> nodeC[Sonar Multibeam]
    nodeR --> nodeQ[Camera HD PTZ]
    nodeC --> nodeR
    nodeQ --> nodeR
    nodeR --> nodeT
    nodeT --> nodeS
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **umbilical cable** (`nodeT`) carries **power, data, and fiber-optic video**.
- `nodeC` (sonar) is essential because **light does not penetrate below ~200 m**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    nodeY -->|Hazardous Chemical or Radiological| nodeZ1[Nuclear Decommissioning Robot]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between ROV and AUV in underwater robotics. Give one example of each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Parameter | ROV | AUV |
> |---|---|---|
> | Full form | Remotely Operated Vehicle | Autonomous Underwater Vehicle |
> | Tether | Required (umbilical cable) | Not required |
> | Power | Supplied from surface via tether | Onboard battery |
> | Control | Real-time from surface | Onboard AI, pre-programmed |
> | Example | *Jason* (used to find Titanic) | *Autosub* (NOC Southampton) |
>
> **Key distinguishing line (1 mark):** ROV is **tethered and human-controlled**; AUV is **untethered and autonomous**. (2 marks for the table.)

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three non-industrial applications of robots and justify why robots are preferred over humans in each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> 1. **Surgical robotics** – Preferred because robots provide **sub-millimeter precision, tremor elimination, and 3D magnified vision**, reducing surgeon fatigue in 4-hour procedures. (1 mark)
> 2. **Space robotics** – Preferred because the **cosmic radiation, vacuum, and extreme temperature (-150 °C to +150 °C)** would kill a human; robots like *Curiosity* have operated for 12+ years on Mars. (1 mark)
> 3. **Nuclear decommissioning (e.g., Chernobyl, Fukushima)** – Preferred because **ionizing radiation** causes acute radiation syndrome in humans; robots like *Brokk* can dismantle reactor cores safely. (1 mark)

### 5.2 Part B Question Bank (14 Marks – Internal Choice)

#### **Question A [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] – 14 Marks**

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **master–slave architecture** of a **surgical robotic system** (e.g., da Vinci). Discuss the role of **force feedback, tremor filtration, and 3D vision** in enhancing surgical outcomes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An autonomous **agricultural robot** is to be deployed for harvesting strawberries in a 1-hectare field. The robot uses a **4-DOF manipulator mounted on a 4-wheel differential drive base**. Justify the choice of DOF, sensors (cameras, LiDAR, IMU), and drive type. Discuss the challenges of **GPS-denied navigation** under the strawberry canopy. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Master–Slave Architecture of the da Vinci Surgical System (7 marks):**

**1. System Components (2 marks):**
- **Surgeon Console (Master side):** Contains a **3D HD viewer**, two **master manipulators** (essentially 7-DOF input handles), and **foot pedals** for camera control, energy activation, and clutching.
- **Patient-Side Cart (Slave side):** Contains **3 to 4 robotic arms** — one holds a 3D endoscope, the other three hold EndoWrist instruments (graspers, scissors, needle drivers, etc.).
- **Vision System:** Cold-light source, camera control unit, and HD/4K monitor stack.

**2. Master–Slave Signal Flow (2 marks):**
The surgeon moves the master handles. Each handle has **optical encoders** that measure joint angles at sub-millimeter resolution. These angles are digitized, processed by a **central control computer with FPGA-based interpolation**, and sent over a **dedicated fiber-optic cable** to the slave arms. The slave arms replicate the motion with a **scale factor of 1:1 to 1:5** (motion scaling).

**3. Tremor Filtration (1.5 marks):**
Physiological hand tremor is in the range **8–12 Hz**. The control software applies a **4th-order low-pass Butterworth filter** with a cutoff at **6 Hz**, eliminating tremor without losing intentional motion.

**4. Force Feedback and 3D Vision (1.5 marks):**
- The da Vinci Xi provides **3D HD vision** with 1080p resolution and 10–15× magnification.
- **Haptic feedback** is limited in commercial da Vinci, but research systems (e.g., *Raven II* from UC Santa Cruz) provide **bilateral force feedback** using motor torques computed from **F/T sensors at the instrument tip**.

**Conclusion:** The master–slave architecture gives the surgeon **super-human dexterity, 7 degrees of freedom, intuitive motion control, and 10× magnification**, enabling minimally invasive procedures with **smaller incisions, less blood loss, and faster patient recovery.**

---

##### **Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Agricultural Strawberry-Harvesting Robot Design Justification (7 marks):**

**1. Choice of DOF (1.5 marks):**
- **Strawberry picking is a 3D task** (the fruit is at varying heights and orientations in 3D space). Therefore, the manipulator requires **at least 4 DOF** — 3 for spatial positioning (base + shoulder + elbow) and 1 for wrist orientation to align the gripper with the stem.
- A **4-DOF base (differential drive)** provides 2 DOF for $(x,y)$ motion. Total = 2 + 4 = **6 DOF system**, which is sufficient and cost-effective (avoiding the complexity of a full 7-DOF arm).

**2. Sensor Suite (2 marks):**
- **RGB-D Camera (e.g., Intel RealSense D435):** For fruit detection and ripeness classification using CNNs (e.g., YOLOv8).
- **Solid-State LiDAR (e.g., Velodyne VLP-16):** For row-following and obstacle detection.
- **IMU (e.g., MPU-9250):** For tilt compensation on uneven terrain.
- **Force/Torque sensor at wrist:** To detect stem contact and apply controlled grip force (avoiding fruit bruising).

**3. Drive Type Justification (1.5 marks):**
- **4-wheel differential drive** is preferred over Ackermann steering because:
  - Agricultural fields have **tight row spacing (~0.5 m)**, requiring zero-radius turning.
  - Differential drive provides **simpler kinematic equations** ($v = (v_R + v_L)/2$, $\omega = (v_R - v_L)/b$).
  - **Lower cost** and **fewer moving parts** (no steering linkages).

**4. GPS-Denied Navigation Challenge (2 marks):**
- Strawberry canopies **attenuate GPS signals**, dropping accuracy from ~1 m to **>5 m** (unusable).
- Solution: **Sensor fusion** using **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** to combine:
  - **Wheel odometry** (short-term, drifts over time)
  - **LiDAR SLAM** (e.g., Cartographer algorithm) for relative localization
  - **Visual odometry from downward camera** for canopy-row tracking
- Reference markers: **Reflective stakes** placed at row ends, detected by LiDAR.

**Conclusion:** The proposed 4-DOF + 4WD architecture is a **cost-effective, accurate, and field-rugged** solution for strawberry harvesting, with **EKF-based sensor fusion** overcoming the GPS-denied canopy challenge.

---

#### **Question B [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] – 14 Marks**

**(a)** With a block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of an autonomous Mars rover**. Discuss the role of **stereo vision, hazard avoidance cameras, and autonomous path planning** in enabling safe navigation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A tethered **ROV is operating at a depth of 3000 m in seawater**. Calculate **(i)** the hydrostatic pressure on the hull, **(ii)** the displaced volume required for neutral buoyancy if the ROV's dry weight is 8000 N. Given: $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $P_{atm} = 101325$ Pa. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Mars Rover Functional Architecture (7 marks):**

**1. System Overview (2 marks):**
The Mars rover (e.g., Perseverance) consists of the following subsystems:
- **Mobility subsystem:** 6-wheel rocker-bogie suspension, each wheel independently driven and steered.
- **Power subsystem:** MMRTG (Multi-Mission Radioisotope Thermoelectric Generator) producing ~110 W continuously.
- **Thermal subsystem:** Heaters + passive insulation to keep electronics above -40 °C.
- **Computing subsystem:** RAD750 radiation-hardened CPU, 128 MB RAM, VxWorks RTOS.
- **Communication subsystem:** UHF (to orbiters), X-band (direct to Earth).
- **Payload (Science) subsystem:** Robotic arm (2.1 m, 5 DOF), drill, spectrometers.

**2. Navigation Architecture (2.5 marks):**
- **NavCam (Navigation Cameras):** A pair of stereo cameras mounted on the mast. Provide **20° stereo baseline** for depth perception. Used for **waypoint identification and long-range path planning** (up to 50 m).
- **HazCam (Hazard Avoidance Cameras):** Two pairs of **wide-angle fish-eye cameras** at front and rear. Used for **close-range obstacle detection** (within 5 m).
- **AutoNav Software:** Implements the **"Keep-Out, Keep-In" map** — every pixel is classified as SAFE, RISKY, or FORBIDDEN. The path planner uses an **A* or D* Lite algorithm** to find the safest path.

**3. Autonomous Path Planning (1.5 marks):**
The rover moves in a **sense → plan → act → sense** loop:
- Sense: Acquire stereo images.
- Plan: Build local 3D map, identify goal waypoint, plan A* path.
- Act: Drive the wheels via PID controllers at 5–25 m/h.
- Sense: Verify arrival, repeat.

**4. Communication Delay and Supervised Autonomy (1 mark):**
- One-way light time to Mars: 4–22 minutes.
- Round-trip: 8–44 minutes.
- Therefore, **real-time teleoperation is impossible**. The rover executes **commanded sequences** (sent in the morning) and reports back via **Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter** as a relay.

**Conclusion:** Mars rovers represent the pinnacle of **supervised autonomous robotics** — combining onboard AI for navigation with strategic human oversight from Earth.

---

##### **Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Depth, $h = 3000$ m
- $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $P_{atm} = 101325$ Pa
- Weight, $W = 8000$ N

---

**Part (i): Hydrostatic Pressure (3.5 marks)**

**Step 1:** Apply the hydrostatic pressure formula.

$$P_{hydro} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 2:** Substitute and compute.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{hydro} &= 1025 \times 9.81 \times 3000 \\
&= 1025 \times 29430 \\
&= 30{,}165{,}750 \text{ Pa} \\
&\approx 30.17 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Add atmospheric pressure for absolute pressure.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{abs} &= P_{atm} + P_{hydro} \\
&= 101325 + 30{,}165{,}750 \\
&= 30{,}267{,}075 \text{ Pa} \\
&\approx 30.27 \text{ MPa} \\
&\approx 299 \text{ atm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result (1 mark for final answer):** The ROV hull experiences an absolute pressure of approximately **$\mathbf{30.27}$ MPa or $\mathbf{299}$ atm**.

---

**Part (ii): Displaced Volume for Neutral Buoyancy (3.5 marks)**

**Step 1:** For neutral buoyancy, $F_B = W$.

**Step 2:** Apply the buoyancy formula.

$$F_B = \rho_{sw} \cdot V \cdot g = W$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $V$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \dfrac{W}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g} \\
&= \dfrac{8000}{1025 \times 9.81} \\
&= \dfrac{8000}{10055.25} \\
&\approx 0.7956 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Add a **safety margin of 5%** for positive buoyancy (in case of sediment or biofouling).

$$
V_{design} = 0.7956 \times 1.05 \approx 0.835 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Result (1 mark):** The ROV must displace at least **$\mathbf{0.836}$ m³** of seawater to achieve neutral buoyancy with a 5% margin.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> **Common mistakes students make in this question (and lose 1–2 marks):**
> 1. **Forgetting to add $P_{atm}$** — Examiners expect *absolute* pressure; if you only report $P_{hydro}$, you lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Unit mismatch** — Always state the final pressure in **Pa (or MPa)** and also convert to **atm** for context. Mixing units without conversion loses marks.
> 3. **Using freshwater density** — For seawater problems, use **$\rho = 1025$ kg/m³** (not 1000). This is a frequent error worth 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping the safety margin** — KTU examiners often give 0.5 marks for a *practical engineering comment* like "5% margin for sediment accumulation."
> 5. **Forgetting to write the formula before substitution** — Always state the governing equation explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 4: Non-Industrial Applications**

- ✅ Non-industrial robotics means **any robot application OUTSIDE factory automation**.
- ✅ The four pillars are: **Medical, Space, Underwater, and Defense/Hazardous**.
- ✅ **Surgical robots** use **master–slave teleoperation** with **tremor filtration** (6 Hz Butterworth low-pass).
- ✅ **da Vinci Surgical System** = 4 arms, 3D HD vision, 7-DOF instruments, motion scaling.
- ✅ **Space robots** are **radiation-hardened**, use **solar/RTG power**, and operate in **supervised autonomy** due to the **4–22 min** one-way light-time delay to Mars.
- ✅ **Canadarm** = 15.2 m, 6 DOF, 410 kg payload (used on Space Shuttle).
- ✅ **Mars rovers** use **stereo vision + hazard cameras + A\* path planning** for AutoNav.
- ✅ **ROVs are tethered**, **AUVs are untethered** — this is the single most important distinction for KTU exams.
- ✅ Underwater robots use **acoustic communication** (not RF, since water absorbs radio waves).
- ✅ **Hydrostatic pressure** $P = \rho g h$ — use $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³ for seawater.
- ✅ **Neutral buoyancy condition:** $F_B = W$ → $V_{displaced} = W / (\rho \cdot g)$.
- ✅ **Domestic robots** (e.g., Roomba) are typically **3-DOF mobile platforms** with simple bump/IR sensors.
- ✅ **Agricultural robots** face **GPS-denied canopy navigation** — solved using **EKF sensor fusion** of LiDAR + IMU + wheel odometry.
- ✅ **EOD robots** (PackBot, TALON) are **battery-powered, tracked, with 6-DOF arms** for IED handling.
- ✅ **Choice of DOF rule:** 3 for planar, 6 for spatial, +1 redundant for every major obstacle.
- ✅ **Master–slave coupling stiffness** $K$ in teleoperation determines the *feel* of force feedback.
- ✅ **Always add 5–10% safety margin** in buoyancy and pressure-hull calculations — examiners reward this.
- ✅ KTU's most-tested sub-topics: **(1) Surgical robot architecture, (2) Hydrostatic pressure numericals, (3) ROV vs AUV comparison, (4) Mars rover navigation.**
